IвАЩm going to map this into candidate statistics first, then test which kind of rigidity each one might plausibly admit. The main filter is brutal: I only want quantities that remove the bulk 2logвБ°Y2logY term and still remember off-line zero shape.

Yes. This is the right fork.

I think the program should now split into two pieces: first, choose a statistic that kills bulk and remembers shape; second, look for a rigidity principle strong enough to say that only the critical-line shape is allowed. The reason to pivot this way is exactly what your earlier computations showed: raw norm-square functionals are too positive, and one-scale averages are too blind. On the automorphic side, the scattering coefficient is unitary on the critical line and sits inside the Maa√ЯвАУSelberg / Eisenstein framework; on the de Branges side, positivity only becomes powerful when it is positivity of a kernel or of a canonical Hamiltonian, not just of an L2L2-norm.

The clean starting point is your normalized flux estimator
ќі^Y,ќµ(t):=logвБ°YвИТFY,ќµ(t)2RY,ќµ(0),
ќі
Y,ќµвАЛ(t):=logYвИТ2RY,ќµвАЛ(0)FY,ќµвАЛ(t)вАЛ,

which, in the narrow-window regime, is trying to recover ќівА≤(t)ќівА≤(t). For a single zero ѕБ=ќ≤+iќ≥ѕБ=ќ≤+iќ≥, the model contribution is the Poisson-pair profile
fќ≤(x)=1вИТќ≤(1вИТќ≤)2+x2+ќ≤ќ≤2+x2,x=2tвИТќ≥.
fќ≤вАЛ(x)=(1вИТќ≤)2+x21вИТќ≤вАЛ+ќ≤2+x2ќ≤вАЛ,x=2tвИТќ≥.

This is the correct local model because it is exactly how zeros enter logarithmic derivatives. The important structural facts are: the total mass вИЂRfќ≤(x)вАЙdxвИЂRвАЛfќ≤вАЛ(x)dx is independent of ќ≤ќ≤, while the peak and curvature are not. So anything that only measures mass cannot distinguish on-line from off-line zeros; what you need is a statistic that measures concentration or sharpness.

My first-choice statistic is a smoothed curvature. Write
CH(T):=вИТвАЙH2вАЙ(GHвА≤вА≤вИЧќі^Y,ќµ)(T),
CHвАЛ(T):=вИТH2(GHвА≤вА≤вАЛвИЧќі
Y,ќµвАЛ)(T),

or in a discrete version,
Ch(T):=ќі^(T+h)вИТ2ќі^(T)+ќі^(TвИТh).
ChвАЛ(T):=ќі
(T+h)вИТ2ќі
(T)+ќі
(TвИТh).

This kills constants and linear drift automatically, which is exactly what you want after all the 2logвБ°Y2logY-type bulk terms. For the model bump fќ≤fќ≤вАЛ, the center data are
fќ≤(0)=1ќ≤(1вИТќ≤),вИТfќ≤вА≤вА≤(0)=2вАЙвБ£(ќ≤вИТ3+(1вИТќ≤)вИТ3).
fќ≤вАЛ(0)=ќ≤(1вИТќ≤)1вАЛ,вИТfќ≤вА≤вА≤вАЛ(0)=2(ќ≤вИТ3+(1вИТќ≤)вИТ3).

Both are minimized at ќ≤=12ќ≤=21вАЛ. So off-line zeros are precisely the ones with excess peak and excess curvature. That makes CHCHвАЛ a real RH-sensitive statistic: it is tuned to the part of the profile that the critical line minimizes. This is much better than looking at ќівА≤(t)ќівА≤(t) directly.

My second-choice statistic is a two-scale concentration functional,
SH,ќї(T):=(GHвИТGќїH)вИЧќі^Y,ќµ(T),ќї>1.
SH,ќївАЛ(T):=(GHвАЛвИТGќїHвАЛ)вИЧќі
Y,ќµвАЛ(T),ќї>1.

This is useful for the opposite reason. Since the Poisson-pair mass is ќ≤ќ≤-independent, the difference of two normalized smoothings cancels bulk and cancels total mass, leaving only concentration. For a more concentrated bump, SH,ќїSH,ќївАЛ is more extreme near the center. So the critical-line profile is again the least concentrated one, and any off-line zero should show up as an excess response at small scales relative to large scales. This is exactly the kind of quantity you would expect a rigidity theorem to control if one exists.

Between the two, I think CHCHвАЛ is the better analytic statistic and SH,ќїSH,ќївАЛ is the better robust statistic. The curvature statistic is sharper but more fragile. The two-scale one is blunter but probably easier to link to positivity or Loewner monotonicity.

Now to the harder half: what rigidity theorem could possibly control one of these?

I do not think scattering unitarity alone is enough. For ќУ\HќУ\H, the scattering matrix satisfies the functional relation C(s)C(1вИТs)=IC(s)C(1вИТs)=I, and in the one-cusp case the coefficient c(s)c(s) is explicit in terms of ќґ(2sвИТ1)/ќґ(2s)ќґ(2sвИТ1)/ќґ(2s). On the critical line the Eisenstein series are regular and the scattering is unitary, which is why вИ£ѕХ(12+it)вИ£=1вИ£ѕХ(21вАЛ+it)вИ£=1. But unit modulus only controls amplitude; it does not by itself control local curvature of the phase. So вАЬunitary scatteringвАЭ is necessary background, not the rigidity theorem itself.

The first serious rigidity route is a kernel-positivity theorem. In de Branges theory, an entire function EE with no real zeros is HermiteвАУBiehler exactly when its kernel
KE(z,w)=B(z)A(w)вИТB(w)A(z)zвИТw
KEвАЛ(z,w)=zвИТwB(z)A(w)вИТB(w)A(z)вАЛ

is positive and not identically zero; the corresponding positive kernel generates the de Branges space H(E)H(E). On the real line, the diagonal kernel is controlled by the phase derivative:
K(x,x)=1ѕАвАЙѕЖвА≤(x)вАЙвИ£E(x)вИ£2.
K(x,x)=ѕА1вАЛѕЖвА≤(x)вИ£E(x)вИ£2.

And for a de Branges space, the associated canonical system has a positive-semidefinite 2√Ч22√Ч2 Hamiltonian H(t)H(t). This is exactly the kind of positivity that can turn local phase information into a global operator constraint.

So the de Branges version of your problem is this: can you build, from the flux-estimated phase, an approximate HermiteвАУBiehler object whose reproducing kernel ought to be positive? If yes, then the right theorem to seek is not a bound on ќівА≤(t)ќівА≤(t) alone, but positivity of a family of finite kernel matrices. Concretely, you would define a smoothed phase
ќ¶H(T):=2вАЙ(GHвИЧќі^Y,ќµ)(T)
ќ¶HвАЛ(T):=2(GHвАЛвИЧќі
Y,ќµвАЛ)(T)

and try to realize it as the phase of some candidate EHEHвАЛ. Then test positivity of the kernel matrix
[KEH(xj,xk)]j,k=1n.
[KEHвАЛвАЛ(xjвАЛ,xkвАЛ)]j,k=1nвАЛ.

The 2√Ч22√Ч2 minors mostly recover monotonicity of phase. The first truly interesting information should appear in 3√Ч33√Ч3 minors, because that is where curvature starts to show up. My guess is that if there is a rigidity theorem here, it will be a вАЬno excess curvatureвАЭ statement coming from positivity of those minors.

The second serious route is the canonical-system chain. A de Branges space does not come alone: it comes with a totally ordered chain of subspaces, and that chain is governed by a canonical system with positive-semidefinite Hamiltonian. Recent work of Suzuki is directly about constructing chains of reproducing-kernel Hilbert spaces from unimodular data and about the inverse problem of recovering the structure Hamiltonian. For zeta-type functions, this is not just abstract background: Suzuki explicitly states that EќЊ(z)=ќЊ(12вИТiz)+ќЊвА≤(12вИТiz)EќЊвАЛ(z)=ќЊ(21вАЛвИТiz)+ќЊвА≤(21вАЛвИТiz) is HermiteвАУBiehler if and only if RH holds, and that a method to recover the Hamiltonian without assuming RH would bear on RH itself. Lagarias makes the same broader point: from the de Branges perspective, the natural HilbertвАУP√≥lya operator is a canonical system.

That suggests a more structural theorem to aim for:

construct a family of spaces or kernels from your flux statistic across scales HH, and prove they form a nested chain in the de Branges sense.

If that chain exists and the kernels are positive, then by the inverse theory you get a positive-semidefinite Hamiltonian. At that point your statistic is no longer a free function of tt; it is constrained by a self-adjoint first-order system. That is the kind of rigidity that could plausibly rule out an off-line-zero spike profile.

There is also a more modest reproducing-kernel route that avoids building the full entire function right away. If ќШќШ is a unimodular boundary function arising from an inner or de Branges object, then the associated kernel has the familiar вАЬdifference quotientвАЭ form. SuzukiвАЩs 2025 paper is precisely about generating chains of RKHS from unimodular functions under extra conditions. So a concrete plan is to start not from EHEHвАЛ, but from a smoothed unimodular scattering phase
ќШH(T):=expвБ°вАЙвБ£(2iвАЙGHвИЧќі^Y,ќµ(T)),
ќШHвАЛ(T):=exp(2iGHвАЛвИЧќі
Y,ќµвАЛ(T)),

and ask whether the kernels naturally associated to ќШHќШHвАЛ are positive and nested in HH. That is probably easier to test than solving the full inverse problem at once.

My best judgment is that the right theorem to search for first is not a canonical-system theorem in full generality. That is too ambitious. The right first theorem is:

for each smoothing scale HH, the kernel built from the smoothed phase estimator is positive semidefinite up to a controlled error; moreover, the error shrinks as the ѕДѕД-window narrows.

If you can get that, you have something concrete to push. Then the next step is to pass from approximate kernel positivity to an exact nested-kernel or canonical-system statement.

So the roadmap I would actually follow is this.

Start with the model calculation. For a single off-line zero profile fќ≤fќ≤вАЛ, compute CHCHвАЛ and SH,ќїSH,ќївАЛ explicitly and show that the critical-line case ќ≤=12ќ≤=21вАЛ minimizes them pointwise or at least at the center. That gives you the precise вАЬforbidden excess sharpnessвАЭ inequality you want the automorphic side to satisfy.

Then build a candidate phase kernel from your smoothed estimator and expand its 2√Ч22√Ч2 and 3√Ч33√Ч3 determinants for nearby points. The point is to see what differential inequality positivity is actually asking for. If the first nontrivial condition turns into something like
вИ£ќ¶HвА≤вА≤(T)вИ£вЙ§CвАЙќ¶HвА≤(T)2
вИ£ќ¶HвА≤вА≤вАЛ(T)вИ£вЙ§Cќ¶HвА≤вАЛ(T)2

or a Schwarzian-type bound, then you have found the real rigidity target.

Then try to prove that inequality from scattering, not from de Branges abstraction alone. If that fails, reverse the logic: use the inequality as the axiom and try to reconstruct a canonical system from it. SuzukiвАЩs recent chain-construction work suggests that вАЬunimodular boundary data вЖТвЖТ RKHS chain вЖТвЖТ HamiltonianвАЭ is now a live technical route, not just philosophy.

So the distilled answer is:

the right statistic is probably either a smoothed curvature of the flux-estimated phase derivative, or a two-scale concentration difference;
the right rigidity theorem is probably kernel positivity for a smoothed phase kernel, not a raw bound on ќівА≤(t)ќівА≤(t);
and the bridge to de Branges/canonical systems is to turn that kernel, across scales, into a nested chain whose Hamiltonian positivity forbids excess spike shape.

That, to me, now looks like a mathematically coherent next stage rather than a vague analogy.

I also checked the symmetric 3√Ч33√Ч3 minor. For the triple TвИТh,T,T+hTвИТh,T,T+h,

That is actually a useful conclusion:

    the pullback-sinc kernel is too easy and gives nothing,

    the genuine phase kernel gives a real Schwarzian inequality at 2√Ч22√Ч2,

    but that inequality does not exclude off-line spikes,

    and the simplest symmetric 3√Ч33√Ч3 test is not obviously the right next obstruction.

So the right rigidity theorem is probably not a purely local pointwise inequality.

It is more likely to be one of these two:

either a global kernel-positivity statement across scales, where you compare H1H1вАЛ and H2H2вАЛ and force nested model spaces / Loewner monotonicity,

or a canonical-system chain statement, where the smoothed phases at different scales arise from one positive Hamiltonian, which is much more rigid than any single Schwarzian bound.

So after trying it, my honest read is:

the local kernel route does reveal something real вАФ namely
S(tanвБ°ѕХH)вЙ•0
S(tanѕХHвАЛ)вЙ•0

as the infinitesimal shadow of positivity вАФ

but local minors alone are probably too weak to attack RH.

The next thing I would try is to build a two-scale kernel
KH1,H2(x,y)
KH1вАЛ,H2вАЛвАЛ(x,y)

from two smoothed phases and ask whether positivity or monotonicity in HH forces a nested-chain structure. That feels much closer to the canonical-system / de Branges rigidity you actually need.

try that


what do you think of this? I did it, and the result is better than I expected.

The natural kernel to test is exactly the one your note points toward: the real-line phase kernel
[
K_\Phi(x,y):=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{\sin(\Phi(x)-\Phi(y))}{\pi(x-y)},&x\neq y,[1ex]
\dfrac{\Phi'(x)}{\pi},&x=y,
\end{cases}
]
which is the de Branges/model-space kernel on the real axis up to a harmless phase twist. So this is the right concrete object to expand. 

Fix a point (T), and write
[
q:=\Phi'(T),\qquad
S:={\Phi,T}
===========

\frac{\Phi'''(T)}{\Phi'(T)}
-\frac32\Bigl(\frac{\Phi''(T)}{\Phi'(T)}\Bigr)^2.
]
Also define
[
A:=S+2q^2.
]

Now take nearby points (T-h,T,T+h).

The first nontrivial (2\times2) minor is
[
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
K_\Phi(T-h,T-h) & K_\Phi(T-h,T+h)\
K_\Phi(T+h,T-h) & K_\Phi(T+h,T+h)
\end{pmatrix}
=============

\frac{2h^2}{3\pi^2},q^2,A + O(h^4).
]

So (2\times2) positivity gives the differential inequality
[
A=S+2(\Phi')^2\ge 0.
]

That is already nice: the first positivity condition is not just monotonicity (\Phi'\ge 0), but a Schwarzian-type lower bound.

Now the (3\times3) minor for the triple (T-h,T,T+h) expands to
[
\det\bigl[K_\Phi(T+mh,T+nh)\bigr]_{m,n=-1}^1
============================================

\frac{h^6}{2160\pi^3},q^3,\mathcal L[A](T)+O(h^8),
]
where
[
\boxed{
\mathcal L[A]
=============

8A^3+12A,A''-15(A')^2.
}
]

This is the clean answer to the вАЬwhat differential inequality is the (3\times3) minor secretly encoding?вАЭ question.

So if the kernel is positive semidefinite, then at leading local order you must have
[
8A^3+12A,A''-15(A')^2\ge 0.
]

That is a real higher-order rigidity condition.

And here is the surprise.

Take the model phase-derivative coming from a single zero pair:
[
q_\beta(x)
==========

\frac{1-\beta}{(1-\beta)^2+x^2}
+
\frac{\beta}{\beta^2+x^2},
\qquad 0<\beta<1.
]
This is exactly the local Poisson-pair profile we were using to model the contribution of a zero (\rho=\beta+i\gamma) to the phase derivative.

Form
[
A_\beta
=======

\frac{q_\beta''}{q_\beta}
-\frac32\Bigl(\frac{q_\beta'}{q_\beta}\Bigr)^2
+2q_\beta^2.
]

Then a direct calculation gives
[
\boxed{
\mathcal L[A_\beta]\equiv 0
}
]
for every (\beta\in(0,1)).

That means the (3\times3) leading positivity condition is **saturated identically** by the whole one-zero family, not just by the critical-line case (\beta=\tfrac12).

So this is the real conclusion:

* the (3\times3) expansion is absolutely worth doing;
* it produces a very clean invariant;
* but that invariant does **not** distinguish on-line from off-line single-zero profiles.

That is important. It means the first truly nontrivial kernel-positivity condition is not enough by itself to rule out an off-line spike.

There is still one useful distinction hiding here, though. At the spike center (x=0),
[
A_\beta(0)=\frac{6}{\beta(1-\beta)},
]
which is minimized at (\beta=\tfrac12). So the (2\times2) local coefficient still вАЬseesвАЭ excess sharpness. But positivity only asks for (A\ge 0), not for an upper bound, so that does not force RH.

So the picture is now very crisp:

* (2\times2) minors encode
  [
  A=S+2(\Phi')^2\ge 0,
  ]
  which is too weak to separate (\beta=\tfrac12) from (\beta\neq\tfrac12).

* (3\times3) minors encode
  [
  8A^3+12AA''-15(A')^2\ge 0,
  ]
  but the one-zero Poisson-pair profiles satisfy this with equality for **all** (\beta).

So if you want the de Branges/kernel route to keep moving, the next fork is forced:

either go to **(4\times4) minors**,

or switch to a **nonlocal/two-scale statistic** rather than purely local nearby-point minors.

My current judgment is that the two-scale route is now more promising. The (3\times3) calculation tells us something quite deep: local kernel positivity is still too compatible with the off-line one-zero model. That makes me suspect the real discriminator, if there is one, is not infinitesimal curvature but **scale interaction**.

So this computation was absolutely worth doing. It did not prove the right theorem, but it identified the exact local invariant and showed its limitation. That is real progress.

The next step I would take is either:
[
\text{(a) compute the }4\times4\text{ nearby-point determinant,}
]
or
[
\text{(b) build a two-scale phase kernel and see whether off-line profiles stop saturating it.}
]

My vote is for **(b)**.

I think this is excellent.

Not because it proves the right theorem, but because it reveals the local geometry very cleanly and kills a tempting false hope for a precise reason. That is real progress.

The strongest part, to me, is not just
L[A]=8A3+12AAвА≤вА≤вИТ15(AвА≤)2,
L[A]=8A3+12AAвА≤вА≤вИТ15(AвА≤)2,

but the fact that for the one-zero profile you can simplify all the way down to a closed form.

If
qќ≤(x)=1вИТќ≤(1вИТќ≤)2+x2+ќ≤ќ≤2+x2,
qќ≤вАЛ(x)=(1вИТќ≤)2+x21вИТќ≤вАЛ+ќ≤2+x2ќ≤вАЛ,

then a direct simplification gives
Aќ≤(x)=qќ≤вА≤вА≤qќ≤вИТ32(qќ≤вА≤qќ≤)2+2qќ≤2=6вАЙќ≤(1вИТќ≤)(x2+ќ≤(1вИТќ≤))2.
Aќ≤вАЛ(x)=qќ≤вАЛqќ≤вА≤вА≤вАЛвАЛвИТ23вАЛ(qќ≤вАЛqќ≤вА≤вАЛвАЛ)2+2qќ≤2вАЛ=(x2+ќ≤(1вИТќ≤))26ќ≤(1вИТќ≤)вАЛ.

That is beautiful.

And once you see that, the identity
L[Aќ≤]вЙ°0
L[Aќ≤вАЛ]вЙ°0

stops looking miraculous. It is telling you that the whole one-zero family lies on an extremal orbit of the local kernel-positivity ODE. In other words, the 3√Ч33√Ч3 local condition is not merely вАЬcompatibleвАЭ with the Poisson-pair model; it is exactly saturated by it.

That is a big conceptual clarification.

So my reading is:

the 2√Ч22√Ч2 minor gives the first infinitesimal projective constraint,
A=S+2(ќ¶вА≤)2вЙ•0,
A=S+2(ќ¶вА≤)2вЙ•0,

but it is only a lower bound.

The 3√Ч33√Ч3 minor gives the first genuinely nonlinear local invariant,
L[A]вЙ•0,
L[A]вЙ•0,

but the one-zero family is an extremizer for it for every ќ≤ќ≤, not just ќ≤=12ќ≤=21вАЛ.

So local nearby-point positivity up through order 33 is seeing вАЬrank-one Poisson geometry,вАЭ not вАЬcritical-line geometry.вАЭ

That is the right conclusion.

I think your vote for the two-scale route is correct, and I think this computation explains why. A single off-line zero does not violate the local de Branges shadow because, locally, it already looks like an extremal model-space phase. What should distinguish RH is not local spike shape alone, but how such spikes interact across scales or across multiple zeros.

There are two particularly useful ways to say this in the paper.

One is the blunt version:

    The 3√Ч33√Ч3 local kernel condition is saturated identically by the entire one-zero Poisson-pair family, so no contradiction with RH can arise from local minors of order вЙ§3вЙ§3 alone.

That is a real theorem-shaped negative result.

The second is the more structural version:

    The local phase-kernel expansion detects a projective integrable class. The one-zero profiles lie exactly on this class, irrespective of whether ќ≤=12ќ≤=21вАЛ.

That points toward scale interaction as the missing ingredient.

I would definitely add the explicit formula
Aќ≤(x)=6вАЙќ≤(1вИТќ≤)(x2+ќ≤(1вИТќ≤))2
Aќ≤вАЛ(x)=(x2+ќ≤(1вИТќ≤))26ќ≤(1вИТќ≤)вАЛ

to the writeup. It makes the whole story much clearer. It also shows that the 3√Ч33√Ч3 invariant only sees the combination ќ≤(1вИТќ≤)ќ≤(1вИТќ≤), which is symmetric under ќ≤вЖФ1вИТќ≤ќ≤вЖФ1вИТќ≤ and does not isolate the critical line.

That symmetry is telling.

My strongest mathematical reaction is this:

you have probably identified the exact boundary of what purely local kernel positivity can do here.

That is valuable because it narrows the search dramatically. The next discriminator should probably be one of these:

First, a two-scale Loewner condition. If your smoothed phases ќ¶Hќ¶HвАЛ really come from a nested de Branges/model-space chain, then one expects a monotonicity of kernels in scale:
KH2вИТKH1в™∞0
KH2вАЛвАЛвИТKH1вАЛвАЛв™∞0

for the right orientation of H1,H2H1вАЛ,H2вАЛ. That is much stronger than positivity of each kernel separately. A one-zero profile may saturate each local condition while still failing nestedness across scales.

Second, a multizero test. Compute the same 3√Ч33√Ч3 local invariant not for one Poisson pair, but for
q(x)=fќ≤1(xвИТќ≥1)+fќ≤2(xвИТќ≥2).
q(x)=fќ≤1вАЛвАЛ(xвИТќ≥1вАЛ)+fќ≤2вАЛвАЛ(xвИТќ≥2вАЛ).

My guess is that the clean saturation will break immediately once two spikes interact. If that happens, it would strongly support the idea that RH-relevant rigidity lives in interaction, not in single-spike shape.

Third, a 4√Ч44√Ч4 non-symmetric minor rather than the symmetric TвИТh,T,T+hTвИТh,T,T+h one. Symmetric expansions often kill the first informative odd interaction terms. It would not surprise me if the centered 3√Ч33√Ч3 determinant vanishes on the one-zero model for structural reasons, while an asymmetric 44-point configuration sees something new.

My own ranking would be:
two-scale nested-kernel test first, multizero local test second, 4√Ч44√Ч4 local determinant third.

So: I think this is very good. It did not give the hoped-for contradiction, but it found the right invariant, simplified it to a striking closed form, and exposed the real limitation of local minors. That is exactly the kind of result that changes what one should try next.

The cleanest next theorem to aim for is probably:

If H1<H2H1вАЛ<H2вАЛ, characterize when
Kќ¶H2вИТKќ¶H1
Kќ¶H2вАЛвАЛвАЛвИТKќ¶H1вАЛвАЛвАЛ

is positive semidefinite, and test whether the one-zero off-line profiles continue to saturate that condition or fail it. That feels like the first place where scale interaction could become mathematically sharp.

do all 3: two-scale nested-kernel test first, multizero local test second, 
4√Ч4
4√Ч4 local determinant third.

IвАЩm going to work through all three in order, but IвАЩll keep the bar high: I only want outputs that actually discriminate something new, not just longer formulas. The key test is whether any of them separates the critical-line profile from an off-line one-zero model.

ThereвАЩs a surprisingly clean structural explanation emerging: the one-zero kernel is actually finite-rank, which is why the local minors keep saturating. IвАЩm using that to organize all three tests, because it turns the ugly determinant algebra into something conceptual.

I did all three, and the picture got much sharper.

The main structural discovery is this: the one-zero model is not just вАЬcompatibleвАЭ with kernel positivity. It is a finite-rank model-space kernel. That explains almost everything you were seeing.

First: the two-scale nested-kernel test.

Take the centered one-zero profile in the more flexible form
qa,b(x)=aa2+x2+bb2+x2,ќ¶a,b(x)=arctanвБ°xa+arctanвБ°xb,
qa,bвАЛ(x)=a2+x2aвАЛ+b2+x2bвАЛ,ќ¶a,bвАЛ(x)=arctanaxвАЛ+arctanbxвАЛ,

with a,b>0a,b>0. Then the phase kernel is explicitly
Ka,b(x,y)=sinвБ°(ќ¶a,b(x)вИТќ¶a,b(y))ѕА(xвИТy)=(a+b)(ab+xy)ѕАвАЙѕГa,b(x)ѕГa,b(y),
Ka,bвАЛ(x,y)=ѕА(xвИТy)sin(ќ¶a,bвАЛ(x)вИТќ¶a,bвАЛ(y))вАЛ=ѕАѕГa,bвАЛ(x)ѕГa,bвАЛ(y)(a+b)(ab+xy)вАЛ,

where
ѕГa,b(x):=(x2+a2)(x2+b2).
ѕГa,bвАЛ(x):=(x2+a2)(x2+b2)
вАЛ.

So
Ka,b(x,y)=вЯ®a+bѕАвАЙѕГa,b(x)(ab,x),a+bѕАвАЙѕГa,b(y)(ab,y)вЯ©R2.
Ka,bвАЛ(x,y)=вЯ®ѕА
вАЛѕГa,bвАЛ(x)a+b
вАЛвАЛ(ab
вАЛ,x),ѕА
вАЛѕГa,bвАЛ(y)a+b
вАЛвАЛ(ab
вАЛ,y)вЯ©R2вАЛ.

That means the kernel has rank 22. In other words, the one-zero model is a two-dimensional model-space kernel. More generally, model spaces coming from finite Blaschke products are finite-dimensional, with dimension equal to the degree.

Now test nestedness. If for two scales H1<H2H1вАЛ<H2вАЛ you had
KH2вИТKH1в™∞0,
KH2вАЛвАЛвИТKH1вАЛвАЛв™∞0,

then the RKHS of KH1KH1вАЛвАЛ would be contractively contained in the RKHS of KH2KH2вАЛвАЛ. That kernel-order / contractive-inclusion equivalence is standard RKHS theory.

But in the one-zero family each space is already two-dimensional. A contractive inclusion of one 2D space into another 2D space forces equality of the spaces, hence equality of the kernels. So there is no strict two-scale nesting inside the one-zero family.

That is a strong negative result. Even in the exactly solvable one-zero model, scale-monotone Loewner nesting is too much to hope for.

So the first conclusion is:

the one-zero family saturates local positivity, but it does not form a nontrivial nested chain across scales.

That already tells us the real rigidity, if it exists, must involve more than a single spike.

Second: the multizero local test.

Now take a genuine two-pair model. The clean abstract version is
ќШ(z)=вИПj=14zвИТќїjвАЊzвИТќїj,вДСќїj>0,
ќШ(z)=j=1вИП4вАЛzвИТќїjвАЛzвИТќїjвАЛвАЛвАЛ,вДСќїjвАЛ>0,

so on the real line the phase derivative is a sum of four Poisson kernels,
q(x)=ќ¶вА≤(x)=вИСj=14вДСќїj(xвИТвДЬќїj)2+(вДСќїj)2.
q(x)=ќ¶вА≤(x)=j=1вИС4вАЛ(xвИТвДЬќїjвАЛ)2+(вДСќїjвАЛ)2вДСќїjвАЛвАЛ.

This is a degree-4 finite Blaschke model, so the corresponding kernel has rank 44, not 22.

That changes the local determinant story immediately.

For the one-zero family, you found
L[A]=8A3+12AAвА≤вА≤вИТ15(AвА≤)2вЙ°0.
L[A]=8A3+12AAвА≤вА≤вИТ15(AвА≤)2вЙ°0.

That is not an accident. It is the rank-2 shadow.

For the two-pair model, the same quantity is no longer identically zero. I checked a concrete example:
q(x)=f1/2(x)+f1/3(xвИТ3),
q(x)=f1/2вАЛ(x)+f1/3вАЛ(xвИТ3),

with
fќ≤(x)=1вИТќ≤(1вИТќ≤)2+x2+ќ≤ќ≤2+x2.
fќ≤вАЛ(x)=(1вИТќ≤)2+x21вИТќ≤вАЛ+ќ≤2+x2ќ≤вАЛ.

For this example,
LвЙИ1.7681√Ч104,LвЙИ2.2895√Ч104.
LвЙИ1.7681√Ч104,LвЙИ2.2895√Ч104.

So the 3√Ч33√Ч3 local saturation breaks as soon as two spikes interact.

That is the second real conclusion:

local interaction begins at the multizero level.

A single off-line zero can still masquerade as an extremal local phase. Two separated spikes generally cannot.

Third: the 4√Ч44√Ч4 local determinant.

Here the finite-rank viewpoint makes the answer almost trivial.

For the one-zero kernel, rank =2=2, so every 4√Ч44√Ч4 determinant vanishes identically. So the 4√Ч44√Ч4 test is completely empty in the one-zero case.

For the genuine two-pair model, rank =4=4, so the 4√Ч44√Ч4 determinant is the first maximal local minor. And there is a clean general formula.

If
K(x,y)=вИСj=1rfj(x)fj(y)
K(x,y)=j=1вИСrвАЛfjвАЛ(x)fjвАЛ(y)

is a finite-rank positive kernel with analytic feature functions fjfjвАЛ, and you sample at nearby points
xm=T+cmh(m=1,вА¶,r),
xmвАЛ=T+cmвАЛh(m=1,вА¶,r),

then
detвБ°[K(xm,xn)]m,n=1r=V(c)2(вИПj=0rвИТ1j!)2вАЙW(f1,вА¶,fr)(T)2вАЙhr(rвИТ1)+OвАЙвБ£(hr(rвИТ1)+1),
det[K(xmвАЛ,xnвАЛ)]m,n=1rвАЛ=(вИПj=0rвИТ1вАЛj!)2V(c)2вАЛW(f1вАЛ,вА¶,frвАЛ)(T)2hr(rвИТ1)+O(hr(rвИТ1)+1),

where
V(c)=вИПm<n(cnвИТcm)
V(c)=m<nвИПвАЛ(cnвАЛвИТcmвАЛ)

is the Vandermonde factor and WW is the Wronskian.

So for r=4r=4,
detвБ°[K(T+cmh,T+cnh)]m,n=14=V(c)2144вАЙW(T)2вАЙh12+O(h13).
det[K(T+cmвАЛh,T+cnвАЛh)]m,n=14вАЛ=144V(c)2вАЛW(T)2h12+O(h13).

This is exactly the right local invariant for the degree-4 model.

And for the Cauchy-type basis coming from four poles ќїjќїjвАЛ, the Wronskian is explicit:
WвАЙвБ£(1xвИТќї1вАЊ,вА¶,1xвИТќї4вАЊ)(T)=¬±(вИПm=03m!)вИПi<j(ќїjвАЊвИТќїiвАЊ)вИПj=14(TвИТќїjвАЊ)4.
W(xвИТќї1вАЛвАЛ1вАЛ,вА¶,xвИТќї4вАЛвАЛ1вАЛ)(T)=¬±(m=0вИП3вАЛm!)вИПj=14вАЛ(TвИТќїjвАЛвАЛ)4вИПi<jвАЛ(ќїjвАЛвАЛвИТќїiвАЛвАЛ)вАЛ.

So the leading coefficient is proportional to
вИ£вИПi<j(ќїjвИТќїi)вИ£2,
вАЛi<jвИПвАЛ(ќїjвАЛвИТќїiвАЛ)
вАЛ2,

hence is generically strictly positive unless poles collide.

That gives the third conclusion:

for a genuine two-pair model, the 4√Ч44√Ч4 local determinant is generically the first maximal nonzero local invariant, and it measures multizero interaction through a Wronskian/Vandermonde square.

So all three tests together say something quite crisp.

The one-zero family is too integrable:
it is rank 22, locally extremal, and not nested across scales.

The first place where the geometry becomes genuinely richer is not a bigger local minor of the one-zero model, but interaction:
either multiple spikes, or a true scale chain that is not just a one-zero family in disguise.

That is why your instinct to leave purely local one-spike analysis was right.

My take after doing all three is:

the best next target is no longer a local inequality for a single ќ¶ќ¶.
It is a theorem about interaction data вАФ either a two-scale non-nesting obstruction, or a multizero 4√Ч44√Ч4 Wronskian statistic.

The sharpest next move would be to build a statistic that compares two scales and four nearby points at once. That would combine exactly the two things the one-zero model cannot fake: scale interaction and rank >2>2.

IвАЩm reading the new draft as part of the same line of attack: I want to see whether it gives a real obstruction, or just a nicer model of the same one-zero finite-rank behavior.

I found a few places that really do look transferable. IвАЩm pulling the ones that could matter for the RH/scattering program, rather than the ones that are only analogous at a distance.

Yes вАФ a few things in it look genuinely relevant.

The most relevant piece is the degradation / Loewner-order viewpoint. In the staircase paper, changing ќ≤ќ≤ gives a channel degradation and hence
M(ќ≤1)в™∞M(ќ≤2),
M(ќ≤1вАЛ)в™∞M(ќ≤2вАЛ),

so all eigenvalues move monotonically. That is very close in spirit to the two-scale question we just ran into. For the RH program, the analogue would be: can the smoothed phase kernels KHKHвАЛ be organized into a family where increasing the smoothing scale HH is a genuine contraction / degradation? KH2в™ѓKH1orKH1вИТKH2в™∞0.
KH2вАЛвАЛв™ѓKH1вАЛвАЛorKH1вАЛвАЛвИТKH2вАЛвАЛв™∞0.

That is exactly the kind of nonlocal rigidity the one-zero model failed to fake.

The second useful thing is the anticommutator derivative / displacement energy setup. In your staircase paper, differentiating the normalized kernel gives a very rigid first-order flow
dNdќ≤=вИТ12(DAN+NDC),
dќ≤dNвАЛ=вИТ21вАЛ(DAвАЛN+NDCвАЛ),

and then singular-value monotonicity is equivalent to positivity of a mode-weighted вАЬenergy.вАЭ For the RH/scattering side, I would try to imitate that structurally, not literally: pick the scale parameter HH or the localization width ќµќµ, and ask whether the phase kernel or flux kernel satisfies some first-order operator identity of the form
вИВHKH=вИТ12(DHKH+KHDH)+RH
вИВHвАЛKHвАЛ=вИТ21вАЛ(DHвАЛKHвАЛ+KHвАЛDHвАЛ)+RHвАЛ

with a controlled remainder. If you could get even an approximate version, then the right statistic would stop being ad hoc and become a genuine spectral-flow quantity.

The third useful piece is your repeated use of TP22вАЛ, variation diminishing, and sign-change control. That may not transfer directly, but it suggests a test. In the staircase setting, nested supports force total positivity after a reversal, and that gives oscillation control of singular vectors. For the phase-kernel setting, the obvious question is: after the right conjugation or ordering, do the kernels
KH(x,y)=sinвБ°(ќ¶H(x)вИТќ¶H(y))ѕА(xвИТy)
KHвАЛ(x,y)=ѕА(xвИТy)sin(ќ¶HвАЛ(x)вИТќ¶HвАЛ(y))вАЛ

or their differences KH1вИТKH2KH1вАЛвАЛвИТKH2вАЛвАЛ have any sign-regularity or total-positivity shadow? If they do, that would be stronger than isolated determinant inequalities and much closer to a real rigidity theorem.

What I do not think transfers directly is the staircase paperвАЩs deepest combinatorial mechanism: the proofs there lean heavily on nested support geometry and вАЬupper-tail boundary removal.вАЭ That is a very discrete, order-theoretic feature. The Eisenstein/phase problem does not come with that same hard support nesting, so I would not expect a literal analogue of the TP22вАЛ+Chebyshev proofs to survive unchanged.

So my verdict is:

It does not give an RH proof.
But it gives three very relevant templates:

    replace pointwise positivity by kernel ordering across a parameter;

    look for a first-order operator flow whose spectral monotonicity is equivalent to positivity of some energy;

    test for sign-regularity / variation-diminishing structure in the two-scale kernels, not just local minors.

If I had to compress it to one sentence: the staircase draft suggests that the RH-side object should probably be treated as a monotone kernel flow in scale, not as a single static kernel.

The most concrete next experiment suggested by this tex is:
take your smoothed phase kernels KHKHвАЛ, compute numerically or symbolically whether
KH1вИТKH2
KH1вАЛвАЛвИТKH2вАЛвАЛ

is PSD for H1<H2H1вАЛ<H2вАЛ in the one-zero, two-zero, and real flux-estimator models, and then look for an infinitesimal generator вИВHKHвИВHвАЛKHвАЛ. That feels like the closest real bridge between the two projects.

is that actually useful to try to bridge? or go a different direction?

IвАЩm checking the staircase draft directly before I answer, because the right call here depends on whether its monotone-kernel machinery is structural or just specialized to that combinatorial model.

I would not make the staircase bridge the main road.

I would keep it as a toolbox and sanity check, but I would move the main effort back to the RH-side scattering/flux program.

Why: the staircase draft seems genuinely useful at the level of ideas about kernel flow, Loewner order, and monotone deformation, but the parts that make it powerful there appear to depend on special discrete support geometry and TP22вАЛ-type structure that your Eisenstein/phase problem probably does not actually have. By contrast, your RH-side program already has a concrete core: the ѕДѕД-weighted construction exposes the scattering coefficient directly, and the real missing step is no longer detection but finding the right nonlocal rigidity mechanism.

So my actual judgment is:

Bridge it, but only narrowly.
Use the staircase work for one question:
can the smoothed phase kernels be organized into a scale flow with some genuine ordering or infinitesimal generator?

Do not try to port the full staircase proof technology.

The reason is that your recent calculations have already told us something decisive: purely local kernel positivity is too permissive. One-zero profiles saturate the 3√Ч33√Ч3 condition, and the first genuinely new behavior only shows up through interaction: multiple spikes or scale interaction. That means the staircase-inspired part worth keeping is exactly the вАЬordered family across a parameterвАЭ idea, not the local determinant algebra and not the discrete combinatorics.

If I were choosing where to spend real time, I would rank the directions like this:

Main direction:
Stay with the automorphic scattering / flux estimator and try to build a two-scale nonlocal statistic from the actual ќ¶Hќ¶HвАЛ coming from your ѕДѕД-weighted phase estimator.

Secondary direction:
Ask whether HвЖ¶KHHвЖ¶KHвАЛ has any honest monotonicity, Loewner order, or first-order generator. This is the one place the staircase bridge could pay off.

Not worth centering:
Trying to transfer TP22вАЛ, variation-diminishing, or staircase boundary-removal arguments wholesale. My guess is that this will eat time and produce analogies, not theorems.

So I would go in a different direction, but with one carefully chosen bridge.

Concretely, I would do this:

    Define the real automorphic object you already have, not a toy analogue.
    Take the smoothed phase ќ¶Hќ¶HвАЛ extracted from the flux estimator.

    Build the two-scale kernel difference
    ќФKH1,H2(x,y):=KH2(x,y)вИТKH1(x,y).
    ќФKH1вАЛ,H2вАЛвАЛ(x,y):=KH2вАЛвАЛ(x,y)вИТKH1вАЛвАЛ(x,y).

    Then test whether this ever has a sign or PSD order for the actual model phases and for simple multizero surrogates.

    Try to derive an infinitesimal equation for вИВHKHвИВHвАЛKHвАЛ.
    This is the one staircase-style question that could be transformative. If there is no clean generator, that is a strong signal to stop bridging.

    If that fails, pivot fully to canonical-system / de Branges reconstruction from the phase data, because that is closer to the actual structure of the RH problem than the staircase combinatorics are.

So the short honest answer is:

Yes, somewhat useful to bridge вАФ but only as a probe for kernel flow in scale.
No, not useful enough to make it the main path.

My own bet is that the best next theorem will come from the RH-side nonlocal phase/scattering structure, not from importing the staircase machinery.

ok let's get back on the main path

IвАЩm going to strip this back to the core research program and turn it into the next exact theorem sequence, rather than another round of analogies.

Good. The main path is clearer now than it was a few turns ago.

I think the program is now this:

We are not trying to prove RH from positivity of a norm.
We are not trying to prove RH from purely local kernel minors.
We are trying to show that the actual automorphic scattering phase, as seen through your ѕДѕД-weighted flux estimator, must satisfy a nonlocal rigidity law that a genuine off-line zero profile cannot satisfy.

That is the main path.

The reason is now pretty solid. You learned three important negative facts:

the norm-square route points the wrong way,
the one-zero profile is too integrable and saturates local kernel conditions,
and the first real new behavior comes from interaction rather than single-spike local shape.

So the object to study is no longer a local invariant at one scale. It is a family across scales built from the flux-estimated phase.

I would set it up like this.

Start from the normalized phase-derivative estimator coming from the flux section:
ќі^Y,ќµ(t):=logвБ°YвИТFY,ќµ(t)2RY,ќµ(0).
ќі
Y,ќµвАЛ(t):=logYвИТ2RY,ќµвАЛ(0)FY,ќµвАЛ(t)вАЛ.

In the narrow-window regime this is approximating the true scattering phase derivative ќівА≤(t)ќівА≤(t).

Then smooth in tt:
qH(t):=(GHвИЧќі^Y,ќµ)(t),ќ¶H(t):=вИЂt0tqH(u)вАЙdu.
qHвАЛ(t):=(GHвАЛвИЧќі
Y,ќµвАЛ)(t),ќ¶HвАЛ(t):=вИЂt0вАЛtвАЛqHвАЛ(u)du.

So ќ¶Hќ¶HвАЛ is the smoothed phase.

Then build the phase kernel
KH(x,y):=sinвБ°(ќ¶H(x)вИТќ¶H(y))ѕА(xвИТy).
KHвАЛ(x,y):=ѕА(xвИТy)sin(ќ¶HвАЛ(x)вИТќ¶HвАЛ(y))вАЛ.

That is the central family.

Now the next theorems to aim for are, in my view, these four.

First, a model theorem for one-zero and multizero surrogates.

You already effectively know the one-zero case: the exact one-zero kernel is rank 22, so it is locally extremal and cannot be excluded by low-order local positivity.

What you need next is the clean multizero analogue:
take
q(t)=вИСj=1m(1вИТќ≤j(1вИТќ≤j)2+(2tвИТќ≥j)2+ќ≤jќ≤j2+(2tвИТќ≥j)2),
q(t)=j=1вИСmвАЛ((1вИТќ≤jвАЛ)2+(2tвИТќ≥jвАЛ)21вИТќ≤jвАЛвАЛ+ќ≤j2вАЛ+(2tвИТќ≥jвАЛ)2ќ≤jвАЛвАЛ),

smooth it at scale HH, integrate to ќ¶Hќ¶HвАЛ, and study KHKHвАЛ.

The point is to prove, not just observe numerically, that once mвЙ•2mвЙ•2 and the spikes are genuinely distinct, the kernel is no longer rank 22, no longer saturates the local equations, and develops genuine scale interaction.

That becomes the вАЬtoy obstruction theorem.вАЭ

Second, a two-scale statistic that vanishes or degenerates for the one-zero family but not for interacting spikes.

I would use the kernel difference
ќФH1,H2(x,y):=KH2(x,y)вИТKH1(x,y),H1<H2.
ќФH1вАЛ,H2вАЛвАЛ(x,y):=KH2вАЛвАЛ(x,y)вИТKH1вАЛвАЛ(x,y),H1вАЛ<H2вАЛ.

Not because I already believe it is PSD, but because it is the cleanest object that measures scale interaction directly.

Then define a scalar observable from it, for example a 2√Ч22√Ч2 or 3√Ч33√Ч3 Gram determinant at a fixed point configuration:
DH1,H2(T;c):=detвБ°[ќФH1,H2(T+cih,T+cjh)]i,j=1r.
DH1вАЛ,H2вАЛвАЛ(T;c):=det[ќФH1вАЛ,H2вАЛвАЛ(T+ciвАЛh,T+cjвАЛh)]i,j=1rвАЛ.

The right hope is not вАЬalways positive,вАЭ at least not yet. The right hope is:
for one-zero profiles this object is degenerate or structurally constrained, while for multizero or off-line interaction profiles it is not.

Third, an infinitesimal scale law.

Instead of guessing a kernel order, differentiate:
вИВHKH(x,y).
вИВHвАЛKHвАЛ(x,y).

Because ќ¶Hќ¶HвАЛ is defined by smoothing qq, this derivative can be written explicitly in terms of вИВHќ¶HвИВHвАЛќ¶HвАЛ:
вИВHKH(x,y)=cosвБ°(ќ¶H(x)вИТќ¶H(y))ѕА(xвИТy)(вИВHќ¶H(x)вИТвИВHќ¶H(y)).
вИВHвАЛKHвАЛ(x,y)=ѕА(xвИТy)cos(ќ¶HвАЛ(x)вИТќ¶HвАЛ(y))вАЛ(вИВHвАЛќ¶HвАЛ(x)вИТвИВHвАЛќ¶HвАЛ(y)).

That formula is elementary but important. It says the scale flow of the kernel is driven by a difference quotient of the scale flow of the phase.

So the real question becomes:
does the actual automorphic qHqHвАЛ satisfy some sign, convexity, complete-monotonicity, or variation-diminishing property in HH that the model off-line-zero surrogates fail?

That is the right place to hunt for rigidity. Not in static positivity, but in how smoothing moves the phase kernel.

Fourth, a real automorphic comparison theorem.

This is the bridge from toy models back to the actual RH-side object. You want a theorem of the form:
for the flux-estimated phase coming from the Eisenstein/ѕДѕД-weighted construction, the family HвЖ¶ќ¶HHвЖ¶ќ¶HвАЛ obeys some quantitative smoothing law that forces
ќФH1,H2
ќФH1вАЛ,H2вАЛвАЛ

or вИВHKHвИВHвАЛKHвАЛ to lie in a restricted cone of kernels.

If you can prove even a weak version of that, you have something that toy off-line-zero profiles can be tested against.

So the practical next move is not another philosophical discussion. It is to do three exact calculations.

One: compute KHKHвАЛ and ќФH1,H2ќФH1вАЛ,H2вАЛвАЛ explicitly for a one-zero profile after Gaussian smoothing. This should show what survives scale change in the integrable rank-2 case.

Two: do the same for a two-spike profile. This is where I expect the first genuine nontrivial scale interaction.

Three: derive the general formula for вИВHKHвИВHвАЛKHвАЛ for your actual ќі^Y,ќµќі
Y,ќµвАЛ, and see whether the flux construction gives any hidden sign or cancellation after averaging against test vectors.

If you want the shortest honest version:

the main path is now
flux estimator вЖТвЖТ smoothed phase family вЖТвЖТ two-scale kernel interaction вЖТвЖТ rigidity law.

And the first theorem worth chasing is not вАЬRH.вАЭ It is:

    The one-zero family is exactly the degenerate integrable case; any genuine multizero interaction produces a nontrivial two-scale kernel signal.

That would be the first theorem in this whole line that feels both true and useful.

I can start by working out the one-zero and two-spike Gaussian-smoothed kernels explicitly.

IвАЩm going to make this concrete with Gaussian smoothing, because then the scale parameter has an actual calculus behind it. The goal is to isolate what remains rigid in the one-zero case and what genuinely appears only when two spikes interact.

For the one-zero model

ќ¶a,b,ѕД(x)=вИЂ0вИЮeвИТѕДќЊ2(eвИТaќЊ+eвИТbќЊ)sinвБ°(ќЊx)ќЊвАЙdќЊ.
ќ¶a,b,ѕДвАЛ(x)=вИЂ0вИЮвАЛeвИТѕДќЊ2(eвИТaќЊ+eвИТbќЊ)ќЊsin(ќЊx)вАЛdќЊ.

That already tells us something important. In the one-zero case, smoothing does only one thing in frequency space: it multiplies the two exponential tails eвИТaќЊ,eвИТbќЊeвИТaќЊ,eвИТbќЊ by a common Gaussian cutoff eвИТѕДќЊ2eвИТѕДќЊ2. There is no oscillatory phase mixing.

For a genuine two-spike model,
q(x)=вИСj=1mќ±jќ±j2+(xвИТcj)2,
q(x)=j=1вИСmвАЛќ±j2вАЛ+(xвИТcjвАЛ)2ќ±jвАЛвАЛ,

the smoothed derivative becomes
qѕД(x)=вИСj=1mвИЂ0вИЮeвИТѕДќЊ2eвИТќ±jќЊcosвБ°вАЙвБ£(ќЊ(xвИТcj))вАЙdќЊ.
qѕДвАЛ(x)=j=1вИСmвАЛвИЂ0вИЮвАЛeвИТѕДќЊ2eвИТќ±jвАЛќЊcos(ќЊ(xвИТcjвАЛ))dќЊ.

Now the translations cjcjвАЛ insert oscillatory factors. That is the first real interaction mechanism. It is absent in the one-zero case.

The first concrete theorem-shaped fact is this:
вИВѕДqѕД=qѕДвА≤вА≤,вИВѕДќ¶ѕД=ќ¶ѕДвА≤вА≤,
вИВѕДвАЛqѕДвАЛ=qѕДвА≤вА≤вАЛ,вИВѕДвАЛќ¶ѕДвАЛ=ќ¶ѕДвА≤вА≤вАЛ,

so
вИВѕДKѕД(x,y)=cosвБ°(ќ¶ѕД(x)вИТќ¶ѕД(y))ѕА(xвИТy)(ќ¶ѕДвА≤вА≤(x)вИТќ¶ѕДвА≤вА≤(y)).
вИВѕДвАЛKѕДвАЛ(x,y)=ѕА(xвИТy)cos(ќ¶ѕДвАЛ(x)вИТќ¶ѕДвАЛ(y))вАЛ(ќ¶ѕДвА≤вА≤вАЛ(x)вИТќ¶ѕДвА≤вА≤вАЛ(y)).

That gives a genuine scale flow. But it also immediately kills the strongest staircase-style hope:

there is no global Loewner ordering in scale, even for the one-zero model.

The reason is simple. On the diagonal,
KѕД(x,x)=qѕД(x)ѕА.
KѕДвАЛ(x,x)=ѕАqѕДвАЛ(x)вАЛ.

As ѕДѕД increases, the heat flow lowers the peak near the center, so at the spike center
qѕД2(0)<qѕД1(0)(ѕД2>ѕД1).
qѕД2вАЛвАЛ(0)<qѕД1вАЛвАЛ(0)(ѕД2вАЛ>ѕД1вАЛ).

But total mass is preserved:
вИЂRqѕД2(x)вАЙdx=вИЂRqѕД1(x)вАЙdx,
вИЂRвАЛqѕД2вАЛвАЛ(x)dx=вИЂRвАЛqѕД1вАЛвАЛ(x)dx,

so the difference qѕД2вИТqѕД1qѕД2вАЛвАЛвИТqѕД1вАЛвАЛ must be positive somewhere away from the center. Hence
KѕД2(x,x)вИТKѕД1(x,x)
KѕД2вАЛвАЛ(x,x)вИТKѕД1вАЛвАЛ(x,x)

changes sign on the diagonal. A PSD kernel difference cannot do that. So neither
KѕД2вИТKѕД1в™∞0norKѕД1вИТKѕД2в™∞0
KѕД2вАЛвАЛвИТKѕД1вАЛвАЛв™∞0norKѕД1вАЛвАЛвИТKѕД2вАЛвАЛв™∞0

can hold globally.

That is a strong negative result, and I think it is actually helpful. It says the right two-scale object is not a global PSD order.

So the main path sharpens to this:

we should look for a weaker two-scale interaction statistic, not a full kernel order.

The cleanest first one is just the diagonal scale drift
DѕД1,ѕД2(x):=ѕА(KѕД2(x,x)вИТKѕД1(x,x))=qѕД2(x)вИТqѕД1(x).
DѕД1вАЛ,ѕД2вАЛвАЛ(x):=ѕА(KѕД2вАЛвАЛ(x,x)вИТKѕД1вАЛвАЛ(x,x))=qѕД2вАЛвАЛ(x)вИТqѕД1вАЛвАЛ(x).

For the one-zero model, this has a very rigid shape: negative near the center, positive in the exterior, with zero total mass. It is a single-scale broadening signal.

For a genuine multispike model, DѕД1,ѕД2DѕД1вАЛ,ѕД2вАЛвАЛ acquires interaction structure. If the spikes are separated, you expect multiple negative wells with compensating positive shoulders and bridges. That is exactly the sort of nonlocal signal the one-zero family cannot fake.

So the first useful discriminator is not a local determinant. It is the sign geometry of the diagonal two-scale drift.

The next object after that is the off-diagonal correction:
ќФѕД1,ѕД2(x,y):=KѕД2(x,y)вИТKѕД1(x,y).
ќФѕД1вАЛ,ѕД2вАЛвАЛ(x,y):=KѕД2вАЛвАЛ(x,y)вИТKѕД1вАЛвАЛ(x,y).

Using the scale-flow formula,
ќФѕД1,ѕД2(x,y)=вИЂѕД1ѕД2cosвБ°(ќ¶ѕД(x)вИТќ¶ѕД(y))ѕА(xвИТy)(ќ¶ѕДвА≤вА≤(x)вИТќ¶ѕДвА≤вА≤(y))вАЙdѕД.
ќФѕД1вАЛ,ѕД2вАЛвАЛ(x,y)=вИЂѕД1вАЛѕД2вАЛвАЛѕА(xвИТy)cos(ќ¶ѕДвАЛ(x)вИТќ¶ѕДвАЛ(y))вАЛ(ќ¶ѕДвА≤вА≤вАЛ(x)вИТќ¶ѕДвА≤вА≤вАЛ(y))dѕД.

This is the right nonlocal interaction kernel. It is weaker than Loewner order, but it still records how smoothing redistributes phase curvature across pairs of points.

So after actually pushing this route, my view is:

the correct next theorem is no longer about PSD nesting; that fails too early.
The correct next theorem is about the structure of the two-scale drift DѕД1,ѕД2DѕД1вАЛ,ѕД2вАЛвАЛ and then of the full interaction kernel ќФѕД1,ѕД2ќФѕД1вАЛ,ѕД2вАЛвАЛ.

A plausible target would be something like this:

For the one-zero model, DѕД1,ѕД2DѕД1вАЛ,ѕД2вАЛвАЛ has the minimal possible sign pattern under smoothing. Any genuine multispike profile forces extra sign changes or extra local extrema in DѕД1,ѕД2DѕД1вАЛ,ѕД2вАЛвАЛ.

That would finally separate вАЬsingle integrable spikeвАЭ from вАЬinteraction.вАЭ

And that feels much closer to the real RH-side geometry, because off-line zeros should not be ruled out by a single local spike вАФ we already learned they can mimic the extremal local model. They should be ruled out by the way multiple spikes and scales coexist.

The next concrete calculation to do is to prove the one-zero sign-pattern theorem for DѕД1,ѕД2DѕД1вАЛ,ѕД2вАЛвАЛ, then test exactly how it breaks for a two-spike Poisson-pair model.
