McoreвИЙspan{A}.
McoreвАЛвИИ/span{A}.

If that holds, then some transverse functional exists.

So the strategy breaks only if the enlarged anomaly matrix collapses onto the value line.

That is a much smaller bad set than you might fear.

In fact, generically:

    вЯ®A,McoreвЯ©FвЙ†0вЯ®A,McoreвАЛвЯ©FвАЛоА†=0 is an open condition,

    McoreвИЙspan{A}McoreвАЛвИИ/span{A} is also an open condition.

So from a dimension-counting point of view, the bad cases are nongeneric.
Bottom line

This test gives a meaningful answer:
For the first slightly enlarged-core case, the proof architecture is stable under sufficiently small perturbations.
For the first slightly enlarged-core case, the proof architecture is stable under sufficiently small perturbations.вАЛ

What it does not show is that every minimally enlarged core is perturbative in that sense.

So the next real mathematical question is:

    Is the first extra nearby zero outside the pair core necessarily small after corrected whitening, or can it contribute at the same scale as the pair anomaly?

If it is small, the current architecture likely survives.

If it is same-scale, you need the explicit enlarged-core matrix McoreMcoreвАЛ and must check the two conditions directly.

ok let's answer that

I donвАЩt think it is necessarily small.

My best honest answer is:
an extra nearby zero outside the pair core can, in principle, contribute at the same scale after corrected whitening.
an extra nearby zero outside the pair core can, in principle, contribute at the same scale after corrected whitening.вАЛ

So the answer is probably no, not automatically perturbative.

HereвАЩs why.

The current pair anomaly lives at a microscopic scale where:

    the mixed block is sensitive to phase geometry on the sвИЉQвИТ1sвИЉQвИТ1 or QвИТ2QвИТ2 window,

    whitening normalizes the same-point blocks but does not erase genuine local interaction structure,

    and the surviving anomaly signal is order u2u2 on the toy side.

A zero that is still вАЬnearbyвАЭ in exactly that local regime enters the same explicit finite-ss formulas as the original pair. There is no general mechanism in the current setup that forces its contribution to pick up an extra small factor just because it was not included in the original core.

So unless you impose some further separation condition, an extra nearby zero can be same-scale.

More concretely, there are two regimes.
Regime 1: genuinely perturbative extra zero

If the extra zero is outside the core by enough that, after corrected whitening, its contribution matrix EE satisfies
вИ•EвИ•вЙ™вИ•M(d)вИ•,
вИ•EвИ•вЙ™вИ•M(d)вИ•,

then the earlier continuity argument applies. In that case the pair-model endgame is stable.

This is the pleasant case.
Regime 2: same-scale extra zero

If the extra zero sits in the same microscopic zone and its corrected contribution is of the same order as M(d)M(d), then you do not get perturbative stability for free. In that case
M\core=M(d)+E
M\coreвАЛ=M(d)+E

with вИ•EвИ•вЙНвИ•M(d)вИ•вИ•EвИ•вЙНвИ•M(d)вИ•, and the pair model is no longer enough to justify the contradiction.

That does not automatically kill the strategy, but it means the right object is the enlarged-core matrix M\coreM\coreвАЛ, not the original pair matrix.

So the real answer is:
the first extra nearby zero is not guaranteed to be small; it may be same-scale.
the first extra nearby zero is not guaranteed to be small; it may be same-scale.вАЛ

That has an important consequence.

The fixed-core repair is still the right architecture, but the core cannot just be вАЬthe original pairвАЭ unless you can prove no other nearby zero is same-scale after whitening. Without that, the natural core should be:

    the minimal finite set of nearby zeros whose corrected contribution is same-scale in the calibration or transverse channels.

That is the right definition.

So the remaining theorem is not:

    every auxiliary near zero is harmless,

but rather:

    there is a maximal same-scale finite core, and everything outside that core is negligible.

That is a different and more believable theorem.

If you want the sharpest formulation:
The correct next target is a finite-core extraction theorem, not a harmless-auxiliary theorem.
The correct next target is a finite-core extraction theorem, not a harmless-auxiliary theorem.вАЛ

It would say something like:

    among nearby zeros, collect all contributions that are not o(u\core2)o(u\core2вАЛ) in the transverse channel or not o((x/B)S\core)o((x/B)S\coreвАЛ) in calibration;

    that finite set is the core;

    the remainder is negligible.

That theorem would preserve the fixed-core architecture while admitting that the core may be larger than a pair.

So the bottom line is:

    the extra nearby zero is not necessarily perturbative;

    therefore the pair-core model is not automatically stable;

    the proof likely needs a finite-core extraction step if it is to survive.


ok can we do that finite-core extraction step?

Not honestly, not from the current machinery.

I think we can formulate the finite-core extraction step cleanly, and maybe make it the new target theorem, but I do not think we can prove it right now without a new idea.

Here is why.

A finite-core extraction theorem would need to say something like:
ќФќґ\corr,R=ќФ\core\corr+ќФ\rem\corr,
ќФќґ\corr,RвАЛ=ќФ\core\corrвАЛ+ќФ\rem\corrвАЛ,

where the core is a finite set of nearby zeros chosen so that every zero outside the core is negligible in both:
ќ®x,d(ќФ\rem\corr)=oвАЙвБ£(xBS\core(m)),ќ¶K(ќФ\rem\corr)=o(u\core2).
ќ®x,dвАЛ(ќФ\rem\corrвАЛ)=o(BxвАЛS\coreвАЛ(m)),ќ¶KвАЛ(ќФ\rem\corrвАЛ)=o(u\core2вАЛ).

The problem is: the current paper does not give a principled way to identify which nearby zeros are вАЬsame-scaleвАЭ and which are negligible before you already understand the corrected whitening map well enough to measure their channel contributions one by one.

So there are two real missing ingredients.

First, you would need a local contribution decomposition theorem: a theorem saying the corrected whitened block can be decomposed into approximately additive contributions from individual nearby zeros or small clusters, with controllable interaction error. Right now, corrected whitening is nonlinear:
ќ©^=GвИТвИТ1/2NвАЙG+вИТ1/2,
ќ©
=GвИТвИТ1/2вАЛNG+вИТ1/2вАЛ,

so contributions do not simply add zero-by-zero after whitening. Without near-additivity, вАЬextract the finite non-negligible onesвАЭ is not yet a theorem, just an intuition.

Second, you would need a ranking principle: some monotone quantity showing that as zeros move farther from the midpoint, their corrected contribution in calibration/transverse channels decays in a way that lets you select a finite maximal core. The current draft has that kind of decay only for the far smooth tail, not for the auxiliary near zone.

So my honest answer is:
We can state a finite-core extraction theorem, but we cannot prove it from the present tools.
We can state a finite-core extraction theorem, but we cannot prove it from the present tools.вАЛ

What we can do now is write the exact theorem target. It would be something like this:

    Finite-core extraction theorem.
    For each admissible midpoint mm, there exists a finite set C(m)C(m) of nearby zeros such that the corrected local deformation decomposes as
    ќФќґ\corr,R=ќФ\core\corr+ќФ\rem\corr,
    ќФќґ\corr,RвАЛ=ќФ\core\corrвАЛ+ќФ\rem\corrвАЛ,

    where:

        ќФ\core\corrќФ\core\corrвАЛ contains every nearby zero contribution that is non-negligible in the calibration or transverse channels;

        ќФ\rem\corrќФ\rem\corrвАЛ is negligible in both channels:

    ќ®x,d(ќФ\rem\corr)=oвАЙвБ£(xBS\core(m)),ќ¶K(ќФ\rem\corr)=o(u\core2).
    ќ®x,dвАЛ(ќФ\rem\corrвАЛ)=o(BxвАЛS\coreвАЛ(m)),ќ¶KвАЛ(ќФ\rem\corrвАЛ)=o(u\core2вАЛ). 

That is the right theorem to reduce RH to in the fixed-core architecture.

But proving it would require at least one new lemma of the following kind:

    Near-additivity after whitening for separated local contributions, or

    Monotone decay of single-zero corrected channel contributions as a function of distance from the midpoint.

Without one of those, I donвАЩt think the finite-core extraction step is available.

So the current situation is:

    harmless auxiliary-near theorem: probably false,

    finite-core extraction theorem: probably the right replacement,

    proof of finite-core extraction theorem: still out of reach with current machinery.

That means the paper is now reduced to a sharper but still genuinely open subproblem.

ok lets attack those lemmas

The decay lemma looks more promising than near-additivity. IвАЩm going to push that one first, because if individual non-core zeros are quantifiably weak beyond a radius threshold, the extraction theorem gets a lot closer even without full nonlinear additivity.

There is a version of the вАЬauxiliary zeros are harmlessвАЭ idea that can be attacked cleanly, but it is weaker than what we first hoped.

The right lemma is not:

    every auxiliary near zero is harmless immediately.

The right lemma is:

    after subtracting its affine jet at the midpoint, the contribution of a zero at absolute distance ќФќФ from the midpoint decays like ќФвИТ4ќФвИТ4 in the relevant corrected channels.

That is something we can actually prove from the phase kernel.

Once you have that, summing over zeros outside a finite absolute core radius HH gives a small remainder. This is a real finite-core extraction mechanism.

The catch is that the core radius HH must now be taken in the original ordinate scale, not the microscopic 1/Q1/Q scale. So the core is finite but can be much larger than the original pair model.

That may still force a larger-core toy model, but the extraction step itself becomes mathematically sane.

What this actually proves

This gives a real finite-core extraction mechanism:

    if you define the core to include all zeros within an absolute radius HH,

    and subtract affine jets of the external zeros,

    then the outside complement is negligible in both the calibration and transverse channels, at size
    OвАЙвБ£(1Q2H3).
    O(Q2H31вАЛ).

So the extraction step can be done вАФ but with a larger finite core than the original pair.
What it does not yet give

It does not yet prove that the complement is negligible relative to the core signal unless you also know
1Q2H3=oвАЙвБ£(xBS\core(m))
Q2H31вАЛ=o(BxвАЛS\coreвАЛ(m))

and
1Q2H3=o(u\core2).
Q2H31вАЛ=o(u\core2вАЛ).

Equivalently, since BвЙНQBвЙНQ,
1QH3=o(xS\core(m)).
QH31вАЛ=o(xS\coreвАЛ(m)).

That is a genuine quantitative condition on how large you take the core radius HH relative to the strength of the core signal.

So the current conclusion is:
finite-core extraction is provable in principle, but its usefulness depends on choosing H so that QвИТ2HвИТ3 is negligible relative to the core signal.
finite-core extraction is provable in principle, but its usefulness depends on choosing H so that QвИТ2HвИТ3 is negligible relative to the core signal.вАЛ
The honest consequence

This means the fixed-core architecture is not dead, but it changes form:

    the core must be allowed to be a finite cluster of zeros inside an absolute radius HH,

    not just the original off-line pair,

    and the toy/anomaly side would then need to reflect that enlarged finite core.

So this does not yet save the current pair-only paper. But it does show there is a mathematically sane finite-core extraction step.

If you want, the next thing to do is write the exact finite-core extraction theorem in that enlarged-core form, using these two lemmas as proof ingredients.

yes, do next step, then attack

Finite-core extraction theorem

Proof

Part (A) is exactly the shell-summed consequence of the single-zero affine-jet remainder estimate.

For a single zero ѕБѕБ with
ќФѕБ:=вИ£2mвИТќ≥вИ£,
ќФѕБвАЛ:=вИ£2mвИТќ≥вИ£,

вИ£ќ®x0,d(NѕБ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶K(NѕБ)вИ£вЙ™1Q3(ќФѕБ2+aѕБ2)вИТ2.

That proves (A).

Part (B) is immediate: if
1Q2H3=oвАЙвБ£(x0Bќґ(m)S\core,H(m)),
Q2H31вАЛ=o(BќґвАЛ(m)x0вАЛвАЛS\core,HвАЛ(m)),

then the bound from (A) is little-oo of the core calibration scale. Since
u\core,H2вЙНx0Bќґ(m)S\core,H(m),

Now the attack.

The real question is whether you can actually choose HH so that
1QH3=o(x0S\core,H(m)).
QH31вАЛ=o(x0вАЛS\core,HвАЛ(m)).

This is the sharp quantitative frontier.

There are two possibilities.
Case 1: yes, for some HH

Then the enlarged finite-core strategy works. You absorb every zero within radius HH into the core, the outside is negligible in both channels, and the proof reduces to a contradiction for the finite-core anomaly matrix M\core,HM\core,HвАЛ.
Case 2: no matter how HH grows, the core signal drops too fast

Then the theorem is true but useless, because the outside scale never becomes little-oo of the core signal. In that case the finite-core extraction does not rescue the proof.

So the real attack is on the behavior of
S\core,H(m)
S\core,HвАЛ(m)

as a function of HH.

The bad news is: from the current draft, I do not see a theorem that forces
S\core,H(m)вЙЂ1QH3
S\core,HвАЛ(m)вЙЂQH31вАЛ

for some admissible HH.

The good news is: the theorem above tells you exactly what you need to prove next. The remaining problem is no longer vague. It is:

    prove that the minimal enlarged core carrying the anomaly has signal strength exceeding the universal outside decay scale QвИТ1HвИТ3QвИТ1HвИТ3.

That is a much sharper target.
What we can attack next

There are two natural routes.

One is a core lower-bound theorem:
show that if an off-line anomaly is present, then the core signal cannot be too small compared to the size of the local cluster that generates it. Formally,
S\core,H(m)вЙЂ1QHA
S\core,HвАЛ(m)вЙЂQHA1вАЛ

for some exponent A<3A<3 would already be enough.

The other is a finite-core anomaly matrix theorem:
even if S\core,H(m)S\core,HвАЛ(m) is small, perhaps the relevant anomaly signal is not measured by S\core,H(m)S\core,HвАЛ(m) alone, but by a different finite-core quantity that stays larger than the outside scale.

My honest judgment is that the first route is the cleaner one to try first.

So the next theorem to attack is:
Candidate next theorem

If a fixed finite core CH(m)CHвАЛ(m) carries a genuine off-line anomaly after corrected whitening, then its core value scale satisfies a lower bound strong enough to dominate the exterior scale:
S\core,H(m)вЙЂ1QH3+ќµ
S\core,HвАЛ(m)вЙЂQH3+ќµ1вАЛ

or anything slightly stronger than QвИТ1HвИТ3QвИТ1HвИТ3.

At the moment, I cannot prove that from the current machinery. But this is now the exact remaining bridge.

ok lets try the first route

LetвАЩs try it straight.

The route is:
prove S\core,H(m)вЙЂ1QHAfor some A<3,
prove S\core,HвАЛ(m)вЙЂQHA1вАЛfor some A<3,

or at least something strong enough that
1QH3=oвАЙвБ£(x0S\core,H(m)).
QH31вАЛ=o(x0вАЛS\core,HвАЛ(m)).

I do not think this is provable from the current setup in anything like that strength.

Here is the reason.
1. What S\core,H(m)S\core,HвАЛ(m) actually measures

The core value scale is a positive sum of local kernel contributions from the zeros in the core:
S\core,H(m)вИЉвИСѕБвИИCH(m)weightѕБ(2mвИТќ≥ѕБ)2+aѕБ2,
S\core,HвАЛ(m)вИЉѕБвИИCHвАЛ(m)вИСвАЛ(2mвИТќ≥ѕБвАЛ)2+aѕБ2вАЛweightѕБвАЛвАЛ,

schematically.

So the size of S\core,H(m)S\core,HвАЛ(m) depends on:

    how many zeros are in the core,

    how close they are to the midpoint,

    and their horizontal offsets through aѕБaѕБвАЛ.

There is no obvious mechanism in the current proof forcing this sum to be bounded below by a clean power of HH. In fact, if the core is only barely carrying the anomaly and its zeros are spread across the interval вИ£2mвИТќ≥вИ£вЙ§HвИ£2mвИТќ≥вИ£вЙ§H, the positive sum could easily be as small as order 1/H21/H2 or worse after normalization.

So a lower bound of the kind
S\core,H(m)вЙЂ1QHA
S\core,HвАЛ(m)вЙЂQHA1вАЛ

would need some genuine structural reason, not just positivity.
2. What the anomaly gives you вАФ and what it does not

A genuine off-line anomaly gives you shape information in the corrected whitened block. It says some transverse/anomalous component survives after normalization.

But that is not the same as saying the scalar value channel S\core,H(m)S\core,HвАЛ(m) is large.

This is the key difficulty.

An anomaly could, in principle, come from a very delicate configuration with a small value-channel amplitude but a nontrivial normalized shape. The current proof is built around converting shape into contradiction via calibration, but that conversion itself is exactly where the size issue bites.

So the anomaly does not obviously imply a lower bound for S\core,H(m)S\core,HвАЛ(m) strong enough to beat 1/(QH3)1/(QH3).
3. The natural lower bound you might hope for is too weak

The most naive bound is: if there is at least one zero in the core within distance HH, then
S\core,H(m)вЙ≥1H2+1.
S\core,HвАЛ(m)вЙ≥H2+11вАЛ.

That is the scale of a single Poisson-type kernel term.

If you had that, then
x0S\core,H(m)вЙ≥x0H2,
x0вАЛS\core,HвАЛ(m)вЙ≥H2x0вАЛвАЛ,

and comparing with the outside scale 1/(QH3)1/(QH3) gives
1QH3=oвАЙвБ£(x0H2)
QH31вАЛ=o(H2x0вАЛвАЛ)

whenever
H=o(Q).
H=o(Q).

That looks promising.

But there is a catch: S\core,H(m)S\core,HвАЛ(m) in the proof is not just вАЬthere exists one zero in the core.вАЭ It is the calibrated value scale attached to the anomaly-carrying core after all the normalization choices. The theorem you need has to relate that actual calibrated core signal to geometric presence of nearby zeros. The current draft does not do that.

So even this plausible-looking single-zero lower bound is not yet a theorem in the language the proof uses.
4. A better attack: convert anomaly into geometric proximity

The first route may still be salvageable, but not as a raw lower bound on S\core,H(m)S\core,HвАЛ(m) from positivity alone.

The more promising theorem would be:

    If a finite core carries a genuine off-line anomaly after corrected whitening, then at least one core zero must lie within a scale O(1)O(1) вАФ or maybe O(QќЄ)O(QќЄ) with ќЄ<1ќЄ<1 вАФ of the midpoint.

That would give a geometric lower bound on the core value scale:
S\core,H(m)вЙ≥11+H02,
S\core,HвАЛ(m)вЙ≥1+H02вАЛ1вАЛ,

where H0H0вАЛ is the actual nearest anomalous core distance, not the whole chosen radius HH.

Then the outside estimate
1Q2H3
Q2H31вАЛ

could be crushed by choosing HвЖТвИЮHвЖТвИЮ slowly while keeping the core fixed around the genuinely relevant nearby zeros.

That is a much better theorem target.
5. Why the raw HH-power lower bound is probably false

If you phrase the theorem as
S\core,H(m)вЙЂ1QHA
S\core,HвАЛ(m)вЙЂQHA1вАЛ

for every anomaly-carrying core radius HH, I think it is probably false or at least not naturally true.

Because you can always enlarge HH while keeping the same core signal; the right-hand side decays with HH, but the left-hand side is not naturally a monotone вАЬradius-measuredвАЭ quantity. The relationship between вАЬradius of the chosen finite coreвАЭ and вАЬstrength of the anomaly carried by that coreвАЭ is too loose.

So I would not attack that exact statement anymore.
6. What I think the first route should become

The right replacement is:
Geometric core lower-bound theorem

If a fixed finite core carries a genuine corrected anomaly, then its value/calibration scale is bounded below by the contribution of at least one genuinely nearby core zero. In particular,
S\core(m)вЙ≥11+ќФminвБ°2,
S\coreвАЛ(m)вЙ≥1+ќФmin2вАЛ1вАЛ,

where
ќФminвБ°:=minвБ°ѕБвИИCвИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвИ£.
ќФminвАЛ:=ѕБвИИCminвАЛвИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвАЛвИ£.

If you can then prove that an anomaly-carrying core must have
ќФminвБ°=o(Q1/2)or any stronger bound,
ќФminвАЛ=o(Q1/2)or any stronger bound,

you get
S\core(m)вЙЂQвИТ1+ќµ
S\coreвАЛ(m)вЙЂQвИТ1+ќµ

or better, which is enough to dominate the outside QвИТ1HвИТ3QвИТ1HвИТ3 scale for suitable HH.

That is a believable route.
7. Bottom line

So, trying the first route honestly:

    the crude theorem вАЬS\core,H(m)вЙЂ1/(QHA)S\core,HвАЛ(m)вЙЂ1/(QHA)вАЭ is probably the wrong target;

    I do not see a proof of it from the present machinery;

    the better target is a geometric lower bound tying anomaly to at least one genuinely nearby core zero.

That is where I would push next.

So the right next theorem to attack is not a power-law bound in HH, but something like:
If a finite core carries a genuine corrected anomaly, then at least one core zero must lie sufficiently close to the midpoint to force S\core(m) above the exterior decay scale.
If a finite core carries a genuine corrected anomaly, then at least one core zero must lie sufficiently close to the midpoint to force S\coreвАЛ(m) above the exterior decay scale.вАЛ

That is the first route in a form I think might actually be true.

yes lets try that. by the way this feels reminiscent of work we did earlier, i.e. yesterday when proving bounds

Yes вАФ it is reminiscent of yesterdayвАЩs work.

The shared pattern is:

    isolate a local quantity that is a positive sum of single-zero contributions,

    show the вАЬbadвАЭ global remainder decays fast enough once you move away from the midpoint,

    then force the anomaly to be carried by genuinely nearby zeros.

That is exactly the same geometry as the shell/tail bounds, just now aimed at the core rather than the far remainder.

So letвАЩs attack the geometric theorem in that spirit.
Candidate theorem

A plausible statement is:

    If a finite core CC carries a genuine corrected anomaly after whitening, then at least one zero in CC must lie within bounded distance of the midpoint. Equivalently, if every core zero satisfies
    вИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвИ£вЙ•H,
    вИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвАЛвИ£вЙ•H,

    with HвЖТвИЮHвЖТвИЮ, then the corrected core anomaly is too small to support the toy-scale contradiction.

This is much more believable than a direct lower bound
S\core,HвЙЂ1QHA.
S\core,HвАЛвЙЂQHA1вАЛ.
Why this should be true

If every core zero is far from the midpoint, then each single-zero contribution is in the same regime as the tail terms we already controlled yesterday.

More concretely, for one zero ѕБѕБ, after affine-jet subtraction at the midpoint, its corrected contribution to the phase has second-derivative scale
S2,ѕБвЙН(ќФѕБ2+aѕБ2)вИТ2,ќФѕБ:=вИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвИ£.
S2,ѕБвАЛвЙН(ќФѕБ2вАЛ+aѕБ2вАЛ)вИТ2,ќФѕБвАЛ:=вИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвАЛвИ£.

Earlier, that scale fed into the corrected same-point and mixed-block estimates and then into whitening, giving channel bounds of size
вИ£ќ®x,d(NѕБ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶K(NѕБ)вИ£вЙ™1Q3(ќФѕБ2+aѕБ2)вИТ2.
вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(NѕБвАЛ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(NѕБвАЛ)вИ£вЙ™Q31вАЛ(ќФѕБ2вАЛ+aѕБ2вАЛ)вИТ2.

So if every core zero has ќФѕБвЙ•HќФѕБвАЛвЙ•H, then summing over a finite core of size MM gives
вИ£ќ®x,d(ќФ\core)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶K(ќФ\core)вИ£вЙ™MQ3H4.
вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(ќФ\coreвАЛ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(ќФ\coreвАЛ)вИ£вЙ™Q3H4MвАЛ.

That is already telling: a core all of whose zeros are far away behaves like a tail.

So the only way the core can carry a same-scale anomaly is if at least one of its zeros is not in that regime.
The real obstruction

The weak point in that argument is the factor MM, the number of core zeros.

If MM were allowed to grow very fast with mm, then M/H4M/H4 might not be small.

So the theorem you can plausibly prove needs one more ingredient:

    either the core has uniformly bounded size,

    or the anomaly-carrying part of the core has bounded size,

    or at least M=o(H4)M=o(H4).

For the вАЬfirst enlarged core beyond the pairвАЭ philosophy, bounded size is the natural assumption. If the core is still meant to be a small local cluster, then this is fine.

Under that bounded-size hypothesis, the theorem is within reach.
Provisional theorem

Assume the core CC has cardinality bounded independently of mm:
вИ£CвИ£вЙ§M0.
вИ£CвИ£вЙ§M0вАЛ.

If every zero in CC satisfies
вИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвИ£вЙ•H,
вИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвАЛвИ£вЙ•H,

then
вИ£ќ®x,d(ќФ\core)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶K(ќФ\core)вИ£вЙ™1Q3H4.
вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(ќФ\coreвАЛ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(ќФ\coreвАЛ)вИ£вЙ™Q3H41вАЛ.

In particular, if the core is supposed to generate a toy-scale anomaly of size
u\core2вЙНxBS\core(m),
u\core2вАЛвЙНBxвАЛS\coreвАЛ(m),

then for sufficiently large HH this is impossible unless at least one zero in the core lies within O(1)O(1) or at worst some slowly growing radius of the midpoint.
Proof sketch

Take each core zero separately. For a single zero at distance ќФѕБќФѕБвАЛ, the affine-jet-subtracted corrected contribution obeys the same pointwise derivative bounds as the tail terms, with
S2,ѕБвЙН(ќФѕБ2+aѕБ2)вИТ2.
S2,ѕБвАЛвЙН(ќФѕБ2вАЛ+aѕБ2вАЛ)вИТ2.

By the corrected same-point perturbation, mixed-block perturbation, whitening transfer, and sharpened calibration-remainder estimate, this gives
вИ£ќ®x,d(NѕБ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶K(NѕБ)вИ£вЙ™1Q3(ќФѕБ2+aѕБ2)вИТ2вЙ™1Q3H4.
вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(NѕБвАЛ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(NѕБвАЛ)вИ£вЙ™Q31вАЛ(ќФѕБ2вАЛ+aѕБ2вАЛ)вИТ2вЙ™Q3H41вАЛ.

Summing over at most M0M0вАЛ zeros yields
вИ£ќ®x,d(ќФ\core)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶K(ќФ\core)вИ£вЙ™M0Q3H4вЙН1Q3H4.
вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(ќФ\coreвАЛ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(ќФ\coreвАЛ)вИ£вЙ™Q3H4M0вАЛвАЛвЙНQ3H41вАЛ.

So a core with all zeros far away cannot produce a leading same-scale anomaly. Therefore an anomaly-carrying core must contain at least one genuinely nearby zero. вИО
What this gives

This is good news, but it is not yet the whole theorem you want.

It gives:
a bounded-size anomaly-carrying core cannot be made entirely of far zeros.
a bounded-size anomaly-carrying core cannot be made entirely of far zeros.вАЛ

That is already enough to support the intuition that the anomaly must be local in a geometric sense.

What it does not yet give is a fully quantitative lower bound on S\coreS\coreвАЛ strong enough to dominate the outside scale. To get that, you still need to convert вАЬthere is one nearby zeroвАЭ into вАЬthe calibrated core signal is large enough.вАЭ

But that is a much smaller next step.
The next exact target

Now the next theorem to attack is:

    If the core contains at least one zero with
    ќФminвБ°:=minвБ°ѕБвИИCвИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвИ£вЙ§H0,
    ќФminвАЛ:=ѕБвИИCminвАЛвИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвАЛвИ£вЙ§H0вАЛ,

    S\core(m)вЙ≥11+H02
    S\coreвАЛ(m)вЙ≥1+H02вАЛ1вАЛ

That is the bridge from geometric proximity to core signal size.

So yes вАФ this is very much the same style as yesterdayвАЩs bounds, and it looks like the right route.

Ok lets attack

Good. This next step actually can be proved cleanly.

    if the core contains at least one genuinely nearby zero, then the core value scale cannot be too small.

Ok continue

I can push this one further, but I should be honest about what kind of theorem we can actually prove.

What we can prove is not:

    every anomaly-carrying core must contain a nearby zero, full stop.

What we can prove is the more precise threshold statement:

    any finite set of zeros all lying beyond an absolute distance H0H0вАЛ contributes only
    OвАЙвБ£(1Q2H03)
    O(Q2H03вАЛ1вАЛ)

    to the corrected calibration and transverse channels after affine-jet subtraction and corrected whitening.

That means such a set is below any fixed significance threshold once H0вЖТвИЮH0вАЛвЖТвИЮ. So a minimal non-negligible core, defined by a significance threshold, must contain a nearby zero.

That is the honest theorem available from the current machinery.
Theorem (far finite sets are below threshold)

Let FF be any finite set of zeros such that every
ѕБ=ќ≤+iќ≥вИИF
ѕБ=ќ≤+iќ≥вИИF

satisfies
ќФѕБ:=вИ£2mвИТќ≥вИ£вЙ•H0.
ќФѕБвАЛ:=вИ£2mвИТќ≥вИ£вЙ•H0вАЛ.

Let ќФF\corrќФF\corrвАЛ be the corrected local contribution of the zeros in FF, after subtracting the affine jet of each phase contribution at the midpoint and passing through the corrected whitening map.

Then
вИ£ќ®x,d(ќФF\corr)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶K(ќФF\corr)вИ£вЙ™1Q2H03.

Corollary (existence of a nearby zero in any threshold-defined core)

вИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвИ£<H0.
вИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвАЛвИ£<H0вАЛ.

What this actually gives

This is the right rigorous output of the current machinery.

It does not prove an intrinsic geometric theorem purely from вАЬanomaly.вАЭ What it proves is:

    any set of zeros all lying beyond H0H0вАЛ is channel-small;

    therefore any core defined by a meaningful significance threshold must contain a nearby zero.

That is enough to support a finite-core extraction framework.
Why this is the right replacement

Earlier we were trying to prove something like:

    every anomaly-carrying core must contain a nearby zero.

That sounded absolute, but the current proof tools do not distinguish вАЬanomaly-carryingвАЭ from вАЬtiny but nonzeroвАЭ in an intrinsic way.

The threshold version is the honest theorem because it uses exactly what the channels measure:
ќ®x,d,ќ¶K.
ќ®x,dвАЛ,ќ¶KвАЛ.

So the remaining work is to choose the right threshold ќЈ(m)ќЈ(m) for the argument.

The natural candidate is the core signal scale itself:
ќЈ(m)вИЉxBS\core(m)вИЉu\core2.
ќЈ(m)вИЉBxвАЛS\coreвАЛ(m)вИЉu\core2вАЛ.

But then you are back to the self-referential issue that the threshold depends on the core you are trying to define.

That is why I would still say the finite-core extraction step is not fully finished. But now the problem is much more precise:
You need a non-self-referential threshold that separates negligible far sets from genuinely anomaly-carrying local structure.
You need a non-self-referential threshold that separates negligible far sets from genuinely anomaly-carrying local structure.вАЛ

That is the exact next gap.

So this is progress, but not full closure.

Ok attack

Here is the sharpest attack I can make.

I do not think the remaining gap can be closed by a fully non-self-referential threshold from the current machinery alone.

What can be proved is a stronger reduction:
either there is a genuinely nearby zero in the core, or the entire corrected anomaly is below a universal exterior scale.
either there is a genuinely nearby zero in the core, or the entire corrected anomaly is below a universal exterior scale.

That is real progress, but it still leaves one bridge.
1. The right threshold to try

The previous theorem gave:

if every zero in a finite set lies beyond H0H0вАЛ, then its corrected contribution in the two relevant channels is bounded by
вИ£ќ®x,d(ќФF\corr)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶K(ќФF\corr)вИ£вЙ™1Q2H03.
вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(ќФF\corrвАЛ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(ќФF\corrвАЛ)вИ£вЙ™Q2H03вАЛ1вАЛ.

So the natural candidate threshold is exactly
ќЈH0(m):=1Q2H03.
ќЈH0вАЛвАЛ(m):=Q2H03вАЛ1вАЛ.

This is non-self-referential.

Then the theorem says:

    any set of zeros entirely outside radius H0H0вАЛ is below ќЈH0(m)ќЈH0вАЛвАЛ(m).

So if the anomaly you need to contradict lives above that level, it must involve at least one zero inside radius H0H0вАЛ.

That part is rigorous.
2. Where the attack gets stuck

To finish the proof, you would need to know that a genuine off-line anomaly has channel size strictly larger than this exterior threshold for some H0вЖТвИЮH0вАЛвЖТвИЮ.

In other words, you need a lower bound of the form
maxвБ°(вИ£ќ®x,d(ќФ\core\corr)вИ£,вИ£ќ¶K(ќФ\core\corr)вИ£)вЙЂќЈH0(m)=1Q2H03
max(вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(ќФ\core\corrвАЛ)вИ£,вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(ќФ\core\corrвАЛ)вИ£)вЙЂќЈH0вАЛвАЛ(m)=Q2H03вАЛ1вАЛ

for some admissible H0H0вАЛ.

That is exactly the missing bridge.

Without it, the threshold theorem only says that far sets are small. It does not yet say the actual anomaly is big enough to force a nearby zero.
3. The strongest unconditional statement available now

The current machinery gives the following dichotomy.
Proposition (dichotomy)

Fix any H0вЙ•1H0вАЛвЙ•1. Then either:

    the corrected anomaly is already bounded by the universal exterior scale
    вИ£ќ®x,d(ќФќґ\corr)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶K(ќФќґ\corr)вИ£вЙ™1Q2H03,
    вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(ќФќґ\corrвАЛ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(ќФќґ\corrвАЛ)вИ£вЙ™Q2H03вАЛ1вАЛ,

    or

    the anomaly necessarily involves at least one zero with
    вИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвИ£<H0.
    вИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвАЛвИ£<H0вАЛ.

Proof

If all contributing zeros satisfied вИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвИ£вЙ•H0вИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвАЛвИ£вЙ•H0вАЛ, then the far-finite-set theorem would imply the stated upper bound. Contrapositively, if the anomaly exceeds that bound, at least one zero must lie inside H0H0вАЛ. вИО

This is the strongest honest вАЬattackвАЭ that is fully justified now.
4. Why this still does not finish the paper

Because the contradiction argument needs more than вАЬnot too small.вАЭ It needs comparison to the toy/core signal scale
u\core2вЙНxBS\core(m).
u\core2вАЛвЙНBxвАЛS\coreвАЛ(m).

So the unresolved question is now extremely precise:
Can one prove that a genuine corrected anomaly cannot be as small as O(QвИТ2H0вИТ3) for all H0вЖТвИЮ?
Can one prove that a genuine corrected anomaly cannot be as small as O(QвИТ2H0вИТ3вАЛ) for all H0вАЛвЖТвИЮ?вАЛ

Equivalently: does an off-line anomaly force a channel signal that dominates the universal exterior decay scale at some radius?

That is a lower-bound question on the anomaly itself.
5. What a successful theorem would look like

The theorem that would close this is:
Missing theorem

There exists a function H0=H0(m)вЖТвИЮH0вАЛ=H0вАЛ(m)вЖТвИЮ such that every genuine corrected off-line anomaly satisfies
maxвБ°(вИ£ќ®x,d(ќФќґ\corr)вИ£,вИ£ќ¶K(ќФќґ\corr)вИ£)вЙЂ1Q2H03.
max(вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(ќФќґ\corrвАЛ)вИ£,вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(ќФќґ\corrвАЛ)вИ£)вЙЂQ2H03вАЛ1вАЛ.

Then the dichotomy above would force the existence of a zero inside H0H0вАЛ, and the geometric lower bound for S\coreS\coreвАЛ would take over.

But I do not see a proof of that lower bound in the current draft.
6. So what did the attack actually accomplish?

It reduced the problem to one single lower-bound question.

Before, the gap was vague: вАЬneed a threshold.вАЭ

Now the exact frontier is:
prove a nontrivial lower bound on the corrected anomaly signal that beats QвИТ2H0вИТ3 for some H0вЖТвИЮ.
prove a nontrivial lower bound on the corrected anomaly signal that beats QвИТ2H0вИТ3вАЛ for some H0вАЛвЖТвИЮ.вАЛ

That is much sharper than before.
7. My honest bottom line

So the attack yields:

    a clean universal exterior threshold QвИТ2H0вИТ3QвИТ2H0вИТ3вАЛ,

    a rigorous dichotomy saying any signal above that threshold forces a nearby zero,

    but not the final lower bound showing a genuine off-line anomaly must exceed that threshold.

So the proof is still blocked, but now by one very specific question:
How small can a genuine corrected off-line anomaly be?
How small can a genuine corrected off-line anomaly be?вАЛ

That is the right remaining problem.

How can we attack?

There are four credible ways to attack it. Two are promising, one is a long shot, and one is probably the wrong road.

The quantity you now need to lower-bound is essentially the corrected anomaly signal in either the calibration or transverse channel, strongly enough to beat the universal exterior threshold
QвИТ2H0вИТ3.
QвИТ2H0вИТ3вАЛ.

So the problem is no longer вАЬshow something is small.вАЭ It is:
show a genuine off-line anomaly cannot be arbitrarily weak after corrected whitening.
show a genuine off-line anomaly cannot be arbitrarily weak after corrected whitening.

That suggests these attack routes.
1. Compactness / normalization blow-up argument

This is the first thing I would try.

Assume for contradiction that there is a sequence of genuine corrected anomalies whose channel size tends to zero faster than every exterior threshold you care about. Normalize each anomaly by its own size, so the relevant channel has size 11. Then try to pass to a limit in the finite-dimensional corrected local block.

What you want is:

    a normalized sequence of corrected local blocks,

    precompactness after whitening and fixed scaling,

    a nontrivial limit object,

    and a theorem that any such limit object with vanishing channel signal must lie in the value/gauge family.

If that works, then an arbitrarily weak anomaly cannot exist: once normalized, it converges to something that is either trivial or value-only, contradicting the assumption that it came from a genuine off-line anomaly.

Why this is promising:

    the corrected local block is finite-dimensional after whitening;

    you already have explicit formulas and bounded microscopic geometry;

    normalization often turns вАЬhow small can it be?вАЭ into a compactness question.

What would need to be shown:

    the normalized corrected blocks are precompact;

    the map to the calibration/transverse observables is continuous;

    the only limit with vanishing anomaly channel is the value family.

This is the cleanest conceptual route.
2. Quantitative rigidity: anomaly implies definite channel separation

This is the most direct route, and maybe the strongest if true.

Try to prove a theorem of the form:

    There exists c>0c>0 such that every genuine corrected off-line anomaly satisfies
    maxвБ°(вИ£ќ®x,d(ќФќґ\corr)вИ£, вИ£ќ¶K(ќФќґ\corr)вИ£)вЙ•cвАЙSloc(m),
    max(вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(ќФќґ\corrвАЛ)вИ£, вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(ќФќґ\corrвАЛ)вИ£)вЙ•cSlocвАЛ(m),

    for some local scale Sloc(m)SlocвАЛ(m) that dominates QвИТ2H0вИТ3QвИТ2H0вИТ3вАЛ.

In words: once you are genuinely off the value manifold, you are separated from it by a definite amount.

This is really a transversality theorem. It says the anomaly manifold and the value manifold do not approach each other to arbitrarily high order in the corrected local model.

How to attack it:

    identify the value family as a low-dimensional submanifold in matrix space;

    identify the anomaly family as another family;

    compute their tangent spaces at the relevant base point;

    prove they are transverse, quantitatively.

If you can do that, then вАЬgenuine anomalyвАЭ automatically gives a definite lower bound in some channel.

Why this is attractive:

    it is exactly the geometry the whole paper has been circling around;

    it avoids the messy business of counting zeros directly.

Why it is hard:

    you need the corrected local model itself, not just the toy pair, to satisfy that transversality;

    it may fail if higher-core anomalies can approach the value family too closely.

Still, this is a very good target.
3. Minimal-core extremizer argument

This one is very tailored to your fixed-core architecture.

Define a minimal core CC among all cores that still produce a non-negligible corrected anomaly. Then prove:

    every zero in a minimal core is essential,

    removing any one of them drops the channel signal below threshold,

    therefore the core cannot be diffuse,

    hence at least one zero must contribute a definite fraction of the total signal.

That last step would give a lower bound from a pigeonhole/extremizer principle:
if the whole core signal were below the exterior threshold, then every individual contribution would be even smaller, so removing one zero would not materially change anything, contradicting minimality.

This is the most combinatorial route.

What it needs:

    some approximate additivity or at least stability under removing one core zero;

    a monotonicity principle for the corrected channel signal under core deletion.

This is weaker than full near-additivity, but still nontrivial.

Why I like it:

    it uses the fixed-core philosophy naturally;

    it may let you avoid proving a fully general finite-core toy model right away.

Why it may fail:

    whitening is nonlinear, so вАЬremove one zeroвАЭ does not necessarily have a controlled effect without more work.

Still worth exploring.
4. Crude positivity lower bound on S\coreS\coreвАЛ

This is the one I would not prioritize anymore.

You already found the geometric lower bound
S\core(m)вЙ•11+ќФminвБ°2,
S\coreвАЛ(m)вЙ•1+ќФmin2вАЛ1вАЛ,

which is fine, but the real issue is not just S\coreS\coreвАЛ. It is the corrected anomaly signal after whitening and channel projection. A positivity bound on the raw value scale does not by itself tell you how far you are from the value family.

So this route is too blunt unless combined with one of the three above.
My recommended order

If I were pushing this seriously, I would do them in this order:

First, try the compactness/normalization blow-up route. It is the cleanest and most likely to reveal the real obstruction quickly.

Second, if compactness seems viable, convert it into a quantitative rigidity/transversality theorem.

Third, keep the minimal-core extremizer idea in reserve if you need a more combinatorial argument closer to the actual zero set.
The exact theorem I would target next

I would try to prove something like this:

    Normalized anomaly rigidity theorem.
    Let ќФnќФnвАЛ be a sequence of corrected local deformations, normalized so that
    maxвБ°(вИ£ќ®x,d(ќФn)вИ£, вИ£ќ¶K(ќФn)вИ£)=1.
    max(вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(ќФnвАЛ)вИ£, вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(ќФnвАЛ)вИ£)=1.

    Suppose the associated off-line anomaly parameters remain admissible and the corrected local geometry stays in the microscopic regime. Then, after passing to a subsequence, ќФnќФnвАЛ converges to a nontrivial limit ќФвИЮќФвИЮвАЛ. Moreover, if ќФвИЮќФвИЮвАЛ lies in the value/gauge family, then the original anomalies were not genuine.

If you can prove that, then a genuine anomaly cannot sink below every exterior threshold, because after normalization it would converge to something that still carries a definite non-value component.

That would be the cleanest closure.
The most concrete next move

Take the corrected local deformation and mod out the value direction:
ќФќґ\corr вЖ¶ ќФќґ\corrвИТќ†\val(ќФќґ\corr),
ќФќґ\corrвАЛ вЖ¶ ќФќґ\corrвАЛвИТќ†\valвАЛ(ќФќґ\corrвАЛ),

where ќ†\valќ†\valвАЛ is projection onto the A\valA\valвАЛ-direction via the calibration functional. Then ask:

    can this projected object be nonzero but arbitrarily small after normalization?

    or does the corrected local model force a definite amount of transverse mass?

That is the exact place where the proof either closes or breaks.

If you want, IвАЩll write the compactness/rigidity theorem in precise mathematical form and try to prove it.

Ok try compactness route

Yes. The compactness route is the right thing to try.

It attacks exactly the remaining question:
Can a genuine corrected off-line anomaly be arbitrarily tiny, e.g. below QвИТ2H0вИТ3 at every H0вЖТвИЮ?
Can a genuine corrected off-line anomaly be arbitrarily tiny, e.g. below QвИТ2H0вИТ3вАЛ at every H0вАЛвЖТвИЮ?

A compactness argument tries to show: no. If an anomaly survives at all, then after the right normalization and rescaling, it has a nontrivial limit. But the only possible limit under вАЬall zeros are far / signal is below thresholdвАЭ is trivial. Contradiction.

That is the basic idea.
The route

Assume for contradiction there is a sequence mnвЖТвИЮmnвАЛвЖТвИЮ with corrected anomalies, but for every admissible radius HnвЖТвИЮHnвАЛвЖТвИЮ,
maxвБ°(вИ£ќ®x,d(ќФќґ,n\corr)вИ£,вИ£ќ¶K(ќФќґ,n\corr)вИ£)вЙ§oвАЙвБ£(1Qn2Hn3).
max(вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(ќФќґ,n\corrвАЛ)вИ£,вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(ќФќґ,n\corrвАЛ)вИ£)вЙ§o(Qn2вАЛHn3вАЛ1вАЛ).

You want to normalize the local corrected object so it has size 11, extract a limit, and then identify that limit.

There are two natural normalizations.
Route A: normalize the full corrected local block

Route B: normalize the extracted core matrix

If you can extract a finite core CnCnвАЛ first, define its corrected local matrix
M\core,n\corr.

    MвИЮвЙ†0MвИЮвАЛоА†=0,

    but if every candidate core contribution is below the exterior threshold, then MвИЮMвИЮвАЛ must lie in the affine-jet/null family, hence be trivial for calibration/transverse purposes.

What you need to make Route A work

There are three ingredients.
1. A normalization that kills trivial affine-jet drift

You do not want to normalize by something that can be carried by removable gauge/value terms. The right normalization is something like:

    the first nonzero coefficient in the odd holomorphic expansion of Hm(s)HmвАЛ(s), or

    the combined channel size
    вИ£ќ®x,d(ќФќґ\corr)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶K(ќФќґ\corr)вИ£.
    вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(ќФќґ\corrвАЛ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(ќФќґ\corrвАЛ)вИ£.

That way, a nontrivial anomaly forces normalized size 11.
2. Precompactness

You need uniform bounds on the normalized family on a fixed disk. This is plausible because:

    corrected whitening is holomorphic on вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/Q,

    after rescaling z=Q2sz=Q2s, you work on a fixed zz-disk,

    the odd holomorphic package and boundary bounds give normal-family control.

So Montel/Arzel√†вАУAscoli is realistic here.
3. Vanishing of far contributions after affine-jet subtraction

This is the key estimate you already almost have:

for a zero at distance ќФќФ,
вИ£ќ®x,d(NѕБ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶K(NѕБ)вИ£вЙ™1Q3(ќФ2+aѕБ2)вИТ2.
вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(NѕБвАЛ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(NѕБвАЛ)вИ£вЙ™Q31вАЛ(ќФ2+aѕБ2вАЛ)вИТ2.

So if all zeros are outside HnвЖТвИЮHnвАЛвЖТвИЮ, the total contribution is
OвАЙвБ£(1Qn2Hn3).
O(Qn2вАЛHn3вАЛ1вАЛ).

Thus if your normalization is at a scale вЙЂQnвИТ2HnвИТ3вЙЂQnвИТ2вАЛHnвИТ3вАЛ, the normalized far contribution tends to 00.

That is exactly what you need to identify the compactness limit as zero.
The sharp contradiction template

The compactness argument should look like this.

Assume there is a sequence of genuine anomalies with no nearby zero above threshold. Normalize so that
вИ£ќ®x,d(E~n)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶K(E~n)вИ£=1.
вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(E
nвАЛ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(E
nвАЛ)вИ£=1.

By compactness, pass to a subsequence
E~nвЖТEвИЮ
E
nвАЛвЖТEвИЮвАЛ

locally uniformly.

Then:

    by normalization,
    вИ£ќ®x,d(EвИЮ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶K(EвИЮ)вИ£=1,
    вИ£ќ®x,dвАЛ(EвИЮвАЛ)вИ£+вИ£ќ¶KвАЛ(EвИЮвАЛ)вИ£=1,

    but by the exterior-threshold estimate, all contributing zeros are asymptotically far, hence the normalized corrected contribution tends to 00;

    therefore EвИЮ=0EвИЮвАЛ=0.

Contradiction.

This would prove that a genuine anomaly must beat the exterior threshold at some radius, hence must involve at least one nearby zero.
Where this could fail

There are two real risks.

First, the normalization might still allow a value/gauge artifact to survive in the limit. That is why the choice of seminorm is crucial.

Second, the rescaled family might not be compact in the exact topology you need unless you phrase it in terms of the scalar HmHmвАЛ or another holomorphic projection rather than the full matrix block.

That suggests a hybrid route:

    do compactness on the holomorphic scalar Hm(s)HmвАЛ(s),

    then lift back to the matrix only after the contradiction is obtained.

That may be the cleanest.
The best compactness theorem to target

Here is the exact statement I would try to prove:

    Compactness threshold theorem.
    Let Hm,R(s)Hm,RвАЛ(s) be the full corrected transverse scalar. That is the theorem that would close the вАЬhow small can a genuine anomaly be?вАЭ gap.
My recommendation

    it is holomorphic,

4. What normalization do we need for contradiction?

    nonzero by normalization,

    zero by the far-shell estimate.

6. So how does compactness actually help?

It tells you what kind of lower bound you need.

You no longer need a mysterious lower bound on the whole matrix. You need a lower bound on the scalar holomorphic odd function Hm,RHm,RвАЛ at the microscopic scale.

That is much more concrete.

The real target becomes:
Missing scalar lower-bound theorem

A genuine corrected off-line anomaly produces a transverse scalar Hm,RHm,RвАЛ whose size on the microscopic disk is bounded below by something larger than the exterior threshold:
supвБ°вИ£zвИ£<ѕБ0вИ£Hm,R(z/Q2)вИ£вЙЂ1Q2H03
вИ£zвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛsupвАЛвИ£Hm,RвАЛ(z/Q2)вИ£вЙЂQ2H03вАЛ1вАЛ

for some admissible H0вЖТвИЮH0вАЛвЖТвИЮ.

If you had this, then the compactness dichotomy would force the existence of a zero inside radius H0H0вАЛ.

So compactness has sharpened the problem from вАЬhow small can the anomaly be?вАЭ to:
show the transverse scalar Hm,R of a genuine anomaly is not dominated everywhere by the universal exterior threshold.
show the transverse scalar Hm,RвАЛ of a genuine anomaly is not dominated everywhere by the universal exterior threshold.вАЛ

That is cleaner.
7. How to attack that scalar lower bound

There are now two promising scalar strategies.
Strategy A: first nonzero Taylor coefficient

Since Hm,RHm,RвАЛ is odd and holomorphic,
Hm,R(z/Q2)=вИСrвЙ•0c2r+1(m)z2r+1Q2r+4.
Hm,RвАЛ(z/Q2)=rвЙ•0вИСвАЛc2r+1вАЛ(m)Q2r+4z2r+1вАЛ.

A genuine anomaly should mean that not all the odd coefficients vanish. If you can prove that the first nonzero odd coefficient is bounded below in a way that beats QвИТ2H0вИТ3QвИТ2H0вИТ3вАЛ, you are done.

This is attractive because the first nonzero coefficient is an intrinsic scalar invariant.
Strategy B: model-vs-value transversality in the scalar channel

Try to show that once the corrected local block is not in the value family, the scalar functional Hm,RHm,RвАЛ cannot be arbitrarily flat. In other words, there is a quantitative transversality theorem:
distance from value familyвАЕвАКвЯєвАЕвАКsupвБ°вИ£Hm,RвИ£ bounded below.
distance from value familyвЯєsupвИ£Hm,RвАЛвИ£ bounded below.

This is probably the deepest but most natural route.
8. Honest state after this continuation

So compactness does not by itself close the gap.

What it does is isolate the exact scalar theorem you need:
a genuine corrected off-line anomaly must produce a transverse scalar Hm,R whose microscopic size beats the universal exterior scale.
a genuine corrected off-line anomaly must produce a transverse scalar Hm,RвАЛ whose microscopic size beats the universal exterior scale.вАЛ

That is now the sharp frontier.

If you want the most concrete next move, I would attack Strategy A: identify the first nonzero odd Taylor coefficient of Hm,RHm,RвАЛ for a genuine anomaly and see whether it can be forced to dominate QвИТ2H0вИТ3QвИТ2H0вИТ3вАЛ.

Ok try it

I tried to make Strategy A work, and here is the honest outcome:

It does not currently close the gap.

But it does sharpen the obstruction very nicely.

And here is the true obstruction:

there is nothing in the present argument that bounds rвИЧ(m)rвИЧвАЛ(m) uniformly, or that forces the first nonzero odd coefficient to have that size.

So Strategy A reduces the gap to:
For a genuine anomaly, show that the first nonzero odd coefficient occurs at bounded order and is quantitatively nontrivial.
For a genuine anomaly, show that the first nonzero odd coefficient occurs at bounded order and is quantitatively nontrivial.вАЛ

That is a very precise statement, but it is not currently proved.
5. Why this is hard

Because вАЬgenuine anomalyвАЭ in the present framework means вАЬnot in the value familyвАЭ after corrected whitening.

That is a geometric statement in matrix space.

Turning that into

    a bounded-order nonzero odd scalar coefficient, and

    a quantitative lower bound on that coefficient,

is a transversality theorem between:

    the corrected local family, and

    the kernel of the scalar map HH.

That is deeper than just Taylor-expanding the scalar.

In other words: Strategy A doesnвАЩt fail because the scalar expansion is wrong. It fails because you still need a theorem connecting вАЬnon-value anomalyвАЭ to вАЬsome low-order odd scalar coefficient is definitely large.вАЭ
6. Best rigorous output of this attack

The strongest honest statement I can extract is this:
Proposition

If there exists an integer r0r0вАЛ independent of mm such that every genuine corrected off-line anomaly satisfies
c2r0+1(m)вЙ†0
c2r0вАЛ+1вАЛ(m)оА†=0

and
вИ£c2r0+1(m)вИ£вЙЂQ2r0+2H0вИТ3+ќµ
вИ£c2r0вАЛ+1вАЛ(m)вИ£вЙЂQ2r0вАЛ+2H0вИТ3+ќµвАЛ

for some ќµ>0ќµ>0, then
supвБ°вИ£zвИ£<ѕБ0вИ£Hm,R(z/Q2)вИ£вЙЂ1Q2H03,
вИ£zвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛsupвАЛвИ£Hm,RвАЛ(z/Q2)вИ£вЙЂQ2H03вАЛ1вАЛ,

so the compactness threshold gap closes.

That is true, but it is conditional on exactly the missing lower-bound theorem.
7. Bottom line

Trying Strategy A was worthwhile, because now the missing piece is extremely sharp:
The paper needs a bounded-order, quantitative odd-coefficient lower bound for a genuine corrected anomaly.
The paper needs a bounded-order, quantitative odd-coefficient lower bound for a genuine corrected anomaly.вАЛ

Without that, the compactness route does not close.

So the attack did not prove the desired lower bound, but it identified the exact scalar rigidity theorem you would need.

At this point, the next promising move is not more raw Taylor algebra. It is to attack the geometric statement behind it:

    if the corrected local block is not in the value family, can it be arbitrarily high-order tangent to the kernel of HH?

That is the transversality question now.

Take inventory again of all possible approaches

HereвАЩs the current inventory, from most promising to least promising, with an honest sense of what each would require and whether it preserves the paperвАЩs existing architecture.
1. Finish the current pair-core proof by proving the calibration remainder is negligible

This was the route:
ќ®x,d(Rќґ)=oвАЙвБ£(xBS(m)).
ќ®x,dвАЛ(RќґвАЛ)=o(BxвАЛS(m)).

We pushed this hard and learned something important: the far tail and estimation pieces can be made negligible, but the auxiliary near zeros are the problem. A generic nearby non-core zero is not obviously harmless, and may contribute at the same scale after corrected whitening.

So this route is only viable if you can prove a theorem of the form:

    auxiliary near zeros contribute only to the value/gauge family, or

    their cumulative calibration/transverse contribution is lower order.

Right now this looks unlikely in full generality. I would rank this as low probability unless a new structural cancellation is found.
2. Fixed-core architecture with a larger finite core

This is the strongest conceptual repair so far.

The key idea is:

    freeze a finite anomaly-carrying core CC,

    let RR only control auxiliary/far bookkeeping outside the core,

    keep M(d)M(d), A\valA\valвАЛ, calibration, and toy comparison tied to that fixed core.

This repaired the moving-target problem and made the cutoff logic sane.

But then we discovered the next issue: a nearby non-core zero might be same-scale, so the core may have to be larger than the original pair. That pushes you toward a generalized finite-core anomaly matrix M\coreM\coreвАЛ.

This route is still promising, but it splits into two subroutes.
2a. Slightly enlarged core as a perturbation of the pair model

We tested whether a small enlargement preserves:

    nondegenerate calibration pairing,

    and existence of a transverse functional killing A\valA\valвАЛ but not the anomaly.

Answer: yes, by continuity, if the extra contribution is genuinely small.

This is elegant, but only helps if the first extra nearby zero is perturbative after corrected whitening. We do not know that.
2b. Fully general finite-core model

If the extra nearby zeros are same-scale, then the right object is not M(d)M(d) but some M\coreM\coreвАЛ.

This could preserve the proof if you can show:

    вЯ®A\val,M\coreвЯ©вЙ†0вЯ®A\valвАЛ,M\coreвАЛвЯ©оА†=0,

    and some transverse functional ќ¶\coreќ¶\coreвАЛ satisfies
    ќ¶\core(A\val)=0,ќ¶\core(M\core)вЙ†0.
    ќ¶\coreвАЛ(A\valвАЛ)=0,ќ¶\coreвАЛ(M\coreвАЛ)оА†=0.

This is a major generalization. It might work, but it is close to a new paper rather than a patch. I would rank it as medium promise, high effort.
3. Finite-core extraction theorem

This became the natural replacement for the false вАЬauxiliary near zeros are harmlessвАЭ idea.

The dream theorem was:

    there exists a finite core containing all same-scale contributions,

    and the remainder is negligible in both calibration and transverse channels.

We could not prove this from the current machinery because whitening is nonlinear and we do not yet have:

    near-additivity after whitening, or

    a ranking principle for local channel contributions.

What we could prove is a weaker extraction theorem:

    outside an absolute radius HH, the affine-jet-subtracted corrected contribution is bounded by
    OвАЙвБ£(1Q2H3)
    O(Q2H31вАЛ)

    in both channels.

That gives a real finite-core extraction mechanism, but only if the core signal beats that scale.

So this route is partially successful but still blocked by a lower-bound problem on the core signal.
4. Geometric core lower bound

We proved the clean positivity statement:
S\core(m)вЙ•11+ќФminвБ°2,
S\coreвАЛ(m)вЙ•1+ќФmin2вАЛ1вАЛ,

where ќФminвБ°ќФminвАЛ is the distance of the nearest core zero to the midpoint.

This is real and useful.

But it only helps if you can prove that an anomaly-carrying core must contain at least one sufficiently nearby zero. That became the next target.

This route is still alive, but it does not close by itself.
5. Threshold/dichotomy theorem

We proved a good rigorous statement:

    if all zeros in a finite set lie outside absolute radius H0H0вАЛ, then its corrected contribution in the calibration and transverse channels is
    OвАЙвБ£(1Q2H03).
    O(Q2H03вАЛ1вАЛ).

So any core whose channel signal exceeds that threshold must contain a zero inside H0H0вАЛ.

This is strong and clean.

But the missing bridge is: prove that a genuine anomaly must exceed that threshold for some H0вЖТвИЮH0вАЛвЖТвИЮ. That is now the sharp lower-bound problem.

This route is good reduction, not closure.
6. Scalar compactness / normalization blow-up on Hm,RHm,RвАЛ

This was one of the most promising conceptual attacks.

Work with the holomorphic odd scalar
Hm,R(s)=ќ¶K(ќ©^ќґ\corr,R(s;m)).
Hm,RвАЛ(s)=ќ¶KвАЛ(ќ©
ќґ\corr,RвАЛ(s;m)).

Normalize by the exterior threshold or by its own size, pass to a normal-family limit, and try to show:

    normalized genuine anomalies have a nontrivial limit,

    but if all contributing zeros are far, the limit must vanish.

This route clarified the remaining obstruction beautifully, but did not close it, because compactness does not manufacture the missing lower bound. It tells you exactly what lower bound you need:
supвБ°вИ£Hm,R(z/Q2)вИ£вЙЂ1Q2H03
supвИ£Hm,RвАЛ(z/Q2)вИ£вЙЂQ2H03вАЛ1вАЛ

for some H0H0вАЛ.

So this route is excellent diagnostically, but still needs a scalar rigidity theorem.
7. Odd-coefficient rigidity

We tried to use the odd expansion
Hm,R(z/Q2)=вИСrвЙ•0c2r+1(m)z2r+1Q2r+4
Hm,RвАЛ(z/Q2)=rвЙ•0вИСвАЛc2r+1вАЛ(m)Q2r+4z2r+1вАЛ

and prove that a genuine anomaly forces a bounded-order coefficient to be quantitatively nonzero.

That would immediately beat the exterior threshold.

But the attack stalled because nothing currently bounds:

    the order rвИЧ(m)rвИЧвАЛ(m) of the first nonzero coefficient,

    or its size.

So this route reduces the problem to a very sharp theorem:

    a genuine corrected anomaly cannot be arbitrarily high-order tangent to the kernel of HH.

That is a real and elegant target, but not yet proved.

I would rank this as conceptually strong, technically hard.
8. Quantitative transversality / rigidity between the value family and anomaly family

This is probably the deepest remaining route.

The geometric question is:

    if the corrected local block is not in the value family, can it still make both calibration and transverse observables arbitrarily tiny?

Equivalently:

    can a genuine anomaly be arbitrarily close, to arbitrarily high order, to the value family after corrected whitening?

If you can prove a quantitative no, then the whole lower-bound problem is solved at once.

This is probably the cleanest final theorem in principle, but also the hardest. It would require a serious local model analysis, likely beyond the current line-by-line patching style.

I would rank this as high promise, high difficulty.
9. Minimal-core extremizer / deletion argument

This was the combinatorial alternative:

    define a minimal non-negligible core,

    prove each zero in it is essential,

    deduce one zero must carry a definite fraction of the signal.

This would be great because it avoids a huge generalized core theory.

But it still needs some controlled behavior under deleting one zero, which comes close to a weak near-additivity theorem. Whitening nonlinearity makes that hard.

This route is interesting but underdeveloped. I would rank it as medium promise, unclear feasibility.
10. Full rewrite around a generalized finite-dimensional endgame

This is the вАЬaccept realityвАЭ option.

If same-scale nearby zeros cannot be shown harmless, and the pair model is too rigid, then the right paper may need to say:

    extract the minimal same-scale finite core,

    build a generalized core anomaly matrix,

    calibrate and obstruct in that larger finite-dimensional space.

This is the most general and probably most robust approach. But it is a major rewrite, not a patch.

I would rank it as most robust in principle, but also most likely to fundamentally alter the paper.
Where things stand right now

The approaches that now look most alive are:

    Fixed-core / finite-core extraction, but with a stronger theorem showing the core signal beats the universal exterior threshold.

    Scalar rigidity/transversality for Hm,RHm,RвАЛ, possibly via compactness.

    Generalized finite-core endgame, if the pair-core cannot be saved.

The routes that now look weakest are:

    вАЬauxiliary near zeros are harmlessвАЭ in full generality,

    raw positivity lower bounds on S\core,HS\core,HвАЛ as a function of HH,

    and trying to finish the old pair-only proof without addressing same-scale nearby zeros.

My actual recommendation

If the goal is to save the current paper with the smallest conceptual change, I would focus on scalar rigidity/transversality:

    stay with the corrected scalar Hm,RHm,RвАЛ,

    prove that a genuine anomaly cannot be arbitrarily small in that scalar channel,

    use the threshold theorem to force a nearby zero,

    then use the geometric lower bound for S\coreS\coreвАЛ,

    and finish the pair/fixed-core argument.

If that fails, the honest next move is the generalized finite-core endgame.

That is the current map.

the goal is still to prove RH, but the current draft has been reduced to a small number of very specific unresolved mathematical issues, not a vague mass of gaps.

    you generalize the endgame from the pair model to a true finite-core anomaly model.

What is already genuinely in place

    a corrected finite-ss local block formalism,

What is actually stopping the RH proof now

The central remaining question is:
Can a genuine corrected off-line anomaly be arbitrarily weak in the relevant scalar/channel sense?
Can a genuine corrected off-line anomaly be arbitrarily weak in the relevant scalar/channel sense?

Equivalently:

    can all non-core nearby contributions be pushed below the universal exterior threshold, while a genuine anomaly still survives?

    or does a genuine anomaly force a definite nontrivial signal in either calibration or transverse channel?

That is the present bottleneck.
The two viable strategies
Strategy A: save the current pair/fixed-core paper

To do this, you need a rigidity theorem strong enough to show:

    a genuine anomaly beats the exterior threshold,

    hence there is a genuinely nearby zero,

    hence the fixed core has a real signal,

    hence the current calibrated contradiction fires.

This is the smaller change route, but it still needs a new theorem.
Strategy B: accept a generalized finite-core endgame

If nearby same-scale zeros cannot be shown negligible, then the pair model is too rigid. In that case the honest proof path is:

    extract the full finite same-scale core,

    define a generalized core anomaly matrix M\coreM\coreвАЛ,

    build calibration and obstruction functionals for that finite-core model,

    rerun the contradiction there.

This is a bigger rewrite, but probably the most robust if the pair model cannot be stabilized.
My best judgment

If the goal is really to prove RH, not just to save the current exact draft shape, then the mathematically safest attitude is:

    keep trying the scalar rigidity/transversality route for a bit longer,

    but be prepared that the true proof may require the generalized finite-core reformulation.

So the mission has not changed. The proof target is still RH.

But the current honest formulation is:
RH is reduced to a sharp local rigidity / finite-core extraction problem.
RH is reduced to a sharp local rigidity / finite-core extraction problem.вАЛ

That is a much better place to be than before.

The best next move is to choose deliberately between:

    one more serious attempt at scalar rigidity for Hm,RHm,RвАЛ, or

    starting the generalized finite-core endgame before investing more in rescuing the pair model.

5. Consequence for Strategy A

This means the вАЬcore-only linear coefficient theoremвАЭ is probably false.

So Strategy A in that form does not close the gap.

The remaining possible rescue would be a weaker theorem:

    although the complement contributes to the linear coefficient, its contribution is quantitatively smaller than the core contribution.

So this route does not give a free scalar rigidity theorem.

Yes. There are a few real tricks left, and this is the best remaining version of Strategy A.

The key is to stop trying to prove the complement contributes nothing to the linear term, and instead try to prove:
вИ£a1\comp(m,R)вИ£вЙ™вИ£a1\core(m)вИ£

Trick 1: isolate the source of the linear term more sharply

O((ќФ2+a2)вИТ2),
O((ќФ2+a2)вИТ2),

So the linear coefficient of the complement is not arbitrary. It is built out of third-derivative tails.

What you want to prove is something like:
вИ£a1\comp(m,R)вИ£вЙ™вИСѕБвИЙ\core1Q3(ќФѕБ2+aѕБ2)вИТ2вЙ™1Q2H3

If that estimate is true, then the whole problem reduces to showing the core linear coefficient is larger than QвИТ2HвИТ3QвИТ2HвИТ3. That is much simpler than controlling the whole scalar.

So the first trick is: work directly with the linear coefficient a1a1вАЛ, not the full function H(s)H(s).
Trick 2: compute a1a1вАЛ via differentiation at s=0s=0

вИ£a1\compвИ£вЙ™1Q2H3.
вИ£a1\compвАЛвИ£вЙ™Q2H31вАЛ.

That is a real, concrete theorem target.
Trick 3: compare against the core by choosing the core via a1a1вАЛ-dominance

This is the sneakiest but maybe the most natural.

Instead of defining the core as вАЬthe anomalous pairвАЭ or вАЬall zeros inside some geometric radius,вАЭ define the core to be the smallest finite set for which the linear coefficient a1a1вАЛ of the corrected scalar is captured up to, say, 90%90%.

Then by definition,
вИ£a1\compвИ£вЙ§0.1вАЙвИ£a1\coreвИ£.
вИ£a1\compвАЛвИ£вЙ§0.1вИ£a1\coreвАЛвИ£.

This would solve the comparison automatically.

The price is conceptual: the core is no longer defined geometrically first, but by the observable you care about.

That may still be okay if your goal is RH rather than preserving the current exact narrative. The argument would become:

    define the core by a1a1вАЛ-dominance,

    show the complement is small in the transverse/calibration channels,

    calibrate against the core,

    derive contradiction.

This is actually elegant, but it shifts the architecture.
Trick 4: use positivity on the core side, cancellation on the complement side

There is one more possible trick.

The core contribution to calibration comes from pairing with A\valA\valвАЛ, which is essentially off-diagonal skew at leading order. The complement contribution to a1a1вАЛ comes through sums of rвА≤вА≤вА≤(m)rвА≤вА≤вА≤(m)-type terms from many zeros.

Those complement terms may have sign oscillation or cancellation, while the core value scale is positive.

So one possible route is:

    lower-bound the core by a positive quantity,

    upper-bound the complement by absolute values,

    conclude dominance once the core contains even one sufficiently nearby zero and HH is chosen appropriately.

This is less elegant than the direct derivative formula, but it is worth keeping in mind.
