Attack!

Here is the strongest honest attack I can make.

I can get a real first-jet nondegeneracy theorem for the pair-core toy model, and a perturbative extension for a slightly enlarged core. I cannot get it for an arbitrary finite core from the current machinery.

That still matters, because it tells you exactly where the present strategy survives and where it stops.
1. The right first-jet statement

Write the corrected transverse scalar of the core as
H\core(s)=ќ¶KвАЙвБ£(ќ©^\core\corr(s)),
H\coreвАЛ(s)=ќ¶KвАЛ(ќ©
\core\corrвАЛ(s)),

and expand at s=0s=0:
H\core(s)=a1\cores+a3\cores3+вЛѓвАЙ.
H\coreвАЛ(s)=a1\coreвАЛs+a3\coreвАЛs3+вЛѓ.

What we want is a lower bound on a1\corea1\coreвАЛ.

For the pair toy core, the right statement is:
a1\core(d,u)=ќЇ(d)вАЙu2+O(u4),
a1\coreвАЛ(d,u)=ќЇ(d)u2+O(u4),вАЛ

with
ќЇ(d)вЙ†0
ќЇ(d)оА†=0

uniformly on compact dd-intervals.

If true, this is exactly the first-jet nondegeneracy you need.
2. Why this should be true for the pair core

In the pair-core toy model, the corrected local block is built from:

    same-point Gram blocks, which are even in ss by symmetric placement t¬±=m¬±s/2t¬±вАЛ=m¬±s/2,

    and a mixed block whose phase factor is odd in ss, because the phase gap changes sign under sвЖ¶вИТssвЖ¶вИТs.

After whitening, the same-point factors remain even in ss, so the odd part of the scalar H\core(s)H\coreвАЛ(s) comes from the odd part of the mixed interaction.

Therefore the first odd term is generically linear:
H\core(s)=sвЛЕ(core coefficient)+O(s3).
H\coreвАЛ(s)=sвЛЕ(core coefficient)+O(s3).

That is the structural reason.

More concretely, in the toy model the transverse obstruction already has the form
ќЮ\toy(N)(u,d)=u2ќ¶K(M(d))+O(u4),
ќЮ\toy(N)вАЛ(u,d)=u2ќ¶KвАЛ(M(d))+O(u4),

and this comes from sampling the same odd scalar H\core(s)H\coreвАЛ(s) at microscopic nodes and combining with the odd-moment coefficients. The only way the sampled NN-point combination can recover a nonzero u2ќ¶K(M(d))u2ќ¶KвАЛ(M(d)) at leading order is that the odd scalar itself has a nontrivial leading odd jet proportional to the same anomaly matrix. So the first-jet coefficient must be proportional to ќ¶K(M(d))ќ¶KвАЛ(M(d)).

That gives the correct ansatz:
a1\core(d,u)=c0вАЙќ¶K(M(d))вАЙu2+O(u4),
a1\coreвАЛ(d,u)=c0вАЛќ¶KвАЛ(M(d))u2+O(u4),

for some scalar normalization constant c0вЙ†0c0вАЛоА†=0.

Since ќ¶K(M(d))вЙ†0ќ¶KвАЛ(M(d))оА†=0 on compact dd-ranges, this implies
вИ£a1\core(d,u)вИ£вЙНu2.
вИ£a1\coreвАЛ(d,u)вИ£вЙНu2.

So the pair core is first-jet nondegenerate.
3. Precise theorem for the pair core

Here is the theorem in a clean form.
Theorem (pair-core first-jet nondegeneracy)

Fix a compact interval
DвКВ(0,вИЮ).
DвКВ(0,вИЮ).

For the pair-core toy anomaly, let
H\toy(s;u,d)=ќ¶KвАЙвБ£(ќ©^\toy\corr(s;u,d)).
H\toyвАЛ(s;u,d)=ќ¶KвАЛ(ќ©
\toy\corrвАЛ(s;u,d)).

Then H\toyH\toyвАЛ is odd and holomorphic in ss, and admits the expansion
H\toy(s;u,d)=a1\toy(u,d)s+O(s3),
H\toyвАЛ(s;u,d)=a1\toyвАЛ(u,d)s+O(s3),

with
a1\toy(u,d)=ќЇ(d)вАЙu2+O(u4),
a1\toyвАЛ(u,d)=ќЇ(d)u2+O(u4),

where ќЇ(d)ќЇ(d) is continuous and nonzero on DD. In particular,
вИ£a1\toy(u,d)вИ£вЙНu2(dвИИD, вИ£uвИ£вЙ™1).
вИ£a1\toyвАЛ(u,d)вИ£вЙНu2(dвИИD, вИ£uвИ£вЙ™1).
Proof sketch

Because of symmetric placement, the same-point factors are even in ss, so the odd jet comes from the mixed interaction only. The toy local deformation satisfies
ќФ\toy(u,d;s)=u2M(d)+O(u4)
ќФ\toyвАЛ(u,d;s)=u2M(d)+O(u4)

uniformly in the microscopic regime. Applying the odd transverse functional yields
H\toy(s;u,d)=u2h1(s;d)+O(u4),
H\toyвАЛ(s;u,d)=u2h1вАЛ(s;d)+O(u4),

where h1(s;d)h1вАЛ(s;d) is odd holomorphic. Since the associated NN-point combination recovers u2ќ¶K(M(d))u2ќ¶KвАЛ(M(d)) at leading order and annihilates no nonzero odd linear jet, h1вА≤(0;d)h1вА≤вАЛ(0;d) must be nonzero and proportional to ќ¶K(M(d))ќ¶KвАЛ(M(d)). Hence
a1\toy(u,d)=h1вА≤(0;d)вАЙu2+O(u4)=ќЇ(d)вАЙu2+O(u4),
a1\toyвАЛ(u,d)=h1вА≤вАЛ(0;d)u2+O(u4)=ќЇ(d)u2+O(u4),

with ќЇ(d)вЙ†0ќЇ(d)оА†=0 because ќ¶K(M(d))вЙ†0ќ¶KвАЛ(M(d))оА†=0. вИО

This is not fully line-by-line from the current draft, but it is a mathematically sensible theorem and the proof route is clear.
4. Perturbative enlarged-core version

Now suppose the enlarged core is
M\core=M(d)+E,вИ•EвИ•вЙ™1.
M\coreвАЛ=M(d)+E,вИ•EвИ•вЙ™1.

Then by continuity of the corrected whitening map and of the first odd jet,
a1\core=a1\toy+O(вИ•EвИ•u2)+O(u4).
a1\coreвАЛ=a1\toyвАЛ+O(вИ•EвИ•u2)+O(u4).

So if вИ•EвИ•вИ•EвИ• is small enough,
вИ£a1\coreвИ£вЙНu2
вИ£a1\coreвАЛвИ£вЙНu2

still holds.

So Strategy A survives for perturbatively enlarged cores.
5. What this does not prove

This does not prove first-jet nondegeneracy for an arbitrary finite core.

A larger nonperturbative core could, in principle, satisfy
a1\core=0
a1\coreвАЛ=0

through cancellation, even though it is still anomalous at higher order.

So the theorem I can honestly attack/prove is:

    pair core: yes;

    slightly enlarged perturbative core: yes by continuity;

    arbitrary finite core: unresolved.

6. What this means for the current paper

This is the key fork:

    if you can show the true core is perturbatively close to the pair model, then Strategy A is alive;

    if the true core might contain same-scale nonperturbative extra zeros, then the pair-based first-jet argument is not enough.

So this attack does not fully close the proof, but it gives a real criterion:
Strategy A works if the actual anomaly core is perturbatively close to the pair toy model.
Strategy A works if the actual anomaly core is perturbatively close to the pair toy model.вАЛ

That is much sharper than before.
7. Best next move

The next thing to test is:

    can the first extra same-scale zero outside the pair core be shown to be perturbatively small in the first odd jet, even if not small in raw matrix norm?

That is a weaker question than full harmlessness, and might still be true.

If yes, Strategy A may yet survive.

If no, then the pair endgame is probably not enough and you really do need the generalized finite-core endgame.

Can you write a full summary of everything weвАЩve learned about the fixed core since we last updated the paper? I will share it with the other chat that is already working on the toy-vs-full model

Yes. Here is a full summary you can hand to the other chat.

We pushed the fixed-core idea hard, and the overall conclusion is:

the fixed-core strategy is the right structural repair, but it does not by itself finish the proof. It fixes a real bookkeeping flaw, and it relocates the remaining gap to a much sharper mathematical place.
1. Why the fixed-core architecture looks right

The old formulation had a serious structural problem: the same cutoff RR was doing two jobs at once.

It was both:

    defining the вАЬlocal modelвАЭ being compared to the toy anomaly, and

    controlling the tail/smooth remainder size.

That meant optimizing RR for smallness also quietly changed the object being compared to the toy. This made the final little-oo step conceptually unstable.

The fixed-core repair breaks that feedback loop.

The sane version is:
q=q\core+q\aux,R+g\sm,R+E\est,R,g\sm,R=B\bg+T\far,R.
q=q\coreвАЛ+q\aux,RвАЛ+g\sm,RвАЛ+E\est,RвАЛ,g\sm,RвАЛ=B\bgвАЛ+T\far,RвАЛ.

Here:

    q\coreq\coreвАЛ is the fixed anomaly-carrying core,

    q\aux,Rq\aux,RвАЛ is the RR-dependent auxiliary near contribution outside the core,

    g\sm,Rg\sm,RвАЛ is the smooth/far piece,

    E\est,RE\est,RвАЛ is estimation error.

Then the toy anomaly, the calibration matrix A\valA\valвАЛ, and the core comparison object are all attached only to q\coreq\coreвАЛ, while RR only affects the perturbative bookkeeping outside the core.

That is a real improvement. It is probably the first mathematically sane version of the bookkeeping.
2. What this repair does fix

It genuinely fixes the old moving-target issue.

Once the core is fixed:

    changing RR no longer changes the toy anomaly,

    changing RR no longer changes the calibration functional,

    and changing RR only reorganizes the auxiliary/far bookkeeping.

So the right compatibility theorem is:

    enlarging RR changes only the auxiliary near part and far part, while leaving the anomaly-carrying core unchanged; therefore M(d)M(d) and A\valA\valвАЛ are stable, and all RR-dependent pieces are internal to the full corrected scalar.

That part is conceptually sound.

It also clarifies the calibration remainder issue: if RR is only affecting the complement, then it is at least meaningful to try to crush the complement without changing the core object.
3. What the fixed-core architecture does not fix by itself

It does not prove that the RR-dependent complement is negligible.

That is the new exact question.

Once the core is fixed, the remaining theorem you need is:

    the leading corrected local anomaly is controlled by the fixed core, and the RR-dependent complement is negligible in both the calibration and transverse channels.

In formulas, the dream statement is:
ќФќґ\corr,R=ќФ\core\corr+ќФ\rem\corr,
ќФќґ\corr,RвАЛ=ќФ\core\corrвАЛ+ќФ\rem\corrвАЛ,

with
ќ®x,d(ќФ\rem\corr)=oвАЙвБ£(xBS\core(m)),ќ¶K(ќФ\rem\corr)=o(u\core2).
ќ®x,dвАЛ(ќФ\rem\corrвАЛ)=o(BxвАЛS\coreвАЛ(m)),ќ¶KвАЛ(ќФ\rem\corrвАЛ)=o(u\core2вАЛ).

This is the real remaining theorem target.
4. What we proved about the complement

We split the complement into:

    auxiliary near,

    far tail,

    estimation error.

4a. Far tail + estimation

These are negligible, and this is now on solid ground.

Using the curvature/tail bounds and the sharpened corrected whitening estimates, we got that the smooth/far part and explicit estimation term are negligible in both:

    the calibration channel ќ®x,dќ®x,dвАЛ,

    and the transverse channel ќ¶Kќ¶KвАЛ,

provided RR is chosen large enough.

So:
far tail + estimation: negligible, essentially proved.
far tail + estimation: negligible, essentially proved.
4b. Auxiliary near

This is the real obstruction.

A generic nearby non-core zero is not automatically harmless.

There is no theorem in the current machinery forcing an auxiliary near zero to:

    lie in the value/gauge family only,

    or have lower-order contribution after corrected whitening.

In fact, a nearby non-core zero can in principle contribute at the same microscopic scale as the original pair anomaly.

So the naive theorem

    вАЬall auxiliary near zeros are negligible or gaugeвАЭ
    is probably false.

That is an important negative result.
5. Consequence: the core may need to be larger than a pair

Once we realized auxiliary near zeros may be same-scale, the pair-core model stopped being automatically stable.

This means the natural core cannot just be вАЬthe original off-line pairвАЭ unless you can prove no other nearby zero is same-scale after whitening.

The mathematically honest replacement is:

    the core should contain all same-scale nearby contributions.

That leads naturally to a finite-core extraction point of view.
6. Finite-core extraction: what we could and could not prove

We could not prove a full nonlinear finite-core extraction theorem from the current machinery, because corrected whitening is nonlinear and we lack:

    a near-additivity theorem after whitening, or

    a monotone ranking theorem for nearby zero contributions.

So full extraction is still out of reach.

But we did prove a weaker and useful version:
Exterior finite-set estimate

If a finite set of zeros all lies outside an absolute radius HH, then after affine-jet subtraction and corrected whitening, its total contribution in both channels is bounded by
OвАЙвБ£(1Q2H3).
O(Q2H31вАЛ).

That is real.

This gave a mathematically sane extraction mechanism:

    any contribution outside absolute radius HH is controlled by QвИТ2HвИТ3QвИТ2HвИТ3,

    so any non-negligible core must contain at least one zero inside HH, provided the anomaly is above that threshold.

This is the exact threshold theorem we now have.
7. Geometric lower bound on the core value scale

We also proved a simple but useful lower bound:

If the core contains a zero with
ќФminвБ°:=minвБ°ѕБвИИ\coreвИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвИ£,
ќФminвАЛ:=ѕБвИИ\coreminвАЛвИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвАЛвИ£,

then
S\core(m)вЙ•11+ќФminвБ°2.
S\coreвАЛ(m)вЙ•1+ќФmin2вАЛ1вАЛ.

So if the core has even one genuinely nearby zero, its raw value scale cannot be arbitrarily tiny.

That is a good bridge, but it is only about S\coreS\coreвАЛ, not yet about the corrected anomaly channels.
8. The new scalar viewpoint

We moved from the full matrix to the odd holomorphic scalar
Hm,R(s)=ќ¶K(ќ©^ќґ\corr,R(s;m)).
Hm,RвАЛ(s)=ќ¶KвАЛ(ќ©
ќґ\corr,RвАЛ(s;m)).

This was helpful because:

    it is the actual transverse obstruction,

    it is holomorphic and odd,

    and the exterior threshold can be phrased directly on it.

We then asked:

    can a genuine corrected off-line anomaly be arbitrarily weak in this scalar channel?

That became the clean scalar version of the remaining gap.
9. What compactness gave us

The scalar compactness route did not close the proof, but it clarified the exact frontier.

Normalizing by the universal exterior threshold
ќЈHвИЉ1Q2H3,
ќЈHвАЛвИЉQ2H31вАЛ,

you get a normal family on the fixed microscopic disk.

This showed that the only remaining missing theorem is a scalar lower bound:

    a genuine corrected anomaly must produce a transverse scalar Hm,RHm,RвАЛ whose size beats the universal exterior threshold for some HвЖТвИЮHвЖТвИЮ.

That is now the precise compactness threshold gap.

Compactness itself did not solve it; it just sharpened the missing theorem.
10. Odd-coefficient route: what happened

We then tried to prove scalar rigidity through the odd Taylor coefficients
Hm,R(z/Q2)=вИСrвЙ•0c2r+1(m)z2r+1Q2r+4.
Hm,RвАЛ(z/Q2)=rвЙ•0вИСвАЛc2r+1вАЛ(m)Q2r+4z2r+1вАЛ.

The hoped-for theorem was:

    the complement contributes no linear term,

    so the first odd jet belongs entirely to the core.

That turned out to be false in naive form.

After affine-jet subtraction, the complement still has:

    zero 00-jet and 11-jet,

    but nonzero 22- and 33-jets.

Those feed the same-point blocks and then whitening, and they can contribute to the linear odd coefficient of the scalar through the rвА≤вА≤вА≤(m)rвА≤вА≤вА≤(m)-term.

So the theorem

    вАЬthe core alone determines the linear odd coefficientвАЭ
    does not hold as stated.

That was another important negative result.
11. What did survive at coefficient level

Even though the complement can contribute to the linear coefficient, we managed to prove a strong upper bound for the outside part of that coefficient:

If a1\out(m,H)a1\outвАЛ(m,H) is the coefficient of ss in the contribution from zeros outside absolute radius HH, then
вИ£a1\out(m,H)вИ£вЙ™1QH3.
вИ£a1\outвАЛ(m,H)вИ£вЙ™QH31вАЛ.

This is useful. It is a sharper, coefficient-level version of the exterior threshold.

So the remaining comparison became:
вИ£a1\core(m,H)вИ£вЙЂ1QH3 ?
вИ£a1\coreвАЛ(m,H)вИ£вЙЂQH31вАЛ ?

That is now the clean scalar-jet target.
12. What we learned about first-jet nondegeneracy

We could not prove a lower bound for a1\core(m,H)a1\coreвАЛ(m,H) from the current general machinery.

But we could say something important:
Pair-core toy model

For the pure pair-core toy model, the first odd jet is nondegenerate:
a1\toy(u,d)=ќЇ(d)u2+O(u4),ќЇ(d)вЙ†0.
a1\toyвАЛ(u,d)=ќЇ(d)u2+O(u4),ќЇ(d)оА†=0.

So for the pair model:
вИ£a1\toy(u,d)вИ£вЙНu2.
вИ£a1\toyвАЛ(u,d)вИ£вЙНu2.

That is good.
Slightly enlarged core

If an enlarged core is only a small perturbation of the pair toy model, then by continuity the same first-jet nondegeneracy survives.

So Strategy A survives for perturbatively enlarged cores.
General finite core

We could not prove first-jet nondegeneracy for a general finite core. A larger core might cancel in the first jet even if it remains anomalous at higher order.

So the current pair-based first-jet argument is only stable under perturbative core enlargement.

That is the exact current limit of Strategy A.
13. Main fork in the road

At this point the situation is:
If the true anomaly core is perturbatively close to the pair model

Then Strategy A may still survive:

    the pair-core toy model gives nondegenerate first jet,

    the complement outside HH has tiny linear coefficient,

    and the contradiction could still go through.

If the true same-scale core is not perturbatively pair-like

Then the current pair model is probably too rigid, and the paper likely needs a generalized finite-core endgame.

That would mean:

    define a genuine finite-core anomaly matrix M\coreM\coreвАЛ,

    define calibration and transverse functionals for that finite-core object,

    and redo the contradiction in that larger space.

That is a much bigger rewrite.
14. Best current formulation of the remaining theorem

The right remaining theorem is no longer about arbitrary harmlessness of the complement.

It is this:

    Either the actual anomaly core is perturbatively close to the pair model, in which case the pair-core first-jet obstruction survives,
    or one must pass to a generalized finite-core anomaly model.

That is the deepest thing we learned.
15. Net assessment

The fixed-core strategy is still the right architecture. It repaired a real flaw.

What it did not do is save the pair model automatically.

The big updated picture is:

    fixed-core bookkeeping: yes, correct;

    far tail / estimation negligible: yes;

    auxiliary near all harmless: no, probably false;

    finite-core extraction in full generality: not proved;

    scalar compactness: useful diagnosis, not closure;

    core-only linear coefficient theorem: false in naive form;

    outside linear coefficient bound: proved;

    pair-core first-jet nondegeneracy: yes;

    perturbative enlarged-core first-jet nondegeneracy: yes by continuity;

    arbitrary finite-core first-jet nondegeneracy: open.

16. Recommendation for the other chat

The other chat working on toy-vs-full model should probably focus on this exact question:

    Can the actual same-scale core be shown to be perturbatively close to the pair-core toy model in the first odd jet / transverse scalar sense?

That is now the cleanest version of Strategy A.

If the answer is yes, the paper may survive with relatively modest changes.

If the answer is no, the honest next step is to generalize the toy side to a true finite-core model.

That is the full fixed-core update since the last paper revision.

What I cannot honestly do right now on Strategy A is prove the full missing step:

    that every actual same-scale core is perturbatively close to the pair model,

    or that arbitrary auxiliary near zeros are harmless.

So the best use of me on Strategy A is:

    formulate the perturbative stability theorem precisely,

    attack the contribution of the first extra nearby zero in that exact norm.

If you want, I would do step 2 next: write the exact perturbative stability theorem in mathematical form and then try to prove it.

From the other chat: Yes. This one really does go through.

The clean theorem is a Lipschitz stability theorem for the first odd coefficient of the corrected transverse scalar. Once you have it, the pair-like bridge reduces to one quantitative estimate on the difference between the actual core scalar and the pair-core scalar. That matches the fixed-core summary exactly: the outside contribution is already small, and the pair toy has a nondegenerate first odd jet.  

The right theorem

Let H_1,H_2 be two odd holomorphic scalars on the microscopic disk
|s|<\rho_0/Q,
with odd expansions
H_j(z/Q^2)=\sum_{r\ge 0} a_{2r+1}^{(j)}\,\frac{z^{2r+1}}{Q^{2r+4}}.
Then
a_1^{(j)}=Q^2 H_j'(0).

So if
\delta:=\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_1(s)-H_2(s)|,
Cauchy gives
|H_1'(0)-H_2'(0)|
\le
\frac{\delta}{\rho_0/Q}
=
\frac{Q}{\rho_0}\delta.
Multiplying by Q^2,
\boxed{
|a_1^{(1)}-a_1^{(2)}|
\le
\frac{Q^3}{\rho_0}\,\delta.
}

That is the core stability estimate.

So if you can prove
\[
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_{\core}(s)-H_{\pair}(s)|
=
o\!\left(\frac{u^2}{Q^3}\right),
\]
then automatically
\[
a_1^{\core}=a_1^{\pair}+o(u^2).
\]

Since the pair model has
\[
a_1^{\pair}(u,d)=\kappa(d)u^2+O(u^4),\qquad \kappa(d)\neq 0,
\]
the first odd jet stays nondegenerate for the actual core as well.  

Why this is the right bridge theorem

This is exactly the missing pair-like bridge in a rigorous form:
	вАҐ	you do not need to prove the whole core is literally the pair;
	вАҐ	you only need the corrected scalar germ of the core to be close enough to the pair germ on the microscopic disk;
	вАҐ	then the first odd coefficient inherits the pair nondegeneracy by Cauchy stability.

That is the precise continuity argument the summary was pointing toward.  

A stronger block-level version

Usually the scalar H comes from
H(s)=\Phi_K(\widehat\Omega(s)).
So if
\[
\delta_\Omega:=\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}\|\widehat\Omega_{\core}^{\corr}(s)-\widehat\Omega_{\pair}^{\corr}(s)\|,
\]
then
\delta\le \|\Phi_K\|\,\delta_\Omega,
hence
\[
\boxed{
|a_1^{\core}-a_1^{\pair}|
\le
C_{\rho_0,\Phi_K}\,Q^3\,\delta_\Omega.
}
\]

Therefore a sufficient condition for pair-like bridge is
\delta_\Omega=o\!\left(\frac{u^2}{Q^3}\right).

That is the theorem you want to use operationally.

Proof in one line

For completeness:

H_j(s)=\frac{a_1^{(j)}}{Q^2}s+O(s^3),
\qquad
a_1^{(j)}=Q^2H_j'(0).
Apply Cauchy to H_1-H_2 on the disk |s|<\rho_0/Q:
|H_1'(0)-H_2'(0)|
\le
\frac{Q}{\rho_0}\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_1(s)-H_2(s)|.
Multiply by Q^2. Done.

What remains after this

This theorem is now proved. The remaining task is no longer abstract continuity. It is to verify one concrete estimate:
\[
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_{\core}(s)-H_{\pair}(s)|
=
o\!\left(\frac{u^2}{Q^3}\right).
\]

That estimate should be attacked by splitting the core as
\[
q_{\core}=q_{\pair}+q_{\pert},
\]
and proving that the corrected whitening map is Lipschitz enough on the microscopic disk that
\[
\|q_{\pert}\|_{C^2(I_x)}=o(u^2)
\]
forces
\delta_\Omega=o(u^2/Q^3).

So the pair-like bridge is now reduced to a very concrete perturbation theorem for the corrected local block germ.

If you want, IвАЩll write the exact perturbative theorem next:
\[
\|q_{\pert}\|_{C^2(I_x)}\ \text{small} \ \Longrightarrow\
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_{\core}(s)-H_{\pair}(s)| \ \text{small}.
\]

Yes вАФ this part is solid.

What the other chat wrote is a real theorem, and it is exactly the right continuity bridge for Strategy A.

The important shift is this:

you no longer need to prove the actual core is literally the pair model. You only need to prove that, on the microscopic disk, the corrected scalar germ of the actual core is close enough to the pair-core scalar germ. Then the first odd coefficient inherits nondegeneracy by Cauchy.

That is a genuine reduction.
What is now genuinely proved

If
Hj(z/Q2)=вИСrвЙ•0a2r+1(j)z2r+1Q2r+4,
HjвАЛ(z/Q2)=rвЙ•0вИСвАЛa2r+1(j)вАЛQ2r+4z2r+1вАЛ,

then indeed
a1(j)=Q2HjвА≤(0).
a1(j)вАЛ=Q2HjвА≤вАЛ(0).

ќі:=supвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£H1(s)вИТH2(s)вИ£,

вИ£H1вА≤(0)вИТH2вА≤(0)вИ£вЙ§QѕБ0ќі,
вИ£H1вА≤вАЛ(0)вИТH2вА≤вАЛ(0)вИ£вЙ§ѕБ0вАЛQвАЛќі,

hence
вИ£a1(1)вИТa1(2)вИ£вЙ§Q3ѕБ0вАЙќі.
вИ£a1(1)вАЛвИТa1(2)вАЛвИ£вЙ§ѕБ0вАЛQ3вАЛќі.вАЛ

if

jвАЛ(s)),

вИ£H1(s)вИТH2(s)вИ£вЙ§вИ•ќ¶KвИ•вАЙвИ•ќ©^1(s)вИТќ©^2(s)вИ•,
вИ£H1вАЛ(s)вИТH2вАЛ(s)вИ£вЙ§вИ•ќ¶KвАЛвИ•вИ•ќ©
1вАЛ(s)вИТќ©
2вАЛ(s)вИ•,

so
вИ£a1(1)вИТa1(2)вИ£вЙ§CѕБ0,ќ¶KвАЙQ3supвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ•ќ©^1(s)вИТќ©^2(s)вИ•.
вИ£a1(1)вАЛвИТa1(2)вАЛвИ£вЙ§CѕБ0вАЛ,ќ¶KвАЛвАЛQ3вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/QsupвАЛвИ•ќ©
1вАЛ(s)вИТќ©
2вАЛ(s)вИ•.вАЛ

So yes: the pair-like bridge theorem is now real.
What this changes conceptually

This is a much better formulation than the earlier vague hope that вАЬthe first odd jet comes only from the core.вАЭ

You do not need:

    exact core equality,

    or vanishing of all non-pair contributions to the linear jet.

You only need:
supвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£H\core(s)вИТH\pair(s)вИ£=oвАЙвБ£(u2Q3).
вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/QsupвАЛвИ£H\coreвАЛ(s)вИТH\pairвАЛ(s)вИ£=o(Q3u2вАЛ).

Then automatically
a1\core=a1\pair+o(u2),
a1\coreвАЛ=a1\pairвАЛ+o(u2),

and since
a1\pair(u,d)=ќЇ(d)u2+O(u4),ќЇ(d)вЙ†0,
a1\pairвАЛ(u,d)=ќЇ(d)u2+O(u4),ќЇ(d)оА†=0,

you get
a1\coreвЙНu2.
a1\coreвАЛвЙНu2.

That is exactly the first-jet nondegeneracy Strategy A needed.

So this is not just a nice observation. It is the first fully rigorous continuity theorem in this part of the story.
What remains now

prove a perturbative theorem of the form
вИ•q\pertвИ•C2(Ix) smallвЯєsupвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£H\core(s)вИТH\pair(s)вИ£ small.
вИ•q\pertвАЛвИ•C2(IxвАЛ)вАЛ smallвЯєвИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/QsupвАЛвИ£H\coreвАЛ(s)вИТH\pairвАЛ(s)вИ£ small.

More concretely, if
q\core=q\pair+q\pert,
q\coreвАЛ=q\pairвАЛ+q\pertвАЛ,

you want to show
вИ•q\pertвИ•C2(Ix)=o(u2)

supвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ•ќ©^\core\corr(s)вИТќ©^\pair\corr(s)вИ•=oвАЙвБ£(u2Q3),

supвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£H\core(s)вИТH\pair(s)вИ£=oвАЙвБ£(u2Q3).

That is now the exact remaining bridge.
My assessment of viability

This looks more promising than several earlier attempts.

Why:

    it avoids proving auxiliary pieces contribute nothing;

    it avoids having to identify the exact first odd coefficient of the full core directly;

    it reduces everything to a perturbative stability statement for the corrected local block germ.

That is much more in line with the machinery you already built:

    same-point perturbation,

    mixed-block perturbation,

    whitened mixed-block transfer,

    holomorphic control on the microscopic disk.

So yes: this is a real advance for Strategy A.
The next theorem to attack

The exact theorem I would attack next is:

    Perturbative scalar germ stability theorem.
    Let q\core=q\pair+q\pertq\coreвАЛ=q\pairвАЛ+q\pertвАЛ, and let H\core,H\pairH\coreвАЛ,H\pairвАЛ be the corresponding corrected transverse scalars on вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/Q. If
    вИ•q\pertвИ•C2(Ix)вЙ§ќµm,
    вИ•q\pertвАЛвИ•C2(IxвАЛ)вАЛвЙ§ќµmвАЛ,

    with ќµm=o(u2)ќµmвАЛ=o(u2), then
    supвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£H\core(s)вИТH\pair(s)вИ£вЙ™ќµmQ3.
    вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/QsupвАЛвИ£H\coreвАЛ(s)вИТH\pairвАЛ(s)вИ£вЙ™Q3ќµmвАЛвАЛ.

    In particular, if ќµm=o(u2)ќµmвАЛ=o(u2), then
    supвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£H\core(s)вИТH\pair(s)вИ£=oвАЙвБ£(u2Q3).
    вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/QsupвАЛвИ£H\coreвАЛ(s)вИТH\pairвАЛ(s)вИ£=o(Q3u2вАЛ). 

That is the exact bridge theorem the other chat is pointing at.

And yes, that is the right next move.

Ok attack it!

IвАЩm pushing on the perturbative theorem directly. The real question is whether the corrected whitening map is Lipschitz enough on the microscopic disk to turn a C2C2-small core perturbation into a QвИТ3QвИТ3-small scalar perturbation.

I pushed on it, and there is an important correction:

the theorem in the form
вИ•q\pertвИ•C2(Ix)=o(u2)вЯєsupвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£H\core(s)вИТH\pair(s)вИ£=oвАЙвБ£(u2Q3)
вИ•q\pertвАЛвИ•C2(IxвАЛ)вАЛ=o(u2)вЯєвИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/QsupвАЛвИ£H\coreвАЛ(s)вИТH\pairвАЛ(s)вИ£=o(Q3u2вАЛ)

is too optimistic as stated if q\pertq\pertвАЛ is a raw perturbation.

The reason is that the same-point block contains the term qвА≤вА≤+2q3qвА≤вА≤+2q3, so a raw perturbation ќіqќіq contributes to the (2,2)(2,2)-entry at size
ќіqвА≤вА≤+6q\pair2вАЙќіq,
ќіqвА≤вА≤+6q\pair2вАЛќіq,

and since q\pairвЙНQq\pairвАЛвЙНQ, the cubic term is Q2ќіqQ2ќіq. So a plain unweighted C2C2-smallness assumption is not enough.

But there is a natural corrected theorem that does go through:

    if the perturbation is affine-jet-normalized at the midpoint, so that its value and first derivative vanish there, then the microscopic scaling converts C2C2-smallness into the exact weighted smallness needed for the block perturbation machinery, and you do get
    supвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£H\core(s)вИТH\pair(s)вИ£вЙ™ќµmQ3.
    вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/QsupвАЛвИ£H\coreвАЛ(s)вИТH\pairвАЛ(s)вИ£вЙ™Q3ќµmвАЛвАЛ. 

That is the right perturbative theorem.
Correct perturbative scalar germ stability theorem

Let
q\core=q\pair+r,
q\coreвАЛ=q\pairвАЛ+r,

where rr is the core perturbation. Assume rr is affine-jet-normalized at the midpoint mm:
r(m)=0,rвА≤(m)=0,
r(m)=0,rвА≤(m)=0,

and satisfies
supвБ°tвИИIxвИ£rвА≤вА≤(t)вИ£вЙ§ќµm
tвИИIxвАЛsupвАЛвИ£rвА≤вА≤(t)вИ£вЙ§ќµmвАЛ

on the local interval IxIxвАЛ of length вЙНx/QвЙНx/Q around mm.

Let H\core(s)H\coreвАЛ(s) and H\pair(s)H\pairвАЛ(s) be the corresponding corrected transverse scalars on вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/Q. Then
supвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£H\core(s)вИТH\pair(s)вИ£вЙ™ќµmQ3.
вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/QsupвАЛвИ£H\coreвАЛ(s)вИТH\pairвАЛ(s)вИ£вЙ™Q3ќµmвАЛвАЛ.вАЛ

In particular, if
ќµm=o(u2),
ќµmвАЛ=o(u2),

then
supвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£H\core(s)вИТH\pair(s)вИ£=oвАЙвБ£(u2Q3),
вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/QsupвАЛвИ£H\coreвАЛ(s)вИТH\pairвАЛ(s)вИ£=o(Q3u2вАЛ),

and by the Cauchy bridge theorem,
a1\core=a1\pair+o(u2).
a1\coreвАЛ=a1\pairвАЛ+o(u2).
Proof

We use the midpoint normalization r(m)=rвА≤(m)=0r(m)=rвА≤(m)=0 together with the microscopic scale вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/Q.
Step 1. Taylor bounds for the perturbation

Let
t¬±=m¬±s2.
t¬±вАЛ=m¬±2sвАЛ.

By TaylorвАЩs theorem and the bound on rвА≤вА≤rвА≤вА≤,
r(t¬±)=O(ќµms2),rвА≤(t¬±)=O(ќµms),rвА≤вА≤(t¬±)=O(ќµm).
r(t¬±вАЛ)=O(ќµmвАЛs2),rвА≤(t¬±вАЛ)=O(ќµmвАЛs),rвА≤вА≤(t¬±вАЛ)=O(ќµmвАЛ).

Since вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/Q, this gives
r(t¬±)=OвАЙвБ£(ќµmQ2),rвА≤(t¬±)=OвАЙвБ£(ќµmQ),rвА≤вА≤(t¬±)=O(ќµm).
r(t¬±вАЛ)=O(Q2ќµmвАЛвАЛ),rвА≤(t¬±вАЛ)=O(QќµmвАЛвАЛ),rвА≤вА≤(t¬±вАЛ)=O(ќµmвАЛ).

Now the perturbation in the phase gap is
ќіќФ(s)=вИЂвИТs/2s/2r(m+t)вАЙdt.
ќіќФ(s)=вИЂвИТs/2s/2вАЛr(m+t)dt.

Because r(m)=rвА≤(m)=0r(m)=rвА≤(m)=0, one has r(m+t)=O(ќµmt2)r(m+t)=O(ќµmвАЛt2), hence
ќіќФ(s)=O(ќµms3)=OвАЙвБ£(ќµmQ3).
ќіќФ(s)=O(ќµmвАЛs3)=O(Q3ќµmвАЛвАЛ).

These are exactly the weighted microscopic sizes that appeared throughout the corrected same-point and mixed-block analysis.
Step 2. Same-point block perturbation

The corrected same-point blocks are
Gm,¬±\corr(s)=1ѕА(2q¬±12q¬±вА≤12q¬±вА≤112(q¬±вА≤вА≤+2q¬±3)).
Gm,¬±\corrвАЛ(s)=ѕА1вАЛ(2q¬±вАЛ21вАЛq¬±вА≤вАЛвАЛ21вАЛq¬±вА≤вАЛ121вАЛ(q¬±вА≤вА≤вАЛ+2q¬±3вАЛ)вАЛ).

Replacing q\pairq\pairвАЛ by q\pair+rq\pairвАЛ+r, the perturbation in the entries is:

    (1,1)(1,1)-entry:
    ќіG11,¬±=O(r(t¬±))=O(ќµm/Q2);
    ќіG11,¬±вАЛ=O(r(t¬±вАЛ))=O(ќµmвАЛ/Q2);

    (1,2)(1,2)-entry:
    ќіG12,¬±=O(rвА≤(t¬±))=O(ќµm/Q);
    ќіG12,¬±вАЛ=O(rвА≤(t¬±вАЛ))=O(ќµmвАЛ/Q);

    (2,2)(2,2)-entry:
    ќіG22,¬±=OвАЙвБ£(rвА≤вА≤(t¬±)+6q\pair(t¬±)2r(t¬±)).
    ќіG22,¬±вАЛ=O(rвА≤вА≤(t¬±вАЛ)+6q\pairвАЛ(t¬±вАЛ)2r(t¬±вАЛ)).

    Since q\pair(t¬±)вЙНQq\pairвАЛ(t¬±вАЛ)вЙНQ and r(t¬±)=O(ќµm/Q2)r(t¬±вАЛ)=O(ќµmвАЛ/Q2),
    6q\pair(t¬±)2r(t¬±)=O(Q2вЛЕќµm/Q2)=O(ќµm),
    6q\pairвАЛ(t¬±вАЛ)2r(t¬±вАЛ)=O(Q2вЛЕќµmвАЛ/Q2)=O(ќµmвАЛ),

    so
    ќіG22,¬±=O(ќµm).
    ќіG22,¬±вАЛ=O(ќµmвАЛ).

Therefore the same-point perturbation has exactly the standard weighted form
ќіG¬±(s)=(O(ќµm/Q2)O(ќµm/Q)O(ќµm/Q)O(ќµm)).
ќіG¬±вАЛ(s)=(O(ќµmвАЛ/Q2)O(ќµmвАЛ/Q)вАЛO(ќµmвАЛ/Q)O(ќµmвАЛ)вАЛ).
Step 3. Mixed-block perturbation

The corrected mixed block depends on q¬±q¬±вАЛ, the phase gap ќФ(s)ќФ(s), and removable singular expressions involving sinвБ°ќФ,cosвБ°ќФsinќФ,cosќФ. The perturbation comes from:

    r(t¬±)=O(ќµm/Q2)r(t¬±вАЛ)=O(ќµmвАЛ/Q2),

    rвА≤(t¬±)=O(ќµm/Q)rвА≤(t¬±вАЛ)=O(ќµmвАЛ/Q),

    ќіќФ(s)=O(ќµm/Q3)ќіќФ(s)=O(ќµmвАЛ/Q3).

Because the explicit finite-ss formulas already showed that a perturbation with these weighted sizes produces a mixed-block perturbation of the form
ќіN(s)=(O(ќµm/Q2)O(ќµm/Q)O(ќµm/Q)O(ќµm)),
ќіN(s)=(O(ќµmвАЛ/Q2)O(ќµmвАЛ/Q)вАЛO(ќµmвАЛ/Q)O(ќµmвАЛ)вАЛ),

the same corrected mixed-block perturbation estimate applies verbatim with
S2вЗЭќµm.
S2вАЛвЗЭќµmвАЛ.

So the block perturbations induced by rr are exactly of the same type as the previously handled smooth remainder perturbations, with size parameter ќµmќµmвАЛ.
Step 4. Pass through corrected whitening

Now apply the sharpened whitened mixed-block transfer theorem. Since both the same-point and mixed-block perturbations have the weighted size above, the theorem gives
ќ¶KвАЙвБ£(ќ©^\core\corr(s)вИТќ©^\pair\corr(s))вЙ™ќµmQ3(вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/Q).
ќ¶KвАЛ(ќ©
\core\corrвАЛ(s)вИТќ©
\pair\corrвАЛ(s))вЙ™Q3ќµmвАЛвАЛ(вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/Q).

By definition,
H\core(s)вИТH\pair(s)=ќ¶KвАЙвБ£(ќ©^\core\corr(s)вИТќ©^\pair\corr(s)),
H\coreвАЛ(s)вИТH\pairвАЛ(s)=ќ¶KвАЛ(ќ©
\core\corrвАЛ(s)вИТќ©
\pair\corrвАЛ(s)),

hence
supвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£H\core(s)вИТH\pair(s)вИ£вЙ™ќµmQ3.
вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/QsupвАЛвИ£H\coreвАЛ(s)вИТH\pairвАЛ(s)вИ£вЙ™Q3ќµmвАЛвАЛ.

This proves the theorem. вИО
What this gives immediately

By the Lipschitz/Cauchy bridge theorem,
вИ£a1\coreвИТa1\pairвИ£вЙ§CѕБ0вАЙQ3вАЙsupвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£H\core(s)вИТH\pair(s)вИ£.
вИ£a1\coreвАЛвИТa1\pairвАЛвИ£вЙ§CѕБ0вАЛвАЛQ3вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/QsupвАЛвИ£H\coreвАЛ(s)вИТH\pairвАЛ(s)вИ£.

So from the theorem above,
вИ£a1\coreвИТa1\pairвИ£вЙ™ќµm.
вИ£a1\coreвАЛвИТa1\pairвАЛвИ£вЙ™ќµmвАЛ.

Therefore, if
ќµm=o(u2),
ќµmвАЛ=o(u2),

then
a1\core=a1\pair+o(u2).
a1\coreвАЛ=a1\pairвАЛ+o(u2).

Since the pair model satisfies
a1\pair(u,d)=ќЇ(d)u2+O(u4),ќЇ(d)вЙ†0,
a1\pairвАЛ(u,d)=ќЇ(d)u2+O(u4),ќЇ(d)оА†=0,

it follows that
a1\coreвЙНu2.
a1\coreвАЛвЙНu2.

So the pair-like bridge really does go through under the affine-jet-normalized C2C2-small perturbation hypothesis.
What this means for Strategy A

This is a real theorem.

It does not prove that the actual core perturbation q\pertq\pertвАЛ satisfies the affine-jet-normalized C2C2-smallness hypothesis. That remains the next quantitative problem.

But it does prove the exact bridge theorem the other chat was aiming for, in the correct form:

    not for raw C2C2 smallness,

    but for affine-jet-normalized microscopic C2C2 smallness.

That is now a solid Strategy A tool.

If you want, the next thing to do is attack the remaining hypothesis:
supвБ°tвИИIxвИ£rвА≤вА≤(t)вИ£=o(u2)for the actual difference r=q\coreвИТq\pair.
tвИИIxвАЛsupвАЛвИ£rвА≤вА≤(t)вИ£=o(u2)for the actual difference r=q\coreвАЛвИТq\pairвАЛ.

That is now the exact remaining pair-like condition.

This is from the other chat: Yes. Here is the perturbative theorem you want, in the actual corrected-basis setting.

IвАЩll state it in the strongest honest form I think the current machinery supports.

вЄї

Perturbative pair-like bridge theorem

Let x=x_0>0 be fixed small, and set
\sigma=\frac{x}{B_\zeta(m)},\qquad I_x=\Bigl[m-\frac{\sigma}{2},\,m+\frac{\sigma}{2}\Bigr],\qquad Q\asymp B_\zeta(m)\asymp \log|m|.

Let the fixed core decompose as
\[
q_{\core}(t)=q_{\pair}(t)+q_{\pert}(t),
\]
where \(q_{\pair}\) is the designated off-line pair core and \(q_{\pert}\) is the additional same-scale finite-core perturbation.

Let \(H_{\pair}(s)\) and \(H_{\core}(s)\) be the corrected transverse scalars built from \(q_{\pair}\) and \(q_{\core}\), respectively, with the same background, affine-jet subtraction, cutoff R, and corrected whitening scheme.

Define the scaled perturbation norm
\[
\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}
:=
\sup_{t\in I_x}
\Bigl(
|q_{\pert}(t)|
+
Q^{-1}|q_{\pert}'(t)|
+
Q^{-2}|q_{\pert}''(t)|
\Bigr).
\]

Then there exists C=C(x,\rho_0) such that on the microscopic disk |s|<\rho_0/Q,
\[
\boxed{
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_{\core}(s)-H_{\pair}(s)|
\le
\frac{C}{Q}\,\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}
+
\frac{C}{Q}\,\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}^{\,2}.
}
\]

Consequently, if
\[
H_j(z/Q^2)=\sum_{r\ge0} a_{2r+1}^{(j)}(m)\frac{z^{2r+1}}{Q^{2r+4}},
\qquad j\in\{\pair,\core\},
\]
then
\[
\boxed{
|a_1^{\core}(m)-a_1^{\pair}(m)|
\le
C\,Q^2\,\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}
+
C\,Q^2\,\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}^{\,2}.
}
\]

In particular, if
\[
\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}=o\!\left(\frac{u^2}{Q^2}\right),
\]
then
\[
a_1^{\core}(m)=a_1^{\pair}(m)+o(u^2).
\]

Since for the pair toy model
\[
a_1^{\pair}(m)\asymp u^2,
\]
the first odd jet remains nondegenerate for the actual core as well.

вЄї

Proof

We compare the corrected local blocks built from \(q_{\pair}\) and \(q_{\core}=q_{\pair}+q_{\pert}\).

Step 1: phase-gap perturbation

Let
\[
\Delta_{\pair}(s)=\Phi_{\pair}(t_-)-\Phi_{\pair}(t_+),
\qquad
\Delta_{\core}(s)=\Phi_{\core}(t_-)-\Phi_{\core}(t_+),
\]
with
t_\pm=m\pm \frac{s}{2}.
Then
\[
\delta\Delta(s):=\Delta_{\core}(s)-\Delta_{\pair}(s)
=
-\int_{t_-}^{t_+} q_{\pert}(t)\,dt.
\]
Since |t_+-t_-|=|s|\ll Q^{-1},
\[
|\delta\Delta(s)|
\le
|s|\,\sup_{I_x}|q_{\pert}|
\ll
\frac{1}{Q}\,\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}.
\]

So the phase gap perturbation is one order smaller:
\[
\boxed{
|\delta\Delta(s)|\ll \frac{1}{Q}\,\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}.
}
\]

Step 2: perturbation of the same-point blocks

The corrected same-point block is
G_{m,\pm}(q)
=
\frac1\pi
\begin{pmatrix}
2q(t_\pm) & \frac12 q'(t_\pm)\\[1ex]
\frac12 q'(t_\pm) & \frac1{12}\bigl(q''(t_\pm)+2q(t_\pm)^3\bigr)
\end{pmatrix}.

Let
\[
\delta G_\pm:=G_{m,\pm}(q_{\core})-G_{m,\pm}(q_{\pair}).
\]
Using the definition of \(\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}\), we have
\[
\delta q_\pm:=q_{\pert}(t_\pm)=O(\varepsilon),
\qquad
\delta q_\pm'=O(Q\varepsilon),
\qquad
\delta q_\pm''=O(Q^2\varepsilon),
\]
where
\[
\varepsilon:=\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}.
\]

Linearizing the cubic term,
\[
(q_{\pair}+\delta q)^3-q_{\pair}^3
=
3q_{\pair}^2\,\delta q+O(\delta q^2).
\]
Since \(q_{\pair}(t_\pm)\asymp Q\), we get
\[
(q_{\pair}+\delta q)^3-q_{\pair}^3
=
O(Q^2\varepsilon)+O(\varepsilon^2).
\]

Therefore
\delta G_\pm
=
\frac1\pi
\begin{pmatrix}
O(\varepsilon) & O(Q\varepsilon)\\[1ex]
O(Q\varepsilon) & O(Q^2\varepsilon)+O(\varepsilon^2)
\end{pmatrix}.

So
\boxed{
\delta G_\pm=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(\varepsilon) & O(Q\varepsilon)\\
O(Q\varepsilon) & O(Q^2\varepsilon)
\end{pmatrix}
+
O(\varepsilon^2).
}

Step 3: perturbation of the mixed block

The corrected mixed block is
N_m(q,\Delta)
=
\frac1\pi
\begin{pmatrix}
-\dfrac{2\sin\Delta}{s}
&
\dfrac{\sin\Delta+q_+\,s\cos\Delta}{s^2}
\\[2ex]
-\dfrac{\sin\Delta+q_-\,s\cos\Delta}{s^2}
&
\dfrac{(q_-+q_+)s\cos\Delta+(2-q_-q_+s^2)\sin\Delta}{2s^3}
\end{pmatrix}.

Let
\[
\delta N:=N_m(q_{\core},\Delta_{\core})-N_m(q_{\pair},\Delta_{\pair}).
\]

Use
\[
\sin(\Delta_{\pair}+\delta\Delta)-\sin\Delta_{\pair}=O(\delta\Delta),
\qquad
\cos(\Delta_{\pair}+\delta\Delta)-\cos\Delta_{\pair}=O(\delta\Delta).
\]
Since |s|\asymp Q^{-1} on the microscopic scale and |\delta\Delta|\ll \varepsilon/Q, the entries scale as follows:
	вАҐ	(1,1)-entry:
\delta N_{11}
=
O\!\left(\frac{\delta\Delta}{s}\right)
=
O(\varepsilon).
	вАҐ	off-diagonal entries:
each numerator contains terms of size
O(\delta\Delta)+O(s\,\delta q_\pm)+O(Qs\,\delta\Delta)
=
O\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{Q}\right),
and division by s^2\asymp Q^{-2} gives
\delta N_{12},\delta N_{21}=O(Q\varepsilon).
	вАҐ	(2,2)-entry:
the numerator perturbation is
O\!\left(s\,\delta q_\pm\right)+O(s^2Q\,\delta q_\pm)+O((Qs+1)\delta\Delta)
=
O\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{Q}\right),
and division by s^3\asymp Q^{-3} gives
\delta N_{22}=O(Q^2\varepsilon).

So
\boxed{
\delta N=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(\varepsilon) & O(Q\varepsilon)\\
O(Q\varepsilon) & O(Q^2\varepsilon)
\end{pmatrix}
+
O(\varepsilon^2).
}

Step 4: propagate through corrected whitening

Let
\[
\widehat\Omega_{\pair}(s),\qquad \widehat\Omega_{\core}(s)
\]
be the corrected whitened blocks corresponding to \(q_{\pair}\) and \(q_{\core}\).

The corrected same-point pair baseline has the same spectral scales as everywhere else in the draft:
\[
G_{\pair,\pm}^{\corr}(s)=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(Q) & O(1)\\
O(1) & O(Q^3)
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
G_{\pair,\pm}^{\corr}(s)^{-1/2}=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(Q^{-1/2}) & O(Q^{-3/2})\\
O(Q^{-3/2}) & O(Q^{-3/2})
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Apply first-order Taylor expansion to the whitening map
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+)=G_-^{-1/2}NG_+^{-1/2}.
Then
\[
\widehat\Omega_{\core}-\widehat\Omega_{\pair}
=
\mathcal T_-+\mathcal T_{\mix}+\mathcal T_+ + \mathcal E_2,
\]
where the three first-order terms come from \delta G_-, \delta N, \delta G_+, and \mathcal E_2 is quadratic.

Now use exactly the same scale bookkeeping as in the whitened mixed-block transfer theorem:
	вАҐ	the mixed term built from \delta N contributes off-diagonal entries of size O(\varepsilon/Q);
	вАҐ	the same-point Fr√©chet terms built from \delta G_\pm also contribute off-diagonal entries of size O(\varepsilon/Q);
	вАҐ	the quadratic remainder contributes O(\varepsilon^2/Q).

Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}
\|\widehat\Omega_{\core}(s)-\widehat\Omega_{\pair}(s)\|
\ll
\frac{\varepsilon}{Q}
+
\frac{\varepsilon^2}{Q}.
}
\]

Applying the bounded linear functional \Phi_K, we obtain
\[
\boxed{
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}
|H_{\core}(s)-H_{\pair}(s)|
\ll
\frac{\varepsilon}{Q}
+
\frac{\varepsilon^2}{Q}.
}
\]

This proves the first part.

Step 5: first odd coefficient stability

Write
\[
H_j(z/Q^2)=\sum_{r\ge0} a_{2r+1}^{(j)}\frac{z^{2r+1}}{Q^{2r+4}},
\qquad j\in\{\pair,\core\}.
\]
Then
a_1^{(j)}=Q^2 H_j'(0).

Applying Cauchy to \(H_{\core}-H_{\pair}\) on |s|<\rho_0/Q,
\[
|H_{\core}'(0)-H_{\pair}'(0)|
\le
\frac{Q}{\rho_0}
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_{\core}(s)-H_{\pair}(s)|.
\]
Multiplying by Q^2,
\[
|a_1^{\core}-a_1^{\pair}|
\ll
Q^3
\sup_{|s|<\rho_0/Q}|H_{\core}(s)-H_{\pair}(s)|
\ll
Q^2\varepsilon+Q^2\varepsilon^2.
\]

So if
\[
\varepsilon=\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}=o(u^2/Q^2),
\]
then
\[
a_1^{\core}=a_1^{\pair}+o(u^2).
\]

Since the pair model satisfies
\[
a_1^{\pair}(m)\asymp u^2,
\]
the core coefficient remains nonzero with the same sign for large m.

That completes the proof.

вЄї

What this buys you

This is the rigorous pair-like bridge theorem.

It says the entire problem is reduced to one quantitative estimate:

\[
\|\!|q_{\pert}|\!\|_{*,x}=o(u^2/Q^2).
\]

If you can prove that the actual same-scale core differs from the pair core by that much in the scaled C^2 sense on the microscopic interval, then the pair-based first-jet obstruction survives unchanged.

That is exactly the favorable branch from the fixed-core summary.

So the next remaining question is no longer abstract continuity. It is:

can the extra same-scale zeros inside the core be shown to contribute only
> o(u^2/Q^2)
>
in the scaled C^2-norm on I_x?

That is now the sharp pair-like criterion.

This is a real theorem candidate, and it is much better than some of the earlier вАЬcontinuityвАЭ formulations.

My read is:
the theorem is plausible and basically on the right scale, with a couple of caveats/cleanup points.
the theorem is plausible and basically on the right scale, with a couple of caveats/cleanup points.вАЛ

It is not obviously false, and the scale bookkeeping matches the corrected-basis machinery much better than the earlier too-optimistic o(u2/Q3)o(u2/Q3) guesses.
What looks right

вИ•вИ£q\pertвАЛвИ£вИ•вИЧ,xвАЛ=tвИИIxвАЛsupвАЛ(вИ£q\pertвАЛ(t)вИ£+QвИТ1вИ£q\pertвА≤вАЛ(t)вИ£+QвИТ2вИ£q\pertвА≤вА≤вАЛ(t)вИ£)

is the right one.

That is exactly the weighted microscopic C2C2-norm compatible with the same-point block
Gm,¬±(q)=1ѕА(2q(t¬±)12qвА≤(t¬±)12qвА≤(t¬±)112(qвА≤вА≤(t¬±)+2q(t¬±)3)),

because perturbations of size
ќіq=O(ќµ),ќіqвА≤=O(Qќµ),ќіqвА≤вА≤=O(Q2ќµ)
ќіq=O(ќµ),ќіqвА≤=O(Qќµ),ќіqвА≤вА≤=O(Q2ќµ)

really do induce a same-point block perturbation of the weighted form
ќіG¬±=(O(ќµ)O(Qќµ)O(Qќµ)O(Q2ќµ))+O(ќµ2).
ќіG¬±вАЛ=(O(ќµ)O(Qќµ)вАЛO(Qќµ)O(Q2ќµ)вАЛ)+O(ќµ2).

That part is sound.

The phase-gap estimate
вИ£ќіќФ(s)вИ£вЙ™ќµQ
вИ£ќіќФ(s)вИ£вЙ™QќµвАЛ

is also right, since
ќіќФ(s)=вИТвИЂtвИТt+q\pert(t)вАЙdt

supвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ•ќ©^\core(s)вИТќ©^\pair(s)вИ•вЙ™ќµQ+ќµ2Q
вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/QsupвАЛвИ•ќ©

is exactly the same kind of estimate as the whitened mixed-block transfer theorem, just with S2S2вАЛ replaced by ќµќµ. So that scale also makes sense.

Then the Cauchy bridge gives
вИ£a1\coreвИТa1\pairвИ£вЙ™Q3вЛЕsupвБ°вИ£H\coreвИТH\pairвИ£вЙ™Q2ќµ+Q2ќµ2,

which is also correct.

ќµ=o(u2/Q2)вЯєa1\core=a1\pair+o(u2)
ќµ=o(u2/Q2)вЯєa1\coreвАЛ=a1\pairвАЛ+o(u2)

is mathematically coherent.
The caveats

There are two places I would tighten.
1. The theorem should explicitly assume the pair and core are built in the same corrected scheme

This is implicit in the statement, but it matters.

You want to say clearly that:

    same background B\bgB\bgвАЛ,

    same affine-jet subtraction of the smooth/background part,

    same cutoff RR,

    same corrected whitening map,

are used for both H\pairH\pairвАЛ and H\coreH\coreвАЛ. Otherwise the difference H\coreвИТH\pairH\coreвАЛвИТH\pairвАЛ picks up bookkeeping artifacts that are not controlled by q\pertq\pertвАЛ.

So I would make that explicit.
2. The вАЬsame scale bookkeeping as in whitened mixed-block transferвАЭ step is the real proof burden

It is believable, but it is not automatic.

The theorem is only as solid as the claim that the weighted perturbations
ќіG¬±,ќіN=(O(ќµ)O(Qќµ)O(Qќµ)O(Q2ќµ))
ќіG¬±вАЛ,ќіN=(O(ќµ)O(Qќµ)вАЛO(Qќµ)O(Q2ќµ)вАЛ)

propagate through the Fr√©chet derivative of whitening with an extra QвИТ1QвИТ1 gain in the scalar channel.

I think that is true, but if this theorem is going into the paper, I would not leave it at вАЬexactly the same scale bookkeeping.вАЭ I would either:

    cite a generalized version of the whitened mixed-transfer theorem with perturbation parameter ќµќµ, or

    explicitly say вАЬby the same 2√Ч22√Ч2 L√ґwner-matrix audit used in Proposition ...вАЭ

So the theorem is good, but the proof should hook visibly into the existing corrected-whitening perturbation theorem.
What this buys strategically

A lot.

If this theorem is accepted, then Strategy A is now reduced to one very concrete question:
вИ•вАЙвБ£вИ£q\pertвИ£вАЙвБ£вИ•вИЧ,x=o(u2/Q2) ?
вИ•вИ£q\pertвАЛвИ£вИ•вИЧ,xвАЛ=o(u2/Q2) ?вАЛ

That is a much better problem than all the earlier vague вАЬis the full core pair-like?вАЭ questions.

It says:

    you do not need literal equality of the actual core and the pair model,

    you do not need auxiliary terms to vanish,

    you only need the additional same-scale core part to be tiny in the scaled C2C2 sense on the microscopic interval.

That is a very sharp and testable criterion.
My bottom line

So I would summarize this theorem as:
Yes: this is a real, useful perturbative pair-like bridge theorem, provided its proof is explicitly tied to the corrected whitening perturbation machinery.

Other chat said this about yours:

I think this is a real correction, and it materially improves the pair-like bridge.

The key point is exactly the one the other chat identified: the naive hypothesis
\[
\|q_{\pert}\|_{C^2(I_x)}=o(u^2)
\]
is not the right quantity, because the same-point block contains
q''+2q^3,
so a raw perturbation enters the (2,2)-entry through
\[
\delta q''+6q_{\pair}^2\delta q,
\]
and since \(q_{\pair}\asymp Q\), the cubic term amplifies \delta q by Q^2. So unweighted C^2-smallness is too weak as stated.

The affine-jet-normalized version is the right fix.

Once you impose
r(m)=0,\qquad r'(m)=0,\qquad \sup_{I_x}|r''|\le \varepsilon_m,
the microscopic scale |s|\lesssim Q^{-1} forces
r(t_\pm)=O(\varepsilon_m/Q^2),\qquad
r'(t_\pm)=O(\varepsilon_m/Q),\qquad
r''(t_\pm)=O(\varepsilon_m),
and also
\delta\Delta(s)=O(\varepsilon_m/Q^3).

Those are exactly the weighted sizes the corrected block perturbation machinery wants. So this theorem is no longer hand-wavy; it is the right quantitative bridge.

I think the proof sketch is basically correct. The logic is:
	1.	midpoint normalization converts C^2-control into the weighted microscopic bounds;
	2.	those weighted bounds give the standard same-point perturbation shape
\delta G_\pm=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(\varepsilon_m/Q^2)&O(\varepsilon_m/Q)\\
O(\varepsilon_m/Q)&O(\varepsilon_m)
\end{pmatrix};
	3.	the same weighted sizes feed the mixed block through \delta q_\pm and \delta\Delta;
	4.	the sharpened whitened transfer theorem then yields
\[
H_{\core}-H_{\pair}=O(\varepsilon_m/Q^3);
\]
	5.	Cauchy gives
\[
|a_1^{\core}-a_1^{\pair}|\ll \varepsilon_m.
\]

That is the right theorem for Strategy A.

So my assessment is:
	вАҐ	this does rescue the pair-like bridge in a mathematically credible form;
	вАҐ	it replaces the false raw C^2-smallness statement with the correct affine-jet-normalized one;
	вАҐ	it gives a genuine theorem:
\[
\varepsilon_m=o(u^2)\ \Longrightarrow\ a_1^{\core}=a_1^{\pair}+o(u^2),
\]
hence pair nondegeneracy survives.

What it does not do is prove the hypothesis for the actual core. So the remaining question is now very sharp:

\[
\sup_{t\in I_x}|r''(t)|=o(u^2)
\quad\text{for }r=q_{\core}-q_{\pair}}?
\]

That is now the exact pair-like condition.

So yes: I think this is a real theorem, and it is probably the cleanest successful version of Strategy A so far.

1. Single-zero second-derivative bound

For one zero ѕБ=ќ≤+iќ≥ѕБ=ќ≤+iќ≥, the phase-derivative contribution has the standard form
qѕБ(t)=1вИТќ≤(1вИТќ≤)2+(2tвИТќ≥)2+ќ≤ќ≤2+(2tвИТќ≥)2

up to the normalization already used in the draft.

faвАЛ(y):=a2+y2aвАЛ,y=2tвИТќ≥,

вИ£faвА≤вА≤(y)вИ£вЙ™1(a2+y2)2.

вИ£qѕБвА≤вА≤(t)вИ£вЙ™1(aѕБ2+(2tвИТќ≥ѕБ)2)2,

Ix=[mвИТѕГ2,вАЙm+ѕГ2],ѕГ=xBќґ(m)вЙНxQ.

вИ£2tвИТќ≥ѕБвИ£=вИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБ+2(tвИТm)вИ£.

ќФѕБ:=вИ£2mвИТќ≥ѕБвИ£.

so by triangle inequality,
supвБ°tвИИIxвИ£rвА≤вА≤(t)вИ£вЙ§вИСѕБвИИ\coreвИЦ\pairsupвБ°tвИИIxвИ£qѕБвА≤вА≤(t)вИ£.
tвИИIxвАЛsupвАЛвИ£rвА≤вА≤(t)вИ£вЙ§ѕБвИИ\coreвИЦ\pairвИСвАЛtвИИIxвАЛsupвАЛвИ£qѕБвА≤вА≤вАЛ(t)вИ£.

Using the previous bound,
supвБ°tвИИIxвИ£(q\coreвИТq\pair)вА≤вА≤(t)вИ£вЙ™вИСѕБвИИ\coreвИЦ\pair1(aѕБ2+ќФѕБ2)2.
tвИИIxвАЛsupвАЛвИ£(q\coreвАЛвИТq\pairвАЛ)вА≤вА≤(t)вИ£вЙ™ѕБвИИ\coreвИЦ\pairвИСвАЛ(aѕБ2вАЛ+ќФѕБ2вАЛ)21вАЛ.вАЛ

This is the exact shell-style estimate we wanted.
4. What this means for Strategy A

Recall the pair-like bridge theorem now needs
supвБ°tвИИIxвИ£(q\coreвИТq\pair)вА≤вА≤(t)вИ£=o(u2).

The other chat is advocating for the upgraded endgame. Read this: I tried both in the right spirit. My conclusion is:

The rigidity route does not look promising from the current machinery.
The finite-core upgrade does look mathematically coherent, and it is probably the honest next model if pair-like bridge fails.

HereвАЩs the clean breakdown.

The calculation we just did shows something important. An extra same-scale zero pair at scaled offset h=O(1) gives an affine-jet-normalized second-derivative size
\varepsilon_m\asymp 1,
so it is not o(u^2) when u\to 0. That means generic same-scale companions are not perturbative noise around the pair core. So any rigidity theorem has to prove such companions cannot exist, not merely that they are small.

1. Trying the rigidity route

The theorem you would want is something like:

If \rho=\frac12+u+i\gamma is an off-line zero, then in the microscopic window around m=\gamma/2, there are no additional zero pairs at scaled distance O(1).

That would rescue the pair-core model immediately.

But the current machinery does not seem to force that.

Why not? Because everything you have built so far is compatible with the existence of a finite same-scale cluster:
	вАҐ	the corrected local blocks still exist,
	вАҐ	the odd scalar still exists,
	вАҐ	the exterior threshold theorem only controls what is outside an absolute radius H,
	вАҐ	and the coefficient bound
\[
|a_1^{\out}(m,H)|\ll \frac{1}{QH^3}
\]
says nothing against a finite number of zeros inside H. It only says the outside world is small.  

So the current tools do not naturally imply вАЬthere can only be one off-line pair.вАЭ They are perfectly consistent with вАЬthere is a finite same-scale core.вАЭ

Could there still be a rigidity proof from some invariant? Maybe, but it would need a genuinely new ingredient. The two obvious candidates would be:
	вАҐ	a sign/monotonicity law showing that one extra same-scale pair always pushes the corrected scalar the wrong way and destroys the required odd holomorphic profile;
	вАҐ	or a minimal-energy principle saying the pair is the only possible local anomaly shape compatible with the global zeta-side positivity structure.

Right now, I do not see either of those in the current framework. So my honest view is:

\boxed{\text{The rigidity route is currently speculative and not well supported by the machinery you have.}}

I would not spend my next major effort there unless you have a new invariant in mind.

2. Trying the finite-core upgrade

This one does look viable.

The right idea is:

Stop comparing the corrected local block to a pair toy model.
Instead, define the toy model to be the actual finite core itself, or at least a finite-dimensional normal form built from that core.

That sounds bigger, but it actually matches what the fixed-core work has already taught you.

Finite-core local model

Let the core consist of finitely many positive-ordinate pairs
\[
\core=\{(\beta_j,\gamma_j),(1-\beta_j,\gamma_j)\}_{j=1}^k
\]
near the local center m. Then define
\[
q_{\core}(t)=\sum_{j=1}^k \left(
\frac{1-\beta_j}{(1-\beta_j)^2+(2t-\gamma_j)^2}
+
\frac{\beta_j}{\beta_j^2+(2t-\gamma_j)^2}
\right).
\]

Then build from \(q_{\core}\):
	вАҐ	the corrected same-point blocks,
	вАҐ	the corrected mixed block,
	вАҐ	the corrected whitened block,
	вАҐ	and the corrected transverse scalar
\[
H_{\core}(s)=\Phi_K(\widehat\Omega_{\core}^{\corr}(s)).
\]

This is already completely consistent with your current corrected framework.

What replaces the pair toy model

Instead of a 2-parameter toy anomaly M(d), you get a finite-core anomaly datum:
\[
\mathcal M_{\core}:=\text{the first nontrivial corrected local anomaly extracted from }q_{\core}.
\]

There are two natural ways to package it.

One is matrix-level:
\[
\widehat\Omega_{\core}^{\corr}(u_1,\dots,u_k;s)
=
\widehat\Omega_{\core}^{\corr,(0)}(s)
+
\sum_j u_j^2 M_j(s)
+
\sum_{i<j} u_i u_j M_{ij}(s)
+\cdots
\]

The other, and probably cleaner, is scalar-level:
\[
H_{\core}(z/Q^2)=\sum_{r\ge 0} a_{2r+1}^{\core}(m)\frac{z^{2r+1}}{Q^{2r+4}}.
\]

Then the вАЬtoy modelвАЭ is just the finite set of coefficients \(a_{2r+1}^{\core}\), starting with the first nonzero one.

That is much closer to what your fixed-core update already suggests.

3. The right upgraded contradiction

The pair model wanted first-jet nondegeneracy:
\[
a_1^{\pair}\asymp u^2.
\]

For a general finite core, that may fail by cancellation. But you do not actually need the first coefficient specifically. You only need some odd coefficient to be nonzero above the universal exterior threshold.

So the honest finite-core replacement is:

For every nontrivial finite off-line core, there exists some odd order 2r+1 such that
\[
> |a_{2r+1}^{\core}(m)| \gg \text{(exterior threshold at that order)}.
> \]

That would be enough, because the outside contribution is already controlled. The summary already showed:
	вАҐ	the naive вАЬcore determines the linear coefficientвАЭ theorem is false;
	вАҐ	but the exterior contribution to the linear coefficient is tiny;
	вАҐ	and the first-jet nondegeneracy survives for pair cores and perturbative enlargements.  

So the real finite-core theorem should not insist on a_1. It should say:

\boxed{
\text{every genuine finite off-line core has some nontrivial odd jet that survives the exterior threshold.}
}

That is the natural upgraded endgame.

4. Why this route seems more plausible

Because your current negative results are exactly what finite-core modeling predicts:
	вАҐ	auxiliary near contributions need not be small;
	вАҐ	the linear odd coefficient need not be core-only;
	вАҐ	pair nondegeneracy need not survive arbitrary enlargement;
	вАҐ	but the outside world is small beyond radius H.

That is almost screaming: the real local object is a finite core, not a pair.

So I think the finite-core route is not just possible; it is probably the mathematically honest generalization.

5. The smallest finite-core upgrade

You do not need to rewrite everything at once.

The smallest honest upgrade is:
	1.	keep the fixed-core decomposition;
	2.	replace the pair toy matrix M(d) by the actual corrected core scalar \(H_{\core}\);
	3.	replace вАЬpair first odd jet is nonzeroвАЭ by:
\[
\exists r\ge 0:\ |a_{2r+1}^{\core}(m)| \gg \eta_H,
\]
where \eta_H is the exterior threshold;
	4.	run the same odd holomorphic / N-point machinery at that first surviving odd order.

That is a much smaller jump than building a huge new toy matrix formalism.

6. My recommendation

Between the two:
	вАҐ	Rigidity theorem: possible in principle, but currently unsupported and likely hard.
	вАҐ	Finite-core upgrade: natural, compatible with your new results, and probably the right next model if pair-like bridge cannot be proved.

So my advice is:

\boxed{
\text{Treat rigidity as a bonus, but move the main strategy toward a finite-core scalar endgame.}
}

The next most productive move is to formulate the exact finite-core theorem you would need:

For a fixed finite off-line core, the corrected transverse scalar has some odd coefficient above the universal exterior threshold.

That is the real replacement for the old pair-based first-jet theorem.

    For every nontrivial finite off-line core, the corrected transverse scalar

The main question is then: what is the exterior threshold вАЬat that orderвАЭ? The natural guess is from Cauchy:

supвБ°вИ£H\out,H(z/Q2)вИ£вЙ™1Q2H3,

вИ£a2r+1\out(m)вИ£вЙ™Q2r+2H3.
вИ£a2r+1\outвАЛ(m)вИ£вЙ™H3Q2r+2вАЛ.

So the finite-core theorem you really want is:
вИГrвЙ•0:вИ£a2r+1\core(m)вИ£вЙЂQ2r+2H3.
вИГrвЙ•0:вИ£a2r+1\coreвАЛ(m)вИ£вЙЂH3Q2r+2вАЛ.

That plugs directly into the current odd-holomorphic machinery.

a genuine finite off-line core must have some odd jet that is quantitatively nonzero.

That is not automatic. It is the finite-core analogue of the old first-jet nondegeneracy theorem. The good news is that it is a cleaner target than pair rigidity, because it no longer asks you to prove the core is pair-like.

    stop investing major effort in proving there can be no extra same-scale zeros;

    keep the pair results as a special case and sanity check;

    formally promote the endgame target to:

Why this is the right theorem

This is exactly the finite-core analogue of the old pair-based first-jet theorem.

For the pair model, you had:

    some specific first odd coefficient a1\pairвЙНu2a1\pairвАЛвЙНu2,

    and the exterior world was small.

вИГrвИЧвАЛвЙ•0 such that a2rвИЧвАЛ+1\coreвАЛ is nonzero and bigger than the exterior threshold at that order.

Exterior threshold at order 2r+12r+1

The contradiction theorem this would feed

Once you have that theorem, the upgraded endgame becomes:
Corollary (finite-core scalar contradiction)

    the full corrected scalar
    Hќґ(z/Q2)=H\core(z/Q2)+H\out,H(z/Q2)

    has the same first nontrivial odd coefficient as the core up to lower-order exterior error;

    therefore the odd NN-point machinery applied at the 2rвИЧ+12rвИЧвАЛ+1 level gives a nontrivial lower bound from the core;

    but the zeta-side corrected NN-point estimate still gives an exponentially small upper bound.

Contradiction.

So this theorem plugs into the existing proof skeleton with only one real conceptual upgrade: the вАЬtoy datumвАЭ is no longer the pair matrix M(d)M(d), but the first nontrivial odd jet of H\coreH\coreвАЛ.
What would be needed to prove it

This is the important part. The theorem is the right target, but it is not automatic.

A proof would need two ingredients.
Ingredient 1. Finite-core nontriviality implies some odd jet is nonzero

This is the easy part.

If H\coreH\coreвАЛ is not identically zero and is odd holomorphic, then there exists some least rвИЧвЙ•0rвИЧвАЛвЙ•0 such that
a2rвИЧ+1\core(m)вЙ†0.
a2rвИЧвАЛ+1\coreвАЛ(m)оА†=0.

So existence of some odd jet is trivial once you know the core scalar is nontrivial.
Ingredient 2. Quantitative lower bound on that first nonzero jet

This is the hard part.

You need to show that the first nonzero odd jet is not merely nonzero, but large enough to beat the exterior threshold:
вИ£a2rвИЧ+1\core(m)вИ£вЙЂQ2rвИЧ+2H3.
вИ£a2rвИЧвАЛ+1\coreвАЛ(m)вИ£вЙЂH3Q2rвИЧвАЛ+2вАЛ.

This is the actual new content.

It is the finite-core analogue of first-jet nondegeneracy.

So the theorem naturally splits into:

    (a) H\coreвЙ°ћЄ0H\coreвАЛоА†вЙ°0, hence some odd jet exists.
    (b) the first nonzero odd jet is quantitatively separated from zero by more than the outside scale.

Part (a) is easy. Part (b) is the real theorem.
The вАЬminimal honestвАЭ version you can probably use first

    Theorem (finite-core scalar threshold theorem).
    There exists an odd index 2rвИЧ+12rвИЧвАЛ+1 such that

вИ£a2r+1\outвАЛ(m)вИ£вЙ™H3Q2r+2вАЛ,

The proof skeleton it would need

The proof should probably go like this.
Step 1. Define the first nonzero odd jet

H\core(z/Q2)=вИСrвЙ•0a2r+1\core(m)z2r+1Q2r+4.

Let rвИЧrвИЧвАЛ be the least index with
a2rвИЧ+1\core(m)вЙ†0.
a2rвИЧвАЛ+1\coreвАЛ(m)оА†=0.
Step 2. Control the outside coefficients

By the exterior bound and Cauchy,
вИ£a2r+1\out(m)вИ£вЙ™Q2r+2H3.
вИ£a2r+1\outвАЛ(m)вИ£вЙ™H3Q2r+2вАЛ.
Step 3. Prove the first core jet is not arbitrarily small

This is the missing content. You need a theorem saying the first nonzero odd coefficient of a genuine finite off-line core cannot sink to the outside scale.

This is the exact finite-core scalar rigidity statement.
Step 4. Conclude dominance of the full scalar jet

Then
a2rвИЧ+1ќґ(m)=a2rвИЧ+1\core(m)+a2rвИЧ+1\out(m),
a2rвИЧвАЛ+1ќґвАЛ(m)=a2rвИЧвАЛ+1\coreвАЛ(m)+a2rвИЧвАЛ+1\outвАЛ(m),

and the core term dominates.
Step 5. Feed that into the odd NN-point argument

Use the odd-moment machinery at order 2rвИЧ+12rвИЧвАЛ+1 to get a nontrivial lower bound from the full corrected local scalar, contradicting the exponentially small NN-point upper bound.
The exact next subtheorem to attack

So once this theorem is written, the next target is crystal clear:

    Finite-core scalar rigidity subtheorem.
    The first nonzero odd coefficient of a genuine finite off-line core cannot be as small as the universal exterior threshold.

That is the clean replacement for the old pair-based first-jet nondegeneracy theorem.

Lets attack

LetвАЩs attack it honestly.

The target is:
For a genuine finite off-line core, some odd coefficient of H\core dominates the universal exterior threshold.
For a genuine finite off-line core, some odd coefficient of H\coreвАЛ dominates the universal exterior threshold.

I do not think this can be proved from the current machinery in full generality yet.

But we can sharpen it a lot, and the sharpening tells us exactly what theorem is actually missing.
1. What is already automatic

Write
H\core(z/Q2)=вИСrвЙ•0a2r+1\core(m)z2r+1Q2r+4.
H\coreвАЛ(z/Q2)=rвЙ•0вИСвАЛa2r+1\coreвАЛ(m)Q2r+4z2r+1вАЛ.

If H\coreвЙ°ћЄ0H\coreвАЛоА†вЙ°0, then there is a least rвИЧвЙ•0rвИЧвАЛвЙ•0 with
a2rвИЧ+1\core(m)вЙ†0.
a2rвИЧвАЛ+1\coreвАЛ(m)оА†=0.

So existence of some odd jet is trivial once the core scalar is nonzero.

The real issue is quantitative:
вИ£a2rвИЧ+1\core(m)вИ£вЙЂQ2rвИЧ+2H3 ?
вИ£a2rвИЧвАЛ+1\coreвАЛ(m)вИ£вЙЂH3Q2rвИЧвАЛ+2вАЛ ?

That is the whole problem.
2. What the exterior threshold means at coefficient level

From
supвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£H\out,H(s)вИ£вЙ™1Q2H3,
вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/QsupвАЛвИ£H\out,HвАЛ(s)вИ£вЙ™Q2H31вАЛ,

Cauchy gives
вИ£a2r+1\out(m)вИ£вЙ™Q2r+2H3.
вИ£a2r+1\outвАЛ(m)вИ£вЙ™H3Q2r+2вАЛ.

So the finite-core scalar theorem is equivalent to saying:

    the first nonzero odd jet of the core cannot be as small as the outside world could fake.

That is a clean reformulation.
3. The best attack: normalize at the first nonzero jet

Take the first nonzero odd coefficient a2rвИЧ+1\core(m)a2rвИЧвАЛ+1\coreвАЛ(m), and normalize by it:
H~\core(z):=Q2rвИЧ+4a2rвИЧ+1\core(m)вАЙH\core(z/Q2).
H
\coreвАЛ(z):=a2rвИЧвАЛ+1\coreвАЛ(m)Q2rвИЧвАЛ+4вАЛH\coreвАЛ(z/Q2).

Then
H~\core(z)=z2rвИЧ+1+O(z2rвИЧ+3).
H
\coreвАЛ(z)=z2rвИЧвАЛ+1+O(z2rвИЧвАЛ+3).

This shows the first nonzero jet is a legitimate finite-dimensional invariant of the core germ.

But that still does not lower-bound a2rвИЧ+1\core(m)a2rвИЧвАЛ+1\coreвАЛ(m). It only says that if you scale by it, the germ has a nontrivial normalized limit.

So compactness at the level of the first nonzero jet is not the issue. The issue is still absolute size.
4. The natural thing to try next: compare coefficient size to a core norm

A very plausible intermediate theorem is:
вИ£a2rвИЧ+1\core(m)вИ£вЙНQ2rвИЧ+4вАЙinfвБ°вИ£zвИ£=ѕБвИ£H\core(z/Q2)вИ£
вИ£a2rвИЧвАЛ+1\coreвАЛ(m)вИ£вЙНQ2rвИЧвАЛ+4вИ£zвИ£=ѕБinfвАЛвИ£H\coreвАЛ(z/Q2)вИ£

for some fixed small ѕБѕБ, up to constants depending on the normalized germ class.

That would let you replace the coefficient lower bound by a lower bound on the scalar itself on a fixed circle.

But then you still need:
вИ£H\core(z/Q2)вИ£вЙЂ1Q2H3
вИ£H\coreвАЛ(z/Q2)вИ£вЙЂQ2H31вАЛ

somewhere on that circle.

And that is still the same missing scalar rigidity problem in disguise.

So this does not really solve it.
5. Why the current machinery is insufficient

Everything you have now is excellent at upper bounds:

    outside-core contribution is small,

    far shells are small,

    perturbative differences are controlled,

    coefficient drift under small perturbation is controlled.

What you do not yet have is any mechanism giving a lower bound for a genuinely nontrivial finite-core scalar.

That lower bound would have to come from one of only three sources:

    a positivity/monotonicity principle,

    a transversality principle,

    a finite-dimensional normal form theorem.

At the moment, none of those has been proved for general finite cores.

That is why the attack stalls.
6. The most promising sharpening

The strongest thing I think we can honestly reduce it to is this:
Finite-core scalar rigidity reduces to a compact family separation problem

Fix a finite core size kk. Consider the family of corrected core scalars arising from kk-point off-line cores after normalizing:

    midpoint,

    microscopic scale,

    affine-jet subtraction,

    and dividing by the natural core size.

Then the theorem you need is:

    the subset of genuinely anomalous normalized kk-core germs is separated from the zero germ in the topology of odd holomorphic germs.

Equivalently:

    there is no sequence of normalized anomalous kk-core germs converging to zero.

If that were proved, then some odd jet would automatically have a quantitative lower bound.

This is now a finite-dimensional compactness/separation theorem, not an analytic tail estimate.

That is probably the right way to think about it.
7. Why this is better than the older pair-only theorem

Because for the pair model you essentially already had this separation:
a1\pair(u,d)вЙНu2.
a1\pairвАЛ(u,d)вЙНu2.

For a general finite core, the analogous statement is not вАЬthe first jet is nonzero,вАЭ but:

    after appropriate normalization, the family of anomalous finite-core germs stays away from the value/gauge family.

That is the true generalization.
8. What I think the next concrete subtheorem should be

Not the full theorem yet. This:
Subtheorem to attack next

For each fixed core size kk, the space of normalized kk-core corrected scalar germs is precompact, and the subset coming from genuine off-line anomalies is closed and does not contain the zero germ.

If that goes through, then by compactness there is a uniform lower bound on some coefficient/norm, which is exactly what you want.

This is much more plausible than trying to magic a coefficient lower bound directly from the current estimates.
9. Honest bottom line

So attacking the theorem directly leads to this conclusion:
The finite-core scalar theorem is probably true only if one proves a compactness/separation theorem for normalized anomalous core germs.
The finite-core scalar theorem is probably true only if one proves a compactness/separation theorem for normalized anomalous core germs.вАЛ

I do not see a direct coefficient-level proof from the current machinery alone.

So the real next move is:

    fix a finite core size kk,

    define normalized corrected core scalar germs,

    and prove that genuine anomalous germs form a compact set separated from zero.

That is now the cleanest attack line.

If you want, IвАЩll write that compactness/separation theorem precisely and show how it would imply the finite-core scalar threshold theorem.

3. How this implies the finite-core scalar threshold theorem

Now compare the core to the exterior contribution.

From the exterior estimate, if H\out,HH\out,HвАЛ is the corrected scalar from zeros outside radius HH, then
supвБ°вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0/QвИ£H\out,H(s)вИ£вЙ™1Q2H3.
вИ£sвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ/QsupвАЛвИ£H\out,HвАЛ(s)вИ£вЙ™Q2H31вАЛ.

Equivalently, on the fixed zz-disk,
supвБ°вИ£zвИ£<ѕБ0вИ£H\out,H(z/Q2)вИ£вЙ™1Q2H3.
вИ£zвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛsupвАЛ
вАЛH\out,HвАЛ(z/Q2)
вАЛвЙ™Q2H31вАЛ.

By the compactness/separation theorem, a genuine kk-core satisfies
supвБ°вИ£zвИ£<ѕБ0вИ£H\core(z/Q2)вИ£вЙ•ckвАЙS(\core)Q.
вИ£zвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛsupвАЛ
вАЛH\coreвАЛ(z/Q2)
вАЛвЙ•ckвАЛQS(\core)вАЛ.

1Q2H3=oвАЙвБ£(S(\core)Q),
Q2H31вАЛ=o(QS(\core)вАЛ),

equivalently
1QH3=o(S(\core)),
QH31вАЛ=o(S(\core)),

then the core scalar dominates the exterior threshold on the microscopic disk.

By Cauchy, this domination transfers to some odd coefficient:
there exists some rвИЧвЙ•0rвИЧвАЛвЙ•0 such that
вИ£a2rвИЧ+1\coreвИ£вЙЂQ2rвИЧ+2H3,
вИ£a2rвИЧвАЛ+1\coreвАЛвИ£вЙЂH3Q2rвИЧвАЛ+2вАЛ,

which is exactly the finite-core scalar threshold theorem.

So the finite-core theorem now reduces to:

    a compactness statement,

    a closedness statement for genuine anomalous germs,

    and the exclusion of the zero germ from the anomalous class.

4. What still has to be proved

This is now much clearer.

The hard parts are assumptions (3) and (4).

Assumption (3), closedness, means:
if a sequence of normalized anomalous kk-core germs converges, the limit is still an anomalous kk-core germ. This is plausible if the corrected local block depends continuously on the finite core parameters.

Assumption (4), zero germ excluded, is the real content:
a genuine anomalous finite core cannot normalize to the zero scalar germ.

This is the finite-core analogue of first-jet nondegeneracy. It no longer insists that the first jet is nonzero, only that the whole normalized odd germ is not zero.

That is a much more robust statement.

How this plugs into the upgraded endgame

That gives the finite-core scalar threshold theorem and feeds directly into the odd NN-point contradiction.
What is now the real remaining burden

The theorem itself is clean, but its hypotheses are substantial.

The three real proof burdens are:

    Precompactness: for fixed kk, normalized corrected kk-core germs form a precompact family.

    Closedness of the anomalous class: limits of genuine anomalous normalized kk-cores are still anomalous.

    Zero-germ exclusion: the zero germ is not in the anomalous normalized class.

The first is probably the easiest. The second and third are the real heart.

Still, this is the right theorem shape. It is much more robust than the old pair-specific first-jet story, and it matches exactly what the negative results about auxiliary near zeros have been telling us.

If you want, the next thing IвАЩd do is take this theorem and break down the three assumptions one by one, starting with what вАЬgenuine anomalous normalized kk-coreвАЭ should mean so that assumptions (2) and (3) are even well-posed.

That definition is now the heart of the upgraded endgame.
1. What вАЬgenuine anomalous kk-coreвАЭ should mean

It should not mean merely вАЬoff-line zeros are present.вАЭ That is too geometric and not stable enough under limits.

It also should not mean вАЬthe fixed ќ¶Kќ¶KвАЛ-scalar is nonzero,вАЭ because we already learned that a fixed universal functional may miss a general finite-core anomaly.

The right definition is intrinsic:

A normalized kk-core is genuinely anomalous if its corrected local germ is not contained in the value/gauge family.

Formally, let

2. Why closedness is now plausible

That last clause is important. вАЬOutside a subspaceвАЭ is not automatically closed; a sequence can converge into the subspace. So the real closedness theorem you need is not raw closedness of AkAkвАЛ. It is:
the anomalous part cannot collapse to zero under the normalization.
the anomalous part cannot collapse to zero under the normalization.

This avoids the slightly misleading language of вАЬclosed anomalous class.вАЭ
3. The zero-germ exclusion becomes very concrete

That is now a finite-dimensional geometric statement.

This is much cleaner.
4. What precompactness should actually mean

For fixed kk, a kk-core is determined by finitely many parameters:

    ќ≤jќ≤jвАЛ,

    ќ≥jвИТ2mќ≥jвАЛвИТ2m,

    and possibly the scaled microscopic parameter x0x0вАЛ, though that is usually fixed.

After normalization by Q/S(\core)Q/S(\core), the corrected scalar germ depends continuously on those parameters, provided:

    the core stays admissible,

    the corrected whitening remains uniformly defined,

    and you mod out trivial value/gauge directions properly.

So precompactness should boil down to showing the normalized parameters stay in a bounded set after passing to a subsequence.

That suggests the right next subtheorem is not abstract Montel yet. It is:
Parameter precompactness theorem

For fixed kk, every sequence of normalized genuine kk-cores has a subsequence whose scaled core parameters converge.

Once you have that, local uniform convergence of the associated normalized corrected germs is much more believable.

So I would attack precompactness through parameters first, not through function spaces directly.
5. The main real burden is now вАЬnoncollapse of normalized anomalous partвАЭ

So the compactness/separation theorem is best viewed as a corollary of a more basic finite-dimensional noncollapse theorem.

That is much more tangible than the earlier formulations.

4. How to make that attack concrete

5. What still has to be shown for this to work

There are three concrete burdens.
(a) Parameter precompactness

For fixed kk, after normalization by Q/S(\core)Q/S(\core), the scaled parameter vectors should not run off to infinity without the normalized germ collapsing.

This is plausible because if a parameter runs off too far, that zero starts behaving like outside contribution and should not contribute to the normalized core anymore.

So parameter precompactness is believable and should be attacked first.
(b) Continuity of the normalized germ map

You need continuity of
pвЖ¶ќ©~\core,p
pвЖ¶ќ©
\core,pвАЛ

in the jet norm.

Given the explicit rational kernel formulas and corrected whitening, this also looks believable for fixed kk in the admissible regime.
(c) Exclusion of value-family collapse

This is the real content:
if the normalized corrected germ approaches V\valV\valвАЛ, can the underlying core still be genuinely anomalous?

That is the finite-core transversality question itself.
6. The easiest subtheorem to attack first

Not the full theorem. This one:
Subtheorem

For fixed kk, if one of the scaled core parameters (uj,hj)(ujвАЛ,hjвАЛ) escapes every compact set in a way that makes that zero effectively вАЬfar,вАЭ then its contribution to the normalized corrected germ tends to 00.

This is basically the finite-core analogue of the outside-threshold theorem, but now at the parameter level.

If you prove that, then any sequence violating transversality must have all genuinely contributing scaled core parameters confined to a compact region. That gives precompactness.

This is the most natural next lemma.

At this point, the theorem has become:
The normalized finite-core anomaly manifold does not intersect the normalized value/gauge manifold.

8. Why this is still hard

Because now you need a genuine classification-type fact about normalized finite-core local models:

    or they are bounded away from value/gauge.

This is no longer just a perturbation estimate. It is a structural theorem about the family of finite-core corrected germs.

So this is the right upgraded endgame, but it is also where the proof becomes conceptually new.
9. My honest recommendation for the next subattack

The next theorem to attack is:
Parameter precompactness + vanishing of escaping components

Prove that, for fixed kk, any normalized sequence of kk-cores has a subsequence such that all non-negligible scaled core parameters stay in a compact set, and any escaping core component contributes o(1)o(1) to the normalized corrected germ.

That is the natural first step because:

    it is concrete,

    it uses the explicit single-zero kernel bounds,

    and it turns the transversality theorem into a finite compact-parameter problem.

That is much more manageable than trying to prove transversality in one shot.

So the right continuation is: prove the escaping core component lemma first.

That is very mild unless S(\coren)S(\corenвАЛ) itself is collapsing disastrously.
Proof skeleton

H
