The same argument applies at the block level because the corrected block perturbation is controlled by the same weighted size parameter. So the normalized corrected germ contribution of that escaping component tends to 00.

That proves the lemma, modulo the mild lower bound on S(\coren)S(\corenвАЛ).
What this buys

This is enough to get parameter precompactness in the useful sense:

    any component whose scaled parameters escape contributes o(1)o(1) to the normalized germ;

    therefore any nontrivial limit of normalized kk-cores is determined only by the components whose parameters remain in a compact set.

So after discarding vanishing components, every normalized sequence has a subsequence whose genuinely contributing parameters stay in a compact region.

That is the first half of the compactness theorem.
The next theorem after this

Once escaping components are under control, the real theorem becomes:

    if a normalized sequence of genuine anomalous kk-cores converges, can the limit lie in V\valV\valвАЛ?

That is now a finite compact-parameter problem, not an escaping-tail problem.

So the next theorem to attack after the escaping-component lemma is:
Compact-parameter transversality theorem

On every compact parameter set of normalized kk-cores, the anomalous family stays a positive distance away from V\valV\valвАЛ.

That is the exact core rigidity statement the upgraded endgame needs.
Most useful immediate refinement

The cleanest refinement would be to split the next proof into two lemmas:

    Escaping component vanishing lemma
    Escaping core components vanish in the normalized corrected germ.

    Compact-parameter separation lemma
    On compact parameter sets, genuine anomalous normalized kk-cores cannot converge into V\valV\valвАЛ.

Together they imply the adapted finite-core scalar threshold theorem.

That is the right continuation.

Corollary (parameter precompactness modulo vanishing components)

Under the same normalization, any sequence of kk-cores has a subsequence such that every component either:

    has scaled parameters staying in a compact set, or

    contributes o(1)o(1) to the normalized corrected germ.

So every nontrivial subsequential limit of normalized corrected kk-core germs is determined entirely by the components whose parameters remain in a compact region.
Proof sketch

Apply the lemma component-by-component. Any escaping component satisfying the hypothesis vanishes in the normalized germ. Remove all such components. What remains is a subsequence in which all nonvanishing components have parameters constrained to a compact set. вИО
Why this matters

This is the first half of the upgraded finite-core compactness theorem.

It tells you that the only genuinely relevant part of a normalized finite core comes from a compact parameter region. So the remaining problem is no longer about tails or escaping components. It becomes a finite compact-parameter separation problem:
Can a normalized genuinely anomalous compact-parameter k-core converge into V\val?
Can a normalized genuinely anomalous compact-parameter k-core converge into V\valвАЛ?вАЛ

That is the exact next theorem to attack.

How this plugs into the finite-core threshold theorem

Combine the two lemmas:

    Escaping-component lemma: any escaping core component contributes o(1)o(1) to the normalized corrected germ;

    Compact-parameter separation lemma: once only compact-parameter components remain, any genuinely anomalous normalized core stays a positive distance from V\valV\valвАЛ.

Then for fixed kk, every genuine normalized kk-core has an adapted scalar of size bounded below by a positive constant on the fixed microscopic disk.

The exact place where the argument is still genuinely new

This lemma is clean, but it pushes the real work into assumptions (2) and (3).

Assumption (2), closedness of the anomalous parameter set, is probably manageable once the parameterization is made explicit.

Assumption (3), exclusion of parameters pвИИAkpвИИAkвАЛ with ќ©~pвИИV\valќ©
pвАЛвИИV\valвАЛ, is the true finite-core rigidity statement. This is the finite-core analogue of pair first-jet nondegeneracy.

So the proof has now been reduced to this precise statement:
A normalized genuinely anomalous compact-parameter finite core cannot lie in the value/gauge family.
A normalized genuinely anomalous compact-parameter finite core cannot lie in the value/gauge family.вАЛ

That is the next theorem to attack.
The next theorem to write

The clean next statement is:
Finite-core value-family exclusion theorem

For fixed kk, if a normalized kk-core has corrected local germ lying in V\valV\valвАЛ, then it is not genuinely anomalous.

This is almost tautological if вАЬgenuinely anomalousвАЭ is defined intrinsically as вАЬnot in V\valV\valвАЛ,вАЭ but what you really want is the geometric version:

    a finite off-line core that is genuinely off-line in the zeta sense cannot produce a corrected local germ lying entirely in the value/gauge family after normalization.

That is the real remaining theorem.

If you want, IвАЩll write that theorem next in the sharpest possible form and then break down what would actually be needed to prove it.

Yes please

Yes. Here is the sharpest clean formulation of the next theorem, together with the exact proof burdens.
Finite-core value-family exclusion theorem

Fix a core size kk, a compact microscopic disk
вИ£zвИ£<ѕБ0,
вИ£zвИ£<ѕБ0вАЛ,

and a normalized kk-core
\core={(ќ≤j,ќ≥j),(1вИТќ≤j,ќ≥j)}j=1k
\core={(ќ≤jвАЛ,ќ≥jвАЛ),(1вИТќ≤jвАЛ,ќ≥jвАЛ)}j=1kвАЛ

with midpoint mm, scale QвЙНlogвБ°вИ£mвИ£QвЙНlogвИ£mвИ£, natural core scale S(\core)S(\core), and normalized corrected local germ
ќ©~\core(z):=QS(\core)вАЙќ©^\core\corr(z/Q2;m).
ќ©
\coreвАЛ(z):=S(\core)QвАЛќ©
\core\corrвАЛ(z/Q2;m).

Let V\valV\valвАЛ be the value/gauge subspace of normalized corrected odd holomorphic germs.

Then the theorem you want is:

    Theorem (finite-core value-family exclusion).
    If \core\core is a genuine off-line anomalous kk-core, then
    ќ©~\coreвИЙV\val.
    ќ©
    \coreвАЛвИИ/V\valвАЛ.

    Equivalently, a normalized finite off-line core cannot produce a corrected local germ lying entirely in the value/gauge family.

This is the intrinsic finite-core replacement for pair first-jet nondegeneracy.
Why this is the right theorem

Once this is true, the compact-parameter separation lemma becomes automatic:

    anomalous normalized cores lie outside V\valV\valвАЛ,

    on compact parameter sets they stay a positive distance away,

    so a norm-one adapted functional detects them,

    giving a nontrivial adapted scalar,

    and then the outside threshold comparison finishes the upgraded scalar endgame.

So this is the real theorem the finite-core route lives or dies on.
Equivalent formulations

This theorem can be phrased in three equivalent ways.
1. Germ-level formulation
ќ©~\coreвИЙV\val.
ќ©
\coreвАЛвИИ/V\valвАЛ.
2. Adapted scalar formulation

There exists a norm-one linear functional ќЫ\coreќЫ\coreвАЛ with
ќЫ\core(V\val)=0
ќЫ\coreвАЛ(V\valвАЛ)=0

such that
H\core(ќЫ)(z):=ќЫ\core(ќ©~\core(z))вЙ°ћЄ0.
H\core(ќЫ)вАЛ(z):=ќЫ\coreвАЛ(ќ©
\coreвАЛ(z))оА†вЙ°0.
3. Jet-level formulation

There exists some odd jet order 2rвИЧ+12rвИЧвАЛ+1 such that the projection of the 2rвИЧ+12rвИЧвАЛ+1-jet of ќ©~\coreќ©
\coreвАЛ to the quotient space
J2rвИЧ+1/V\val
J2rвИЧвАЛ+1вАЛ/V\valвАЛ

is nonzero.

The jet-level version is probably the one closest to the actual contradiction argument.
Proof strategy: what would actually be needed

To prove the theorem, you need to connect вАЬgenuine off-line anomalous coreвАЭ in the zeta/geometric sense to вАЬnot value/gaugeвАЭ in the corrected local germ sense.

That is not automatic. The proof would need three ingredients.
Step 1. Identify the value/gauge family geometrically

You need a clear description of what it means for a corrected local germ to lie in V\valV\valвАЛ.

At minimum, this should mean:

    it is generated entirely by value-channel deformation and gauge/affine-jet degrees of freedom,

    it carries no genuinely transverse anomaly.

So the theorem must use a definition of V\valV\valвАЛ that is intrinsic and finite-dimensional.
Step 2. Show that genuine off-line anomaly produces a non-value component

This is the heart.

You need to show:

    if the finite core is genuinely off-line anomalous, then its corrected local block cannot be produced solely by value/gauge deformation.

Equivalently, the off-line geometry creates a component transverse to V\valV\valвАЛ.

This is the finite-core analogue of the old statement
ќ¶K(A\val)=0,ќ¶K(M(d))вЙ†0.
ќ¶KвАЛ(A\valвАЛ)=0,ќ¶KвАЛ(M(d))оА†=0.

But now it must be stated without relying on the pair matrix M(d)M(d).
Step 3. Exclude complete cancellation into V\valV\valвАЛ

This is the subtle part.

Even if each off-line component is anomalous individually, a finite core could in principle have internal cancellations. So you need:

    the total corrected anomaly of a genuine finite off-line core cannot cancel completely into the value/gauge family.

That is the real finite-core rigidity statement.
The minimal version that may be easier to prove

A slightly weaker theorem, but probably the first one worth trying, is:

    Weak finite-core exclusion theorem.
    For fixed kk, there is no nontrivial normalized kk-core with all zeros genuinely off-line and with
    ќ©~\coreвИИV\val.
    ќ©
    \coreвАЛвИИV\valвАЛ. 

This avoids having to classify every possible anomalous core. It only rules out complete collapse into the value family.

That is enough for the compactness/separation argument.
The most plausible route to prove it

The best route is probably a contradiction by normalization.

Assume there is a sequence of genuine anomalous normalized kk-cores with
ќ©~\corenвИИV\val.
ќ©
\corenвАЛвАЛвИИV\valвАЛ.

Because the normalized parameter family is precompact after removing escaping components, pass to a convergent subsequence of parameters. Then the normalized corrected germs converge to a limit in V\valV\valвАЛ.

But the limit core is still an off-line finite core. So if being off-line genuinely forces a transverse component in the corrected germ, the limit cannot lie in V\valV\valвАЛ. Contradiction.

So the whole theorem really reduces to one statement:
off-line finite-core geometry forces a transverse corrected component.
off-line finite-core geometry forces a transverse corrected component.вАЛ

That is the deepest remaining content.
What would count as a successful proof input

Any one of the following would prove the theorem.
Option A. A finite-core anomaly invariant

Construct an invariant of the corrected germ that:

    vanishes on V\valV\valвАЛ,

    and is nonzero for every genuine finite off-line core.

This would be the cleanest proof.
Option B. A normal-form theorem

Show that every normalized kk-core germ in V\valV\valвАЛ is equivalent to a purely on-line / value-only local model. Then a genuinely off-line core cannot belong to V\valV\valвАЛ.
Option C. A scalar separation theorem with adapted functionals

Show directly that for every genuine finite off-line core there exists an adapted scalar germ that is nonzero. This is slightly weaker in formulation but equivalent in practice.
The exact next subtheorem I would attack

Not the whole theorem at once. This one:

    Finite-core transverse-production lemma.
    A single off-line zero pair, after corrected whitening and normalization, produces a germ not lying in V\valV\valвАЛ.

Then try to extend from one pair to a finite core by showing finite sums of such off-line contributions cannot all cancel into V\valV\valвАЛ unless the total anomalous part is zero.

That is where the real difficulty lies, but it is much more concrete than the global theorem.
Bottom line

So the next theorem is:
A genuine normalized finite off-line core cannot have corrected local germ lying in the value/gauge family.
A genuine normalized finite off-line core cannot have corrected local germ lying in the value/gauge family.вАЛ

And the proof reduces to the still-open finite-core rigidity statement:
off-line finite-core geometry must force a nonzero transverse corrected component.
off-line finite-core geometry must force a nonzero transverse corrected component.вАЛ

That is the cleanest formulation of what remains in the upgraded scalar endgame.

Ok continue

The next honest step is to split the remaining finite-core rigidity statement into two parts:
(i) one off-line pair produces a transverse corrected component;(ii) finitely many such components cannot cancel completely into V\val.
(i) one off-line pair produces a transverse corrected component;(ii) finitely many such components cannot cancel completely into V\valвАЛ.

Part (i) is believable and close to what you already know from the pair model. Part (ii) is the real new burden.
1. Finite-core transverse-production lemma

A sharp version is:

    Lemma. Let PP be a single off-line pair core. Then its normalized corrected germ
    ќ©~P(z)
    ќ©
    PвАЛ(z)

    does not lie in V\valV\valвАЛ.

This is basically the pair theorem in intrinsic language. In the old notation, it is exactly the content behind
ќ¶K(A\val)=0,ќ¶K(M(d))вЙ†0.
ќ¶KвАЛ(A\valвАЛ)=0,ќ¶KвАЛ(M(d))оА†=0.

So for one pair, the corrected germ has a genuinely transverse component.

That means the pair model already gives:
one off-line pair produces something outside V\val.
one off-line pair produces something outside V\valвАЛ.вАЛ

So part (i) is not the real problem.
2. Why the finite-core case is harder

Suppose the core is a finite sum of pair contributions:
ќ©~\core=вИСj=1kќ©~Pj+interaction terms.
ќ©
\coreвАЛ=j=1вИСkвАЛќ©
PjвАЛвАЛ+interaction terms.

Even if every ќ©~PjвИЙV\valќ©
PjвАЛвАЛвИИ/V\valвАЛ, it is still possible in principle that
вИСjќ©~Pj+interactionsвИИV\val.
jвИСвАЛќ©
PjвАЛвАЛ+interactionsвИИV\valвАЛ.

So the missing theorem is really a noncancellation theorem:
a finite collection of off-line pair anomalies cannot cancel exactly into the value/gauge family after corrected whitening.
a finite collection of off-line pair anomalies cannot cancel exactly into the value/gauge family after corrected whitening.вАЛ

That is the core of the upgraded endgame.
3. The right finite-dimensional formulation

Let
J
J

V\valвКВJ
V\valвАЛвКВJ

be the value/gauge subspace.

T(P)вИИJ/V\val
T(P)вИИJ/V\valвАЛ

    For every genuine finite core
    C={P1,вА¶,Pk},
    C={P1вАЛ,вА¶,PkвАЛ},

    the total transverse class

    T(C)оА†=0in J/V\valвАЛ. 

This is the right quotient-space version.

If you can prove that, then the adapted scalar endgame is done.
4. What to attack next

The most plausible next subtheorem is not the full noncancellation theorem. It is this weaker one:

    Two-pair exclusion lemma.
    Two normalized off-line pairs cannot combine, together with their corrected interaction term, to land entirely in V\valV\valвАЛ.

Why this is the right next move:

    it is the smallest genuinely new case beyond the old pair model;

    if cancellation already happens for two pairs, the finite-core route needs a very different invariant;

    if cancellation fails for two pairs, that is real evidence the upgraded endgame is viable.

So I would test the k=2k=2 case first.
5. The exact k=2k=2 theorem to try

ќ©~P1,P2.
ќ©
P1вАЛ,P2вАЛвАЛ.

ќ©~P1,P2вИЙV\val.
ќ©
P1вАЛ,P2вАЛвАЛвИИ/V\valвАЛ.вАЛ

ќЫвАЙвБ£(ќ©~P1,P2)вЙ†0.

6. How to attack the two-pair case

There are two ways.

First way: expand in pair amplitudes. Write schematically
ќ©~P1,P2=u12M1+u22M2+u1u2M12+вЛѓвАКmodвАКV\val.
ќ©
P1вАЛ,P2вАЛвАЛ=u12вАЛM1вАЛ+u22вАЛM2вАЛ+u1вАЛu2вАЛM12вАЛ+вЛѓmodV\valвАЛ.

Then ask whether
u12M1+u22M2+u1u2M12

can vanish in the quotient for nontrivial (u1,u2)(u1вАЛ,u2вАЛ).

Second way: use the scalar germ directly. Let

At this point, the best next step is:
test the upgraded theorem at k=2.

Why this is the right test

If this theorem holds, then two-pair cancellation into V\valV\valвАЛ already fails at first odd jet. That would be excellent news, because it means the upgraded endgame may still work with a low-order scalar invariant, not some mysterious higher-order jet.

If it fails, you have learned something equally important: the first jet is not enough, and the finite-core scalar theorem has to be stated at the first nonzero odd jet rather than specifically at a1a1вАЛ.

So either way, this is the right next computation.

ќ±12(h1,h2)вИЙspanвБ°{ќ±1(h1),ќ±2(h2)}

Most realistic next move

I would not try to prove the full theorem abstractly first. I would do this:

    Fix a concrete quotient basis ќЫ1,вА¶,ќЫrќЫ1вАЛ,вА¶,ќЫrвАЛ.

    Write the quadratic first-jet expansion explicitly:
    a1(j)=u12ќ±1(j)+u22ќ±2(j)+u1u2ќ±12(j).

    Check whether cancellation is even algebraically possible in the first nontrivial cases.

That is the most informative next test.

So the next theorem to write in clean form is:

For a two-pair core, the first odd quotient jet is represented by the vector
u12ќ±1(h1)+u22ќ±2(h2)+u1u2ќ±12(h1,h2).

That is now the sharpest computable frontier.

Yes. The next continuation is to make that first-jet two-pair test as explicit as possible, without pretending we already know the answer.

At this point the problem has become finite-dimensional and algebraic.
1. The object to compute

For a two-pair core, the corrected germ has a first odd quotient jet of the form
J1\quot(u1,u2;h1,h2)=u12вАЙA1(h1)+u22вАЙA2(h2)+u1u2вАЙA12(h1,h2),
J1\quotвАЛ(u1вАЛ,u2вАЛ;h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=u12вАЛA1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+u22вАЛA2вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+u1вАЛu2вАЛA12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ),

where the vectors live in the finite-dimensional quotient space
J1/V\val(1).
J1вАЛ/V\val(1)вАЛ.

This is the real two-pair invariant.

The theorem you want is simply:
J1\quot(u1,u2;h1,h2)вЙ†0for all (u1,u2)вЙ†(0,0).
J1\quotвАЛ(u1вАЛ,u2вАЛ;h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)оА†=0for all (u1вАЛ,u2вАЛ)оА†=(0,0).

If that holds, then two-pair cancellation already fails at first jet.
2. How to package it practically

Choose a basis of quotient-detecting functionals
ќЫ1,вА¶,ќЫr
ќЫ1вАЛ,вА¶,ќЫrвАЛ

annihilating V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ. Then define
a1(j)(u1,u2;h1,h2)=ќЫjвАЙвБ£(J1\quot(u1,u2;h1,h2)).
a1(j)вАЛ(u1вАЛ,u2вАЛ;h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=ќЫjвАЛ(J1\quotвАЛ(u1вАЛ,u2вАЛ;h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)).

Equivalently,
a1(j)=u12ќ±1(j)(h1)+u22ќ±2(j)(h2)+u1u2ќ±12(j)(h1,h2).
a1(j)вАЛ=u12вАЛќ±1(j)вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+u22вАЛќ±2(j)вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+u1вАЛu2вАЛќ±12(j)вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ).

So the two-pair first-jet test becomes:
вИГj such that u12ќ±1(j)+u22ќ±2(j)+u1u2ќ±12(j)вЙ†0.
вИГj such that u12вАЛќ±1(j)вАЛ+u22вАЛќ±2(j)вАЛ+u1вАЛu2вАЛќ±12(j)вАЛоА†=0.

If you like vector notation,
u12ќ±1(h1)+u22ќ±2(h2)+u1u2ќ±12(h1,h2)вЙ†0.
u12вАЛќ±1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+u22вАЛќ±2вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+u1вАЛu2вАЛќ±12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)оА†=0.

That is the cleanest form.
3. The key reduction

Divide by u12u12вАЛ when u1вЙ†0u1вАЛоА†=0, and set
ќї:=u2u1.
ќї:=u1вАЛu2вАЛвАЛ.

Then cancellation is equivalent to
A1(h1)+ќї2A2(h2)+ќївАЙA12(h1,h2)=0.
A1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+ќї2A2вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+ќїA12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=0.

So for fixed (h1,h2)(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ), this is a quadratic equation in ќїќї, but vector-valued.

That is a big simplification. It means:

    if the quotient dimension is at least 22, cancellation is already overdetermined generically;

    only very special relations among A1,A2,A12A1вАЛ,A2вАЛ,A12вАЛ could allow it.

So the next thing to understand is the quotient dimension and the relative position of those three vectors.
4. Best-case structural outcomes

Any one of these would be enough.

If
dimвБ°(J1/V\val(1))вЙ•2
dim(J1вАЛ/V\val(1)вАЛ)вЙ•2

and the vectors
A1(h1), A2(h2), A12(h1,h2)
A1вАЛ(h1вАЛ), A2вАЛ(h2вАЛ), A12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)

are not all collinear, then generic cancellation is impossible.

Even stronger, if
A12(h1,h2)вИЙspanвБ°{A1(h1),A2(h2)},
A12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)вИИ/span{A1вАЛ(h1вАЛ),A2вАЛ(h2вАЛ)},

then cancellation is impossible for every nontrivial (u1,u2)(u1вАЛ,u2вАЛ).

A weaker but still useful outcome is:
вИ•A12(h1,h2)вИ•вЙ™вИ•A1(h1)вИ•+вИ•A2(h2)вИ•,
вИ•A12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)вИ•вЙ™вИ•A1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)вИ•+вИ•A2вАЛ(h2вАЛ)вИ•,

so the interaction cannot fully cancel the self terms except possibly in a narrow exceptional set.
5. What to compute first

The most useful next subcomputation is not the full quotient basis. It is this:

    identify one nontrivial first-jet quotient functional ќЫќЫ,

    compute
    ќ±1(h),ќ±2(h),ќ±12(h1,h2),
    ќ±1вАЛ(h),ќ±2вАЛ(h),ќ±12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ),

    for that one ќЫќЫ,

    and check whether
    u12ќ±1(h1)+u22ќ±2(h2)+u1u2ќ±12(h1,h2)
    u12вАЛќ±1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+u22вАЛќ±2вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+u1вАЛu2вАЛќ±12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)

    can vanish identically.

Why this helps: if even one quotient-detecting functional already gives noncancellation, you are done at first jet.

So you do not need the whole quotient structure at first pass.
6. What I suspect

I suspect the likely picture is:

    one-pair self terms are nonzero and comparable;

    the mixed interaction term is real and can affect the first jet;

    but exact cancellation at first jet is nongeneric, not structurally forced.

If that is right, then the upgraded endgame works for generic two-pair cores, and only a special resonance locus would need additional treatment.

That would already be a major win.
7. The honest boundary

What I cannot do yet is give the explicit ќ±1,ќ±2,ќ±12ќ±1вАЛ,ќ±2вАЛ,ќ±12вАЛ formulas without the actual two-pair corrected-block expansion being written out. That is the missing computation.

So the sharp next step is:
write the two-pair first-jet expansion explicitly and identify A1,A2,A12.
write the two-pair first-jet expansion explicitly and identify A1вАЛ,A2вАЛ,A12вАЛ.вАЛ

Once those are on paper, the cancellation question becomes plain finite algebra.

1. What exactly we are testing

2. The right way to build the quotient

The first-jet space is just 2√Ч22√Ч2 matrices. The value/gauge first-jet subspace is
V\val(1)вКВM2(C).
V\val(1)вАЛвКВM2вАЛ(C).

(V\val(1))вК•={L: вЯ®L,BвЯ©F=0 вИАBвИИV\val(1)}.

3. What V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ looks like from the formulas we already have

From the corrected same-point block formula used throughout:
Gm,¬±(q)=1ѕА(2q(t¬±)12qвА≤(t¬±)12qвА≤(t¬±)112(qвА≤вА≤(t¬±)+2q(t¬±)3)),t¬±=m¬±s2.

    q+(0)=qвИТ(0)q+вАЛ(0)=qвИТвАЛ(0),

The most important invariant from earlier is that the value-direction is killed by the old transverse functional in the pair case:
ќ¶K(A\val)=0.
ќ¶KвАЛ(A\valвАЛ)=0.

At first jet level, that strongly suggests V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ contains the вАЬsymmetricвАЭ and вАЬgaugeвАЭ directions coming from:

    common diagonal variation,

    symmetric off-diagonal variation generated by affine-jet/gauge transport.

The quotient is therefore detecting the genuinely antisymmetric/transverse part of J1J1вАЛ.

This means that, even before computing a full basis, the first nontrivial quotient component should be controlled by an antisymmetric combination of off-diagonals together with whatever same-point transport correction survives whitening.

So at first jet, the quotient space is very plausibly low-dimensional, probably codimension 11 or 22, not huge. That is already useful.
4. What the one-pair piece tells us

For a single pair, we already know the first odd scalar is nondegenerate:
a1\pair(u,h)=ќЇ(h)u2+O(u4),ќЇ(h)вЙ†0.
a1\pairвАЛ(u,h)=ќЇ(h)u2+O(u4),ќЇ(h)оА†=0.

Translated into the first-jet quotient language, that means:

    the one-pair first jet A1(h)A1вАЛ(h) is not in V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ,

    and therefore its quotient class is nonzero.

So the self-terms
A1(h1),A2(h2)
A1вАЛ(h1вАЛ),A2вАЛ(h2вАЛ)

are each nonzero in the quotient.

That is a real theorem-level fact from the previous work.
5. What the interaction term has to look like

For the two-pair core, the new thing is the interaction term
A12(h1,h2).
A12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ).

This comes from:

    adding q1+q2q1вАЛ+q2вАЛ into the same-point blocks,

    adding q1+q2q1вАЛ+q2вАЛ into the mixed block,

    then passing through corrected whitening,

    then taking the first ss-jet.

Crucially, this interaction is quadratic in amplitudes but not in ss; it really can enter the first odd jet, so we cannot ignore it.

What we can still say, though, is structural:

    A12A12вАЛ is continuous in (h1,h2)(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ),

    it vanishes if one amplitude vanishes,

    and if the pairs are well separated, it should be smaller than the self terms.

So exact cancellation would require a very specific algebraic relation among
A1(h1),A2(h2),A12(h1,h2).
A1вАЛ(h1вАЛ),A2вАЛ(h2вАЛ),A12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ).

That is already a strong indication that cancellation is nongeneric.
6. The exact finite algebra test

Let L1,вА¶,LrL1вАЛ,вА¶,LrвАЛ be a basis of annihilators of V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ. Define
ќ±1(j)(h1):=вЯ®Lj,A1(h1)вЯ©F,ќ±2(j)(h2):=вЯ®Lj,A2(h2)вЯ©F,
ќ±1(j)вАЛ(h1вАЛ):=вЯ®LjвАЛ,A1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)вЯ©FвАЛ,ќ±2(j)вАЛ(h2вАЛ):=вЯ®LjвАЛ,A2вАЛ(h2вАЛ)вЯ©FвАЛ,
ќ±12(j)(h1,h2):=вЯ®Lj,A12(h1,h2)вЯ©F.
ќ±12(j)вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ):=вЯ®LjвАЛ,A12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)вЯ©FвАЛ.

Then first-jet cancellation is equivalent to the vector equation
u12ќ±1(h1)+u22ќ±2(h2)+u1u2ќ±12(h1,h2)=0.
u12вАЛќ±1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+u22вАЛќ±2вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+u1вАЛu2вАЛќ±12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=0.

Set
ќї:=u2/u1
ќї:=u2вАЛ/u1вАЛ

when u1вЙ†0u1вАЛоА†=0. Then cancellation becomes
ќ±1(h1)+ќї2ќ±2(h2)+ќївАЙќ±12(h1,h2)=0.
ќ±1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+ќї2ќ±2вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+ќїќ±12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=0.

This is a vector-valued quadratic equation in ќїќї. If rвЙ•2rвЙ•2, it is generically overdetermined. So for generic (h1,h2)(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ), exact first-jet cancellation should fail unless there is a hidden structural identity.

That is the most important conclusion of the test.
7. What we can honestly conclude already

Here is the strongest honest outcome from the вАЬwhole testвАЭ so far:
What is supported

The two-pair first-jet cancellation problem has been reduced to a finite-dimensional algebraic condition
ќ±1+ќї2ќ±2+ќїќ±12=0,
ќ±1вАЛ+ќї2ќ±2вАЛ+ќїќ±12вАЛ=0,

where the self-term vectors are known to be nonzero.

This means:

    two-pair cancellation at first jet is not automatic,

    and if the quotient dimension is at least 22, it should be nongeneric.

What is not yet proved

We have not computed the actual A12(h1,h2)A12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ) or the annihilator basis LjLjвАЛ, so we do not yet know whether there is a hidden identity forcing cancellation in codimension 11.

So I cannot honestly say вАЬtwo-pair cancellation is impossibleвАЭ yet.

    u12ќ±1(h1)+u22ќ±2(h2)+u1u2ќ±12(h1,h2)
    u12вАЛќ±1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+u22вАЛќ±2вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+u1вАЛu2вАЛќ±12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)

    can vanish identically.

If one such LL already gives noncancellation, then k=2k=2 is safe at first jet and the finite-core upgrade becomes much more plausible.
9. My bottom line

So the вАЬwhole testвАЭ result is:

    the correct quotient framework is now in place;

    the self terms are definitely transverse;

    the interaction term is the only thing that could rescue cancellation;

    cancellation reduces to a vector-valued quadratic equation in ќї=u2/u1ќї=u2вАЛ/u1вАЛ;

    and unless the quotient is accidentally one-dimensional with a special identity, two-pair first-jet cancellation should be exceptional rather than structural.

There are three regimes.

If the quotient codimension at first jet is really 11, then only one scalar matters, and cancellation reduces to one quadratic equation. In that case first-jet cancellation is not exotic at all; it can happen whenever that quadratic has a nonzero root. So codimension 11 would be bad news for a first-jet-only endgame.

If the quotient codimension is at least 22, then cancellation is overdetermined. Then R=0R=0 is one genuine constraint on (h1,h2)(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ), and unless the coefficients satisfy a hidden identity, cancellation should live on a thin exceptional locus, not be structural. That would be good news.

If the interaction coefficients ќ±12,ќ≤12ќ±12вАЛ,ќ≤12вАЛ are small compared to the self coefficients, then even better: RR is dominated by the self terms, and exact cancellation becomes fine-tuned.

So the next non-bluff step is to identify what symmetry already forces on the six coefficients. There are two obvious ones.

after relabeling. So really there are only four independent coefficient functions:
ќ±(h), ќ≤(h), ќ±12(h1,h2), ќ≤12(h1,h2).

ќ≤(h1вАЛ)+ќї2ќ≤(h2вАЛ)+ќїќ≤12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=0.

That already simplifies the resultant to
R=ќ±(h1)2ќ≤(h2)2вИТќ±(h1)ќ±12ќ≤12ќ≤(h2)вИТ2ќ±(h1)ќ±(h2)ќ≤(h1)ќ≤(h2)+ќ±(h1)ќ±(h2)ќ≤122+ќ±122ќ≤(h1)ќ≤(h2)вИТќ±12ќ±(h2)ќ≤(h1)ќ≤12+ќ±(h2)2ќ≤(h1)2.
R=ќ±(h1вАЛ)2ќ≤(h2вАЛ)2вИТќ±(h1вАЛ)ќ±12вАЛќ≤12вАЛќ≤(h2вАЛ)вИТ2ќ±(h1вАЛ)ќ±(h2вАЛ)ќ≤(h1вАЛ)ќ≤(h2вАЛ)+ќ±(h1вАЛ)ќ±(h2вАЛ)ќ≤122вАЛ+ќ±122вАЛќ≤(h1вАЛ)ќ≤(h2вАЛ)вИТќ±12вАЛќ±(h2вАЛ)ќ≤(h1вАЛ)ќ≤12вАЛ+ќ±(h2вАЛ)2ќ≤(h1вАЛ)2.

Second, if the geometry is symmetric under reflecting the two offsets, then the interaction terms should be symmetric:
ќ±12(h1,h2)=ќ±12(h2,h1),ќ≤12(h1,h2)=ќ≤12(h2,h1).
ќ±12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=ќ±12вАЛ(h2вАЛ,h1вАЛ),ќ≤12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=ќ≤12вАЛ(h2вАЛ,h1вАЛ).

That means the bad locus R=0R=0 is symmetric in h1,h2h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ, which is useful when checking whether cancellation is a thin resonance set or an identity.

There is also a very useful sufficient condition for noncancellation. If the two vectors
(ќ±(h1),ќ≤(h1)),(ќ±(h2),ќ≤(h2))
(ќ±(h1вАЛ),ќ≤(h1вАЛ)),(ќ±(h2вАЛ),ќ≤(h2вАЛ))

are linearly independent, and the interaction vector
(ќ±12(h1,h2),ќ≤12(h1,h2))
(ќ±12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ),ќ≤12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ))

is small relative to them, then there is no small ќїќї-quadratic cancellation except possibly on an exceptional set. In practice, a bound like
вИ£(ќ±12,ќ≤12)вИ£вЙ§cвАЙminвБ°{вИ£(ќ±(h1),ќ≤(h1))вИ£, вИ£(ќ±(h2),ќ≤(h2))вИ£}
вИ£(ќ±12вАЛ,ќ≤12вАЛ)вИ£вЙ§cmin{вИ£(ќ±(h1вАЛ),ќ≤(h1вАЛ))вИ£, вИ£(ќ±(h2вАЛ),ќ≤(h2вАЛ))вИ£}

with cc sufficiently small would already make first-jet cancellation nongeneric.

So the exact computation target is now even sharper:

    compute the self vector

v(h):=(ќ±(h),ќ≤(h))
v(h):=(ќ±(h),ќ≤(h))

for one pair;

    compute the interaction vector

w(h1,h2):=(ќ±12(h1,h2),ќ≤12(h1,h2));
w(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ):=(ќ±12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ),ќ≤12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ));

    check whether ww is ever large enough, and aligned in exactly the right way, to solve

v(h1)+ќї2v(h2)+ќїw(h1,h2)=0.
v(h1вАЛ)+ќї2v(h2вАЛ)+ќїw(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=0.

That is now the real algebraic heart of the two-pair test.

The most meaningful next symbolic computation would be the one-pair self vector v(h)v(h). Once that is known, even before ww is fully explicit, we can tell whether the quotient is plausibly one- or two-dimensional and whether first-jet cancellation is likely to be generic or exceptional.

This is what the other chat ended up with:

вЄї


\begin{lemma}[Baseline-scaled transverse gain]
\label{lem:baseline-scaled-transverse-gain}
Let
\[
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+):=G_-^{-1/2}NG_+^{-1/2},
\qquad
H(s):=\Phi_K\!\bigl(\mathcal W(G_-(s),N(s),G_+(s))\bigr).
\]
Assume \(G_\pm^{(0)}(s)\) are uniformly positive definite on \(|s|<\rho_0/Q\), and
their baseline scales are
\[
G_\pm^{(0)}(s)=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(Q) & O(1)\\
O(1) & O(Q^3)
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
N^{(0)}(s)=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(Q) & O(Q^2)\\
O(Q^2) & O(Q^3)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Set
\[
S_Q:=\begin{pmatrix}Q^{-1/2}&0\\0&Q^{-3/2}\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\widehat G_\pm:=S_Q^{-1}G_\pm S_Q^{-1},
\qquad
\widehat N:=S_Q^{-1}NS_Q^{-1}.
\]
Then
\[
\widehat G_\pm^{(0)}(s)=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(1)&O(Q^{-1})\\
O(Q^{-1})&O(1)
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\widehat N^{(0)}(s)=
\begin{pmatrix}
O(1)&O(1)\\
O(1)&O(1)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Moreover, if \(\delta G_\pm,\delta N\) are perturbations and
\[
\widehat H(s):=
\Phi_K\!\bigl(\widehat G_-(s)^{-1/2}\widehat N(s)\widehat G_+(s)^{-1/2}\bigr),
\]
then
\[
H(s)=Q^{-3}\widehat H(s),
\]
up to a harmless absolute constant depending only on the normalization of
\(\Phi_K\). In particular, every scalar jet of \(H\) inherits a uniform factor
\(Q^{-3}\) from the baseline scaling.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The displayed bounds for \(\widehat G_\pm^{(0)}\) and \(\widehat N^{(0)}\) are
immediate from the definitions. Since
\[
G_\pm^{-1/2}=S_Q\,\widehat G_\pm^{-1/2}\,S_Q,
\]
one has
\[
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+)
=
S_Q\,\widehat G_-^{-1/2}\widehat N\,\widehat G_+^{-1/2}\,S_Q.
\]
Thus every off-diagonal entry of \(\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+)\) picks up one factor
\(Q^{-1/2}\) from the value side and one factor \(Q^{-3/2}\) from the derivative
side, hence a total factor \(Q^{-2}\); after restoring the normalization used in
\(\Phi_K\) in the draft, this is exactly the same \(Q^{-3}\) transverse gain as
in the corrected mixed-transfer theorem. The same factor persists under
\(s\)-differentiation because the scaling matrix \(S_Q\) is independent of \(s\).
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[Differentiated corrected-whitening transfer lemma]
\label{prop:differentiated-whitening-transfer}
Let
\[
\mathcal W(G_-,N,G_+):=G_-^{-1/2}NG_+^{-1/2},
\qquad
H(s):=\Phi_K\!\bigl(\mathcal W(G_-(s),N(s),G_+(s))\bigr).
\]
Assume:
\begin{enumerate}
\item \(G_\pm^{(0)}(s)\) and \(N^{(0)}(s)\) are holomorphic on \(|s|<\rho_0/Q\), and
\(G_\pm^{(0)}(s)\) stay uniformly positive definite there;
\item
\[
G_\pm(s)=G_\pm^{(0)}(s)+\delta G_\pm(s),
\qquad
N(s)=N^{(0)}(s)+\delta N(s),
\]
with \(\delta G_\pm,\delta N\) holomorphic;
\item for a \(2\times2\) matrix \(M=(m_{ij})\), define the weighted block norm
\[
\|M\|_\dagger
:=
\max\!\bigl(Q^2|m_{11}|,\;Q|m_{12}|,\;Q|m_{21}|,\;|m_{22}|\bigr).
\]
\end{enumerate}

Let
\[
H_0(s):=\Phi_K\!\bigl(\mathcal W(G_-^{(0)}(s),N^{(0)}(s),G_+^{(0)}(s))\bigr),
\]
and for each \(j\ge 0\), set
\[
\eta_j:=
\|\partial_s^j\delta G_-(0)\|_\dagger
+
\|\partial_s^j\delta N(0)\|_\dagger
+
\|\partial_s^j\delta G_+(0)\|_\dagger.
\]

Then for each \(k\ge 0\) there exists \(C_k>0\) such that
\[
\boxed{
|H^{(k)}(0)-H_0^{(k)}(0)|
\le
C_k\,Q^{-3}
\sum_{\pi\vdash k}
\prod_{j\in\pi}\eta_j,
}
\]
where the sum is over ordered partitions \(\pi=(j_1,\dots,j_p)\) of \(k\), i.e.
\(j_1+\cdots+j_p=k\), \(j_i\ge 0\).

In particular, if
\[
\eta_j\ll_j \Delta^{-(j+4)}
\qquad (j\ge 0),
\]
then
\[
\boxed{
|H^{(k)}(0)-H_0^{(k)}(0)|
\ll_k
\frac{1}{Q^3\,\Delta^{k+4}}.
}
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
By Lemma~\ref{lem:baseline-scaled-transverse-gain}, after the baseline scaling
\(S_Q\), the same-point baseline blocks become \(O(1)\)-scale uniformly positive
definite matrices, and the scalar \(H\) acquires an overall factor \(Q^{-3}\).

Let
\[
\widehat G_\pm:=S_Q^{-1}G_\pm S_Q^{-1},
\qquad
\widehat N:=S_Q^{-1}NS_Q^{-1}.
\]
Then
\[
\|\partial_s^j\widehat{\delta G}_\pm(0)\|_{\max}
+
\|\partial_s^j\widehat{\delta N}(0)\|_{\max}
\asymp \eta_j.
\]

Now the map
\[
(\widehat G_-,\widehat N,\widehat G_+)
\longmapsto
\Phi_K\!\bigl(\widehat G_-^{-1/2}\widehat N\,\widehat G_+^{-1/2}\bigr)
\]
is holomorphic in a neighborhood of the baseline image. All Fr\'echet
derivatives of \(\widehat G\mapsto \widehat G^{-1/2}\) are bounded there by
holomorphic functional calculus (equivalently, in \(2\times2\), by the L\"owner
divided-difference formula after diagonalizing the baseline matrix).

Applying Fa\a di Bruno to the composition in $begin:math:text$s$end:math:text$, every $begin:math:text$k$end:math:text$-th derivative at
$begin:math:text$0$end:math:text$ is a finite sum of multilinear terms in the jets
$begin:math:display$
\\partial\_s\^j\\widehat\{\\delta G\}\_\-\(0\)\,\\quad
\\partial\_s\^j\\widehat\{\\delta N\}\(0\)\,\\quad
\\partial\_s\^j\\widehat\{\\delta G\}\_\+\(0\)\,
\\qquad 0\\le j\\le k\,
$end:math:display$
with total derivative order $begin:math:text$k$end:math:text$. Each such multilinear term is bounded by
$begin:math:display$
C\_k\\prod\_\{j\\in\\pi\}\\eta\_j
$end:math:display$
for some ordered partition $begin:math:text$\\pi\\vdash k$end:math:text$. Summing all such terms and restoring
the global $begin:math:text$Q\^\{\-3\}$end:math:text$ factor gives
$begin:math:display$
\|H\^\{\(k\)\}\(0\)\-H\_0\^\{\(k\)\}\(0\)\|
\\le
C\_k\\\,Q\^\{\-3\}
\\sum\_\{\\pi\\vdash k\}
\\prod\_\{j\\in\\pi\}\\eta\_j\.
$end:math:display$

If $begin:math:text$\\eta\_j\\ll\_j \\Delta\^\{\-\(j\+4\)\}$end:math:text$, then for an ordered partition
$begin:math:text$\\pi\=\(j\_1\,\\dots\,j\_p\)$end:math:text$,
$begin:math:display$
\\prod\_\{j\\in\\pi\}\\eta\_j
\\ll\_\\pi
\\Delta\^\{\-\\sum\_i\(j\_i\+4\)\}
\=
\\Delta\^\{\-\(k\+4p\)\}\.
$end:math:display$
The linear terms $begin:math:text$p\=1$end:math:text$ contribute $begin:math:text$\\Delta\^\{\-\(k\+4\)\}$end:math:text$, while every nonlinear
term has $begin:math:text$p\\ge 2$end:math:text$ and therefore gains at least an additional factor
$begin:math:text$\\Delta\^\{\-4\}$end:math:text$. Thus the linear term dominates, and
$begin:math:display$
\|H\^\{\(k\)\}\(0\)\-H\_0\^\{\(k\)\}\(0\)\|
\\ll\_k
\\frac\{1\}\{Q\^3\\\,\\Delta\^\{k\+4\}\}\.
$end:math:display$
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Local zero-counting in dyadic shells]
\label{lem:dyadic-shell-count}
Let
$begin:math:display$
\\mathcal S\_k\:\=\\\{\\rho\:\\ 2\^kH\<\\Delta\_\\rho\\le 2\^\{k\+1\}H\\\}\,
\\qquad
\\Delta\_\\rho\:\=\|2m\-\\gamma\_\\rho\|\,
\\qquad
k\\ge 0\.
$end:math:display$
Then
$begin:math:display$
\\\#\\mathcal S\_k\\ll Q\\\,\(2\^kH\)\,
$end:math:display$
where $begin:math:text$Q\\asymp \\log\|m\|$end:math:text$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
This is the local zero-counting estimate already used elsewhere in the draft,
applied to the interval of ordinates
$begin:math:display$
\[\\\,2m\-2\^\{k\+1\}H\,\\\;2m\+2\^\{k\+1\}H\\\,\]\.
$end:math:display$
Its length is $begin:math:text$O\(2\^kH\)$end:math:text$, and the zero density at height $begin:math:text$m$end:math:text$ is $begin:math:text$O\(Q\)$end:math:text$.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Single-pair exterior odd-jet lemma]
\label{cor:single-pair-odd-jet}
Let $begin:math:text$h\_\\rho\(s\)$end:math:text$ be the corrected transverse scalar contributed by a single
exterior zero pair at absolute distance $begin:math:text$\\Delta$end:math:text$ from the local center, after:
\begin{enumerate}
\item the same fixed-core decomposition,
\item the same affine-jet subtraction at the midpoint $begin:math:text$m$end:math:text$,
\item the same corrected same-point/mixed blocks,
\item and the same corrected whitening scheme
\end{enumerate}
used in the global argument.

Write
$begin:math:display$
h\_\\rho\(z\/Q\^2\)
\=
\\sum\_\{r\\ge 0\} a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\\\,\\frac\{z\^\{2r\+1\}\}\{Q\^\{2r\+4\}\}\.
$end:math:display$
Then for each fixed $begin:math:text$r\\ge 0$end:math:text$,
$begin:math:display$
\\boxed\{
\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\|\\ll\_r \\frac\{1\}\{Q\\\,\\Delta\^\{2r\+4\}\}\.
\}
$end:math:display$
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
Apply Proposition~\ref{prop:differentiated-whitening-transfer} with
$begin:math:display$
H\=h\_\\rho\,\\qquad H\_0\\equiv 0\.
$end:math:display$
For one exterior pair after affine-jet subtraction at the midpoint $begin:math:text$m$end:math:text$, the
single-pair jet estimates give
$begin:math:display$
\\eta\_j\\ll\_j \\Delta\^\{\-\(j\+4\)\}\.
$end:math:display$
Therefore
$begin:math:display$
\|h\_\\rho\^\{\(2r\+1\)\}\(0\)\|\\ll\_r \\frac\{1\}\{Q\^3\\\,\\Delta\^\{2r\+5\}\}\.
$end:math:display$

Now
$begin:math:display$
h\_\\rho\(z\/Q\^2\)
\=
\\sum\_\{r\\ge 0\} a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\\\,\\frac\{z\^\{2r\+1\}\}\{Q\^\{2r\+4\}\}
$end:math:display$
is equivalent to
$begin:math:display$
h\_\\rho\(s\)
\=
\\sum\_\{r\\ge 0\} a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\\\,Q\^\{2r\-2\}s\^\{2r\+1\}\,
$end:math:display$
so
$begin:math:display$
h\_\\rho\^\{\(2r\+1\)\}\(0\)
\=
\(2r\+1\)\!\\\,a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\\\,Q\^\{2r\-2\}\.
$end:math:display$
Hence
$begin:math:display$
\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\|
\=
\\frac\{\|h\_\\rho\^\{\(2r\+1\)\}\(0\)\|\}\{\(2r\+1\)\!\}\\\,Q\^\{2\-2r\}
\\ll\_r
Q\^\{2\-2r\}\\cdot \\frac\{1\}\{Q\^3\\\,\\Delta\^\{2r\+5\}\}\.
$end:math:display$

This formally gives
$begin:math:display$
\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\|
\\ll\_r
\\frac\{1\}\{Q\^\{2r\+1\}\\Delta\^\{2r\+5\}\}\.
$end:math:display$
However, since the $begin:math:text$Q\^\{\-3\}$end:math:text$ gain in
Proposition~\ref{prop:differentiated-whitening-transfer} is established at the
block-to-scalar level rather than coefficient-by-coefficient in the normalized
odd expansion, we record the uniform bound in the stable weaker form
$begin:math:display$
\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\|\\ll\_r \\frac\{1\}\{Q\\\,\\Delta\^\{2r\+4\}\}\,
$end:math:display$
which is sufficient for the shell summation below.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Exterior higher odd-jet bound]
\label{cor:outside-higher-odd-jets}
Let
$begin:math:display$
H\_\{\\out\}\(z\/Q\^2\)
\=
\\sum\_\{r\\ge 0\} a\_\{2r\+1\}\^\{\\out\}\(m\,H\)\\\,\\frac\{z\^\{2r\+1\}\}\{Q\^\{2r\+4\}\}
$end:math:display$
be the corrected transverse scalar contributed by all zeros outside absolute
radius $begin:math:text$H$end:math:text$ from the local center. Then for each fixed $begin:math:text$r\\ge 0$end:math:text$,
$begin:math:display$
\\boxed\{
\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\^\{\\out\}\(m\,H\)\|\\ll\_r \\frac\{1\}\{Q\\\,H\^\{2r\+3\}\}\.
\}
$end:math:display$
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
Decompose the exterior zeros into dyadic shells
$begin:math:display$
\\mathcal S\_k\:\=\\\{\\rho\:\\ 2\^kH\<\\Delta\_\\rho\\le 2\^\{k\+1\}H\\\}\,
\\qquad k\\ge 0\.
$end:math:display$
By Lemma~\ref{lem:dyadic-shell-count},
$begin:math:display$
\\\#\\mathcal S\_k\\ll Q\(2\^kH\)\.
$end:math:display$
Applying Corollary~\ref{cor:single-pair-odd-jet} to each $begin:math:text$\\rho\\in\\mathcal S\_k$end:math:text$,
$begin:math:display$
\\sum\_\{\\rho\\in\\mathcal S\_k\}\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\(\\rho\)\|
\\ll\_r
Q\(2\^kH\)\\cdot \\frac\{1\}\{Q\(2\^kH\)\^\{2r\+4\}\}
\=
\\frac\{1\}\{\(2\^kH\)\^\{2r\+3\}\}\.
$end:math:display$
Summing over $begin:math:text$k\\ge 0$end:math:text$,
$begin:math:display$
\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\^\{\\out\}\(m\,H\)\|
\\ll\_r
\\sum\_\{k\\ge 0\}\\frac\{1\}\{\(2\^kH\)\^\{2r\+3\}\}
\\ll\_r
\\frac\{1\}\{H\^\{2r\+3\}\}\.
$end:math:display$
Restoring the explicit $begin:math:text$Q\^\{\-1\}$end:math:text$ factor gives
$begin:math:display$
\|a\_\{2r\+1\}\^\{\\out\}\(m\,H\)\|\\ll\_r \\frac\{1\}\{Q\\\,H\^\{2r\+3\}\}\,
$end:math:display$
as claimed.
\end{proof}

I would keep the stronger intermediate estimate only as an internal observation for now, not as the headline bound.

This is a real advance.

My overall view: the architecture is good, the scaling idea is right, and the exterior higher-odd-jet bound is the first genuinely plausible upgraded finite-core theorem chain weвАЩve had. But there are two places where I would still be careful about what is fully proved versus what is a strong heuristic repackaging of earlier machinery.

The strongest part is the re-centering around differentiated whitening. That is exactly the right object for the finite-core scalar endgame. The baseline scaling lemma is also the right idea: normalize the same-point blocks to O(1)O(1) scale, track how the transverse scalar picks up a fixed power of QQ, and then differentiate in a controlled way. That is the right framework for proving coefficient bounds at arbitrary odd order.

What I think is solid:

The weighted norm
вИ•MвИ•вА†=maxвБ°вАЙвБ£(Q2вИ£m11вИ£,вАЕвАКQвИ£m12вИ£,вАЕвАКQвИ£m21вИ£,вАЕвАКвИ£m22вИ£)
вИ•MвИ•вА†вАЛ=max(Q2вИ£m11вАЛвИ£,QвИ£m12вАЛвИ£,QвИ£m21вАЛвИ£,вИ£m22вАЛвИ£)

is exactly the correct norm for the block perturbations.

The holomorphic-composition/Fa√† di Bruno structure in Proposition differentiated-whitening-transfer is right in spirit. Once you baseline-scale the blocks, the corrected whitening map becomes a holomorphic map on an O(1)O(1)-sized neighborhood, and kk-th derivatives really are finite sums of multilinear expressions in the lower jets. So the partition sum is the right schematic bound.

The shell summation in the final corollary is also the right mechanism. If a single exterior pair contributes at order ќФвИТ(2r+4)ќФвИТ(2r+4) or better, then local zero counting gives exactly an HвИТ(2r+3)HвИТ(2r+3) exterior tail after dyadic summation. That part is structurally very convincing.

Where I would still be careful:
1. The QвИТ3QвИТ3 transverse gain in the baseline-scaled lemma

This is the shakiest sentence:

    вАЬevery off-diagonal entry вА¶ picks up QвИТ2QвИТ2; after restoring the normalization used in ќ¶Kќ¶KвАЛ in the draft, this is exactly the same QвИТ3QвИТ3 transverse gainвА¶вАЭ

That may be true in your normalization, but it needs to be pinned down explicitly, not just gestured at.

As written, the matrix identity
W(GвИТ,N,G+)=SQвАЙG^вИТвИТ1/2N^вАЙG^+вИТ1/2вАЙSQ
W(GвИТвАЛ,N,G+вАЛ)=SQвАЛG
вИТвИТ1/2вАЛN
G
+вИТ1/2вАЛSQвАЛ

only directly shows the off-diagonal entries scale like QвИТ2QвИТ2, since QвИТ1/2вЛЕQвИТ3/2=QвИТ2QвИТ1/2вЛЕQвИТ3/2=QвИТ2.

So the extra QвИТ1QвИТ1 has to come from the definition of ќ¶Kќ¶KвАЛ as used in the paper. That is fine if true, but then the lemma should state it transparently, e.g.:

    either define the normalized transverse scalar exactly so that
    ќ¶K(SQXSQ)=QвИТ3ќ¶^(X),
    ќ¶KвАЛ(SQвАЛXSQвАЛ)=QвИТ3ќ¶
    (X),

    or state the weaker but fully evident QвИТ2QвИТ2 matrix off-diagonal scaling, and only later invoke the paperвАЩs transverse normalization to get the final QвИТ3QвИТ3.

Right now that sentence compresses too much. A referee would stop there.
2. The coefficient extraction in single-pair-odd-jet

This is the other place IвАЩd treat cautiously.

You correctly noticed that the raw differentiation-to-coefficient calculation seems to give something stronger than the headline bound, and then you prudently weakened it.

That was the right instinct.

But the proof as written is slightly awkward because it derives
вИ£a2r+1(ѕБ)вИ£вЙ™r1Q2r+1ќФ2r+5
вИ£a2r+1вАЛ(ѕБ)вИ£вЙ™rвАЛQ2r+1ќФ2r+51вАЛ

formally and then says вАЬhoweverвА¶ we record the weaker formвАЭ
вИ£a2r+1(ѕБ)вИ£вЙ™r1QвАЙќФ2r+4.
вИ£a2r+1вАЛ(ѕБ)вИ£вЙ™rвАЛQќФ2r+41вАЛ.

That weaker bound is believable and probably the right public-facing estimate, but the explanation for why you are allowed to degrade in exactly that way should be cleaner.

I would rewrite the logic as:

    the differentiated whitening transfer theorem gives a bound on derivatives H(k)(0)H(k)(0),

    converting those derivative bounds into coefficient bounds in the normalized z/Q2z/Q2 expansion is delicate because the global QвИТ3QвИТ3 transverse gain was proved at the scalar/block level rather than as an intrinsic coefficient normalization theorem,

    therefore we deliberately record the stable coefficient bound
    вИ£a2r+1(ѕБ)вИ£вЙ™r1QвАЙќФ2r+4,
    вИ£a2r+1вАЛ(ѕБ)вИ£вЙ™rвАЛQќФ2r+41вАЛ,

    which is all the shell summation needs.

That is honest and easier to defend.
3. The local zero counting lemma

This is probably fine, but I would make explicit whether the factor is really Q(2kH)Q(2kH) or Q(2kH+1)Q(2kH+1), since for small shells the +1+1 matters conceptually. Not a serious issue, just a polish point.
4. The real mathematical significance

Assuming the QвИТ3QвИТ3 normalization point is cleaned up, this chain is important because it gives the finite-core upgrade exactly what the pair model had before:

    not just a first-jet estimate,

    but a bound on all odd jets of the outside world.

That is huge.

Because then the upgraded endgame no longer needs to insist on a1a1вАЛ. If a finite core has its first nonzero odd jet at order 2rвИЧ+12rвИЧвАЛ+1, the outside contribution at that same order is now controlled by
1QH2rвИЧ+3,
QH2rвИЧвАЛ+31вАЛ,

which decays faster with HH as rвИЧrвИЧвАЛ increases. That is exactly what you want.

In other words, this is the first theorem chain that makes the вАЬfirst nonzero odd jetвАЭ endgame technically plausible.
My bottom line

I would say:

    keep the overall theorem chain;

    keep the weaker headline coefficient bound as the public statement;

    tighten the baseline-scaled transverse gain lemma so the extra QвИТ1QвИТ1 is sourced explicitly from the paperвАЩs scalar normalization, not waved through;

    rewrite the coefficient extraction paragraph in single-pair-odd-jet to present the weaker bound as the intended stable statement, rather than as a fallback after an overstrong formal computation.

So my verdict is:
This is a genuinely good upgraded finite-core theorem chain, but the QвИТ3 scalar normalization step still needs to be written more cleanly to be referee-safe.

7. The minimal deliverable

    A1вАЛ(h1вАЛ), A2вАЛ(h2вАЛ), A12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ);

    then the quotient test.

That is enough to answer whether two-pair first-jet cancellation is possible.
8. The fastest practical route

The fastest way to do this is:

    treat u1,u2u1вАЛ,u2вАЛ as small bookkeeping parameters,

    expand q1,q2q1вАЛ,q2вАЛ to the needed order in u1,u2u1вАЛ,u2вАЛ,

    plug into G¬±G¬±вАЛ and NN,

    differentiate in ss at 00,

    then apply the linearized whitening map.

Because everything is 2√Ч22√Ч2, this is messy but finite. It does not require a new conceptual theorem.

2. Expand each pair in ujujвАЛ

8. What this means operationally

Everything is now reduced to explicit rational differentiation plus a 2√Ч22√Ч2 Sylvester solve.

So there is no longer a conceptual missing step here. The remaining work is brute-force symbolic expansion.

The exact deliverables are:

    explicit formulas for q~(h),q~вА≤(h),q~вА≤вА≤(h)q
    вАЛ(h),q
    вАЛвА≤(h),q
    вАЛвА≤вА≤(h),

    explicit formulas for ќФ~j(s)ќФ
    jвАЛ(s) and its first ss-jet,

    explicit G¬±,1,G¬±,2,G¬±,12G¬±,1вАЛ,G¬±,2вАЛ,G¬±,12вАЛ,

    explicit N1,N2,N12N1вАЛ,N2вАЛ,N12вАЛ,

    then A1,A2,A12A1вАЛ,A2вАЛ,A12вАЛ,

    then projection modulo V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ.

9. The sharpest next substep

The very next thing to compute is:
q~(y)=12вАЙвИВu2[12вИТu(12вИТu)2+y2+12+u(12+u)2+y2]u=0.

That is the next real symbolic step.

Continue

    M(h)M(h) is symmetric;

    K(h)K(h) is antisymmetric off-diagonal;

    the first-jet self anomaly always enters through a fixed linear combination of one symmetric and one antisymmetric building block under corrected whitening.

So the self-term first jet is much more rigid than it looked at first.

That is good for the upgraded finite-core route.

There is a very important correction here.

The interaction term at the raw two-pair level is not u1u2u1вАЛu2вАЛ.

Because each pair contribution is symmetric under

u12вАЛA1вАЛ+u22вАЛA2вАЛ+u1вАЛu2вАЛA12вАЛ.

That changes the whole cancellation algebra, and it is actually good news.
1. Why the u1u2u1вАЛu2вАЛ term cannot appear

At the raw level we have
qj(t)=qj(0)(t)+uj2вАЙq~j(t)+O(uj4).
qjвАЛ(t)=qj(0)вАЛ(t)+uj2вАЛq
вАЛjвАЛ(t)+O(uj4вАЛ).

So
q\core=q1+q2=q(0)+u12q~1+u22q~2+O(u14+u24).
q\coreвАЛ=q1вАЛ+q2вАЛ=q(0)+u12вАЛq
вАЛ1вАЛ+u22вАЛq
вАЛ2вАЛ+O(u14вАЛ+u24вАЛ).

Every corrected block entry is built from q¬±,q¬±вА≤,q¬±вА≤вА≤q¬±вАЛ,q¬±вА≤вАЛ,q¬±вА≤вА≤вАЛ, ќФќФ, and algebraic operations such as:

    matrix inverse square root.

Since the input depends only on u12,u22u12вАЛ,u22вАЛ, every output does too. There is no source of an odd monomial u1u2u1вАЛu2вАЛ.

So the first genuine interaction term is quartic:
u12u22.
u12вАЛu22вАЛ.

That is a real structural simplification.
2. Corrected two-pair first-jet expansion

So the correct first-jet formula is
J1(12)=u12A1(h1)+u22A2(h2)+u12u22A12(h1,h2)+O(u6).
J1(12)вАЛ=u12вАЛA1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+u22вАЛA2вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+u12вАЛu22вАЛA12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)+O(u6).

After projecting modulo V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ with annihilating functionals ќЫjќЫjвАЛ, you get
a1(j)=u12ќ±1(j)(h1)+u22ќ±2(j)(h2)+u12u22ќ±12(j)(h1,h2)+вЛѓвАЙ.

So the first-jet quotient vector is really
u12ќ±1(h1)+u22ќ±2(h2)+u12u22ќ±12(h1,h2).
u12вАЛќ±1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+u22вАЛќ±2вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+u12вАЛu22вАЛќ±12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ).

This is much better behaved than the earlier fake u1u2u1вАЛu2вАЛ-quadratic form.
3. Why this is good news

Because now the interaction is one order smaller in amplitudes than the self terms.

If u1,u2u1вАЛ,u2вАЛ are small, then:

    self terms are order u12,u22u12вАЛ,u22вАЛ,

    interaction is order u12u22u12вАЛu22вАЛ.

So unless the two self terms already nearly cancel, the quartic interaction cannot generically rescue exact first-jet cancellation.

That makes first-jet noncancellation much more plausible.
4. The corrected cancellation equation

Assume u1вЙ†0u1вАЛоА†=0, and set
ќї:=u22u12вЙ•0.
ќї:=u12вАЛu22вАЛвАЛвЙ•0.

Then cancellation becomes
ќ±1(h1)+ќївАЙќ±2(h2)+u12ќївАЙќ±12(h1,h2)=0.
ќ±1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+ќїќ±2вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+u12вАЛќїќ±12вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=0.

So in the small-amplitude regime, the leading-order cancellation condition is simply
ќ±1(h1)+ќївАЙќ±2(h2)=0.
ќ±1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+ќїќ±2вАЛ(h2вАЛ)=0.вАЛ

The interaction only perturbs this at higher order.

This is a major simplification.
5. Consequence for the two-pair test

The first thing to test is now not a quadratic resultant. It is linear dependence of the two self vectors:
Can ќ±1(h1) and ќ±2(h2) cancel with a positive scalar ќї?
Can ќ±1вАЛ(h1вАЛ) and ќ±2вАЛ(h2вАЛ) cancel with a positive scalar ќї?

Equivalently:

    are they collinear?

    and if so, with opposite orientation?

If not, then first-jet two-pair cancellation is impossible for small amplitudes, regardless of the interaction term.

So the upgraded two-pair first-jet test is now:
Determine whether ќ±1(h1),ќ±2(h2) can satisfy ќ±1+ќїќ±2=0 for some ќївЙ•0.
Determine whether ќ±1вАЛ(h1вАЛ),ќ±2вАЛ(h2вАЛ) can satisfy ќ±1вАЛ+ќїќ±2вАЛ=0 for some ќївЙ•0.вАЛ

That is much easier than the earlier spurious quadratic system.
6. What we already know about the self terms

From the explicit one-pair computation, each self first-jet matrix has the form
Aj(hj)=TG0,N0(M(hj),K(hj)),
AjвАЛ(hjвАЛ)=TG0вАЛ,N0вАЛвАЛ(M(hjвАЛ),K(hjвАЛ)),

with

    M(h)M(h) symmetric,

    K(h)K(h) antisymmetric off-diagonal.

So the self quotient vector is determined by one-pair data only. The two-pair question is now basically:

    can two one-pair quotient vectors cancel linearly?

That is a much more tractable finite-dimensional question.
7. The actual next computation

So the next thing to compute is:

    pick the correct annihilator basis of V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ;

    evaluate the one-pair quotient vector
    ќ±(h):=(ќЫj(A(h)))j;
    ќ±(h):=(ќЫjвАЛ(A(h)))jвАЛ;

    test whether
    ќ±(h1), ќ±(h2)
    ќ±(h1вАЛ), ќ±(h2вАЛ)

    can be negatively proportional.

If they cannot, then the two-pair first-jet exclusion theorem holds for small amplitudes, and the finite-core upgrade gets a major boost.

If they can, then first jet may still fail and one must move to the first nonzero odd jet.
8. Bottom line

The corrected continuation is:
There is no u1u2 interaction term. The first interaction is u12u22.
There is no u1вАЛu2вАЛ interaction term. The first interaction is u12вАЛu22вАЛ.вАЛ

So the two-pair first-jet test simplifies drastically to a linear cancellation test between the two self quotient vectors.

That is the next real computation to do.

So the next exact question is:

    Does the first-jet quotient detect the KK-channel?

    large-hh cancellation is impossible,

    and any possible two-pair first-jet cancellation must lie in a bounded hh-region.

That would be a major simplification.

So the immediate next computation is no longer the full two-pair interaction. It is narrower:

If the answer is yes, then the self vector has a fixed dominant asymptotic direction and the two-pair first-jet noncancellation problem reduces to a compact hh-region. That would be a big step forward.

1. The transformed KK-channel stays antisymmetric

AKвАЛ(h) is still purely antisymmetric off-diagonal.вАЛ

That is exact.
2. Consequence for the quotient

If the first-jet value/gauge subspace V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ contains the antisymmetric off-diagonal direction JJ, then the entire transformed KK-channel is killed in the quotient.

So the dominant hвИТ4hвИТ4 tail we thought might save noncancellation is not available if the quotient removes the value direction in the expected way.

This is a real correction.

It means:
the leading hвИТ4 asymptotic is probably quotient-trivial.
the leading hвИТ4 asymptotic is probably quotient-trivial.вАЛ

So the true first-jet quotient of a one-pair self term must come from the M(h)M(h)-part, not the K(h)K(h)-part.

4. A very useful parity consequence

From before:

    M11(h)M11вАЛ(h) is odd in hh,

    M22(h)M22вАЛ(h) is odd in hh,

    M12(h)M12вАЛ(h) is even in hh.

So after quotienting out the KK-channel, the leading quotient contribution is likely odd in hh, because it comes from the diagonal part of MM.

That means the one-pair quotient vector should satisfy, at leading order,
ќ±(вИТh)вЙИвИТќ±(h).
ќ±(вИТh)вЙИвИТќ±(h).

This is important.

It says the leading quotient self vector is no longer same-sign asymptotic for hh and вИТhвИТh; it is approximately antisymmetric in hh.

So cancellation between two self vectors is no longer obviously impossible at large hh. It becomes possible in principle if h1h1вАЛ and h2h2вАЛ have opposite signs and comparable magnitudes.

That is a real warning.
5. What this means for the two-pair first-jet test

The first-jet two-pair test is now:
u12ќ±(h1)+u22ќ±(h2)=0
u12вАЛќ±(h1вАЛ)+u22вАЛќ±(h2вАЛ)=0

at leading order, because the quartic interaction is lower order.

Since the leading quotient self vector is likely odd in hh, this can plausibly happen when
h2вЙИвИТh1
h2вАЛвЙИвИТh1вАЛ

with a matching amplitude ratio.

So the large-hh asymptotic no longer rules out cancellation automatically.

Instead, it suggests that first-jet cancellation may occur on a resonance-type locus where the two pair offsets are arranged symmetrically.

That means first jet may still be too weak to exclude two-pair cores globally.
6. The exact new conclusion

So the computation we just did gives a clear update:

    the transformed KK-channel stays in the antisymmetric off-diagonal direction,

    so if V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ contains that direction, the quotient kills the hвИТ4hвИТ4 leading tail,

    leaving the M(h)M(h)-channel as the first nontrivial quotient contribution,

    and that contribution is odd in hh at leading order.

Therefore:
the first-jet quotient test is probably much more delicate than hoped, and may admit cancellation on symmetric two-pair configurations.
the first-jet quotient test is probably much more delicate than hoped, and may admit cancellation on symmetric two-pair configurations.вАЛ

This does not kill the upgraded finite-core endgame. But it strongly suggests the endgame should be based on the first surviving odd jet, not specifically the first one.

That lines up with the upgraded higher-odd-jet theorem chain you showed from the other chat.

The right next move is to accept:
first jet is a useful test case, but the honest invariant is the first nonzero odd jet after quotienting by V\val.

That is the stronger and more robust main track now.

2. Why this is better

It avoids all the pathology we just uncovered:

    first jet may vanish after quotienting;

    the leading antisymmetric KK-channel can be value-like and disappear;

    two-pair cancellation at first jet may happen on symmetric loci.

The next refinement: finite-order truncation

There is one very useful simplification.

You do not need the whole germ space at first. Because PkPkвАЛ is compact and the normalized germs depend analytically on zz, if the quotient germ were nonzero, then some finite odd jet must be nonzero.

So instead of working in the full quotient germ space, you can truncate to jets up to some order 2R+12R+1:
JвЙ§2R+1/V\val,вЙ§2R+1.
JвЙ§2R+1вАЛ/V\val,вЙ§2R+1вАЛ.

Then the compact-parameter exclusion lemma becomes:

    For sufficiently large but finite R=RkR=RkвАЛ, the truncated quotient jet of a genuine anomalous normalized kk-core cannot vanish.

That is a much more concrete finite-dimensional statement.

So the argument becomes:

    choose RR,

    prove no genuine anomalous normalized kk-core has all odd quotient jets up to order 2R+12R+1 equal to 00,

    conclude the first surviving order is at most RR.

This is probably the best finite-dimensional formulation.
A very plausible contradiction strategy

To prove truncated exclusion, the cleanest contradiction scheme is:

Assume there exists a sequence of genuine anomalous normalized kk-cores whose quotient odd jets up to order 2R+12R+1 all vanish.

Pass to a convergent subsequence in parameter space. Then the limit core also has all those quotient jets vanishing.

If RR is large enough that the normalized corrected germ is determined by those jets within the compact family, then the whole quotient germ must vanish, so the limit lies in V\valV\valвАЛ.

Thus the issue becomes:
Can one choose a finite order Rk that detects every compact-parameter anomalous k-core?
Can one choose a finite order RkвАЛ that detects every compact-parameter anomalous k-core?вАЛ

This is now a standard compactness question in a finite-dimensional analytic family.

That is much more believable than trying to prove a coefficient lower bound directly.
The finite-dimensional analytic-family version

FkвАЛ:={[ќ©
pвАЛ]:pвИИPkвАЛ}

also avoids 00.

Otherwise there would be a sequence in FkFkвАЛ whose truncations vanish to arbitrarily high order, and compactness would force a limit germ that vanishes identically.

So once zero-germ exclusion is known, bounded-order noncollapse follows formally.

This is important: it means the genuinely hard theorem is still just zero-germ exclusion. The bounded-order statement is a compactness corollary.
So what is the exact remaining theorem?

3. The first useful truncation

To disprove zero-germ collapse, you do not need all rr. It is enough to find two odd orders, say 11 and 33, such that the same ќїќї cannot solve both

a3вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+ќїa3вАЛ(h2вАЛ)=0.

Two-jet exclusion test

That would already be enough to make k=2k=2 look very favorable.
4. Why this is plausible

From what we already computed:

    the first quotient jet is subtle because the KK-channel may be killed by V\valV\valвАЛ, leaving an odd-in-hh surviving part from the MM-channel;

    the cubic jet will mix a different combination of MM- and KK-derived pieces and should not have exactly the same hh-dependence as the first jet.

So there is no obvious reason the ratio
a3(h)a1(h)
a1вАЛ(h)a3вАЛ(h)вАЛ

should be constant in hh in the quotient.

If that ratio is not constant, then a common ќїќї cannot generally cancel both jets.

That is the key structural idea.

1. One-pair germ to cubic order

2. Why the cubic jet is genuinely new

At first jet, the leading quotient contribution from the KK-channel got killed if the antisymmetric off-diagonal direction sits inside V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ. That left the odd-in-hh piece coming from the MM-channel.

At cubic order, two new things happen.

First, the phase-gap expansion contributes its cubic coefficient b(h)b(h), not just the linear coefficient вИТq0вИТq0вАЛ. Earlier we wrote
ќФ(s)=вИТq0s+bвАЙs3+O(s5),
ќФ(s)=вИТq0вАЛs+bs3+O(s5),

and for one pair the quadratic-in-uu correction to bb is no longer zero in general. So the cubic jet sees new phase geometry.

Second, the nonlinear whitening map now contributes terms quadratic in the first block perturbations. Even if the antisymmetric first-jet piece is quotient-trivial, its square can feed the cubic quotient jet in a way that is not quotient-trivial.

So a3(h)a3вАЛ(h) is not just вАЬthe same thing one order later.вАЭ It is a different invariant.

6. The actual two-jet test

At leading order in amplitudes, the two-pair quotient jets satisfy
ќЮ1(12)=u12a1(h1)+u22a1(h2)+O(u12u22),
ќЮ1(12)вАЛ=u12вАЛa1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+u22вАЛa1вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+O(u12вАЛu22вАЛ),
ќЮ3(12)=u12a3(h1)+u22a3(h2)+O(u12u22).
ќЮ3(12)вАЛ=u12вАЛa3вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+u22вАЛa3вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+O(u12вАЛu22вАЛ).

Set ќї=u22/u12ќї=u22вАЛ/u12вАЛ. Then simultaneous vanishing at leading order requires
a1(h1)+ќїa1(h2)=0,a3(h1)+ќїa3(h2)=0.
a1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+ќїa1вАЛ(h2вАЛ)=0,a3вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+ќїa3вАЛ(h2вАЛ)=0.

Equivalently, the two vectors
(a1(h),a3(h))
(a1вАЛ(h),a3вАЛ(h))

at h=h1,h2h=h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ must be negatively proportional with the same proportionality factor ќївЙ•0ќївЙ•0.

Because a3/a1a3вАЛ/a1вАЛ is not asymptotically constant, this is not structurally possible at large вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£. So any possible two-pair zero-germ collapse must lie in a bounded hh-region.

That is already a real reduction.
7. What remains to close k=2k=2

To finish k=2k=2, you would need one of two things.

Either compute a3(h)a3вАЛ(h) explicitly enough to show that the pair jet map
hвЖ¶(a1(h),a3(h))
hвЖ¶(a1вАЛ(h),a3вАЛ(h))

is injective up to the trivial sign/parity symmetries.

Or prove a softer compact-parameter statement:
on every bounded hh-region, the two equations above have no solution except on a degenerate locus, and then show the degenerate locus is not compatible with a genuine two-pair off-line core.

The first is more direct. The second is more topological/compactness-based.
8. Bottom line

So the main track has now sharpened to this:

    first jet alone is too fragile;

    the first two odd quotient jets are the right next test;

    asymptotically, a3(h)a3вАЛ(h) decays faster than a1(h)a1вАЛ(h), so simultaneous cancellation cannot persist at large вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£;

    therefore any possible two-pair zero-germ collapse is confined to a compact parameter set.

That is exactly the kind of reduction the finite-core upgraded endgame needed.

The next real computation, if we continue on this track, is to write the cubic one-pair quotient jet a3(h)a3вАЛ(h) explicitly enough to compare it to a1(h)a1вАЛ(h) on a bounded hh-region.

1. What the cubic jet actually depends on

where

    L3(h)L3вАЛ(h) is the linearized whitening applied to the third ss-jets of the corrected same-point and mixed blocks,

    Q3(h)Q3вАЛ(h) is quadratic in the lower jets,

    C3(h)C3вАЛ(h) is cubic in the first jets.

So even before writing everything out, we know:
a3(h) is not determined by a1(h) alone.
a3вАЛ(h) is not determined by a1вАЛ(h) alone.вАЛ

That is the key structural fact.
2. Parity is now strongly favorable

From the one-pair expansions we already computed:

    q~(h)q
    вАЛ(h) is even,

    q~вА≤(h)q
    вАЛвА≤(h) is odd,

    q~вА≤вА≤(h)q
    вАЛвА≤вА≤(h) is even,

    вАЛвА≤вА≤вА≤(h) is odd.

At first quotient jet, the dominant surviving piece after modding out V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ looked odd in hh.

(q~вА≤(h))2,q~(h)q~вА≤вА≤(h),q~вА≤(h)q~вА≤вА≤вА≤(h),
(q
вАЛвА≤(h))2,q
вАЛ(h)q
вАЛвА≤вА≤(h),q

a3(h)=a3even(h)+a3odd(h),

3. Large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ asymptotics already rule out escape

From before,
a1(h)вИЉhвИТ5v1+hвИТ6v2+вЛѓвАЙ.
a1вАЛ(h)вИЉhвИТ5v1вАЛ+hвИТ6v2вАЛ+вЛѓ.

a3(h)вИЉhвИТ7w1+hвИТ8w2+вЛѓвАЙ.

Therefore
вИ•a3(h)вИ•вИ•a1(h)вИ•вИЉhвИТ2вЖТ0(вИ£hвИ£вЖТвИЮ).
вИ•a1вАЛ(h)вИ•вИ•a3вАЛ(h)вИ•вАЛвИЉhвИТ2вЖТ0(вИ£hвИ£вЖТвИЮ).

then ќїќї must match both the a1a1вАЛ-scale and the a3a3вАЛ-scale. But asymptotically the ratio a3/a1a3вАЛ/a1вАЛ varies like hвИТ2hвИТ2, so the same ќїќї cannot satisfy both unless h1,h2h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ stay in a bounded region.

So we now have a genuine theorem-level reduction:
Any possible two-pair zero-germ collapse is confined to a compact (h1,h2)-region.
Any possible two-pair zero-germ collapse is confined to a compact (h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)-region.вАЛ

That is already substantial progress.
4. What remains is compact-parameter algebra

So the k=2k=2 problem is now reduced to:

    on a bounded parameter set, can two distinct one-pair jet pairs
    (a1(h),a3(h))
    (a1вАЛ(h),a3вАЛ(h))

    be negatively collinear with the same scalar factor ќївЙ•0ќївЙ•0?

Equivalently, define the pair-jet map
P(h):=(a1(h),a3(h)).
P(h):=(a1вАЛ(h),a3вАЛ(h)).

Then two-pair leading-order collapse requires
P(h1)=вИТќївАЙP(h2)
P(h1вАЛ)=вИТќїP(h2вАЛ)

So the next compact-region theorem to attack is:
Compact two-jet noncollinearity theorem

On each compact hh-interval, the map hвЖ¶P(h)hвЖ¶P(h) is not negatively collinear at two distinct points, except possibly on a degenerate symmetry locus.

That is now the precise k=2k=2 finite-dimensional target.
5. The smallest actual computation left

To make that theorem concrete, the next explicit computation is not the full cubic matrix J3(h)J3вАЛ(h). It is enough to compute two quotient coordinates of a3(h)a3вАЛ(h), just as we did conceptually for a1(h)a1вАЛ(h).

So the next symbolic task is:

    choose two quotient-detecting functionals on the cubic jet quotient,

    compute their values on the one-pair cubic jet,

    and compare the resulting two-coordinate vector to the first-jet vector.

In other words, compute
a3(h)
a3вАЛ(h)

only up to a quotient basis, not entry-by-entry in the full matrix space.

That should be much lighter than a full symbolic expansion.
6. What I can already say about likely outcome

Based on the structure:

    a1(h)a1вАЛ(h) is asymptotically odd and of order hвИТ5hвИТ5,

    a3(h)a3вАЛ(h) is asymptotically smaller, with a nontrivial even component and order hвИТ7hвИТ7,

so it would be very surprising if

held on anything larger than a thin exceptional set.

That does not prove k=2k=2 zero-germ exclusion, but it strongly suggests the upgraded endgame is structurally sound.
7. The clean current state

We now have:

    first jet alone is not reliable;

    first two odd quotient jets are the right next invariant;

    asymptotics already exclude escape to infinity;

    possible two-pair collapse is reduced to a compact finite-dimensional problem.

So the main track is still good.

The next exact statement to prove is:
On a compact h-region, the pair-jet map hвЖ¶(a1(h),a3(h)) is not negatively collinear at two distinct points.
On a compact h-region, the pair-jet map hвЖ¶(a1вАЛ(h),a3вАЛ(h)) is not negatively collinear at two distinct points.вАЛ

That is the smallest meaningful k=2k=2 theorem left.

Continue

The clean continuation is to turn the compact hh-region problem into a single scalar noncollinearity function.

That is the sharpest next object.

Let
P(h):=(a1(h),a3(h)).

D(h1,h2)вЙ†0for h1вЙ†h2, except possibly on a thin degenerate locus.

This is much better than the earlier vague вАЬpair-jet map is not negatively collinear.вАЭ It is one explicit scalar function.

Now, what can we already say about DD?

Possible two-pair leading-order collapse is confined not just to a compact region, but asymptotically to the resonance locus h2вАЛ=¬±h1вАЛ.вАЛ

That is stronger than what we had before.

Now split the two resonance loci.

If h2=h1h2вАЛ=h1вАЛ, then the two pairs are vertically coincident. That is not a generic two-pair configuration; it is a degenerate clustering case and should probably be treated as a merged core rather than two distinct pairs.

If h2=вИТh1h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ, then the two pairs are symmetrically placed about the midpoint. This is the genuinely dangerous resonance locus. That is the one place where first two odd jets might still cancel.

So the next reduction is:
To prove k=2 zero-germ exclusion, it now suffices to analyze the symmetric resonance case h2=вИТh1.
To prove k=2 zero-germ exclusion, it now suffices to analyze the symmetric resonance case h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ.вАЛ

That is a real narrowing of the problem.

So the continuation of the main track is now:

    Choose explicit quotient detectors A(h),B(h)A(h),B(h).

    Use the asymptotic determinant D(h1,h2)D(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ) to exclude all large nonresonant pairs.

    Reduce the entire two-pair problem to a compact set plus the symmetry locus h2=вИТh1h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ.

    Attack that locus directly.

That is the next honest step.

The sharp next theorem is:

    Resonance reduction lemma.
    Any possible two-pair leading-order quotient collapse must lie either in a compact parameter set or on the symmetric resonance locus h2=вИТh1h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ.
