Ok lets prove it

Yes. We can prove the resonance reduction lemma in the form the current machinery actually supports.
Statement

Let
A(h):=ќЫ(1)(a1(h)),B(h):=ќЫ(3)(a3(h)),
A(h):=ќЫ(1)(a1вАЛ(h)),B(h):=ќЫ(3)(a3вАЛ(h)),

where ќЫ(1),ќЫ(3)ќЫ(1),ќЫ(3) are quotient-detecting linear functionals chosen so that the leading asymptotic coefficients of A,BA,B are nonzero. Define
D(h1,h2):=A(h1)B(h2)вИТA(h2)B(h1).
D(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ):=A(h1вАЛ)B(h2вАЛ)вИТA(h2вАЛ)B(h1вАЛ).

Assume the one-pair quotient jets have large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ asymptotics
A(h)=c1hвИТ5+O(hвИТ6),B(h)=c3hвИТ7+O(hвИТ8),
A(h)=c1вАЛhвИТ5+O(hвИТ6),B(h)=c3вАЛhвИТ7+O(hвИТ8),

with c1c3вЙ†0c1вАЛc3вАЛоА†=0.

Then there exists H0>0H0вАЛ>0 such that whenever вИ£h1вИ£,вИ£h2вИ£вЙ•H0вИ£h1вАЛвИ£,вИ£h2вАЛвИ£вЙ•H0вАЛ and вИ£h1вИ£вЙ†вИ£h2вИ£вИ£h1вАЛвИ£оА†=вИ£h2вАЛвИ£, one has
D(h1,h2)вЙ†0.
D(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)оА†=0.

Consequently, any large-parameter two-pair leading-order collapse can only occur on the resonance locus
вИ£h1вИ£=вИ£h2вИ£,
вИ£h1вАЛвИ£=вИ£h2вАЛвИ£,

that is, asymptotically on h2=¬±h1h2вАЛ=¬±h1вАЛ.

That is the useful version.
Proof

Start from the asymptotics:
A(h)=c1hвИТ5+r1(h),r1(h)=O(hвИТ6),
A(h)=c1вАЛhвИТ5+r1вАЛ(h),r1вАЛ(h)=O(hвИТ6),
B(h)=c3hвИТ7+r3(h),r3(h)=O(hвИТ8).
B(h)=c3вАЛhвИТ7+r3вАЛ(h),r3вАЛ(h)=O(hвИТ8).

Then
D(h1,h2)=(c1h1вИТ5+r1(h1))(c3h2вИТ7+r3(h2))вИТ(c1h2вИТ5+r1(h2))(c3h1вИТ7+r3(h1)).
D(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=(c1вАЛh1вИТ5вАЛ+r1вАЛ(h1вАЛ))(c3вАЛh2вИТ7вАЛ+r3вАЛ(h2вАЛ))вИТ(c1вАЛh2вИТ5вАЛ+r1вАЛ(h2вАЛ))(c3вАЛh1вИТ7вАЛ+r3вАЛ(h1вАЛ)).

Expand the leading terms:
D(h1,h2)=c1c3(h1вИТ5h2вИТ7вИТh2вИТ5h1вИТ7)+E(h1,h2),
D(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=c1вАЛc3вАЛ(h1вИТ5вАЛh2вИТ7вАЛвИТh2вИТ5вАЛh1вИТ7вАЛ)+E(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ),

where the error satisfies
E(h1,h2)=O(h1вИТ6h2вИТ7)+O(h1вИТ5h2вИТ8)+O(h2вИТ6h1вИТ7)+O(h2вИТ5h1вИТ8).
E(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=O(h1вИТ6вАЛh2вИТ7вАЛ)+O(h1вИТ5вАЛh2вИТ8вАЛ)+O(h2вИТ6вАЛh1вИТ7вАЛ)+O(h2вИТ5вАЛh1вИТ8вАЛ).

Factor the main term:
h1вИТ5h2вИТ7вИТh2вИТ5h1вИТ7=h1вИТ7h2вИТ7(h12вИТh22).
h1вИТ5вАЛh2вИТ7вАЛвИТh2вИТ5вАЛh1вИТ7вАЛ=h1вИТ7вАЛh2вИТ7вАЛ(h12вАЛвИТh22вАЛ).

So
D(h1,h2)=c1c3вАЙh1вИТ7h2вИТ7(h12вИТh22)+E(h1,h2).
D(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=c1вАЛc3вАЛh1вИТ7вАЛh2вИТ7вАЛ(h12вАЛвИТh22вАЛ)+E(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ).

Now fix H0H0вАЛ large enough that for вИ£h1вИ£,вИ£h2вИ£вЙ•H0вИ£h1вАЛвИ£,вИ£h2вАЛвИ£вЙ•H0вАЛ,
вИ£E(h1,h2)вИ£вЙ§вИ£c1c3вИ£2вАЙвИ£h1вИТ7h2вИТ7(h12вИТh22)вИ£
вИ£E(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)вИ£вЙ§2вИ£c1вАЛc3вАЛвИ£вАЛвИ£h1вИТ7вАЛh2вИТ7вАЛ(h12вАЛвИТh22вАЛ)вИ£

whenever вИ£h1вИ£вЙ†вИ£h2вИ£вИ£h1вАЛвИ£оА†=вИ£h2вАЛвИ£.

Then for such h1,h2h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ,
вИ£D(h1,h2)вИ£вЙ•вИ£c1c3вИ£2вАЙвИ£h1вИТ7h2вИТ7(h12вИТh22)вИ£.
вИ£D(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)вИ£вЙ•2вИ£c1вАЛc3вАЛвИ£вАЛвИ£h1вИТ7вАЛh2вИТ7вАЛ(h12вАЛвИТh22вАЛ)вИ£.

If вИ£h1вИ£вЙ†вИ£h2вИ£вИ£h1вАЛвИ£оА†=вИ£h2вАЛвИ£, the right-hand side is nonzero, hence
D(h1,h2)вЙ†0.
D(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)оА†=0.

So asymptotic cancellation is only possible when
h12вИТh22=0,
h12вАЛвИТh22вАЛ=0,

i.e.
h2=¬±h1.
h2вАЛ=¬±h1вАЛ.

вИО
What this gives

This proves the reduction you want:
Outside a compact region, two-pair leading-order collapse can only occur on the resonance locus h2=¬±h1.
Outside a compact region, two-pair leading-order collapse can only occur on the resonance locus h2вАЛ=¬±h1вАЛ.вАЛ

More precisely:

    nonresonant large pairs are excluded by the asymptotic determinant;

    only the diagonal h2=h1h2вАЛ=h1вАЛ and symmetric h2=вИТh1h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ remain asymptotically dangerous.

How this fits the finite-core track

This is enough to narrow the k=2k=2 zero-germ problem to two cases:

    a compact parameter region;

    the resonance locus h2=¬±h1h2вАЛ=¬±h1вАЛ.

And among those, h2=h1h2вАЛ=h1вАЛ is basically a coincident/degenerate clustering case. The real new case is
h2=вИТh1.
h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ.

So the next theorem to attack is the symmetric-resonance exclusion:

    a genuinely symmetric two-pair off-line core cannot have all quotient odd jets vanish.

That is now the sharp next target.

There is one caveat I should keep explicit: this proof assumes we really do have detectors A,BA,B with nonzero leading asymptotic coefficients c1,c3c1вАЛ,c3вАЛ. That matches the current asymptotic picture, but the exact choice of quotient-detecting functionals still needs to be frozen carefully when writing this into the paper.

Do we freeze that now, or continue the attack?

Freeze it now.

Not the entire endgame, but the choice of quotient detectors A,BA,B and the exact asymptotic normalization they use. That is the right move before pushing the resonance attack further.

The reason is simple: the resonance reduction lemma you just proved already depends on having
A(h)=c1hвИТ5+O(hвИТ6),B(h)=c3hвИТ7+O(hвИТ8),
A(h)=c1вАЛhвИТ5+O(hвИТ6),B(h)=c3вАЛhвИТ7+O(hвИТ8),

with c1c3вЙ†0c1вАЛc3вАЛоА†=0. Until those detectors are frozen, the resonance argument is still partly schematic.

So I would do this in two steps.

First, freeze a minimal quotient basis at the first and cubic odd orders:

    one detector AA for the first surviving quotient contribution,

    one detector BB for the cubic quotient contribution,
    chosen from the annihilator of V\valV\valвАЛ, with the explicit requirement that their leading asymptotic constants are nonzero.

Second, once those are fixed, continue the attack on the symmetric resonance case
h2=вИТh1.
h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ.

That is the right order. If you keep attacking without freezing, you risk proving things that depend on an unstable choice of detectors. If you freeze too much, you waste time overformalizing the whole quotient apparatus before testing the real obstruction.

So the right answer is:
Freeze the two detectors now, then continue the attack immediately.
Freeze the two detectors now, then continue the attack immediately.вАЛ

What I would freeze exactly is:
A(h):=ќЫ(1)(a1(h)),B(h):=ќЫ(3)(a3(h)),
A(h):=ќЫ(1)(a1вАЛ(h)),B(h):=ќЫ(3)(a3вАЛ(h)),

where ќЫ(1)ќЫ(1) and ќЫ(3)ќЫ(3) are norm-one quotient functionals chosen so that
A(h)вИЉc1hвИТ5,B(h)вИЉc3hвИТ7,c1c3вЙ†0.
A(h)вИЉc1вАЛhвИТ5,B(h)вИЉc3вАЛhвИТ7,c1вАЛc3вАЛоА†=0.

That is enough structure for the resonance lemma and the next symmetric-case attack, without prematurely hard-coding the whole finite-core scalar machinery.

The next move after freezing is then very clear:
attack the resonance locus h2=вИТh1 using the first two frozen quotient jets.
attack the resonance locus h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ using the first two frozen quotient jets.вАЛ

Ok do the freezing

Here is the freezing in the strongest honest form we can support now.

We will freeze two quotient detectors, one at first odd order and one at cubic odd order, by choosing them from the annihilator of the value/gauge subspace and normalizing them by their large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ asymptotics.
1. Freeze the quotient spaces

Let
J1,J3
J1вАЛ,J3вАЛ

be the first and cubic odd jet spaces of corrected local germs, and let
V\val(1)вКВJ1,V\val(3)вКВJ3
V\val(1)вАЛвКВJ1вАЛ,V\val(3)вАЛвКВJ3вАЛ

be the corresponding value/gauge subspaces.

For a one-pair core at scaled offset hh, write its quotient jets as
a1(h)вИИJ1/V\val(1),a3(h)вИИJ3/V\val(3).

4. Resonance exclusion lemma

Here is the precise statement.
Lemma

Assume the frozen detectors satisfy
A(h)=a5hвИТ5+O(hвИТ6),a5вЙ†0,
A(h)=a5вАЛhвИТ5+O(hвИТ6),a5вАЛоА†=0,

with leading odd parity, and
B(h)=bќљhвИТќљ+O(hвИТќљвИТ1),bќљвЙ†0,
B(h)=bќљвАЛhвИТќљ+O(hвИТќљвИТ1),bќљвАЛоА†=0,

with leading even parity. Then there exists H1>0H1вАЛ>0 such that for вИ£hвИ£вЙ•H1вИ£hвИ£вЙ•H1вАЛ, the symmetric resonance system
A(h)+ќїA(вИТh)=0,B(h)+ќїB(вИТh)=0,ќївЙ•0,
A(h)+ќїA(вИТh)=0,B(h)+ќїB(вИТh)=0,ќївЙ•0,

has no solution.
Proof

From the first equation, as above,
ќї=1+O(hвИТ1).
ќї=1+O(hвИТ1).

Since BB has leading even parity,
B(вИТh)=B(h)+O(hвИТќљвИТ1).
B(вИТh)=B(h)+O(hвИТќљвИТ1).

Therefore
B(h)+ќїB(вИТh)=(1+ќї)bќљhвИТќљ+O(hвИТќљвИТ1).
B(h)+ќїB(вИТh)=(1+ќї)bќљвАЛhвИТќљ+O(hвИТќљвИТ1).

Using ќї=1+O(hвИТ1)ќї=1+O(hвИТ1),
1+ќї=2+O(hвИТ1),
1+ќї=2+O(hвИТ1),

so
B(h)+ќїB(вИТh)=2bќљhвИТќљ+O(hвИТќљвИТ1),
B(h)+ќїB(вИТh)=2bќљвАЛhвИТќљ+O(hвИТќљвИТ1),

which is nonzero for sufficiently large вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ because bќљвЙ†0bќљвАЛоА†=0. Contradiction. вИО

This is the clean resonance-killing mechanism.
5. What this means

So the entire large-parameter k=2k=2 problem now reduces to one very specific question:
Can we choose the frozen cubic detector B so that its leading quotient asymptotic is even and nonzero?
Can we choose the frozen cubic detector B so that its leading quotient asymptotic is even and nonzero?вАЛ

If yes, then:

    nonresonant large pairs were already excluded by the determinant argument;

    symmetric resonance h2=вИТh1h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ is now excluded by parity;

    therefore all possible two-pair collapse is confined to a compact parameter set.

That would be a major step.
6. Why this is plausible

This matches the structural picture we already had:

    AA comes from the first surviving quotient self jet and is dominated by the odd MM-channel;

    BB comes from cubic jet contributions, and those include quadratic terms like
    (q~вА≤(h))2,q~(h)q~вА≤вА≤(h),
    (q
    вАЛвА≤(h))2,q
    вАЛ(h)q
    вАЛвА≤вА≤(h),

    which are even in hh.

So it is very plausible that the cubic quotient has a nonzero even component, and that we can freeze BB to detect it.

That is exactly the kind of detector we should now choose.
7. The next move

So the next step is not broad anymore. It is this:
Refine the frozen cubic detector B to detect the leading even quotient component of a3(h).

That is exactly what makes the resonance attack work.
1. Refine the cubic asymptotic decomposition

3. Why this is plausible

This only needs two facts:

    the cubic quotient space is at least two-dimensional at the asymptotic level, so odd and even components are not forced into the same line;

    the even cubic quotient component is nonzero.

The first is plausible because the cubic jet receives qualitatively different contributions from linear and quadratic terms.

The second is also plausible because even contributions arise from nonlinear combinations that are not obviously value-like after quotienting. In particular, the squared antisymmetric channel K2K2 and mixed products like q~вАЙq~вА≤вА≤q
вАЛq

XвАЙK(h)вАЙX,X=G0вИТ1/2.

Favorable case

    Nonlinear same-point cubic lemma.

That is the sharp replacement for the failed bb-channel idea.
6. What I can honestly say now

We have not proved that lemma yet.

But the structure is now much cleaner:

    linear mixed-block cubic terms are not enough;

    nonlinear same-point whitening is the right source;

    if that source survives in the quotient, the large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ symmetric resonance case is dead.

So the main track is still alive. The next real computation is to write the X1,X2X1вАЛ,X2вАЛ Sylvester-expansion explicitly and isolate the even-in-hh part of
X1N0X2+X2N0X1.
X1вАЛN0вАЛX2вАЛ+X2вАЛN0вАЛX1вАЛ.

That is the next place to attack.

Continue

1. The coefficient equations for X1,X2,X3X1вАЛ,X2вАЛ,X3вАЛ

That is the exact formal expansion.
2. Parity of M1,M2,M3M1вАЛ,M2вАЛ,M3вАЛ

From the one-pair same-point expansion we already established:

    M1(h)M1вАЛ(h) is built from first ss-jets, and its entries have mixed parity:
    diagonal part odd in hh, symmetric off-diagonal part even in hh.

    M2(h)M2вАЛ(h) comes from second ss-jets, so its parity is the opposite pattern:
    diagonal even, symmetric off-diagonal odd.

    M3(h)M3вАЛ(h) flips back again:
    diagonal odd, symmetric off-diagonal even.

The only fact we really need is:
M1 has a nontrivial odd-in-h part and a nontrivial even-in-h part.
M1вАЛ has a nontrivial odd-in-h part and a nontrivial even-in-h part.вАЛ

Because LL is linear and hh-independent, the same is true of X1=L(M1)X1вАЛ=L(M1вАЛ).

Now look at X2X2вАЛ. It contains terms quadratic in M1M1вАЛ, namely
L(X1G0X1+X1M1X0+X0M1X1),
L(X1вАЛG0вАЛX1вАЛ+X1вАЛM1вАЛX0вАЛ+X0вАЛM1вАЛX1вАЛ),

and those are automatically even in hh.

So:
X2 has a genuine even-in-h component coming from the quadratic M1-channel.
X2вАЛ has a genuine even-in-h component coming from the quadratic M1вАЛ-channel.вАЛ

That is the structural fact we needed.
3. Where the cubic odd term in WW comes from

Now expand

Among these, the cleanest same-point nonlinear contribution is

    therefore X2N0X1вИТX1N0X2X2вАЛN0вАЛX1вАЛвИТX1вАЛN0вАЛX2вАЛ is odd in ss but even in hh.

4. Parity check of the candidate term

Let
X1(h)=X1,odd(h)+X1,even(h),X2(h)=X2,even(h)+X2,odd(h).

C(вИТh)=C(h)

So:
C(h) is the right candidate for a nonzero even cubic quotient contribution.
C(h) is the right candidate for a nonzero even cubic quotient contribution.вАЛ
5. Why this is better than the failed bb-channel

The bb-channel gave an antisymmetric off-diagonal odd-ss contribution, which is likely value-like and quotient-trivial.

By contrast, C(h)C(h) is built from same-point whitening and is not forced into that antisymmetric off-diagonal line. It typically lands in a diagonal/symmetric sector after the commutator with N0N0вАЛ.

So it is much more plausible that its quotient class is nonzero.
6. What is now the exact next lemma

The sharp next target is:

    Nonlinear same-point even-cubic lemma.
    The quotient class of
    C(h)=X2[2](h)N0X1(h)вИТX1(h)N0X2[2](h)
    C(h)=X2[2]вАЛ(h)N0вАЛX1вАЛ(h)вИТX1вАЛ(h)N0вАЛX2[2]вАЛ(h)

    is nonzero for large вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£, and its leading asymptotic is even in hh.

7. What we already get conditionally

    A(h)A(h) frozen from the first quotient jet with odd leading parity:

    the linear mixed-block cubic route failed;

    the right even cubic detector must come from the nonlinear same-point whitening;

Continue

The honest continuation is to stop one layer before a full proof and isolate the exact algebraic obstruction in the nonlinear same-point cubic term.

We have the candidate
C(h):=X2[2](h)вАЙN0вАЙX1(h)вИТX1(h)вАЙN0вАЙX2[2](h),

1. What X1X1вАЛ and X2[2]X2[2]вАЛ look like

X1вАЛ=L(M1вАЛ),

where M1M1вАЛ is the first same-point jet. Its large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ structure is

[V\evenN0U\oddвИТU\oddN0V\even]вЙ†0in J3/V\val(3) ?

This is finite-dimensional linear algebra.
2. Why this is promising

If that commutator-type matrix vanished identically, it would mean the leading odd and even same-point whitening directions commute through the baseline mixed block N0N0вАЛ in exactly the wrong way. That is a very rigid algebraic condition.

Generically, for fixed non-scalar matrices U\odd,V\even,N0U\oddвАЛ,V\evenвАЛ,N0вАЛ, there is no reason for
V\evenN0U\oddвИТU\oddN0V\even
V\evenвАЛN0вАЛU\oddвАЛвИТU\oddвАЛN0вАЛV\evenвАЛ

to vanish, nor to lie automatically in the value/gauge cubic subspace.

So this is now a concrete genericity claim, not a vague analytic one.
3. The exact next lemma

The right next theorem is:

    Leading nonlinear same-point cubic quotient lemma.
    Let
    U\odd:=limвБ°вИ£hвИ£вЖТвИЮh5X1(h),V\even:=limвБ°вИ£hвИ£вЖТвИЮh10X2[2](h).
    U\oddвАЛ:=вИ£hвИ£вЖТвИЮlimвАЛh5X1вАЛ(h),V\evenвАЛ:=вИ£hвИ£вЖТвИЮlimвАЛh10X2[2]вАЛ(h).

    Then
    C0:=V\evenN0U\oddвИТU\oddN0V\even

    has nonzero image in J3/V\val(3)J3вАЛ/V\val(3)вАЛ.

[C(h)]=hвИТ15[C0]+O(hвИТ16),
[C(h)]=hвИТ15[C0вАЛ]+O(hвИТ16),

so the cubic detector BB can be frozen to detect [C0][C0вАЛ], and it will have even leading parity.

Then the symmetric resonance case is dead.
4. How to test it without full symbolic chaos

You do not need full formulas for every entry of X1,X2[2]X1вАЛ,X2[2]вАЛ.

You only need the leading matrices U\odd,V\evenU\oddвАЛ,V\evenвАЛ. Those come from:

    the leading odd part of M1M1вАЛ, which is diagonal and order hвИТ5hвИТ5,

    the quadratic part of X2X2вАЛ, which is built from M1M1вАЛ and hence from that same diagonal odd leading piece.

So the leading asymptotic algebra is much simpler than the full finite-hh formulas.

At leading order, you can replace
M1(h)вИЉhвИТ5D,
M1вАЛ(h)вИЉhвИТ5D,

with DD a fixed diagonal odd matrix. Then

This is the real asymptotic core.
5. What can happen

There are only three possibilities.

If [C0]вЙ†0[C0вАЛ]оА†=0, then the large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ symmetric resonance case is excluded. That would be a big win.

So the next attack is extremely focused.
6. The main-track status after this reduction

At this point, the upgraded finite-core track has reduced the k=2k=2 large-parameter problem to a single finite-dimensional check:

Does the leading nonlinear same-point cubic asymptotic survive in the cubic quotient?вАЛ

    the candidate is identified;

    its leading asymptotic form is identified;

    and the remaining task is one explicit asymptotic matrix-quotient computation.

Attack

I attacked the candidate directly, and here is the honest outcome:

I can reduce it one step further, but I cannot finish the check from the current formulas alone.

The candidate was
C(h)=X2[2](h)вАЙN0вАЙX1(h)вИТX1(h)вАЙN0вАЙX2[2](h),
C(h)=X2[2]вАЛ(h)N0вАЛX1вАЛ(h)вИТX1вАЛ(h)N0вАЛX2[2]вАЛ(h),

with
X1(h)вИЉhвИТ5U\odd,X2[2](h)вИЉhвИТ10V\even.

So the leading asymptotic is
C(h)вИЉhвИТ15C0,C0:=V\evenN0U\oddвИТU\oddN0V\even.

That is exact at the structural level.

Now, the real question is whether C0C0вАЛ is zero or value-like.
What we can say without bluffing

At leading order, the odd first same-point jet M1M1вАЛ is dominated by its diagonal part. So write
M1(h)вИЉhвИТ5D,D=(d100d2),DвИЭћЄI.
M1вАЛ(h)вИЉhвИТ5D,D=(d1вАЛ0вАЛ0d2вАЛвАЛ),DоА†вИЭI.

Then
U\odd=L(D),
U\oddвАЛ=L(D),

where LL is the Sylvester inverse for the baseline G01/2G01/2вАЛ.

Because DD is diagonal and not scalar, U\oddU\oddвАЛ is generally symmetric and not scalar.

The quadratic piece V\evenV\evenвАЛ comes from the quadratic part of X2X2вАЛ, so schematically
V\even=LвАЙвБ£(U\oddG0U\odd+U\oddDX0+X0DU\odd),
V\evenвАЛ=L(U\oddвАЛG0вАЛU\oddвАЛ+U\oddвАЛDX0вАЛ+X0вАЛDU\oddвАЛ),

with X0=G0вИТ1/2X0вАЛ=G0вИТ1/2вАЛ.

So V\evenV\evenвАЛ is also generally symmetric.

Therefore the obstruction matrix is
C0=V\evenN0U\oddвИТU\oddN0V\even,
C0вАЛ=V\evenвАЛN0вАЛU\oddвАЛвИТU\oddвАЛN0вАЛV\evenвАЛ,

a commutator twisted by N0N0вАЛ between two symmetric matrices.

That already gives a useful reduction:
C0=0 only if V\evenN0U\odd=U\oddN0V\even.
C0вАЛ=0 only if V\evenвАЛN0вАЛU\oddвАЛ=U\oddвАЛN0вАЛV\evenвАЛ.вАЛ

That is a very rigid algebraic condition.
Why this is promising

3. Why this matters

This means the nonlinear same-point cubic candidate is not automatically good.

It only helps if the actual baseline geometry is genuinely noncommutative in the relevant sense вАФ roughly, if G0G0вАЛ and the leading odd same-point direction DD do not commute, so that U\oddU\oddвАЛ and V\evenV\evenвАЛ are not both diagonal in the same frame.

So the current state is:
The C0 candidate works only if the actual baseline is sufficiently noncommutative.
The C0вАЛ candidate works only if the actual baseline is sufficiently noncommutative.вАЛ

That is a real narrowing, but not yet a closure.
4. What this suggests strategically

This pushes me toward a more sober conclusion:

trying to kill the large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ symmetric resonance case by one specially chosen cubic detector is getting fragile.

We have now seen:

    first-jet detector is fragile;

    linear mixed-block cubic bb-channel is value-like;

    nonlinear same-point cubic C0C0вАЛ can also collapse back into the antisymmetric channel in commuting cases.

So while the branch is not dead, it is no longer the cleanest path.
5. What is next

I think the best next step is to back out one level and go to the more invariant theorem we were converging toward:
bounded-order quotient odd-jet noncollapse on compact parameter sets.

    keep the large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ reductions we already got,

    instead prove that on any compact normalized k=2k=2 parameter set, the quotient germ cannot collapse to zero,

    and use finite-dimensional compactness to get a bounded surviving odd order.

That route is less elegant locally, but more robust globally.

So my answer is:
Next: shift from the fragile cubic-detector branch to the compact-parameter quotient noncollapse theorem.

5. The best new attack on zero-germ exclusion

Do not chase individual jets. Attack the full germ through an ODE-style uniqueness principle.

The corrected germ F(p;z)F(p;z) is not an arbitrary holomorphic function. It comes from a finite core through explicit rational kernel formulas and corrected whitening. For fixed kk, its Taylor coefficients are algebraic-differential functions of finitely many parameters.

So if
F(p;z)вЙ°0,
F(p;z)вЙ°0,

that means every quotient odd coefficient vanishes, giving an infinite system of algebraic equations in finitely many core parameters.

For a finite-dimensional analytic family, that is extremely rigid.

This suggests the right theorem shape:

    in the finite-dimensional parameter family of normalized kk-core germs, the subfamily lying in V\valV\valвАЛ is a proper analytic subset.

If true, then a genuine anomalous core cannot vary into it generically, and on a compact set it is separated away unless it actually belongs to it.

That is stronger than earlier formulations.

5. Why (ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5)(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ) is the right first package

would have to be negatively collinear at two distinct points.

That is a codimension-вЙ•2вЙ•2 phenomenon generically, not a stable one-dimensional resonance.

So the strongest practical next theorem to target is:
Two-pair three-jet exclusion theorem

There is no nondegenerate normalized two-pair core with
ќЮ1=ќЮ3=ќЮ5=0
ќЮ1вАЛ=ќЮ3вАЛ=ќЮ5вАЛ=0

except possibly on a finite union of degenerate symmetry loci.

That would already be enough for the compact-parameter route.
6. The dimension count is favorable

The reduced parameter space is 44-real-dimensional.

If each of ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ contributes even one independent scalar quotient constraint, then three jets already give roughly 33 scalar conditions. In reality, each quotient jet may have codimension 11 or 22, so the true constraint count can be higher.

That means the common zero set of (ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5)(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ) should be at most a thin set, and often discrete or empty, unless there is a hidden structural identity.

So from a finite-dimensional analytic viewpoint, the three-jet route is very promising.
7. The exact next finite-dimensional object

The right object is the three-jet map
F2(a1,h1,a2,h2):=(ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5).
F2вАЛ(a1вАЛ,h1вАЛ,a2вАЛ,h2вАЛ):=(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ).

The question is whether
F2вИТ1(0)
F2вИТ1вАЛ(0)

contains any genuinely anomalous normalized two-pair cores.

So the next theorem to aim for is:
F2вИТ1(0)вИ©{genuine anomalous normalized two-pair cores}=вИЕ
F2вИТ1вАЛ(0)вИ©{genuine anomalous normalized two-pair cores}=вИЕвАЛ

or at worst consists only of degenerate clustering/symmetry loci.

That is the sharp k=2k=2 finite-order exclusion statement.
8. What I can already conclude structurally

Without explicit ќЮ5ќЮ5вАЛ-formulas, the honest conclusion is:

    ќЮ1ќЮ1вАЛ alone is too fragile;

    (ќЮ1,ќЮ3)(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ) already force any collapse into a compact resonance-type set;

    adding ќЮ5ќЮ5вАЛ should overdetermine that set unless there is a hidden analytic identity.

So the compact-parameter noncollapse route is now reduced to a very concrete finite-dimensional analytic problem on R4R4.

That is a much stronger position than before.
9. The next useful theorem to write

The exact next statement is:

    Finite-order two-pair exclusion criterion.
    If the three-jet map
    F2=(ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5)
    F2вАЛ=(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ)

    has no genuinely anomalous zeros on the compact normalized parameter set, then the compact-parameter quotient noncollapse theorem holds for k=2k=2.

That is the right theorem to put on paper next.

If you want, IвАЩll now write that theorem and its proof as a clean finite-dimensional implication.

Yes please

Why this is useful

This theorem is deliberately modest. It does not prove the hard part. It isolates it.

If that is verified, then the compact-parameter noncollapse theorem for k=2k=2 follows immediately.

So this theorem is the right bridge from the local jet computations to the full compactness argument.
The next exact target

The next theorem to attack is therefore:
Three-jet two-pair exclusion theorem

There is no genuinely anomalous normalized two-pair core with
ќЮ1=ќЮ3=ќЮ5=0.
ќЮ1вАЛ=ќЮ3вАЛ=ќЮ5вАЛ=0.

That is now the sharpest k=2k=2 statement.

Once that is proved, the compact-parameter quotient noncollapse theorem for two pairs is done.

Ok prove it

I canвАЩt honestly prove the full three-jet exclusion theorem from what we have so far.

What I can prove is the exact reduction and the strongest partial statement that survives scrutiny.

The theorem to prove was:
ќЮ1=ќЮ3=ќЮ5=0вЯєthe normalized two-pair core is not genuinely anomalous.
ќЮ1вАЛ=ќЮ3вАЛ=ќЮ5вАЛ=0вЯєthe normalized two-pair core is not genuinely anomalous.

At this stage, that still requires one missing ingredient: an explicit enough description of the three quotient jet maps

as functions of
(a1,h1,a2,h2),
(a1вАЛ,h1вАЛ,a2вАЛ,h2вАЛ),

We do not have that yet.

What is proved now is the following.
1. Large-parameter reduction

Any possible two-pair leading-order collapse is confined to:

    a compact parameter region, or

    the resonance locus h2=¬±h1h2вАЛ=¬±h1вАЛ.

Then the nonresonant large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ case is excluded by the determinant argument built from two frozen quotient detectors A,BA,B. So any failure of three-jet exclusion must lie in a compact normalized parameter set, possibly with resonance.

That part is real.
2. Finite-order implication theorem

If one can show
F2(a1,h1,a2,h2):=(ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5)
F2вАЛ(a1вАЛ,h1вАЛ,a2вАЛ,h2вАЛ):=(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ)

has no genuinely anomalous zeros on the compact normalized parameter set, then compact-parameter quotient noncollapse for k=2k=2 follows immediately.

That implication is proved.
3. What remains unproved

The actual statement
F2вИТ1(0)вИ©A2=вИЕ
F2вИТ1вАЛ(0)вИ©A2вАЛ=вИЕ

is still open.

To prove it, one needs one of two things:

Either explicit formulas for the first three quotient odd jets, enough to show their common zero set is only degenerate.

Or a structural theorem that says a genuine two-pair anomalous core cannot make the first three quotient jets all vanish.

We do not yet have either.
4. The strongest honest partial theorem

The strongest theorem I can defend now is:

    Partial theorem.
    Assume the three-jet exclusion statement
    ќЮ1=ќЮ3=ќЮ5=0вЯєnot genuinely anomalous
    ќЮ1вАЛ=ќЮ3вАЛ=ќЮ5вАЛ=0вЯєnot genuinely anomalous

    holds on the compact normalized two-pair parameter set. Then quotient-germ collapse is impossible for k=2k=2, and every genuinely anomalous normalized two-pair core has a nonzero quotient odd jet among orders 1,3,51,3,5.

That is fully sound, but conditional.
5. My honest judgment

So: no, I cannot honestly say I have proved the three-jet exclusion theorem itself.

The next real mathematical task is still to compute or structurally control
ќЮ1, ќЮ3, ќЮ5
ќЮ1вАЛ, ќЮ3вАЛ, ќЮ5вАЛ

for k=2k=2.

The best next move is to make that problem even smaller:
pick a quotient basis and compute the first three scalar quotient coordinates explicitly for a normalized two-pair core. That is the shortest path to an actual proof or counterexample.

D13вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ):=ќ±1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)ќ±3вАЛ(h2вАЛ)вИТќ±1вАЛ(h2вАЛ)ќ±3вАЛ(h1вАЛ),

D15вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ):=ќ±1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)ќ±5вАЛ(h2вАЛ)вИТќ±1вАЛ(h2вАЛ)ќ±5вАЛ(h1вАЛ),
D35(h1,h2):=ќ±3(h1)ќ±5(h2)вИТќ±3(h2)ќ±5(h1).
D35вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ):=ќ±3вАЛ(h1вАЛ)ќ±5вАЛ(h2вАЛ)вИТќ±3вАЛ(h2вАЛ)ќ±5вАЛ(h1вАЛ).

If all three vanish, then the three scalar quotient coordinates are pairwise collinear, which is exactly the leading-order collapse condition.

Now, the whole mechanism only needs one robust fact:
ќ±2r+1(h)вИЉc2r+1вАЙhвИТmr
ќ±2r+1вАЛ(h)вИЉc2r+1вАЛhвИТmrвАЛ

with strictly increasing exponents mrmrвАЛ, and c2r+1вЙ†0c2r+1вАЛоА†=0.

For the first few orders, the natural pattern is
ќ±1(h)вИЉc1hвИТ5,ќ±3(h)вИЉc3hвИТ7,ќ±5(h)вИЉc5hвИТ9,
ќ±1вАЛ(h)вИЉc1вАЛhвИТ5,ќ±3вАЛ(h)вИЉc3вАЛhвИТ7,ќ±5вАЛ(h)вИЉc5вАЛhвИТ9,

assuming the chosen quotient detectors really see the first surviving asymptotics at those orders.

Under that hypothesis, the minors have explicit leading behavior.

For D13D13вАЛ,
D13(h1,h2)=c1c3(h1вИТ5h2вИТ7вИТh2вИТ5h1вИТ7)+lower order
D13вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=c1вАЛc3вАЛ(h1вИТ5вАЛh2вИТ7вАЛвИТh2вИТ5вАЛh1вИТ7вАЛ)+lower order

so

D13вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=c1вАЛc3вАЛh1вИТ7вАЛh2вИТ7вАЛ(h12вАЛвИТh22вАЛ)+O(h1вИТ8вАЛh2вИТ7вАЛ+h1вИТ7вАЛh2вИТ8вАЛ).вАЛ

Similarly,
D15(h1,h2)=c1c5вАЙh1вИТ9h2вИТ9(h14вИТh24)+O(h1вИТ10h2вИТ9+h1вИТ9h2вИТ10),
D15вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=c1вАЛc5вАЛh1вИТ9вАЛh2вИТ9вАЛ(h14вАЛвИТh24вАЛ)+O(h1вИТ10вАЛh2вИТ9вАЛ+h1вИТ9вАЛh2вИТ10вАЛ),вАЛ

and
D35(h1,h2)=c3c5вАЙh1вИТ9h2вИТ9(h12вИТh22)+O(h1вИТ10h2вИТ9+h1вИТ9h2вИТ10).
D35вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)=c3вАЛc5вАЛh1вИТ9вАЛh2вИТ9вАЛ(h12вАЛвИТh22вАЛ)+O(h1вИТ10вАЛh2вИТ9вАЛ+h1вИТ9вАЛh2вИТ10вАЛ).вАЛ

So asymptotically, all three minors vanish only on the resonance locus
h12=h22,
h12вАЛ=h22вАЛ,

that is,
h2=¬±h1.
h2вАЛ=¬±h1вАЛ.

This is a real improvement over the earlier two-jet reduction, because it shows the three-jet collinearity condition does not create a new large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ bad set. It still collapses onto the same resonance geometry.

So the large-parameter picture is now:
If the three quotient detectors have nonzero leading asymptotics of orders 5,7,9, then D13=D15=D35=0 forces h2=¬±h1 asymptotically.
If the three quotient detectors have nonzero leading asymptotics of orders 5,7,9, then D13вАЛ=D15вАЛ=D35вАЛ=0 forces h2вАЛ=¬±h1вАЛ asymptotically.вАЛ

That means the three-jet problem is not getting worse. It is still concentrated on the resonance locus.

Now split the two loci.

If h2=h1h2вАЛ=h1вАЛ, the two pairs are vertically coincident. That is essentially a clustering/degeneracy case, not a generic two-pair geometry.

If h2=вИТh1h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ, we are back at the symmetric resonance case. So the three-jet reduction says:
Outside a compact set, any possible leading-order three-jet collapse is confined to h2=вИТh1.
Outside a compact set, any possible leading-order three-jet collapse is confined to h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ.вАЛ

That is the strongest honest continuation I can make from the current reduction.

So the next real attack is still the symmetric resonance case, but now phrased as:

    On the locus h2=вИТh1h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ, can the three leading scalar quotient coordinates
    ќ±1(h), ќ±3(h), ќ±5(h)

ќ±1(h)+ќївАЙќ±1(вИТh)=0,

1. Split each scalar coefficient into even and odd parts

Write
ќ±2r+1(h)=ќ±2r+1odd(h)+ќ±2r+1even(h),
ќ±2r+1вАЛ(h)=ќ±2r+1oddвАЛ(h)+ќ±2r+1evenвАЛ(h),

with
ќ±2r+1odd(вИТh)=вИТќ±2r+1odd(h),ќ±2r+1even(вИТh)=ќ±2r+1even(h).
ќ±2r+1oddвАЛ(вИТh)=вИТќ±2r+1oddвАЛ(h),ќ±2r+1evenвАЛ(вИТh)=ќ±2r+1evenвАЛ(h).

Then the resonance equations become
(1вИТќї)ќ±1odd(h)+(1+ќї)ќ±1even(h)=0,
(1вИТќї)ќ±1oddвАЛ(h)+(1+ќї)ќ±1evenвАЛ(h)=0,
(1вИТќї)ќ±3odd(h)+(1+ќї)ќ±3even(h)=0,
(1вИТќї)ќ±3oddвАЛ(h)+(1+ќї)ќ±3evenвАЛ(h)=0,
(1вИТќї)ќ±5odd(h)+(1+ќї)ќ±5even(h)=0.
(1вИТќї)ќ±5oddвАЛ(h)+(1+ќї)ќ±5evenвАЛ(h)=0.

That is the exact resonance system.
2. Solve for the resonance ratio

Assume for the moment that ќ±1odd(h)вЙ†0ќ±1oddвАЛ(h)оА†=0. Then the first equation gives
1вИТќї1+ќї=вИТќ±1even(h)ќ±1odd(h).
1+ќї1вИТќївАЛ=вИТќ±1oddвАЛ(h)ќ±1evenвАЛ(h)вАЛ.

So
ќї=1+ќ±1even(h)ќ±1odd(h)1вИТќ±1even(h)ќ±1odd(h).
ќї=1вИТќ±1oddвАЛ(h)ќ±1evenвАЛ(h)вАЛ1+ќ±1oddвАЛ(h)ќ±1evenвАЛ(h)вАЛвАЛ.вАЛ

For large вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£, the first quotient jet is expected to be odd-dominated, so
ќ±1odd(h)вИЉc1hвИТ5,ќ±1even(h)=O(hвИТ6),
ќ±1oddвАЛ(h)вИЉc1вАЛhвИТ5,ќ±1evenвАЛ(h)=O(hвИТ6),

hence
ќї=1+O(hвИТ1).
ќї=1+O(hвИТ1).

That recovers the earlier resonance fact, now in the correct parity language.
3. Compatibility condition across higher jets

Now substitute this same ќїќї into the cubic and quintic equations. A cleaner way is to eliminate ќїќї abstractly.

Define
R1,3(h):=ќ±1odd(h)ќ±3even(h)вИТќ±3odd(h)ќ±1even(h),
R1,3вАЛ(h):=ќ±1oddвАЛ(h)ќ±3evenвАЛ(h)вИТќ±3oddвАЛ(h)ќ±1evenвАЛ(h),
R1,5(h):=ќ±1odd(h)ќ±5even(h)вИТќ±5odd(h)ќ±1even(h).
R1,5вАЛ(h):=ќ±1oddвАЛ(h)ќ±5evenвАЛ(h)вИТќ±5oddвАЛ(h)ќ±1evenвАЛ(h).

Then the three resonance equations can hold only if
R1,3(h)=0,R1,5(h)=0.
R1,3вАЛ(h)=0,R1,5вАЛ(h)=0.вАЛ

This is the right scalar reduction of the symmetric resonance problem.

Why? Because each equation determines the same ratio
1вИТќї1+ќї=вИТќ±2r+1even(h)ќ±2r+1odd(h),
1+ќї1вИТќївАЛ=вИТќ±2r+1oddвАЛ(h)ќ±2r+1evenвАЛ(h)вАЛ,

so those ratios must agree for r=0,1,2r=0,1,2. Equating the r=0r=0 and r=1r=1 ratios gives R1,3=0R1,3вАЛ=0, and equating r=0r=0 and r=2r=2 gives R1,5=0R1,5вАЛ=0.

So the symmetric resonance collapse has now been reduced to two scalar conditions in one variable hh.

That is a major simplification.
4. Asymptotic consequences

Now assume the natural asymptotics
ќ±1odd(h)вИЉc1hвИТ5,ќ±1even(h)вИЉe1hвИТ6,
ќ±1oddвАЛ(h)вИЉc1вАЛhвИТ5,ќ±1evenвАЛ(h)вИЉe1вАЛhвИТ6,
ќ±3odd(h)вИЉc3hвИТ7,ќ±3even(h)вИЉe3hвИТ8,
ќ±3oddвАЛ(h)вИЉc3вАЛhвИТ7,ќ±3evenвАЛ(h)вИЉe3вАЛhвИТ8,
ќ±5odd(h)вИЉc5hвИТ9,ќ±5even(h)вИЉe5hвИТ10,
ќ±5oddвАЛ(h)вИЉc5вАЛhвИТ9,ќ±5evenвАЛ(h)вИЉe5вАЛhвИТ10,

with nonzero relevant constants.

Then
R1,3(h)вИЉ(c1e3вИТc3e1)hвИТ13,
R1,3вАЛ(h)вИЉ(c1вАЛe3вАЛвИТc3вАЛe1вАЛ)hвИТ13,

and
R1,5(h)вИЉ(c1e5вИТc5e1)hвИТ15.
R1,5вАЛ(h)вИЉ(c1вАЛe5вАЛвИТc5вАЛe1вАЛ)hвИТ15.

Therefore, if
c1e3вИТc3e1вЙ†0orc1e5вИТc5e1вЙ†0,

then for large вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£,
R1,3(h)вЙ†0orR1,5(h)вЙ†0,
R1,3вАЛ(h)оА†=0orR1,5вАЛ(h)оА†=0,

and symmetric resonance collapse is impossible.

So the large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ symmetric resonance problem is now reduced to checking whether at least one of the asymptotic determinants
c1e3вИТc3e1,c1e5вИТc5e1
c1вАЛe3вАЛвИТc3вАЛe1вАЛ,c1вАЛe5вАЛвИТc5вАЛe1вАЛ

is nonzero.

That is much better than the earlier matrix-level C0C0вАЛ obstruction.
5. What this buys

We no longer need one magical cubic detector. We only need that the odd/even asymptotic ratios are not identical across jet orders.

In other words, it is enough to show that
ќ±3even(h)ќ±3odd(h)вЙ°ћЄќ±1even(h)ќ±1odd(h)
ќ±3oddвАЛ(h)ќ±3evenвАЛ(h)вАЛоА†вЙ°ќ±1oddвАЛ(h)ќ±1evenвАЛ(h)вАЛ

or
ќ±5even(h)ќ±5odd(h)вЙ°ћЄќ±1even(h)ќ±1odd(h)
ќ±5oddвАЛ(h)ќ±5evenвАЛ(h)вАЛоА†вЙ°ќ±1oddвАЛ(h)ќ±1evenвАЛ(h)вАЛ

asymptotically.

That is a much softer and more believable condition.
6. The next exact target

So the next theorem to attack is:

    Asymptotic ratio nondegeneracy lemma.
    For the frozen scalar quotient coordinates ќ±1,ќ±3,ќ±5ќ±1вАЛ,ќ±3вАЛ,ќ±5вАЛ, the odd/even asymptotic ratios are not all equal:
    e3c3вЙ†e1c1ore5c5вЙ†e1c1.
    c3вАЛe3вАЛвАЛоА†=c1вАЛe1вАЛвАЛorc5вАЛe5вАЛвАЛоА†=c1вАЛe1вАЛвАЛ. 

If that lemma is true, then the symmetric resonance locus is excluded for large вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£.

That is now the sharpest continuation of the main track.

Continue

Here is the strongest honest continuation I can make.

The key improvement is this:

for the resonance equations, we do not need exact constants cr,ercrвАЛ,erвАЛ. It is already enough to know the orders of the odd and even parts of the first few scalar quotient coefficients.

And those orders are much more accessible than the full coefficients.
1. Write the parity split explicitly

For the frozen scalar quotient coordinates,
ќ±2r+1(h)=ќ±2r+1\odd(h)+ќ±2r+1\even(h),
ќ±2r+1вАЛ(h)=ќ±2r+1\oddвАЛ(h)+ќ±2r+1\evenвАЛ(h),

with
ќ±2r+1\odd(вИТh)=вИТќ±2r+1\odd(h),ќ±2r+1\even(вИТh)=ќ±2r+1\even(h).
ќ±2r+1\oddвАЛ(вИТh)=вИТќ±2r+1\oddвАЛ(h),ќ±2r+1\evenвАЛ(вИТh)=ќ±2r+1\evenвАЛ(h).

(1вИТќї)ќ±1\odd(h)+(1+ќї)ќ±1\even(h)=0,

1вИТќї1+ќї=вИТќ±2r+1\even(h)ќ±2r+1\odd(h)

for r=0,1,2r=0,1,2.

ѕБ1(h):=ќ±1\even(h)ќ±1\odd(h),ѕБ3(h):=ќ±3\even(h)ќ±3\odd(h),ѕБ5(h):=ќ±5\even(h)ќ±5\odd(h)
ѕБ1вАЛ(h):=ќ±1\oddвАЛ(h)ќ±1\evenвАЛ(h)вАЛ,ѕБ3вАЛ(h):=ќ±3\oddвАЛ(h)ќ±3\evenвАЛ(h)вАЛ,ѕБ5вАЛ(h):=ќ±5\oddвАЛ(h)ќ±5\evenвАЛ(h)вАЛ

to agree.

So the symmetric resonance problem is now:
ѕБ1(h)=ѕБ3(h)=ѕБ5(h).
ѕБ1вАЛ(h)=ѕБ3вАЛ(h)=ѕБ5вАЛ(h).вАЛ

That is the right reduced scalar condition.
2. What we already know for ѕБ1ѕБ1вАЛ

From the one-pair first-jet analysis:

    the leading surviving odd part comes from the diagonal MM-channel and is order hвИТ5hвИТ5,

    the even correction is one order smaller, order hвИТ6hвИТ6.

ќ±1\odd(h)вИЉc1hвИТ5,ќ±1\even(h)вИЉe1hвИТ6,
ќ±1\oddвАЛ(h)вИЉc1вАЛhвИТ5,ќ±1\evenвАЛ(h)вИЉe1вАЛhвИТ6,

hence
ѕБ1(h)вИЉe1c1вАЙhвИТ1.
ѕБ1вАЛ(h)вИЉc1вАЛe1вАЛвАЛhвИТ1.вАЛ

That is solid at the structural level.
3. The crucial order estimate for ѕБ3ѕБ3вАЛ

Now use the cubic-jet structure carefully.

The odd cubic quotient part comes from the linear third-order same-point contribution. That is one more pair of derivatives than the first jet, so it is naturally order
ќ±3\odd(h)вИЉc3hвИТ7.
ќ±3\oddвАЛ(h)вИЉc3вАЛhвИТ7.

For the even cubic quotient part, the clean surviving candidate is not linear. It comes from nonlinear same-point whitening terms, quadratic in the first same-point jet. Since
M1(h)вИЉhвИТ5,
M1вАЛ(h)вИЉhвИТ5,

a quadratic same-point term is order
hвИТ10.
hвИТ10.

So the natural structural estimate is
ќ±3\even(h)вИЉe3hвИТ10
ќ±3\evenвАЛ(h)вИЉe3вАЛhвИТ10

provided the even cubic quotient does not vanish identically.

Therefore
ѕБ3(h)вИЉe3c3вАЙhвИТ3.
ѕБ3вАЛ(h)вИЉc3вАЛe3вАЛвАЛhвИТ3.вАЛ

This is the key point.

It is not just вАЬa different constant.вАЭ It is a different power of hh.
4. What that implies immediately

Since
ѕБ1(h)вИЉC1hвИТ1,ѕБ3(h)вИЉC3hвИТ3,
ѕБ1вАЛ(h)вИЉC1вАЛhвИТ1,ѕБ3вАЛ(h)вИЉC3вАЛhвИТ3,

these cannot be equal for large вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£, unless one of the coefficients vanishes in a very special way.

So, assuming the even cubic quotient part is genuinely nonzero, we get:
ѕБ1(h)вЙ†ѕБ3(h)for all sufficiently large вИ£hвИ£.
ѕБ1вАЛ(h)оА†=ѕБ3вАЛ(h)for all sufficiently large вИ£hвИ£.вАЛ

And that already kills the symmetric resonance system. We do not need ќ±5ќ±5вАЛ.

So the large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ symmetric resonance problem reduces to a single condition:
ќ±3\even(h)вЙ°ћЄ0 in the quotient.
ќ±3\evenвАЛ(h)оА†вЙ°0 in the quotient.вАЛ

If that holds, then the orders force
ѕБ1(h)вЙНhвИТ1,ѕБ3(h)вЙНhвИТ3,
ѕБ1вАЛ(h)вЙНhвИТ1,ѕБ3вАЛ(h)вЙНhвИТ3,

hence the resonance equations cannot be simultaneously satisfied for large вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£.

This is stronger and cleaner than the earlier constant-determinant approach.
5. The resulting theorem

So the sharp next theorem is:
Order-separation resonance exclusion lemma

Assume:
ќ±1\odd(h)вИЉc1hвИТ5,ќ±1\even(h)=O(hвИТ6),c1вЙ†0,
ќ±1\oddвАЛ(h)вИЉc1вАЛhвИТ5,ќ±1\evenвАЛ(h)=O(hвИТ6),c1вАЛоА†=0,

and
ќ±3\odd(h)вИЉc3hвИТ7,ќ±3\even(h)вИЉe3hвИТ10,c3e3вЙ†0.
ќ±3\oddвАЛ(h)вИЉc3вАЛhвИТ7,ќ±3\evenвАЛ(h)вИЉe3вАЛhвИТ10,c3вАЛe3вАЛоА†=0.

Then there exists HвИЧ>0HвИЧвАЛ>0 such that for вИ£hвИ£вЙ•HвИЧвИ£hвИ£вЙ•HвИЧвАЛ, the symmetric resonance system
ќ±1(h)+ќїќ±1(вИТh)=0,ќ±3(h)+ќїќ±3(вИТh)=0,ќївЙ•0,
ќ±1вАЛ(h)+ќїќ±1вАЛ(вИТh)=0,ќ±3вАЛ(h)+ќїќ±3вАЛ(вИТh)=0,ќївЙ•0,

has no solution.
Proof

From the first equation,
1вИТќї1+ќї=вИТѕБ1(h)вИЉвИТC1hвИТ1.
1+ќї1вИТќївАЛ=вИТѕБ1вАЛ(h)вИЉвИТC1вАЛhвИТ1.

From the second,
1вИТќї1+ќї=вИТѕБ3(h)вИЉвИТC3hвИТ3.
1+ќї1вИТќївАЛ=вИТѕБ3вАЛ(h)вИЉвИТC3вАЛhвИТ3.

These cannot both hold for sufficiently large вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£, since hвИТ1hвИТ1 and hвИТ3hвИТ3 are asymptotically distinct powers. вИО

That is a real theorem, conditional only on the nonvanishing of the even cubic quotient part.
6. What this means for the main track

This is better than the earlier attempts:

    we do not need an exact cubic detector formula;

    we do not need to compute delicate constants;

    we only need to show the even cubic quotient contribution exists and is order hвИТ10hвИТ10, not killed by V\val(3)V\val(3)вАЛ.

Then the order mismatch alone excludes large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ symmetric resonance.

So the main local obstacle has been reduced again:
Show that the cubic quotient has a nonzero even component of order hвИТ10.
Show that the cubic quotient has a nonzero even component of order hвИТ10.вАЛ

That is now the only thing needed to finish the large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ k=2k=2 resonance analysis.
7. The honest status

I cannot yet prove from the present frozen formulas that
ќ±3\even(h)вИЉe3hвИТ10,e3вЙ†0,
ќ±3\evenвАЛ(h)вИЉe3вАЛhвИТ10,e3вАЛоА†=0,

    cubic odd/even ratio should be hвИТ3hвИТ3,

    therefore large symmetric resonance is impossible if the even cubic quotient is nonzero.

Continue

That means this same-point cubic commutator candidate lies in the same symmetry type as the old value-like antisymmetric off-diagonal channel. If the cubic value/gauge family kills that channel too, then this candidate is quotient-trivial automatically.

1. Freeze the finite-order map

For k=2k=2, work with
F2(a1,h1,a2,h2):=(ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5),
F2вАЛ(a1вАЛ,h1вАЛ,a2вАЛ,h2вАЛ):=(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ),

where each ќЮ2r+1ќЮ2r+1вАЛ is a quotient jet coordinate map on the compact normalized two-pair parameter set.
2. Use a Jacobian/rank criterion

To prove
F2вИТ1(0)вИ©A2=вИЕ,
F2вИТ1вАЛ(0)вИ©A2вАЛ=вИЕ,

it is enough to show:

    any zero of F2F2вАЛ is either degenerate/clustering/symmetry-forced, or

    the Jacobian of F2F2вАЛ has rank 44 at any purported genuine anomalous zero.

Because the reduced parameter space is 44-real-dimensional:
(a1,h1,a2,h2),
(a1вАЛ,h1вАЛ,a2вАЛ,h2вАЛ),

while (ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5)(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ) usually gives at least 33 independent scalar constraints, and often more depending on quotient codimensions.

So the new local theorem to attack is:
At any nondegenerate two-pair configuration, the three-jet map F2 has no genuine anomalous zero.
At any nondegenerate two-pair configuration, the three-jet map F2вАЛ has no genuine anomalous zero.вАЛ
3. The cleanest practical version

Instead of trying to prove that globally at once, split into cases.

First case: h1,h2h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ outside a large compact set.
That is already mostly controlled by the asymptotic minor arguments: any collapse must lie near h2=¬±h1h2вАЛ=¬±h1вАЛ.

Second case: h1,h2h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ in a fixed compact set.
There, the problem is finite-dimensional and analytic. The right criterion is:

    if F2F2вАЛ vanishes at a point and the Jacobian with respect to (a1,h1,a2,h2)(a1вАЛ,h1вАЛ,a2вАЛ,h2вАЛ) has full rank, then that zero is isolated.
    Then use symmetry/degeneracy analysis to show no isolated zero can correspond to a genuine anomalous normalized two-pair core.

So the next concrete object is the Jacobian
DF2=вИВ(ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5)вИВ(a1,h1,a2,h2).
DF2вАЛ=вИВ(a1вАЛ,h1вАЛ,a2вАЛ,h2вАЛ)вИВ(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ)вАЛ.
4. Why this is now the right move

Because weвАЩve learned:

    first jet alone is too fragile;

    one cubic detector is fragile too;

    but the full three-jet map is finite-dimensional and analytic.

That means the robust tool is not another clever asymptotic invariant. It is finite-dimensional analytic transversality.

So my next move would be:

 Continue

The right continuation is to make the Jacobian attack precise enough that it becomes a real local theorem, not just a slogan.

p=(a1,h1,a2,h2),a1,a2вЙ•0,

1. The Jacobian criterion we actually want

The clean local statement is:

    If p0p0вАЛ is a putative zero of F2F2вАЛ and the differential
    DF2(p0)
    DF2вАЛ(p0вАЛ)

    has full rank in the quotient target directions, then p0p0вАЛ is an isolated zero of the three-jet map.

That by itself does not prove exclusion, but it is useful because then the only possible zeros are isolated analytic accidents. Those can then be checked against:

    permutation symmetry,

    resonance symmetry h2=¬±h1h2вАЛ=¬±h1вАЛ,

    clustering degeneracy h1=h2h1вАЛ=h2вАЛ,

    and the requirement of genuine anomaly.

So the Jacobian attack should be split into two parts:

    show generic full rank away from obvious symmetry loci,

    then analyze those symmetry loci separately.

2. How F2F2вАЛ depends on the parameters

At leading order,
ќЮ2r+1(p)=a1вАЙa2r+1(h1)+a2вАЙa2r+1(h2)+a1a2вАЙb2r+1(h1,h2)+вЛѓвАЙ.
ќЮ2r+1вАЛ(p)=a1вАЛa2r+1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+a2вАЛa2r+1вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+a1вАЛa2вАЛb2r+1вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)+вЛѓ.

So the derivatives are structurally:
вИВa1ќЮ2r+1=a2r+1(h1)+a2вАЙb2r+1(h1,h2)+вЛѓвАЙ,
вИВa1вАЛвАЛќЮ2r+1вАЛ=a2r+1вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+a2вАЛb2r+1вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)+вЛѓ,
вИВa2ќЮ2r+1=a2r+1(h2)+a1вАЙb2r+1(h1,h2)+вЛѓвАЙ,
вИВa2вАЛвАЛќЮ2r+1вАЛ=a2r+1вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+a1вАЛb2r+1вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)+вЛѓ,
вИВh1ќЮ2r+1=a1вАЙa2r+1вА≤(h1)+a1a2вАЙвИВh1b2r+1(h1,h2)+вЛѓвАЙ,
вИВh1вАЛвАЛќЮ2r+1вАЛ=a1вАЛa2r+1вА≤вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+a1вАЛa2вАЛвИВh1вАЛвАЛb2r+1вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)+вЛѓ,
вИВh2ќЮ2r+1=a2вАЙa2r+1вА≤(h2)+a1a2вАЙвИВh2b2r+1(h1,h2)+вЛѓвАЙ.
вИВh2вАЛвАЛќЮ2r+1вАЛ=a2вАЛa2r+1вА≤вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+a1вАЛa2вАЛвИВh2вАЛвАЛb2r+1вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)+вЛѓ.

This is already enough to see the mechanism.

Away from special loci:

    вИВa1вИВa1вАЛвАЛ and вИВa2вИВa2вАЛвАЛ point in the directions of the one-pair jet vectors at h1,h2h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ,

    вИВh1вИВh1вАЛвАЛ and вИВh2вИВh2вАЛвАЛ point in the directions of the derivatives of those jet vectors.

So the Jacobian will have full rank unless there is a strong linear dependence among
a2r+1(h1), a2r+1(h2), a2r+1вА≤(h1), a2r+1вА≤(h2)
a2r+1вАЛ(h1вАЛ), a2r+1вАЛ(h2вАЛ), a2r+1вА≤вАЛ(h1вАЛ), a2r+1вА≤вАЛ(h2вАЛ)

across r=0,1,2r=0,1,2.

That is a very restrictive condition.
3. The right generic-rank theorem

The theorem to target is:
Generic-rank theorem

Away from the symmetry loci
h1=¬±h2
h1вАЛ=¬±h2вАЛ

and away from the boundary a1a2=0a1вАЛa2вАЛ=0, the Jacobian of the three-jet map has maximal possible rank.

That would imply any zero of F2F2вАЛ off those loci is isolated and nongeneric.

This is very plausible because the first three odd quotient jets vary differently with hh, and the derivatives with respect to ajajвАЛ and hjhjвАЛ probe genuinely different directions.
4. Why the symmetry loci are special

There are exactly three bad loci where rank can naturally drop.

First,
a1=0ora2=0.
a1вАЛ=0ora2вАЛ=0.

Then you are back to a one-pair core, which should not be a genuine zero because the pair anomaly survives in the quotient.

Second,
h1=h2.
h1вАЛ=h2вАЛ.

This is the coincident clustering locus. The two-pair parameterization is no longer genuinely transverse there, so Jacobian rank can drop for trivial reasons.

Third,
h1=вИТh2.
h1вАЛ=вИТh2вАЛ.

This is the symmetric resonance locus. We already know parity can force cancellations here, so it needs a separate analysis.

So the Jacobian strategy is:

    prove generic rank away from these loci,

    then treat those loci one by one.

5. The local linear algebra criterion

To make this usable, pick scalar quotient coordinates for ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ. Then F2F2вАЛ becomes a map into RmRm or CmCm for some finite mm.

The Jacobian columns are:
вИВa1F2,вИВh1F2,вИВa2F2,вИВh2F2.
вИВa1вАЛвАЛF2вАЛ,вИВh1вАЛвАЛF2вАЛ,вИВa2вАЛвАЛF2вАЛ,вИВh2вАЛвАЛF2вАЛ.

At leading order these are approximately
(a1(h1),a3(h1),a5(h1)),
(a1вАЛ(h1вАЛ),a3вАЛ(h1вАЛ),a5вАЛ(h1вАЛ)),
(a1a1вА≤(h1),a1a3вА≤(h1),a1a5вА≤(h1)),
(a1вАЛa1вА≤вАЛ(h1вАЛ),a1вАЛa3вА≤вАЛ(h1вАЛ),a1вАЛa5вА≤вАЛ(h1вАЛ)),
(a1(h2),a3(h2),a5(h2)),
(a1вАЛ(h2вАЛ),a3вАЛ(h2вАЛ),a5вАЛ(h2вАЛ)),
(a2a1вА≤(h2),a2a3вА≤(h2),a2a5вА≤(h2)).
(a2вАЛa1вА≤вАЛ(h2вАЛ),a2вАЛa3вА≤вАЛ(h2вАЛ),a2вАЛa5вА≤вАЛ(h2вАЛ)).

So full rank reduces to saying these four vectors are generically independent.

That is the finite-dimensional transversality statement.
6. What we can conclude already

Even without explicit formulas, we now have a clean reduction:
To prove two-pair three-jet exclusion, it is enough to show the Jacobian of F2 has generic full rank, and then analyze only the symmetry loci.
To prove two-pair three-jet exclusion, it is enough to show the Jacobian of F2вАЛ has generic full rank, and then analyze only the symmetry loci.вАЛ

That is a real simplification. It means we do not need one perfect invariant. We need transversality plus casework.
7. The next theorem to write

The exact next statement is:
Local transversality lemma

At every nondegenerate point with
a1a2>0,h1вЙ†¬±h2,
a1вАЛa2вАЛ>0,h1вАЛоА†=¬±h2вАЛ,

the differential
DF2(p)
DF2вАЛ(p)

has full rank.

If that lemma is true, then any zero of F2F2вАЛ must lie on one of the symmetry/degeneracy loci:
a1a2=0,h1=h2,h1=вИТh2.
a1вАЛa2вАЛ=0,h1вАЛ=h2вАЛ,h1вАЛ=вИТh2вАЛ.

Then each of those can be attacked separately.
8. The honest next move

So the best continuation now is not to compute more asymptotics in the abstract. It is to formalize and attack:
Local transversality of the three-jet map F2.
Local transversality of the three-jet map F2вАЛ.вАЛ

That is the strongest main track at this point.

Ok take a big step back, give me the current story of what we are doing in context of paper, and state what gaps remain with RH even after this

Here is the current story, in paper terms rather than local calculations.

The original pair-based endgame was trying to show: if there is an off-line pair, then the corrected local scalar has a nontrivial odd signal, while the outside world is too small to cancel it, yielding the RH contradiction.

What we learned is that this was too optimistic in its original form. The main reason is that a pair is not automatically isolated in the way that argument wanted. Extra same-scale nearby zeros are not obviously perturbative. Once we tested that carefully, the вАЬpair-like bridgeвАЭ stopped looking like a theorem and started looking like a special case.

That forced a structural upgrade.

The upgraded picture is now:

You fix a finite local core of off-line zeros near the center mm, and you separate the argument into two parts.

The first part is the core, which carries the possible anomaly.

The second part is the outside world, everything beyond a chosen absolute radius HH, which should be small after corrected whitening and affine-jet subtraction.

That is the fixed-core architecture. It is much saner than the earlier moving-cutoff bookkeeping, because the local object you are trying to contradict no longer changes when you change HH.

Then the scalar side also had to be upgraded.

A single universal scalar ќ¶Kќ¶KвАЛ was good for the pair toy model, but not trustworthy for a general finite core. A larger core could be genuinely anomalous while landing in the kernel of that one scalar. So the right object is not вАЬthe scalarвАЭ but the quotient corrected germ modulo the value/gauge family. Equivalently, for a given core one should use an adapted transverse functional that kills the value family and detects the actual anomalous direction.

That leads to the current endgame target:

For a genuine finite off-line core, the quotient corrected germ should have some nonzero odd jet. Take the first surviving odd jet. Then compare it to the outside contribution at the same odd order. The outside higher-odd-jet bounds are now much better than before, because the differentiated-whitening chain gives decay like
вИ£a2r+1\outвИ£вЙ™1QвАЙH2r+3,
вИ£a2r+1\outвАЛвИ£вЙ™QH2r+31вАЛ,

so higher odd order is actually favorable for the outside tail.

That is the conceptual gain of the new approach: we no longer need the first odd jet specifically. Any surviving odd jet would do.

So, in one sentence, the paperвАЩs local contradiction architecture has changed from

вАЬpair anomaly vs. outsideвАЭ

to

вАЬfinite-core quotient odd jet vs. outside.вАЭ

That is a real improvement.

Now for the gaps.

The biggest remaining gap is still the local noncollapse theorem. In its current best form, it says:

For a genuine normalized finite off-line core, the quotient corrected germ should not vanish. More quantitatively, on a compact normalized parameter set, some bounded odd jet should survive uniformly away from zero.

That is the actual theorem the RH proof now depends on.

Everything else is either done or conditional on that.

More specifically, the current remaining gaps are:

First, the paper still does not prove the finite-core quotient noncollapse theorem. We reduced it to finite-dimensional analytic problems, and for k=2k=2 further to the three-jet map
F2=(ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5),
F2вАЛ=(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ),

but we did not prove that
F2вИТ1(0)
F2вИТ1вАЛ(0)

contains no genuinely anomalous two-pair cores. That is still open.

Second, even for k=2k=2, the local analysis is not closed. We proved a lot of reductions:

    nonresonant large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ configurations are heavily constrained;

    possible collapse gets pushed toward compact regions and symmetry loci;

    first jet alone is too fragile;

    first few odd jets are the right objects.
    But we did not finish the actual exclusion of all genuine two-pair zeros of the quotient germ.

Third, the compactness/transversality step is still more of a framework than a theorem. We now know what theorem we want:
there exists a bounded odd order 2Rk+12RkвАЛ+1 such that a genuine normalized kk-core cannot have all quotient odd jets up to that order vanish. But that statement is not yet proved.

Fourth, the adapted-functional story is conceptually right, but the paper would still need to present it cleanly. Right now the old universal scalar language and the new quotient/adapted language are not yet unified into one final theorem chain.

Fifth, the higher-odd-jet outside theorem chain looks promising, and I think it is the right upgraded machinery, but even there I would still call it вАЬstrong draft mathematics,вАЭ not fully hostile-referee-ready mathematics, until the scalar normalization and coefficient extraction are written more cleanly.

So in RH terms, what remains is not bookkeeping anymore. It is not a missing cutoff lemma, and not primarily a tail estimate. The proof has now been reduced to a genuinely local finite-dimensional obstruction theorem:
A genuine finite off-line core must produce a nonzero quotient odd jet that survives against the outside bound.
A genuine finite off-line core must produce a nonzero quotient odd jet that survives against the outside bound.

That is the current heart of the matter.

So if you ask, вАЬhow far are we from RH now?вАЭ, my answer is:

We have improved the architecture a lot. The paper is in a much better structural place than before. The false or fragile pair-rigidity intuition has largely been replaced by a coherent finite-core framework. The outside higher-odd-jet estimates now match that framework well.

But the proof is still not complete, because the central local noncollapse theorem for the finite core is still missing.

That is the decisive gap.

If you want it in the bluntest form:

    The paper no longer looks blocked by tail bookkeeping.

    It no longer looks blocked by the old mixed-transfer theorem in the same way.

    It is still blocked by the absence of a finished theorem showing that a genuine finite off-line core must leave a nonzero trace in the quotient odd-jet structure.

That is where RH still lives.

Continue with Jacobian; formalize and attack:

\boxed{
\text{Local transversality of the three-jet map } \mathcal F_2.
}


Yes. LetвАЩs formalize the Jacobian route cleanly and push it as far as it honestly goes.

This is now the right local theorem for the k=2k=2 branch.
1. The object

Work on the reduced normalized two-pair parameter space
p=(a1,h1,a2,h2),a1,a2вЙ•0,
p=(a1вАЛ,h1вАЛ,a2вАЛ,h2вАЛ),a1вАЛ,a2вАЛвЙ•0,

and define the finite-order quotient jet map
F2(p):=(ќЮ1(p),ќЮ3(p),ќЮ5(p)).
F2вАЛ(p):=(ќЮ1вАЛ(p),ќЮ3вАЛ(p),ќЮ5вАЛ(p)).

The theorem to target is:
At any nondegenerate two-pair configuration p with a1a2>0, h1вЙ†¬±h2, DF2(p) has full rank.
At any nondegenerate two-pair configuration p with a1вАЛa2вАЛ>0, h1вАЛоА†=¬±h2вАЛ, DF2вАЛ(p) has full rank.вАЛ

That is the local transversality theorem.

If true, it implies any zero of F2F2вАЛ must lie on one of the symmetry/degeneracy loci
a1a2=0,h1=h2,h1=вИТh2.
a1вАЛa2вАЛ=0,h1вАЛ=h2вАЛ,h1вАЛ=вИТh2вАЛ.

That would be a major reduction.

That is the key simplification.
3. Formal local transversality criterion

So the Jacobian will have full rank provided these four vectors are independent in the target quotient coordinates, up to the perturbative interaction corrections.

Thus the natural criterion is:
A(h1), A(h2), AвА≤(h1), AвА≤(h2) are linearly independent.
A(h1вАЛ), A(h2вАЛ), AвА≤(h1вАЛ), AвА≤(h2вАЛ) are linearly independent.вАЛ

More precisely, if the leading 4√Ч44√Ч4 minor formed by these vectors is nonzero, then the full Jacobian remains full rank after adding sufficiently small higher-order corrections.

This is the correct transversality condition.
4. Where rank can fail structurally

Now we can see exactly why the symmetry loci are special.

If h1=h2h1вАЛ=h2вАЛ, then
A(h1)=A(h2),AвА≤(h1)=AвА≤(h2),
A(h1вАЛ)=A(h2вАЛ),AвА≤(h1вАЛ)=AвА≤(h2вАЛ),

so rank drops automatically.

If h1=вИТh2h1вАЛ=вИТh2вАЛ, parity relations make A(h1)A(h1вАЛ) and A(h2)A(h2вАЛ) partially dependent, and similarly for the derivatives. So resonance can force rank loss.

If a1=0a1вАЛ=0 or a2=0a2вАЛ=0, one pair is absent, and again rank drops.

So these are exactly the places where we expected trouble. Away from them, there is no structural reason for dependence.

That is a good sign.
5. The local theorem we can actually state

Here is the clean formal theorem.
Proposition (local transversality criterion for F2F2вАЛ)

Let p=(a1,h1,a2,h2)p=(a1вАЛ,h1вАЛ,a2вАЛ,h2вАЛ) satisfy
a1a2>0,h1вЙ†¬±h2.
a1вАЛa2вАЛ>0,h1вАЛоА†=¬±h2вАЛ.

Assume that, in some fixed quotient coordinate system for (ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5)(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ), the four vectors
A(h1), A(h2), AвА≤(h1), AвА≤(h2)
A(h1вАЛ), A(h2вАЛ), AвА≤(h1вАЛ), AвА≤(h2вАЛ)

are linearly independent. Then DF2(p)DF2вАЛ(p) has full rank.
Proof

The Jacobian columns are the four vectors above plus analytic interaction corrections. At the point pp, the leading 4√Ч44√Ч4 minor is nonzero by hypothesis. Hence the full Jacobian has full rank by continuity of determinants. вИО

This is formal but correct.
6. How to attack the independence condition

Now the problem is reduced to one genuinely finite-dimensional analytic statement about the pair-jet map
hвЖ¶A(h)=(a1(h),a3(h),a5(h)).
hвЖ¶A(h)=(a1вАЛ(h),a3вАЛ(h),a5вАЛ(h)).

The condition says that away from resonance, the two position vectors A(h1),A(h2)A(h1вАЛ),A(h2вАЛ) and their derivatives should span four dimensions.

That is equivalent to saying the curve hвЖ¶A(h)hвЖ¶A(h) is not trapped in a low-dimensional affine cone and has enough curvature.

So the right next theorem to target is:
The pair-jet curve hвЖ¶A(h) is locally nondegenerate: A(h),AвА≤(h),AвА≤вА≤(h) do not satisfy a fixed low-order linear relation.
The pair-jet curve hвЖ¶A(h) is locally nondegenerate: A(h),AвА≤(h),AвА≤вА≤(h) do not satisfy a fixed low-order linear relation.вАЛ

If that holds, then for h1вЙ†¬±h2h1вАЛоА†=¬±h2вАЛ, the four-vector independence above should be generic.
7. Asymptotic evidence

The asymptotics we already extracted strongly support this.

At large вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£,
a1(h)вИЉhвИТ5,a3(h)вИЉhвИТ7,a5(h)вИЉhвИТ9,

(hвИТ5,вАЙhвИТ7,вАЙhвИТ9,вА¶вАЙ),
(hвИТ5,hвИТ7,hвИТ9,вА¶),

AвА≤(h)вИЉ(вИТ5hвИТ6,вИТ7hвИТ8,вИТ9hвИТ10,вА¶вАЙ).
AвА≤(h)вИЉ(вИТ5hвИТ6,вИТ7hвИТ8,вИТ9hвИТ10,вА¶).

This does not prove the compact-region case, but it is strong evidence that only the symmetry loci matter.
8. Honest status

What is proved:

    the correct local transversality criterion;

    the Jacobian reduction to the pair-jet curve and its derivative;

    the reason symmetry loci are the only obvious rank-drop sets.

What is not yet proved:

    actual linear independence of

    for the real quotient coordinates.

3. Why this matters for the Jacobian

If the curve is locally nondegenerate, then for nearby but distinct h1,h2h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ, the four vectors
A(h1), A(h2), AвА≤(h1), AвА≤(h2)
A(h1вАЛ), A(h2вАЛ), AвА≤(h1вАЛ), AвА≤(h2вАЛ)

should generically span a large space.

More concretely, write
h2=h1+ќµ.
h2вАЛ=h1вАЛ+ќµ.

Then
A(h2)=A(h1)+ќµAвА≤(h1)+ќµ22AвА≤вА≤(h1)+вЛѓвАЙ,
A(h2вАЛ)=A(h1вАЛ)+ќµAвА≤(h1вАЛ)+2ќµ2вАЛAвА≤вА≤(h1вАЛ)+вЛѓ,
AвА≤(h2)=AвА≤(h1)+ќµAвА≤вА≤(h1)+вЛѓвАЙ.
AвА≤(h2вАЛ)=AвА≤(h1вАЛ)+ќµAвА≤вА≤(h1вАЛ)+вЛѓ.

So the span of
A(h1),A(h2),AвА≤(h1),AвА≤(h2)
A(h1вАЛ),A(h2вАЛ),AвА≤(h1вАЛ),AвА≤(h2вАЛ)

contains the span of
A(h1),AвА≤(h1),AвА≤вА≤(h1),
A(h1вАЛ),AвА≤(h1вАЛ),AвА≤вА≤(h1вАЛ),

up to lower-order errors.

Thus the nonvanishing of ќФ(h1)ќФ(h1вАЛ) is exactly the right local signal that the Jacobian should have maximal rank near nonresonant points.

That is the bridge from curve nondegeneracy to transversality.
4. The resulting local theorem, asymptotically

So we get the following conditional asymptotic theorem:
Asymptotic local transversality theorem

Assume the frozen quotient coordinates satisfy
a1(h)вИЉc1hвИТ5,a3(h)вИЉc3hвИТ7,a5(h)вИЉc5hвИТ9,c1c3c5вЙ†0.
a1вАЛ(h)вИЉc1вАЛhвИТ5,a3вАЛ(h)вИЉc3вАЛhвИТ7,a5вАЛ(h)вИЉc5вАЛhвИТ9,c1вАЛc3вАЛc5вАЛоА†=0.

Then the pair-jet curve hвЖ¶A(h)hвЖ¶A(h) satisfies
detвБ°(A,AвА≤,AвА≤вА≤)вЙ†0
det(A,AвА≤,AвА≤вА≤)оА†=0

for all sufficiently large вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£. Consequently, the Jacobian of the three-jet map F2F2вАЛ is asymptotically full rank away from the resonance loci h1=¬±h2h1вАЛ=¬±h2вАЛ.

This is not yet the full compact-parameter theorem, but it is a real large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ transversality result.
5. What remains after this

This means the k=2k=2 problem is being squeezed from both sides.

Large nonresonant region: controlled by determinant/minor and now pair-jet-curve transversality.

Large resonant region: still delicate, but already heavily constrained by the parity-ratio analysis.

What remains is the compact parameter set, especially near:
h1=h2,h1=вИТh2,
h1вАЛ=h2вАЛ,h1вАЛ=вИТh2вАЛ,

and possibly small amplitudes.

So the next theorem to attack is no longer вАЬgeneric transversalityвАЭ in the large. That we now basically have at the asymptotic-model level.

The next theorem is:
Compact-region transversality or exclusion near the symmetry loci.
Compact-region transversality or exclusion near the symmetry loci.вАЛ

In practical terms, that means a local analysis near
h1=h2andh1=вИТh2.
h1вАЛ=h2вАЛandh1вАЛ=вИТh2вАЛ.
6. The sharp next move

The cleanest next step is to linearize around the symmetry loci.

For example, set
h2=h1+ќµ
h2вАЛ=h1вАЛ+ќµ

near the coincident locus, or
h2=вИТh1+ќµ
h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ+ќµ

near the symmetric resonance locus, and expand the three-jet map in ќµќµ.

That will tell us whether those loci are genuine zero sets or only apparent degeneracies.

So the next real target is:
Symmetry-locus expansion theorem

Expand F2F2вАЛ to first order in the transverse displacement from
h2=h1orh2=вИТh1,
h2вАЛ=h1вАЛorh2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ,

and show the transverse derivative is nonzero unless the configuration is degenerate.

That is the strongest next continuation of the Jacobian track.

Continue

LetвАЩs continue on exactly that branch.

The next useful move is to expand the three-jet map transversely to the two symmetry loci
h2=h1andh2=вИТh1.
h2вАЛ=h1вАЛandh2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ.

That is the right local test of whether those loci are genuine zero sets or just places where rank drops for trivial symmetry reasons.
1. Near the coincident locus h2=h1h2вАЛ=h1вАЛ

ќЮ2r+1=a1вАЙa2r+1(h)+a2вАЙa2r+1(h+ќµ)+a1a2вАЙb2r+1(h,h+ќµ)+вЛѓвАЙ.
ќЮ2r+1вАЛ=a1вАЛa2r+1вАЛ(h)+a2вАЛa2r+1вАЛ(h+ќµ)+a1вАЛa2вАЛb2r+1вАЛ(h,h+ќµ)+вЛѓ.

ќЮ2r+1=(a1+a2)a2r+1(h)+ќµвАЙa2вАЙa2r+1вА≤(h)+a1a2вАЙb2r+1(h,h)+O(ќµ2)+вЛѓвАЙ.

вИВќµќЮ2r+1вИ£ќµ=0=a2вАЙa2r+1вА≤(h)+a1a2вАЙвИВ2b2r+1(h,h)+вЛѓвАЙ.
вИВќµвАЛќЮ2r+1вАЛ
вАЛќµ=0вАЛ=a2вАЛa2r+1вА≤вАЛ(h)+a1вАЛa2вАЛвИВ2вАЛb2r+1вАЛ(h,h)+вЛѓ.

At leading order in amplitudes, that is just
a2вАЙa2r+1вА≤(h).
a2вАЛa2r+1вА≤вАЛ(h).

Therefore:
Near h2=h1, the transverse direction is controlled by the derivative of the one-pair jet curve.
Near h2вАЛ=h1вАЛ, the transverse direction is controlled by the derivative of the one-pair jet curve.вАЛ

So unless
a1вА≤(h)=a3вА≤(h)=a5вА≤(h)=0
a1вА≤вАЛ(h)=a3вА≤вАЛ(h)=a5вА≤вАЛ(h)=0

simultaneously, the coincident locus is not a stable zero set of the three-jet map.

That is a real reduction.
2. Near the symmetric resonance locus h2=вИТh1h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ

Now write
h1=h,h2=вИТh+ќµ.
h1вАЛ=h,h2вАЛ=вИТh+ќµ.

Then
a2r+1(h2)=a2r+1(вИТh+ќµ)=a2r+1(вИТh)+ќµвАЙa2r+1вА≤(вИТh)+O(ќµ2).
a2r+1вАЛ(h2вАЛ)=a2r+1вАЛ(вИТh+ќµ)=a2r+1вАЛ(вИТh)+ќµa2r+1вА≤вАЛ(вИТh)+O(ќµ2).

So the leading-order jets become
ќЮ2r+1=a1вАЙa2r+1(h)+a2вАЙa2r+1(вИТh)+ќµвАЙa2вАЙa2r+1вА≤(вИТh)+a1a2вАЙb2r+1(h,вИТh)+O(ќµ2)+вЛѓвАЙ.
ќЮ2r+1вАЛ=a1вАЛa2r+1вАЛ(h)+a2вАЛa2r+1вАЛ(вИТh)+ќµa2вАЛa2r+1вА≤вАЛ(вИТh)+a1вАЛa2вАЛb2r+1вАЛ(h,вИТh)+O(ќµ2)+вЛѓ.

Split into odd/even parts:
a2r+1(h)=a2r+1\odd(h)+a2r+1\even(h),
a2r+1вАЛ(h)=a2r+1\oddвАЛ(h)+a2r+1\evenвАЛ(h),

so
a2r+1(вИТh)=вИТa2r+1\odd(h)+a2r+1\even(h).
a2r+1вАЛ(вИТh)=вИТa2r+1\oddвАЛ(h)+a2r+1\evenвАЛ(h).

Hence on the exact symmetric locus ќµ=0ќµ=0,
ќЮ2r+1=(a1вИТa2)a2r+1\odd(h)+(a1+a2)a2r+1\even(h)+a1a2вАЙb2r+1(h,вИТh)+вЛѓвАЙ.
ќЮ2r+1вАЛ=(a1вАЛвИТa2вАЛ)a2r+1\oddвАЛ(h)+(a1вАЛ+a2вАЛ)a2r+1\evenвАЛ(h)+a1вАЛa2вАЛb2r+1вАЛ(h,вИТh)+вЛѓ.

That is the exact local resonance normal form.

This is much better than the earlier scalar ratio version, because it works directly at the quotient-vector level.

So the leading symmetric-resonance cancellation conditions are:
(a1вИТa2)a1\odd(h)+(a1+a2)a1\even(h)вЙИ0,
(a1вАЛвИТa2вАЛ)a1\oddвАЛ(h)+(a1вАЛ+a2вАЛ)a1\evenвАЛ(h)вЙИ0,
(a1вИТa2)a3\odd(h)+(a1+a2)a3\even(h)вЙИ0,
(a1вАЛвИТa2вАЛ)a3\oddвАЛ(h)+(a1вАЛ+a2вАЛ)a3\evenвАЛ(h)вЙИ0,
(a1вИТa2)a5\odd(h)+(a1+a2)a5\even(h)вЙИ0.
(a1вАЛвИТa2вАЛ)a5\oddвАЛ(h)+(a1вАЛ+a2вАЛ)a5\evenвАЛ(h)вЙИ0.

This is the right finite-dimensional resonance system.
3. The transverse derivative to resonance

Now differentiate in ќµќµ. At ќµ=0ќµ=0,
вИВќµќЮ2r+1=a2вАЙa2r+1вА≤(вИТh)+a1a2вАЙвИВ2b2r+1(h,вИТh)+вЛѓвАЙ.
вИВќµвАЛќЮ2r+1вАЛ=a2вАЛa2r+1вА≤вАЛ(вИТh)+a1вАЛa2вАЛвИВ2вАЛb2r+1вАЛ(h,вИТh)+вЛѓ.

At leading order in amplitudes this is
a2вАЙa2r+1вА≤(вИТh).
a2вАЛa2r+1вА≤вАЛ(вИТh).

So:
Near h2=вИТh1, the transverse escape from the resonance locus is controlled to first order by the derivative of the one-pair jet curve evaluated at вИТh.
Near h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ, the transverse escape from the resonance locus is controlled to first order by the derivative of the one-pair jet curve evaluated at вИТh.вАЛ

That means even if exact symmetry creates partial cancellation, moving off the resonance locus should generically restore a nonzero three-jet signal unless the derivative data are also specially aligned.

That is another real reduction.
4. What this implies for the Jacobian

Now we can state the symmetry-locus transversality test.

At a point on either symmetry locus, the Jacobian columns corresponding to h1,h2h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ reduce to combinations of
AвА≤(h),AвА≤(вИТh),
AвА≤(h),AвА≤(вИТh),

while the a1,a2a1вАЛ,a2вАЛ columns reduce to combinations of
A(h),A(вИТh).
A(h),A(вИТh).

So the Jacobian can only fail badly if the four vectors
A(h), A(вИТh), AвА≤(h), AвА≤(вИТh)
A(h), A(вИТh), AвА≤(h), AвА≤(вИТh)

are specially dependent.

Away from exact parity collapse, that is still a strong condition.

Thus the right next local theorem is:
On the symmetry loci, the transverse derivative of F2 is nonzero unless the one-pair jet curve and its derivative satisfy an additional special dependence relation.
On the symmetry loci, the transverse derivative of F2вАЛ is nonzero unless the one-pair jet curve and its derivative satisfy an additional special dependence relation.вАЛ

This means the symmetry loci are not automatically zero sets. They are only potential zero sets if another finite-dimensional condition holds.
5. The clean finite-dimensional obstruction

So the whole k=2k=2 local problem has now been reduced to checking whether there exist (a1,a2,h)(a1вАЛ,a2вАЛ,h) such that the three vectors
(a1вИТa2)a1\odd(h)+(a1+a2)a1\even(h),
(a1вАЛвИТa2вАЛ)a1\oddвАЛ(h)+(a1вАЛ+a2вАЛ)a1\evenвАЛ(h),
(a1вИТa2)a3\odd(h)+(a1+a2)a3\even(h),
(a1вАЛвИТa2вАЛ)a3\oddвАЛ(h)+(a1вАЛ+a2вАЛ)a3\evenвАЛ(h),
(a1вИТa2)a5\odd(h)+(a1+a2)a5\even(h)
(a1вАЛвИТa2вАЛ)a5\oddвАЛ(h)+(a1вАЛ+a2вАЛ)a5\evenвАЛ(h)

all vanish, and their transverse derivatives along ќµќµ vanish compatibly.

That is now a one-variable finite-dimensional algebra problem along each symmetry locus.

This is a much smaller problem than where we started.
6. What is proved, and what is not

What is now genuinely established structurally:

    off the symmetry loci, local transversality reduces to nondegeneracy of the pair-jet curve;

    near h2=h1h2вАЛ=h1вАЛ, transverse escape is controlled by AвА≤(h)AвА≤(h);

    near h2=вИТh1h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ, transverse escape is controlled by AвА≤(вИТh)AвА≤(вИТh);

    so the symmetry loci are not automatically persistent zero sets.

What is still not proved:

    that the symmetry-locus algebra system has no genuinely anomalous solutions;

    equivalently, that the pair-jet curve and its parity split do not satisfy the necessary dependence relations.

7. The next theorem to attack

The sharp next statement is:
Symmetry-locus exclusion lemma

On each symmetry locus h2=¬±h1h2вАЛ=¬±h1вАЛ, the reduced one-variable three-jet system has no genuinely anomalous solutions except possibly degenerate clustering/boundary points.

That is now the smallest meaningful theorem left on the Jacobian branch.

And the nice part is: it is genuinely one-dimensional in hh, not four-dimensional anymore.

That is the right next continuation.

Continue

Good. We can now reduce the Jacobian branch one step further.

At this point the k=2k=2 problem is no longer really four-dimensional. Along each symmetry locus it becomes a one-variable problem in hh, with one amplitude ratio parameter.

That is the right place to attack.
1. Symmetric locus h2=вИТh1h2вАЛ=вИТh1вАЛ

Set
h1=h,h2=вИТh,ќЉ:=a1вИТa2,ќљ:=a1+a2.
h1вАЛ=h,h2вАЛ=вИТh,ќЉ:=a1вАЛвИТa2вАЛ,ќљ:=a1вАЛ+a2вАЛ.

Then the leading-order three quotient jets become
ќЮ2r+1=ќЉвАЙa2r+1\odd(h)+ќљвАЙa2r+1\even(h)+a1a2вАЙb2r+1(h,вИТh)+вЛѓвАЙ.
ќЮ2r+1вАЛ=ќЉa2r+1\oddвАЛ(h)+ќљa2r+1\evenвАЛ(h)+a1вАЛa2вАЛb2r+1вАЛ(h,вИТh)+вЛѓ.

Ignoring the higher interaction term for the moment, the vanishing condition is
ќЉвАЙa2r+1\odd(h)+ќљвАЙa2r+1\even(h)=0,r=0,1,2.
ќЉa2r+1\oddвАЛ(h)+ќљa2r+1\evenвАЛ(h)=0,r=0,1,2.

Since (ќЉ,ќљ)вЙ†(0,0)(ќЉ,ќљ)оА†=(0,0), this means that for a given hh, the odd and even parts must be collinear with the same scalar ratio across the first three odd orders.

Equivalently, define
ѕБ2r+1(h):=вИТa2r+1\even(h)a2r+1\odd(h)
ѕБ2r+1вАЛ(h):=вИТa2r+1\oddвАЛ(h)a2r+1\evenвАЛ(h)вАЛ

in any fixed scalar quotient coordinate where the denominator is nonzero. Then resonance requires
ѕБ1(h)=ѕБ3(h)=ѕБ5(h)=ќЉќљ.
ѕБ1вАЛ(h)=ѕБ3вАЛ(h)=ѕБ5вАЛ(h)=ќљќЉвАЛ.

So the symmetric-locus problem is reduced to:
Can the three parity ratios ѕБ1(h),ѕБ3(h),ѕБ5(h) coincide?
Can the three parity ratios ѕБ1вАЛ(h),ѕБ3вАЛ(h),ѕБ5вАЛ(h) coincide?вАЛ

That is now a one-variable obstruction problem.
2. Why this is promising

From the asymptotic structure already extracted:

    a1\odd(h)a1\oddвАЛ(h) is order hвИТ5hвИТ5,

    a1\even(h)a1\evenвАЛ(h) is order hвИТ6hвИТ6,

so
ѕБ1(h)вЙНhвИТ1.
ѕБ1вАЛ(h)вЙНhвИТ1.

For cubic order, assuming the even cubic quotient really survives, we have

    a3\odd(h)a3\oddвАЛ(h) order hвИТ7hвИТ7,

    a3\even(h)a3\evenвАЛ(h) order hвИТ10hвИТ10,

so
ѕБ3(h)вЙНhвИТ3.
ѕБ3вАЛ(h)вЙНhвИТ3.

And similarly, the quintic ratio should decay even faster:
ѕБ5(h)вЙНhвИТ5
ѕБ5вАЛ(h)вЙНhвИТ5

under the same structural pattern.

So asymptotically these three ratios live on different scales:
ѕБ1(h)вИЉC1hвИТ1,ѕБ3(h)вИЉC3hвИТ3,ѕБ5(h)вИЉC5hвИТ5.
ѕБ1вАЛ(h)вИЉC1вАЛhвИТ1,ѕБ3вАЛ(h)вИЉC3вАЛhвИТ3,ѕБ5вАЛ(h)вИЉC5вАЛhвИТ5.

That immediately implies they cannot all agree for large вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£, unless some coefficient vanishes unexpectedly.

So the large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ symmetric-locus case is already morally dead once the even cubic and quintic quotient parts survive.
3. Coincident locus h2=h1h2вАЛ=h1вАЛ

Now set
h1=h,h2=h.
h1вАЛ=h,h2вАЛ=h.

Then the leading three jets are
ќЮ2r+1=(a1+a2)a2r+1(h)+a1a2вАЙb2r+1(h,h)+вЛѓвАЙ.
ќЮ2r+1вАЛ=(a1вАЛ+a2вАЛ)a2r+1вАЛ(h)+a1вАЛa2вАЛb2r+1вАЛ(h,h)+вЛѓ.

So unless the interaction term precisely cancels the common one-pair direction, the coincident locus is not a zero set.

At leading order in small amplitudes, this is even simpler:
ќЮ2r+1вЙИ(a1+a2)a2r+1(h).
ќЮ2r+1вАЛвЙИ(a1вАЛ+a2вАЛ)a2r+1вАЛ(h).

Since a1+a2>0a1вАЛ+a2вАЛ>0 for a genuine two-pair core, this can vanish only if the one-pair quotient jet itself vanishes. But that would contradict the one-pair nondegeneracy built into the whole framework.

So the coincident locus is much less threatening than the symmetric one. It looks more like a degenerate clustering parameterization than a genuine obstruction.
4. The true remaining local theorem

So the sharp local theorem is now:
Symmetric-locus ratio exclusion theorem

There is no hh in the compact normalized range such that
ѕБ1(h)=ѕБ3(h)=ѕБ5(h)
ѕБ1вАЛ(h)=ѕБ3вАЛ(h)=ѕБ5вАЛ(h)

for a genuinely anomalous two-pair core.

That is the one-dimensional core problem on the Jacobian branch.

Everything else has been reduced away:

    off the symmetry loci, generic transversality should rule out zeros;

    on h1=h2h1вАЛ=h2вАЛ, the leading map is basically one-pair and should not vanish;

    on h1=вИТh2h1вАЛ=вИТh2вАЛ, the whole problem is whether these parity ratios can coincide.

5. The cleanest next attack

The next thing to do is not more abstract Jacobian talk. It is to turn the ratio problem into scalar equations.

Define
R13(h):=ѕБ1(h)вИТѕБ3(h),R15(h):=ѕБ1(h)вИТѕБ5(h).
R13вАЛ(h):=ѕБ1вАЛ(h)вИТѕБ3вАЛ(h),R15вАЛ(h):=ѕБ1вАЛ(h)вИТѕБ5вАЛ(h).

Then the symmetric-locus obstruction reduces to
R13(h)=0,R15(h)=0.
R13вАЛ(h)=0,R15вАЛ(h)=0.вАЛ

So if one can show these two one-variable functions do not vanish simultaneously on the compact normalized range except at degenerate points, then the symmetric resonance locus is excluded.

That is now the sharpest next theorem.
6. Where RH still depends on this

Even after all these reductions, RH is still not proved because we still do not have the final local theorem that a genuine finite off-line core must leave a nonzero quotient odd-jet trace.

On the k=2k=2 branch, that has now been reduced to either:

    proving the three-jet map F2F2вАЛ has no genuine anomalous zeros, or more specifically

    proving the symmetry-locus ratio system above has no genuine anomalous solutions.

That is a much smaller target than where we started, but it is still open.

The next exact move is to formalize R13,R15R13вАЛ,R15вАЛ and attack whether they can have common zeros on the compact normalized hh-range.

Continue

The next honest continuation is to make R13R13вАЛ and R15R15вАЛ more explicit in terms of asymptotic orders, because that already gives a strong obstruction.

Recall the symmetric-locus ratios
ѕБ1(h)=ќ±1\even(h)ќ±1\odd(h),ѕБ3(h)=ќ±3\even(h)ќ±3\odd(h),ѕБ5(h)=ќ±5\even(h)ќ±5\odd(h),
ѕБ1вАЛ(h)=ќ±1\oddвАЛ(h)ќ±1\evenвАЛ(h)вАЛ,ѕБ3вАЛ(h)=ќ±3\oddвАЛ(h)ќ±3\evenвАЛ(h)вАЛ,ѕБ5вАЛ(h)=ќ±5\oddвАЛ(h)ќ±5\evenвАЛ(h)вАЛ,

up to the harmless overall sign convention from the resonance equations.

We defined
R13(h):=ѕБ1(h)вИТѕБ3(h),R15(h):=ѕБ1(h)вИТѕБ5(h).
R13вАЛ(h):=ѕБ1вАЛ(h)вИТѕБ3вАЛ(h),R15вАЛ(h):=ѕБ1вАЛ(h)вИТѕБ5вАЛ(h).

A symmetric-resonance collapse can only happen if
R13(h)=R15(h)=0.
R13вАЛ(h)=R15вАЛ(h)=0.

So the right next step is to understand the asymptotics of these two one-variable functions.
1. Asymptotic orders

From the current structure, the natural orders are
ќ±1\odd(h)вИЉc1hвИТ5,ќ±1\even(h)вИЉe1hвИТ6,
ќ±1\oddвАЛ(h)вИЉc1вАЛhвИТ5,ќ±1\evenвАЛ(h)вИЉe1вАЛhвИТ6,

so
ѕБ1(h)вИЉe1c1hвИТ1.
ѕБ1вАЛ(h)вИЉc1вАЛe1вАЛвАЛhвИТ1.

For cubic order, provided the even cubic quotient survives,
ќ±3\odd(h)вИЉc3hвИТ7,ќ±3\even(h)вИЉe3hвИТ10,
ќ±3\oddвАЛ(h)вИЉc3вАЛhвИТ7,ќ±3\evenвАЛ(h)вИЉe3вАЛhвИТ10,

so
ѕБ3(h)вИЉe3c3hвИТ3.
ѕБ3вАЛ(h)вИЉc3вАЛe3вАЛвАЛhвИТ3.

And at quintic order, the same pattern suggests
ќ±5\odd(h)вИЉc5hвИТ9,ќ±5\even(h)вИЉe5hвИТ14,
ќ±5\oddвАЛ(h)вИЉc5вАЛhвИТ9,ќ±5\evenвАЛ(h)вИЉe5вАЛhвИТ14,

hence
ѕБ5(h)вИЉe5c5hвИТ5.
ѕБ5вАЛ(h)вИЉc5вАЛe5вАЛвАЛhвИТ5.

So asymptotically,
R13(h)вИЉe1c1hвИТ1вИТe3c3hвИТ3,

R15(h)вИЉe1c1hвИТ1вИТe5c5hвИТ5.
R15вАЛ(h)вИЉc1вАЛe1вАЛвАЛhвИТ1вИТc5вАЛe5вАЛвАЛhвИТ5.

This is already very strong.

If e1/c1вЙ†0e1вАЛ/c1вАЛоА†=0, then for large вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£,
R13(h)вИЉe1c1hвИТ1,R15(h)вИЉe1c1hвИТ1,
R13вАЛ(h)вИЉc1вАЛe1вАЛвАЛhвИТ1,R15вАЛ(h)вИЉc1вАЛe1вАЛвАЛhвИТ1,

so neither can vanish.

If e1=0e1вАЛ=0 but e3вЙ†0e3вАЛоА†=0, then
R13(h)вИЉвИТe3c3hвИТ3,
R13вАЛ(h)вИЉвИТc3вАЛe3вАЛвАЛhвИТ3,
