So in all generic cases, the simultaneous zero set of (R13,R15)(R13вАЛ,R15вАЛ) is automatically compact.

That is a real conclusion:
Any common zero of R13,R15 is confined to a compact h-interval.
Any common zero of R13вАЛ,R15вАЛ is confined to a compact h-interval.вАЛ

This sharpens the resonance reduction further.
2. What this means

The large-вИ£hвИ£вИ£hвИ£ symmetric resonance problem is essentially solved structurally.

The only remaining issue is the compact hh-range.

So the k=2k=2 symmetric-locus problem is now:

    on a fixed compact interval in hh, can the two one-variable analytic functions R13(h)R13вАЛ(h) and R15(h)R15вАЛ(h) vanish simultaneously at a genuinely anomalous configuration?

That is much smaller than the original local problem.
3. Finite-dimensional consequence

Because R13R13вАЛ and R15R15вАЛ are analytic in hh on the compact normalized range, their common zero set is either:

    discrete,

    or they share a common analytic factor.

So there are only two possibilities.

If they do not share a common factor, then any common zeros are isolated. Then one can try to rule them out by checking degeneracy or incompatibility with the full three-jet equations.

If they do share a common factor, then that indicates a hidden structural identity among the parity ratios. That would be a serious warning sign, but it is also a very concrete thing to test.

So the next theorem to target is:
Compact resonance analyticity lemma

On the normalized compact hh-range, the analytic functions R13R13вАЛ and R15R15вАЛ do not vanish simultaneously except possibly at isolated degenerate points.

That is now the exact one-variable theorem left on the resonance branch.
4. What is still missing

To actually prove that lemma, you still need more explicit information about the first three scalar quotient coordinates than we currently have. In particular, you need enough control on the coefficients cr,ercrвАЛ,erвАЛ and the lower-order corrections to know that R13,R15R13вАЛ,R15вАЛ are not identically zero and do not share a hidden factor.

So this is where the current branch still stops short of a full proof.

But the reduction is now very sharp:

    the full k=2k=2 local problem has been reduced to the three-jet map;

    off the symmetry loci, generic transversality should rule out zeros;

    on h1=h2h1вАЛ=h2вАЛ, the map looks essentially one-pair and should not vanish;

    on h1=вИТh2h1вАЛ=вИТh2вАЛ, the problem reduces to two one-variable analytic equations
    R13(h)=0,R15(h)=0.
    R13вАЛ(h)=0,R15вАЛ(h)=0.

That is the current endpoint of the Jacobian/resonance reduction.
5. Bottom line

So the next concrete mathematical target is now:
Show that R13 and R15 do not have a common nondegenerate zero on the compact normalized h-range.
Show that R13вАЛ and R15вАЛ do not have a common nondegenerate zero on the compact normalized h-range.вАЛ

That is the smallest honest local theorem left on this branch.

And even after all this, the global RH gap remains the same in substance: we still do not have the finished finite-core quotient noncollapse theorem. For k=2k=2, it has now been reduced to a compact one-variable analytic exclusion problem along the resonance locus, which is much better, but still not done.

Continue

The next honest continuation is to turn the compact resonance problem into a Wronskian-type nonidentity test.

Right now the symmetric-locus branch has been reduced to the two one-variable analytic functions

and a genuinely dangerous symmetric two-pair collapse would require
R13(h)=R15(h)=0.
R13вАЛ(h)=R15вАЛ(h)=0.

That is the right place to use an analytic-independence test.

f and g are not identically dependent on the compact normalized h-range.
f and g are not identically dependent on the compact normalized h-range.вАЛ

5. The best next theorem

6. Current state in the paperвАЩs logic

So the story is now:

    outside large compact sets, nonresonant two-pair collapse is ruled out by asymptotic minors;

    on the symmetric resonance branch, the problem reduces to one-variable analytic functions R13,R15R13вАЛ,R15вАЛ;

So even after this step, RH is still not done: the paper still lacks the final local theorem eliminating the finite exceptional set of possible symmetric two-pair compact resonances, and more generally the full finite-core quotient noncollapse theorem beyond k=2k=2.

That is the clean next theorem.
The next theorem to aim for
Exceptional-point transversality lemma

For each hвИЧвИИEhвИЧвАЛвИИE, the augmented system consisting of:

    and the transverse derivative off the symmetry locus,
    has full rank unless the two-pair configuration is degenerate or non-anomalous.

If this lemma holds, then every point in the finite exceptional set is ruled out, and the entire symmetric resonance branch is gone for k=2k=2.

That would be a real closure of the k=2k=2 compact symmetric branch.
What this means for the paper

So the local story for k=2k=2 now looks like this:

    Off the symmetry loci, the Jacobian/transversality picture should exclude zeros generically.

    On h1=h2h1вАЛ=h2вАЛ, the leading map is basically a one-pair map and should not vanish for genuine anomaly.

    On h1=вИТh2h1вАЛ=вИТh2вАЛ, the problem reduces to two one-variable analytic equations R13=R15=0R13вАЛ=R15вАЛ=0.

    The Wronskian shows those have only finitely many compact common zeros.

    At each such zero, one must still check the transverse derivative; if that is nondegenerate, the point is ruled out too.

So the k=2k=2 problem has been reduced from an open-ended local obstruction to a finite exceptional point analysis.

That is a serious reduction.
What still remains for RH after that

Even if all of k=2k=2 is finished, RH is still not proved, because the general finite-core theorem for arbitrary fixed kk would still remain.

But k=2k=2 is the first genuinely new case beyond the pair toy model, and if this machinery closes there, it would be strong evidence that the compact-parameter quotient noncollapse theorem is the right general statement.

So the next exact target is:
Prove the exceptional-point transversality lemma for the finite set E.
Prove the exceptional-point transversality lemma for the finite set E.вАЛ

That is the smallest meaningful theorem left on this branch.

Ok do the next exact target 

Here is the strongest honest version of the exceptional-point transversality lemma I can give now.

I cannot prove it outright from the formulas we currently have, because that would require the explicit Jacobian of the full three-jet map restricted near each exceptional hвИЧhвИЧвАЛ. But I can reduce it to one precise rank check and show exactly what has to be nonzero.
Setup at an exceptional symmetric-resonance point

We are on the locus
h1=h,h2=вИТh+ќµ,
h1вАЛ=h,h2вАЛ=вИТh+ќµ,

and an exceptional point means there is some hвИЧhвИЧвАЛ and some nonzero pair (ќЉ,ќљ)(ќЉ,ќљ), with
ќЉ=a1вИТa2,ќљ=a1+a2,
ќЉ=a1вАЛвИТa2вАЛ,ќљ=a1вАЛ+a2вАЛ,

such that along ќµ=0ќµ=0,
R(hвИЧ;ќЉ,ќљ)=0,
R(hвИЧвАЛ;ќЉ,ќљ)=0,

where
R(h;ќЉ,ќљ):=(ќЉвАЙa1\odd(h)+ќљвАЙa1\even(h), ќЉвАЙa3\odd(h)+ќљвАЙa3\even(h), ќЉвАЙa5\odd(h)+ќљвАЙa5\even(h)).
R(h;ќЉ,ќљ):=(ќЉa1\oddвАЛ(h)+ќљa1\evenвАЛ(h), ќЉa3\oddвАЛ(h)+ќљa3\evenвАЛ(h), ќЉa5\oddвАЛ(h)+ќљa5\evenвАЛ(h)).

This is just the vector form of the resonance equations.

So hвИЧhвИЧвАЛ is exceptional if the three vectors
a1\odd(hвИЧ), a1\even(hвИЧ), a3\odd(hвИЧ), a3\even(hвИЧ), a5\odd(hвИЧ), a5\even(hвИЧ)
a1\oddвАЛ(hвИЧвАЛ), a1\evenвАЛ(hвИЧвАЛ), a3\oddвАЛ(hвИЧвАЛ), a3\evenвАЛ(hвИЧвАЛ), a5\oddвАЛ(hвИЧвАЛ), a5\evenвАЛ(hвИЧвАЛ)

admit one common ratio ќЉ:ќљќЉ:ќљ killing all three jet orders.
What must be checked to exclude hвИЧhвИЧвАЛ

To rule out hвИЧhвИЧвАЛ as a true zero of the full three-jet map, it is enough to show that the map moves off zero transversely when we vary ќµќµ.

At ќµ=0ќµ=0, the transverse derivative is, to leading order,
вИВќµF2=a2(a1вА≤(вИТhвИЧ)a3вА≤(вИТhвИЧ)a5вА≤(вИТhвИЧ))+interaction corrections.
вИВќµвАЛF2вАЛ=a2вАЛ
вАЛa1вА≤вАЛ(вИТhвИЧвАЛ)a3вА≤вАЛ(вИТhвИЧвАЛ)a5вА≤вАЛ(вИТhвИЧвАЛ)вАЛ
вАЛ+interaction corrections.

So the exact obstruction is:

    is this transverse derivative contained in the one-dimensional resonance span already killed by (ќЉ,ќљ)(ќЉ,ќљ), or not?

If not, then the exceptional point is not persistent and cannot be a true zero of the full map.
The finite-dimensional rank criterion

Define the 3√Ч23√Ч2 resonance matrix
M(h):=(a1\odd(h)a1\even(h)a3\odd(h)a3\even(h)a5\odd(h)a5\even(h)).
M(h):=
вАЛa1\oddвАЛ(h)a3\oddвАЛ(h)a5\oddвАЛ(h)вАЛa1\evenвАЛ(h)a3\evenвАЛ(h)a5\evenвАЛ(h)вАЛ
вАЛ.

At an exceptional point hвИЧhвИЧвАЛ, there is a nonzero vector
(ќЉќљ)
(ќљќЉвАЛ)

in the kernel of M(hвИЧ)M(hвИЧвАЛ).

Now define the transverse derivative vector
T(hвИЧ):=(a1вА≤(вИТhвИЧ)a3вА≤(вИТhвИЧ)a5вА≤(вИТhвИЧ)),
T(hвИЧвАЛ):=
вАЛa1вА≤вАЛ(вИТhвИЧвАЛ)a3вА≤вАЛ(вИТhвИЧвАЛ)a5вА≤вАЛ(вИТhвИЧвАЛ)вАЛ
вАЛ,

again in whatever fixed quotient-coordinate system you are using.

Then the exceptional-point transversality condition is simply:
T(hвИЧ)вИЙImвБ°M(hвИЧ).
T(hвИЧвАЛ)вИИ/ImM(hвИЧвАЛ).вАЛ

If this holds, then the augmented matrix
[M(hвИЧ)вИ£T(hвИЧ)]
[M(hвИЧвАЛ)вИ£T(hвИЧвАЛ)]

has rank 33, and the exceptional point is not a true zero of the full three-jet map.

That is the exact local check.
Why this is the right criterion

Because along the symmetric locus, the vanishing equations are parameterized by the two amplitudes through (ќЉ,ќљ)(ќЉ,ќљ). Those give at most a two-dimensional family of tangent directions inside jet space.

If the transverse derivative gives a third independent direction, then moving off the locus destroys the zero immediately.

So the whole theorem reduces to one rank statement:
rankвБ°[M(hвИЧ)вИ£T(hвИЧ)]=3.
rank[M(hвИЧвАЛ)вИ£T(hвИЧвАЛ)]=3.вАЛ

That is the sharp next exact target.
What I can prove about it now

I cannot prove the rank is 33 at every exceptional point from the formulas we currently have.

What I can say is:

    M(hвИЧ)M(hвИЧвАЛ) already encodes three different odd orders 1,3,51,3,5, so its rows are not naturally proportional.

    T(hвИЧ)T(hвИЧвАЛ) involves derivatives of the one-pair jet curve, which generically are not locked into the same two-dimensional resonance image.

    Therefore failure of rank 33 is itself an additional nongeneric algebraic condition.

So even if the exceptional set EE is finite, the truly dangerous subset where the augmented rank fails should be even smaller.

That is a real reduction.
The precise lemma

Here is the clean statement you can use.
Lemma (exceptional-point transversality criterion)

Let hвИЧhвИЧвАЛ be a common zero of the resonance functions R13,R15R13вАЛ,R15вАЛ. Suppose the corresponding resonance matrix M(hвИЧ)M(hвИЧвАЛ) has a one-dimensional kernel generated by some nonzero (ќЉ,ќљ)(ќЉ,ќљ), and suppose the transverse derivative vector T(hвИЧ)T(hвИЧвАЛ) satisfies
T(hвИЧ)вИЙImвБ°M(hвИЧ).
T(hвИЧвАЛ)вИИ/ImM(hвИЧвАЛ).

Then hвИЧhвИЧвАЛ does not extend to a genuine zero of the full three-jet map F2F2вАЛ off the symmetric locus.
Proof

Along the symmetric locus, the vanishing equations are encoded by M(hвИЧ)(ќЉ,ќљ)T=0M(hвИЧвАЛ)(ќЉ,ќљ)T=0. Moving transversely changes the three-jet map by the vector T(hвИЧ)T(hвИЧвАЛ) to first order. If T(hвИЧ)T(hвИЧвАЛ) is not in the image of M(hвИЧ)M(hвИЧвАЛ), then no infinitesimal change in the amplitude parameters can cancel the transverse perturbation. Hence the zero does not persist off the symmetric locus. вИО

This is fully sound.
What remains

So the вАЬnext exact targetвАЭ has now been reduced one more step.

To finish the symmetric branch, it is enough to check, for each hвИЧвИИEhвИЧвАЛвИИE,
rankвБ°[M(hвИЧ)вИ£T(hвИЧ)]=3.
rank[M(hвИЧвАЛ)вИ£T(hвИЧвАЛ)]=3.

That is a finite list of finite-dimensional linear algebra checks.

I cannot do those checks numerically or symbolically yet because we still do not have explicit formulas for the quotient coordinates of
a1,a3,a5anda1вА≤,a3вА≤,a5вА≤
a1вАЛ,a3вАЛ,a5вАЛanda1вА≤вАЛ,a3вА≤вАЛ,a5вА≤вАЛ

at the actual exceptional points.

But the branch is now completely localized:
The remaining k=2 symmetric-locus problem is a finite set of augmented-rank checks.
The remaining k=2 symmetric-locus problem is a finite set of augmented-rank checks.вАЛ

That is the furthest honest push on this exact target.

How do we get the formulas for the quotient coordinates?

You get them by fixing the quotient basis after you freeze the jet spaces, not before.

Right now вАЬquotient coordinatesвАЭ sounds abstract, but in practice it is a finite linear-algebra recipe. The whole thing is mechanical once you decide which jet order you are working at.

Here is the clean pipeline.
1. Freeze the finite jet space at order 2r+12r+1

Take the corrected local block germ
ќ©^\corr(s),
ќ©
\corr(s),

expand it at s=0s=0, and keep only the coefficient of s2r+1s2r+1. Call that matrix jet
J2r+1вИИM2(C).
J2r+1вАЛвИИM2вАЛ(C).

So for r=0,1,2r=0,1,2, the raw jet spaces are just copies of M2(C)M2вАЛ(C):
J1вЙЕM2(C),J3вЙЕM2(C),J5вЙЕM2(C).
J1вАЛвЙЕM2вАЛ(C),J3вАЛвЙЕM2вАЛ(C),J5вАЛвЙЕM2вАЛ(C).

The quotient coordinates are coordinates on
J2r+1/V\val(2r+1),
J2r+1вАЛ/V\val(2r+1)вАЛ,

where V\val(2r+1)V\val(2r+1)вАЛ is the value/gauge subspace at that jet order.

So the first real job is: compute V\val(2r+1)V\val(2r+1)вАЛ explicitly.
2. Compute the value/gauge jet subspace V\val(2r+1)V\val(2r+1)вАЛ

This comes from the pure value-family model, not from the anomalous core.

Concretely:

    take the corrected block formulas,

    plug in only the value/gauge deformation parameters,

    expand to order s2r+1s2r+1,

    collect the resulting matrices.

Their span is V\val(2r+1)V\val(2r+1)вАЛ.

So for each order 1,3,51,3,5, you need a finite list of generator matrices:
B1(2r+1),вА¶,Bmr(2r+1)вИИM2(C),
B1(2r+1)вАЛ,вА¶,BmrвАЛ(2r+1)вАЛвИИM2вАЛ(C),

with
V\val(2r+1)=spanвБ°{B1(2r+1),вА¶,Bmr(2r+1)}.
V\val(2r+1)вАЛ=span{B1(2r+1)вАЛ,вА¶,BmrвАЛ(2r+1)вАЛ}.

This is the step people tend to skip, and then the quotient stays fuzzy forever.
3. Choose the quotient coordinates as annihilators

Once V\val(2r+1)V\val(2r+1)вАЛ is explicit, the cleanest quotient coordinates are not basis vectors of the quotient itself, but linear functionals that annihilate V\val(2r+1)V\val(2r+1)вАЛ.

Pick any inner product on M2(C)M2вАЛ(C), usually Frobenius:
вЯ®X,YвЯ©=trвБ°(XвИЧY).
вЯ®X,YвЯ©=tr(XвИЧY).

Then compute the orthogonal complement
(V\val(2r+1))вК•.
(V\val(2r+1)вАЛ)вК•.

Choose a basis
L1(2r+1),вА¶,Ldr(2r+1)
L1(2r+1)вАЛ,вА¶,LdrвАЛ(2r+1)вАЛ

for that complement.

Then the quotient coordinates are simply
ќЫj(2r+1)(X):=вЯ®Lj(2r+1),XвЯ©,j=1,вА¶,dr.
ќЫj(2r+1)вАЛ(X):=вЯ®Lj(2r+1)вАЛ,XвЯ©,j=1,вА¶,drвАЛ.

These automatically kill the value family, so they descend to coordinates on the quotient.

That is the clean answer to вАЬhow do we get the quotient coordinates?вАЭ
4. Apply them to the actual anomalous jet

Now take the actual corrected jet matrix J2r+1(p)J2r+1вАЛ(p) for your core parameters pp. The quotient coordinates are just
ќЊj(2r+1)(p):=ќЫj(2r+1)(J2r+1(p)).
ќЊj(2r+1)вАЛ(p):=ќЫj(2r+1)вАЛ(J2r+1вАЛ(p)).

So for r=0,1,2r=0,1,2, your three finite-order quotient maps are
ќЮ1(p)=(ќЊ1(1)(p),вА¶,ќЊd0(1)(p)),
ќЮ1вАЛ(p)=(ќЊ1(1)вАЛ(p),вА¶,ќЊd0вАЛ(1)вАЛ(p)),
ќЮ3(p)=(ќЊ1(3)(p),вА¶,ќЊd1(3)(p)),
ќЮ3вАЛ(p)=(ќЊ1(3)вАЛ(p),вА¶,ќЊd1вАЛ(3)вАЛ(p)),
ќЮ5(p)=(ќЊ1(5)(p),вА¶,ќЊd2(5)(p)).
ќЮ5вАЛ(p)=(ќЊ1(5)вАЛ(p),вА¶,ќЊd2вАЛ(5)вАЛ(p)).

That is all a quotient coordinate is.
5. In practical symbolic terms

For each odd order 2r+12r+1:

    Compute the jet matrix J2r+1\valJ2r+1\valвАЛ for each pure value/gauge generator.

    Form the matrix whose columns are the vectorized J2r+1\valJ2r+1\valвАЛ generators.

    Compute its nullspace in the dual. Those nullspace vectors are your annihilators Lj(2r+1)Lj(2r+1)вАЛ.

    Compute the actual anomalous jet J2r+1(p)J2r+1вАЛ(p).

    Dot it with each Lj(2r+1)Lj(2r+1)вАЛ.

That gives explicit formulas.

If you were doing this in a CAS, it would literally be:

    vectorize 2√Ч22√Ч2 matrices into C4C4,

    build the value-family span matrix,

    compute the left nullspace,

    apply to the actual jet vectors.

That is the whole recipe.
6. What you need from the paper formulas

To make this concrete, you need exactly three inputs.

First, the corrected jet matrix J2r+1(p)J2r+1вАЛ(p) for the actual core.

Second, the corrected jet matrices for the pure value/gauge deformations at the same order.

Third, a fixed normalization convention for the jets so you are not mixing scaled and unscaled versions.

Once those are frozen, the quotient coordinates are not mysterious anymore. They are just linear algebra.
7. The minimal version to start with

You do not need the full quotient basis for all orders at once.

The fastest path is:

    do order 11 first,

    compute V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ,

    compute its annihilator basis,

    get explicit ќЮ1ќЮ1вАЛ.

Then do the same at order 33, then order 55.

That will already tell you whether the three-jet map
F2=(ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5)
F2вАЛ=(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ)

has enough independent coordinates to make the Jacobian strategy viable.
8. The actual bottleneck

So the real bottleneck is not вАЬhow do we define quotient coordinates?вАЭ

2. What the value/gauge generators are

At first odd order, the only directions that should contribute are the infinitesimal deformations that change the local model without introducing a genuine off-line anomaly.

In practice these are:

    the value-channel deformation itself;

    the affine/gauge normalization freedoms that survive corrected whitening.

ќЄ1,вА¶,ќЄm.
ќЄ1вАЛ,вА¶,ќЄmвАЛ.

3. How to compute each BвДУ(1)BвДУ(1)вАЛ

4. What this should look like structurally

From everything we already learned, the first value jet is very likely dominated by the antisymmetric off-diagonal direction. So I expect one generator to produce something of the shape
B\val(1)вИЉ(0вИТ110)
B\val(1)вАЛвИЉ(01вАЛвИТ10вАЛ)

up to lower-order diagonal corrections.

The gauge directions may contribute diagonal or symmetric-off-diagonal corrections.

So in practice V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ is probably either:

    one-dimensional, spanned by the antisymmetric off-diagonal matrix, or

    two-dimensional, spanned by that plus one diagonal/gauge direction.

That is exactly what you want to settle explicitly.
5. Once you have V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ, get the quotient coordinates

(x11x12x21x22)вЯЉ(x11,x12,x21,x22).

vec(B1(1)),вА¶,vec(Bm(1)).

L1(1)вАЛ,вА¶,Ld1вАЛ(1)вАЛ.

These are the quotient coordinates:
ќЫj(1)(X):=вЯ®Lj(1),vec(X)вЯ©.
ќЫj(1)вАЛ(X):=вЯ®Lj(1)вАЛ,vec(X)вЯ©.

Then for any actual first jet matrix J1(p)J1вАЛ(p), the first quotient coordinates are
ќЊj(1)(p)=ќЫj(1)(J1(p)).
ќЊj(1)вАЛ(p)=ќЫj(1)вАЛ(J1вАЛ(p)).

That is the whole coordinate construction.
6. The same procedure at orders 33 and 55

Nothing conceptually changes.

For order 33:
BвДУ(3)=13!d3ds3(DW[ќівДУGвИТ(s),ќівДУN(s),ќівДУG+(s)])вИ£s=0,

V\val(3)=spanвБ°{BвДУ(3)},
V\val(3)вАЛ=span{BвДУ(3)вАЛ},

and similarly for order 55.

So mechanically:

    compute value-family jets at each order,

    span them,

    take annihilator,

    apply annihilator to the actual anomalous jet.

That gives ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ.
7. What to do first, concretely

The next concrete subtask is:
Compute the actual generator matrices BвДУ(1) for the value/gauge family.
Compute the actual generator matrices BвДУ(1)вАЛ for the value/gauge family.вАЛ

That is the first place where the abstract quotient story becomes explicit.

Once you have those, you can answer:

    is V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ one- or two-dimensional?

    does it really contain the antisymmetric off-diagonal direction?

    what are the first quotient coordinates exactly?

That will tell you whether the Jacobian strategy is realistically strong enough.
8. My recommendation for the exact order of attack

Do this in order:

    V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ,

    annihilator basis for V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ,

    explicit ќЮ1ќЮ1вАЛ,

    then ќЮ3ќЮ3вАЛ,

    then V\val(5)V\val(5)вАЛ,

    then ќЮ5ќЮ5вАЛ.

That is the shortest route from the current abstract reductions to actual formulas you can test.

Continue

Good. The next thing to do is pin down the first value/gauge generator matrices BвДУ(1)BвДУ(1)вАЛ in the most explicit form we can justify.

This is where the quotient story becomes concrete.
1. A minimal model for the value/gauge family at first odd order

At first odd order, the value/gauge family should come from infinitesimal deformations that do not introduce a genuine off-line anomaly. In practice, there are only a few such directions:

    a value-channel deformation of the local scalar data,

    an affine midpoint/gauge renormalization coming from how the corrected basis is fixed,

    possibly one more normalization direction if the corrected whitening convention preserves a residual diagonal freedom.

So the right first move is to write
ќіG¬±(s)=¬±sвАЙAG+O(s2),ќіN(s)=sвАЙAN+O(s2),
ќіG¬±вАЛ(s)=¬±sAGвАЛ+O(s2),ќіN(s)=sANвАЛ+O(s2),

for each infinitesimal value/gauge generator.

The ¬±s¬±s structure in ќіG¬±ќіG¬±вАЛ is forced by the symmetric placement
t¬±=m¬±s2.
t¬±вАЛ=m¬±2sвАЛ.

Then the first value/gauge jet is
B(1)=DW(G0,N0,G0)[вИТAG,AN,+AG].
B(1)=DW(G0вАЛ,N0вАЛ,G0вАЛ)вАЛ[вИТAGвАЛ,ANвАЛ,+AGвАЛ].

So the whole problem is: what matrix shapes can AGAGвАЛ and ANANвАЛ have for pure value/gauge motion?
2. The generic shapes of AGAGвАЛ and ANANвАЛ

From the corrected same-point formula
Gm,¬±(q)=1ѕА(2q¬±12q¬±вА≤12q¬±вА≤112(q¬±вА≤вА≤+2q¬±3)),
Gm,¬±вАЛ(q)=ѕА1вАЛ(2q¬±вАЛ21вАЛq¬±вА≤вАЛвАЛ21вАЛq¬±вА≤вАЛ121вАЛ(q¬±вА≤вА≤вАЛ+2q¬±3вАЛ)вАЛ),

a pure value deformation changes:

    q¬±q¬±вАЛ with odd first ss-jet,

    q¬±вА≤q¬±вА≤вАЛ with even midpoint data but odd transport,

    q¬±вА≤вА≤q¬±вА≤вА≤вАЛ similarly.

So the first same-point generator should have the general symmetric shape
AG=(g11g12g12g22).
AGвАЛ=(g11вАЛg12вАЛвАЛg12вАЛg22вАЛвАЛ).

From the corrected mixed block, the first odd jet we already computed has the generic structure
NЋЩ(0)=(0nвИТn0)
NЋЩ(0)=(0вИТnвАЛn0вАЛ)

for the anomaly side, and the value-family first odd jet has the same symmetry type to leading order.

So the first mixed value/gauge generator should have the generic antisymmetric shape
AN=(0nвИТn0).
ANвАЛ=(0вИТnвАЛn0вАЛ).

This is the first key simplification:
At first odd order, AG is symmetric and AN is antisymmetric.
At first odd order, AGвАЛ is symmetric and ANвАЛ is antisymmetric.вАЛ

That already narrows V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ a lot.
3. Apply the linearized whitening map to these generic shapes

Recall
DW(G0,N0,G0)[ќіGвИТ,ќіN,ќіG+]=ќіXвИТвАЙN0X0+X0вАЙќіNвАЙX0+X0N0вАЙќіX+,

with
X0=G0вИТ1/2,ќіX¬±=L(ќіG¬±).
X0вАЛ=G0вИТ1/2вАЛ,ќіX¬±вАЛ=L(ќіG¬±вАЛ).

At first odd order for a value/gauge generator,
ќіGвИТ=вИТAG,ќіG+=+AG,ќіN=AN,
ќіGвИТвАЛ=вИТAGвАЛ,ќіG+вАЛ=+AGвАЛ,ќіN=ANвАЛ,

so
ќіXвИТ=вИТL(AG),ќіX+=+L(AG).
ќіXвИТвАЛ=вИТL(AGвАЛ),ќіX+вАЛ=+L(AGвАЛ).

Therefore
B(1)=вИТL(AG)N0X0+X0ANX0+X0N0L(AG).

That is the exact symbolic formula for a first value/gauge generator matrix.

So the first value/gauge space is spanned by matrices of the form
B(1)(AG,AN)=X0ANX0+X0N0L(AG)вИТL(AG)N0X0,
B(1)(AGвАЛ,ANвАЛ)=X0вАЛANвАЛX0вАЛ+X0вАЛN0вАЛL(AGвАЛ)вИТL(AGвАЛ)N0вАЛX0вАЛ,вАЛ

with AGAGвАЛ symmetric and ANANвАЛ antisymmetric of the allowed value/gauge types.

That is the first explicit formula you wanted.
4. The most likely basis generators

Because symmetric 2√Ч22√Ч2 matrices are 33-dimensional and antisymmetric ones are 11-dimensional, a natural trial basis is:

For the mixed antisymmetric direction,
AN(1)=(01вИТ10),AG(1)=0.
AN(1)вАЛ=(0вИТ1вАЛ10вАЛ),AG(1)вАЛ=0.

For the symmetric same-point directions,
AG(2)=(1000),AN(2)=0,
AG(2)вАЛ=(10вАЛ00вАЛ),AN(2)вАЛ=0,
AG(3)=(0110),AN(3)=0,
AG(3)вАЛ=(01вАЛ10вАЛ),AN(3)вАЛ=0,
AG(4)=(0001),AN(4)=0.
AG(4)вАЛ=(00вАЛ01вАЛ),AN(4)вАЛ=0.

Then compute
Bj(1):=B(1)(AG(j),AN(j)),j=1,2,3,4.
Bj(1)вАЛ:=B(1)(AG(j)вАЛ,AN(j)вАЛ),j=1,2,3,4.

The actual value/gauge subspace V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ will be the span of whichever of these are genuinely realized by the paperвАЩs value-family model. In practice, it will almost certainly be a proper subspace of the full 4-generator trial span.

But this basis is enough to get started algebraically.
5. What this tells you immediately

The first generator gives
B1(1)=X0(01вИТ10)X0.
B1(1)вАЛ=X0вАЛ(0вИТ1вАЛ10вАЛ)X0вАЛ.

Since X0X0вАЛ is symmetric,
X0(01вИТ10)X0
X0вАЛ(0вИТ1вАЛ10вАЛ)X0вАЛ

is still antisymmetric. So the antisymmetric off-diagonal direction is definitely present in the candidate value-family space.

That strongly supports the earlier conclusion that the old skew detector is not a quotient detector at first order.

The other generators produce symmetric and diagonal corrections through the commutator-like term
X0N0L(AG)вИТL(AG)N0X0.
X0вАЛN0вАЛL(AGвАЛ)вИТL(AGвАЛ)N0вАЛX0вАЛ.

So V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ is likely spanned by:

    one antisymmetric direction,

    possibly one or two symmetric/diagonal directions.

This is exactly what we needed to know qualitatively.
6. How to get the annihilator basis

Now vectorize the Bj(1)Bj(1)вАЛ into C4C4. For example,
(x11x12x21x22)вЖ¶(x11,x12,x21,x22).

B(1)=[vec(B1(1)) vec(B2(1)) vec(B3(1)) vec(B4(1))].
B(1)=[vec(B1(1)вАЛ) vec(B2(1)вАЛ) vec(B3(1)вАЛ) vec(B4(1)вАЛ)].

Then compute the left nullspace:
LвИИkerвБ°((B(1))вК§).
LвИИker((B(1))вК§).

Each basis vector LL in that nullspace gives a quotient coordinate
ќЫ(1)(X)=LвЛЕvec(X).
ќЫ(1)(X)=LвЛЕvec(X).

That is the full symbolic procedure.
7. What comes next after V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ

Once you have this:

F2=(ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5)

become fully explicit.

5. Structural simplifications

You do not need to compute everything blindly. The symmetry types help a lot.

At cubic order:

    AN,1AN,1вАЛ is typically antisymmetric,

    AN,2AN,2вАЛ is typically symmetric/diagonal,

    AN,3AN,3вАЛ may again have mixed symmetry, but the bb-channel we checked sits in antisymmetric off-diagonal.

On the same-point side:

    AG,1AG,1вАЛ is symmetric,

    AG,2AG,2вАЛ is symmetric,

    AG,3AG,3вАЛ is symmetric.

Since LL preserves symmetry for symmetric input, all
X1, X2, X3
X1вАЛ, X2вАЛ, X3вАЛ

are symmetric.

So every cubic value/gauge generator is built out of:

    symmetric XjXjвАЛ,

    symmetric N0N0вАЛ,

    antisymmetric/symmetric AN,jAN,jвАЛ.

That means the cubic value-family is not arbitrary in M2(C)M2вАЛ(C); its image has strong symmetry restrictions. That is exactly what you later exploit to find quotient annihilators.
6. How to get the cubic quotient coordinates

Exactly as at first order:

    Vectorize each BвДУ(3)вИИM2(C)BвДУ(3)вАЛвИИM2вАЛ(C) into C4C4.

    Form the matrix
    B(3)=[vec(B1(3)) вЛѓ vec(Bm(3))].
    B(3)=[vec(B1(3)вАЛ) вЛѓ vec(Bm(3)вАЛ)].

    Compute the left nullspace
    LвИИkerвБ°((B(3))вК§).
    LвИИker((B(3))вК§).

    Each basis vector Lj(3)Lj(3)вАЛ gives a cubic quotient coordinate
    ќЫj(3)(X)=Lj(3)вЛЕvec(X).
    ќЫj(3)вАЛ(X)=Lj(3)вАЛвЛЕvec(X).

Then for the actual anomalous cubic jet J3(p)J3вАЛ(p),
ќЊj(3)(p)=ќЫj(3)(J3(p)).
ќЊj(3)вАЛ(p)=ќЫj(3)вАЛ(J3вАЛ(p)).

Those are the explicit cubic quotient coordinates.
7. Why this matters for the Jacobian branch

Once V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ and V\val(3)V\val(3)вАЛ are explicit, you can compute:

    first quotient coordinates ќЮ1ќЮ1вАЛ,

    cubic quotient coordinates ќЮ3ќЮ3вАЛ,

    and later quintic ќЮ5ќЮ5вАЛ.

Then the three-jet map
F2=(ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5)
F2вАЛ=(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ)

is a concrete analytic map in (a1,h1,a2,h2)(a1вАЛ,h1вАЛ,a2вАЛ,h2вАЛ), not an abstract one.

At that point:

    the Jacobian can be written,

    the resonance functions R13,R15R13вАЛ,R15вАЛ can be written,

    the Wronskian can be written,

    and the exceptional-point rank checks become actual finite computations.

8. The bottleneck now

The true bottleneck is:
Determine the actual realized value/gauge generators (AG,1,AG,2,AG,3;AN,1,AN,2,AN,3) from the paperвАЩs corrected value model.
Determine the actual realized value/gauge generators (AG,1вАЛ,AG,2вАЛ,AG,3вАЛ;AN,1вАЛ,AN,2вАЛ,AN,3вАЛ) from the paperвАЩs corrected value model.вАЛ

Once those are frozen, V\val(3)V\val(3)вАЛ is just algebra.

That is the next concrete subtask on this path.

Continue

The next honest continuation is to pin down what the realized cubic value/gauge generators must look like, even before we know their exact coefficients.

That is enough to narrow V\val(3)V\val(3)вАЛ substantially.
1. What can actually generate cubic value jets

At cubic order, a value/gauge generator can only come from three sources:

the cubic same-point transport,
AG,3,
AG,3вАЛ,

the quadratic interaction of lower same-point jets through the inverse-square-root expansion,
X2(AG,1,AG,2)andX3(AG,1,AG,2,AG,3),
X2вАЛ(AG,1вАЛ,AG,2вАЛ)andX3вАЛ(AG,1вАЛ,AG,2вАЛ,AG,3вАЛ),

and the cubic mixed-block value terms,
AN,3,
AN,3вАЛ,

together with lower mixed terms AN,1,AN,2AN,1вАЛ,AN,2вАЛ coupled to X1,X2X1вАЛ,X2вАЛ.

So V\val(3)V\val(3)вАЛ is spanned by matrices of the form
J3\val=S3(AG,1,AG,2,AG,3)+M3(AN,1,AN,2,AN,3),
J3\valвАЛ=S3вАЛ(AG,1вАЛ,AG,2вАЛ,AG,3вАЛ)+M3вАЛ(AN,1вАЛ,AN,2вАЛ,AN,3вАЛ),

where S3S3вАЛ is the same-point part and M3M3вАЛ the mixed part.

The useful thing is that symmetry already restricts these a lot.
2. Symmetry types at cubic order

Because X1,X2,X3X1вАЛ,X2вАЛ,X3вАЛ all come from symmetric AG,jAG,jвАЛ, they are symmetric matrices.

Because N0N0вАЛ is symmetric, the same-point contribution
вИТX3N0X0+X2N0X1вИТX1N0X2+X0N0X3
вИТX3вАЛN0вАЛX0вАЛ+X2вАЛN0вАЛX1вАЛвИТX1вАЛN0вАЛX2вАЛ+X0вАЛN0вАЛX3вАЛ

is always of the form
symmetric+commutator of symmetric matrices through N0.
symmetric+commutator of symmetric matrices through N0вАЛ.

That means its antisymmetric part is very restricted: it only appears through commutator-type pieces like
X2N0X1вИТX1N0X2.
X2вАЛN0вАЛX1вАЛвИТX1вАЛN0вАЛX2вАЛ.

On the mixed side:

    AN,1AN,1вАЛ is antisymmetric,

    AN,2AN,2вАЛ is symmetric,

    AN,3AN,3вАЛ can have both, but the linear bb-channel showed up antisymmetric.

So the cubic value-family can only contain:

a symmetric/diagonal sector from same-point transport and symmetric mixed terms,

and an antisymmetric sector from commutator-type and odd mixed terms.

This is good, because it suggests V\val(3)V\val(3)вАЛ is probably not all of M2M2вАЛ, and likely low-dimensional.
3. A minimal trial spanning set for V\val(3)V\val(3)вАЛ

Without pretending we know exact coefficients, the right trial generators are:

Take three symmetric prototypes
S1=(1000),S2=(0110),S3=(0001).

4. What to do with that set

Vectorize each trial generator into C4C4, stack them as columns, and row-reduce.

That tells you two things immediately:

the actual dimension of V\val(3)V\val(3)вАЛ,

and an explicit annihilator basis for the cubic quotient.

That is exactly what we need for ќЮ3ќЮ3вАЛ, and later for the Jacobian and resonance functions.
5. The key payoff

L1(3)вАЛ,вА¶,Ld3вАЛ(3)вАЛ,

ќЊj(3)(p)=Lj(3)вЛЕvec(J3(p)).
ќЊj(3)вАЛ(p)=Lj(3)вАЛвЛЕvec(J3вАЛ(p)).

Then the parity-split scalar functions
ќ±3\odd(h),ќ±3\even(h)
ќ±3\oddвАЛ(h),ќ±3\evenвАЛ(h)

become explicit, because they are just the odd/even parts of these coordinates evaluated on the one-pair cubic jet.

This explains why our earlier cubic-detector attempts kept falling back into the same symmetry type.

It also tells us something important about strategy.

If the actual paper-realized cubic value-family behaves like this model, then the cubic quotient could still be large, because many anomalous cubic terms may survive outside this line. But it also means that trying to isolate one cubic detector by hand is fragile, because the value-family itself may already be concentrated in that same antisymmetric channel.

So the Jacobian branch still makes sense, but the next thing to do is not keep guessing cubic detectors. It is to compare the actual anomalous cubic jet against this very small trial value-family span.

    therefore the three-jet map may still have enough quotient room to be transverse, but you now need to compare it against a specific very low-dimensional value-family model, rather than an unknown large one.

That is progress, even if it is not yet a proof.

1. Freeze the trial cubic quotient coordinates

ќЫ11(3)(X):=X11,ќЫ22(3)(X):=X22,ќЫ\sym(3)(X):=X12+X21.

That is a big simplification.
2. What this means for the anomalous cubic jet

To be value-like at cubic order, the actual anomalous cubic jet J3(p)J3вАЛ(p) would have to lie entirely in the skew direction. Equivalently,
J3(p)11=0,J3(p)22=0,J3(p)12+J3(p)21=0.
J3вАЛ(p)11вАЛ=0,J3вАЛ(p)22вАЛ=0,J3вАЛ(p)12вАЛ+J3вАЛ(p)21вАЛ=0.

So the cubic quotient vanishes iff the anomalous cubic jet has the form
J3(p)=(0ќЈвИТќЈ0).
J3вАЛ(p)=(0вИТќЈвАЛќЈ0вАЛ).

That is now a concrete matrix shape condition.
3. How this helps the three-jet map

Now the three-jet map becomes much more explicit in the trial model:

    at order 11, quotient away whatever V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ is;

    at order 33, quotient by the skew line, so the only surviving data are
    J311, J322, J312+J321;
    J3вАЛ11вАЛ, J3вАЛ22вАЛ, J3вАЛ12вАЛ+J3вАЛ21вАЛ;

    similarly at order 55, once a trial V\val(5)V\val(5)вАЛ is fixed.

So the k=2k=2 exclusion problem now includes a very sharp cubic condition:

    Can a genuinely anomalous two-pair cubic jet ever be purely skew?

That is much easier to think about than the full quotient abstraction.
4. The next exact local theorem

So the next theorem to attack is:
Cubic non-skew lemma

For a genuinely anomalous normalized two-pair core, the cubic jet J3(p)J3вАЛ(p) is not purely antisymmetric off-diagonal. Equivalently, at least one of
J3(p)11,J3(p)22,J3(p)12+J3(p)21
J3вАЛ(p)11вАЛ,J3вАЛ(p)22вАЛ,J3вАЛ(p)12вАЛ+J3вАЛ(p)21вАЛ

is nonzero.

If that lemma holds, then the cubic quotient is automatically nonzero in the trial model.

That would be a major win.
5. Why this is plausible

Because the actual anomalous cubic jet receives contributions from:

    same-point transport terms,

    mixed-block cubic terms,

    nonlinear whitening terms.

We already saw many value-family trial generators collapse into the skew direction. But that does not mean the anomalous jet does too.

In fact, the anomalous same-point contributions M1,M2,M3M1вАЛ,M2вАЛ,M3вАЛ naturally generate diagonal and symmetric pieces. So unless there is an unexpected cancellation, a genuine anomaly should not produce a purely skew cubic matrix.

So this is a plausible and testable next statement.
6. A sharper matrix decomposition

Decompose any 2√Ч22√Ч2 matrix as
X=trвБ°X2IвПЯtrace+X+XT2вИТtrвБ°X2IвПЯsymmetric traceless+XвИТXT2вПЯskew.
X=trace
2trXвАЛIвАЛвАЛ+symmetric traceless
2X+XTвАЛвИТ2trXвАЛIвАЛвАЛ+skew
2XвИТXTвАЛвАЛвАЛ.

If the cubic value-family is really only skew, then the quotient is just the trace plus symmetric-traceless part.

So a still cleaner cubic quotient basis is:
ќЫ\tr(3)(X)=X11+X22,
ќЫ\tr(3)вАЛ(X)=X11вАЛ+X22вАЛ,
ќЫ\diff(3)(X)=X11вИТX22,
ќЫ\diff(3)вАЛ(X)=X11вАЛвИТX22вАЛ,
ќЫ\sym(3)(X)=X12+X21.
ќЫ\sym(3)вАЛ(X)=X12вАЛ+X21вАЛ.

These three kill the skew line and split the quotient into natural symmetry sectors.

That is probably the best cubic coordinate choice to freeze next.
7. What the next computation should be

The next concrete thing to do is not more abstract reduction. It is to write the actual anomalous cubic jet in these three coordinates:
ѕД3(p):=J3(p)11+J3(p)22,
ѕД3вАЛ(p):=J3вАЛ(p)11вАЛ+J3вАЛ(p)22вАЛ,
ќі3(p):=J3(p)11вИТJ3(p)22,
ќі3вАЛ(p):=J3вАЛ(p)11вАЛвИТJ3вАЛ(p)22вАЛ,
ѕГ3(p):=J3(p)12+J3(p)21.
ѕГ3вАЛ(p):=J3вАЛ(p)12вАЛ+J3вАЛ(p)21вАЛ.

Then ask:
ѕД3(p)=ќі3(p)=ѕГ3(p)=0 ?
ѕД3вАЛ(p)=ќі3вАЛ(p)=ѕГ3вАЛ(p)=0 ?

If not, cubic quotient noncollapse is proved in the trial model.
8. What this changes strategically

This is better than chasing one magical detector, because now the cubic quotient is represented by three concrete symmetry coordinates.

So the next main-track target becomes:
Show the anomalous cubic jet has a nonzero trace, symmetric-traceless, or symmetric-off-diagonal component.
Show the anomalous cubic jet has a nonzero trace, symmetric-traceless, or symmetric-off-diagonal component.вАЛ

That is the cleanest continuation from the skew-line computation.

If you want, IвАЩll now formalize the corresponding trial three-jet map using these explicit cubic quotient coordinates and show how the Jacobian looks in those coordinates.

Continue

Good. LetвАЩs freeze the trial three-jet map in explicit coordinates.

This is the cleanest version of the Jacobian branch weвАЩve had.
1. Freeze the cubic quotient coordinates

Under the minimal diagonal-baseline trial model, take
V\val(3)=CвАЙk,k:=(01вИТ10).
V\val(3)вАЛ=Ck,k:=(0вИТ1вАЛ10вАЛ).

Then a natural cubic quotient coordinate system is:
ѕД3(X):=X11+X22,
ѕД3вАЛ(X):=X11вАЛ+X22вАЛ,
ќі3(X):=X11вИТX22,
ќі3вАЛ(X):=X11вАЛвИТX22вАЛ,
ѕГ3(X):=X12+X21.
ѕГ3вАЛ(X):=X12вАЛ+X21вАЛ.

These all kill kk, so they descend to the quotient.

So for the actual anomalous cubic jet J3(p)J3вАЛ(p), define
ѕД3(p):=ѕД3(J3(p)),ќі3(p):=ќі3(J3(p)),ѕГ3(p):=ѕГ3(J3(p)).
ѕД3вАЛ(p):=ѕД3вАЛ(J3вАЛ(p)),ќі3вАЛ(p):=ќі3вАЛ(J3вАЛ(p)),ѕГ3вАЛ(p):=ѕГ3вАЛ(J3вАЛ(p)).

ќЮ3\trial(p):=(ѕД3(p),ќі3(p),ѕГ3(p)).
ќЮ3\trialвАЛ(p):=(ѕД3вАЛ(p),ќі3вАЛ(p),ѕГ3вАЛ(p)).

In this trial model, cubic quotient vanishing is equivalent to
ѕД3(p)=ќі3(p)=ѕГ3(p)=0.
ѕД3вАЛ(p)=ќі3вАЛ(p)=ѕГ3вАЛ(p)=0.

That is already much more concrete.
2. Freeze the first-jet quotient coordinates similarly

Do the same at order 11, but do not over-assume the exact value-family dimension yet.

Let V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ be spanned by the first-order value/gauge generators. In the likely case that it contains the skew direction and perhaps one extra gauge direction, pick any annihilator basis
ќЫ1(1),вА¶,ќЫd1(1).
ќЫ1(1)вАЛ,вА¶,ќЫd1вАЛ(1)вАЛ.

Then define the first quotient vector
ќЮ1\trial(p):=(ќЊ1(1)(p),вА¶,ќЊd1(1)(p)),ќЊj(1)(p):=ќЫj(1)(J1(p)).
ќЮ1\trialвАЛ(p):=(ќЊ1(1)вАЛ(p),вА¶,ќЊd1вАЛ(1)вАЛ(p)),ќЊj(1)вАЛ(p):=ќЫj(1)вАЛ(J1вАЛ(p)).

For the Jacobian branch, you really only need enough first-jet coordinates to detect the non-value part. So d1d1вАЛ is probably 11 or 22.
3. Freeze a minimal quintic trial quotient too

At order 55, do the analogous thing:
ќЮ5\trial(p):=(ќЊ1(5)(p),вА¶,ќЊd5(5)(p)),
ќЮ5\trialвАЛ(p):=(ќЊ1(5)вАЛ(p),вА¶,ќЊd5вАЛ(5)вАЛ(p)),

using an annihilator basis of V\val(5)V\val(5)вАЛ.

For the moment, you do not need the full basis. One or two quintic quotient coordinates may be enough for transversality.
4. The trial three-jet map

Now define the explicit trial finite-order map
F2\trial(p):=(ќЮ1\trial(p), ќЮ3\trial(p), ќЮ5\trial(p)).
F2\trialвАЛ(p):=(ќЮ1\trialвАЛ(p), ќЮ3\trialвАЛ(p), ќЮ5\trialвАЛ(p)).

In the simplest useful version, that could be something like
F2\trial(p)=(ќЊ1(1)(p), ѕД3(p), ќі3(p), ѕГ3(p), ќЊ1(5)(p)),
F2\trialвАЛ(p)=(ќЊ1(1)вАЛ(p), ѕД3вАЛ(p), ќі3вАЛ(p), ѕГ3вАЛ(p), ќЊ1(5)вАЛ(p)),

or with one more first/quintic coordinate if needed.

p=(a1,h1,a2,h2).
p=(a1вАЛ,h1вАЛ,a2вАЛ,h2вАЛ).
5. The Jacobian in these coordinates

DF2\trial(p)=вИВвИВ(a1,h1,a2,h2)(ќЊ1(1), ѕД3, ќі3, ѕГ3, ќЊ1(5)).

вИВa1F2\trial,вИВh1F2\trial,вИВa2F2\trial,вИВh2F2\trial.

вИВa1F2\trialвИЉ(ќ±1(h1), ѕД3(1)(h1), ќі3(1)(h1), ѕГ3(1)(h1), ќ±5(h1)),

and similarly at h2h2вАЛ.

That is the fully explicit trial pair-jet curve.
6. The new transversality criterion

In these coordinates, local transversality away from symmetry loci reduces to:
the four vectors V(h1), VвА≤(h1), V(h2), VвА≤(h2) are linearly independent,
the four vectors V(h1вАЛ), VвА≤(h1вАЛ), V(h2вАЛ), VвА≤(h2вАЛ) are linearly independent,вАЛ

where
V(h):=(ќ±1(h), ѕД3(1)(h), ќі3(1)(h), ѕГ3(1)(h), ќ±5(h)).
V(h):=(ќ±1вАЛ(h), ѕД3(1)вАЛ(h), ќі3(1)вАЛ(h), ѕГ3(1)вАЛ(h), ќ±5вАЛ(h)).

This is exactly the same geometry as before, but now in explicit coordinates.
7. Why this is better

This version has two big advantages.

First, the cubic quotient is no longer abstract. It is just:

    trace,

    diagonal difference,

    symmetric off-diagonal.

Second, the question вАЬis the anomalous cubic jet purely skew?вАЭ becomes literally
ѕД3=ќі3=ѕГ3=0?
ѕД3вАЛ=ќі3вАЛ=ѕГ3вАЛ=0?

That is something you can actually test once you write the anomalous cubic jet.
8. The next exact target

So the next concrete theorem to attack is:
Trial cubic non-skew theorem

For a genuinely anomalous normalized two-pair core,
(ѕД3,ќі3,ѕГ3)вЙ†(0,0,0)
(ѕД3вАЛ,ќі3вАЛ,ѕГ3вАЛ)оА†=(0,0,0)

except possibly on degenerate symmetry loci.

If this holds, the cubic quotient is nonzero in the trial model, and the three-jet map has much more room to be transverse.

That is now the sharpest usable local theorem.
9. What remains to make it fully real

To go from this trial map to the actual paper proof, you still need:

    the real V\val(1)V\val(1)вАЛ,

    the real V\val(3)V\val(3)вАЛ,

    the real V\val(5)V\val(5)вАЛ,
    not just the minimal trial model.

But this trial model is now explicit enough to serve as a serious testbed:
if transversality already fails here, the branch is in trouble; if it succeeds here, it is strong evidence the full quotient strategy is right.

So the next actual computation is:
Write J3(p) for the anomalous two-pair core and extract ѕД3(p), ќі3(p), ѕГ3(p).
Write J3вАЛ(p) for the anomalous two-pair core and extract ѕД3вАЛ(p), ќі3вАЛ(p), ѕГ3вАЛ(p).вАЛ

That is the next place the argument stops being abstract and starts producing concrete formulas.

Continue

LetвАЩs continue and extract the anomalous cubic coordinates
ѕД3(p),ќі3(p),ѕГ3(p)
ѕД3вАЛ(p),ќі3вАЛ(p),ѕГ3вАЛ(p)

at the structural level.

I still cannot honestly give final closed formulas in (a1,h1,a2,h2)(a1вАЛ,h1вАЛ,a2вАЛ,h2вАЛ) without freezing the actual baseline and the full anomalous cubic jet recursion. But we can now say exactly what these three coordinates are built from, and that already sharpens the k=2k=2 local problem.
1. The anomalous cubic jet J3(p)J3вАЛ(p)

For the two-pair core,
p=(a1,h1,a2,h2),aj=uj2,
p=(a1вАЛ,h1вАЛ,a2вАЛ,h2вАЛ),ajвАЛ=uj2вАЛ,

write the corrected local block expansion
ќ©^\corr(s;p)=ќ©0+sJ1(p)+s2J2(p)+s3J3(p)+O(s4).
ќ©
\corr(s;p)=ќ©0вАЛ+sJ1вАЛ(p)+s2J2вАЛ(p)+s3J3вАЛ(p)+O(s4).

At cubic order,
J3(p)
J3вАЛ(p)

comes from three kinds of terms:

the linear cubic same-point and mixed block jets,
L3(p),
L3вАЛ(p),

J3вАЛ(p)=L3вАЛ(p)+Q3вАЛ(p)+C3вАЛ(p).вАЛ

That decomposition is exact in structure.
2. Decompose J3J3вАЛ into symmetry sectors

X=trвБ°X2I+12(X11вИТX22X12+X21X12+X21вИТ(X11вИТX22))+12(0X12вИТX21X21вИТX120).

ѕД3(p)=J3(p)11+J3(p)22,

ќі3(p)=J3(p)11вИТJ3(p)22,
ќі3вАЛ(p)=J3вАЛ(p)11вАЛвИТJ3вАЛ(p)22вАЛ,

ѕГ3вАЛ(p)=J3вАЛ(p)12вАЛ+J3вАЛ(p)21вАЛ.

3. Expansion in pair amplitudes

Because each pair enters evenly in its own off-line parameter, the cubic jet has the expansion
J3(p)=a1вАЙA3(h1)+a2вАЙA3(h2)+a1a2вАЙB3(h1,h2)+вЛѓвАЙ,

    B3(h1,h2)B3вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ) is the first genuine two-pair interaction cubic jet.

ѕД3(p)=a1вАЙѕД3(1)(h1)+a2вАЙѕД3(1)(h2)+a1a2вАЙѕД3(12)(h1,h2)+вЛѓвАЙ,

ѕГ3вАЛ(p)=a1вАЛѕГ3(1)вАЛ(h1вАЛ)+a2вАЛѕГ3(1)вАЛ(h2вАЛ)+a1вАЛa2вАЛѕГ3(12)вАЛ(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)+вЛѓ.

This is the cubic analogue of the earlier scalar expansions.
4. What the one-pair cubic coordinates should look like

For one pair, the cubic jet splits into odd/even hh-parity pieces:
A3(h)=A3\odd(h)+A3\even(h).
A3вАЛ(h)=A3\oddвАЛ(h)+A3\evenвАЛ(h).

So each coordinate does too:
ѕД3(1)(h)=ѕД3,\odd(1)(h)+ѕД3,\even(1)(h),
ѕД3(1)вАЛ(h)=ѕД3,\odd(1)вАЛ(h)+ѕД3,\even(1)вАЛ(h),

and similarly for ќі3(1),ѕГ3(1)ќі3(1)вАЛ,ѕГ3(1)вАЛ.

From the earlier structure:

    the odd part comes from linear cubic same-point transport terms, so it should be of order hвИТ7hвИТ7;

    the even part comes from nonlinear quadratic same-point terms, so it should be of order hвИТ10hвИТ10, if it survives.

So the natural asymptotic model is
ѕД3(1)(h)вИЉcѕДhвИТ7+eѕДhвИТ10+вЛѓвАЙ,
ѕД3(1)вАЛ(h)вИЉcѕДвАЛhвИТ7+eѕДвАЛhвИТ10+вЛѓ,
ќі3(1)(h)вИЉcќіhвИТ7+eќіhвИТ10+вЛѓвАЙ,
ќі3(1)вАЛ(h)вИЉcќівАЛhвИТ7+eќівАЛhвИТ10+вЛѓ,
ѕГ3(1)(h)вИЉcѕГhвИТ7+eѕГhвИТ10+вЛѓвАЙ.
ѕГ3(1)вАЛ(h)вИЉcѕГвАЛhвИТ7+eѕГвАЛhвИТ10+вЛѓ.

The important point is not the exact constants. It is that the cubic quotient coordinates should contain both hвИТ7hвИТ7 odd data and hвИТ10hвИТ10 even data.
5. What it means for J3J3вАЛ to be purely skew

In the trial model, cubic quotient vanishing means
ѕД3(p)=0,ќі3(p)=0,ѕГ3(p)=0.
ѕД3вАЛ(p)=0,ќі3вАЛ(p)=0,ѕГ3вАЛ(p)=0.

At leading order, that requires
a1(ѕД3(1)(h1)ќі3(1)(h1)ѕГ3(1)(h1))+a2(ѕД3(1)(h2)ќі3(1)(h2)ѕГ3(1)(h2))вЙИ0
a1вАЛ
вАЛѕД3(1)вАЛ(h1вАЛ)ќі3(1)вАЛ(h1вАЛ)ѕГ3(1)вАЛ(h1вАЛ)вАЛ
вАЛ+a2вАЛ
вАЛѕД3(1)вАЛ(h2вАЛ)ќі3(1)вАЛ(h2вАЛ)ѕГ3(1)вАЛ(h2вАЛ)вАЛ
вАЛвЙИ0

before interaction corrections are even considered.

So the cubic non-skew problem is just the collinearity/cancellation problem for the one-pair cubic coordinate curve
hвЯЉ(ѕД3(1)(h),ќі3(1)(h),ѕГ3(1)(h)).
hвЯЉ(ѕД3(1)вАЛ(h),ќі3(1)вАЛ(h),ѕГ3(1)вАЛ(h)).

That is the cubic analogue of the earlier pair-jet curve.
6. Why this helps the Jacobian branch

The full trial three-jet map can now be viewed as
V(h):=(ќ±1(h),ѕД3(1)(h),ќі3(1)(h),ѕГ3(1)(h),ќ±5(h)).
V(h):=(ќ±1вАЛ(h),ѕД3(1)вАЛ(h),ќі3(1)вАЛ(h),ѕГ3(1)вАЛ(h),ќ±5вАЛ(h)).

Then away from symmetry loci, the Jacobian columns are controlled by
V(h1),VвА≤(h1),V(h2),VвА≤(h2).
V(h1вАЛ),VвА≤(h1вАЛ),V(h2вАЛ),VвА≤(h2вАЛ).

So local transversality reduces to whether this five-coordinate curve is nondegenerate.

That is much better than working with abstract quotient classes.
7. The next exact target

The next real theorem to attack is:
Trial cubic non-skew / curve nondegeneracy lemma

The one-pair cubic coordinate curve
hвЯЉ(ѕД3(1)(h),ќі3(1)(h),ѕГ3(1)(h))
hвЯЉ(ѕД3(1)вАЛ(h),ќі3(1)вАЛ(h),ѕГ3(1)вАЛ(h))

is not identically zero and is not confined to a line that would allow generic two-pair cancellation.

If true, then the cubic quotient contributes genuine independent data to the three-jet map.

That would make the Jacobian route much stronger.
8. What still blocks a full formula

To make ѕД3(1),ќі3(1),ѕГ3(1)ѕД3(1)вАЛ,ќі3(1)вАЛ,ѕГ3(1)вАЛ explicit, you still need the actual one-pair cubic jet matrix A3(h)A3вАЛ(h), not just its abstract decomposition. That means carrying the same-point and mixed-block cubic recursion through for the anomalous core.

Continue

I pushed the next subtask, and there is a real correction.

In the minimal diagonal-baseline trial model, the same-point cubic contribution also collapses to the skew line.

Here is the calculation.
1. The same-point cubic term

X0вАЛ=(x1вАЛ0вАЛ0x2вАЛвАЛ),N0вАЛ=(abвАЛbdвАЛ).

    for the diagonal generators S1,S3S1вАЛ,S3вАЛ,
    X1 is diagonal,X2 is diagonal;
    X1вАЛ is diagonal,X2вАЛ is diagonal;

    for the off-diagonal symmetric generator S2S2вАЛ,
    X1 is symmetric off-diagonal,X2 is diagonal.
    X1вАЛ is symmetric off-diagonal,X2вАЛ is diagonal.

    for S1,S3S1вАЛ,S3вАЛ, X3X3вАЛ is diagonal;

    for S2S2вАЛ, X3X3вАЛ is symmetric off-diagonal.

5. What this means

This is a serious warning.

It means the trial model is too symmetric/commutative to generate cubic quotient information. So the nice Jacobian story with explicit cubic quotient coordinates does not close in this simplified model.

There are only two ways out:

Either the actual baseline/value model is less commutative than this trial model, so V\val(3)V\val(3)вАЛ and the anomalous cubic jet are richer than this simplified calculation suggests.

Or cubic order is genuinely too low, and the first reliable quotient information appears at quintic order.
6. Strategic consequence

This strongly suggests:
Do not rely on cubic quotient transversality in the minimal model.
Do not rely on cubic quotient transversality in the minimal model.вАЛ

So the next serious options are:

    compute the actual V\val(3)V\val(3)вАЛ and the actual anomalous cubic jet, not the minimal diagonal approximation, and see whether cubic quotient survives there;

    or, more robustly, shift the finite-order exclusion/Jacobian branch to
    (ќЮ1,ќЮ5,ќЮ7)or(ќЮ1,ќЮ5)
    (ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ,ќЮ7вАЛ)or(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ)

    instead of (ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5)(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ).

Given what we just found, I think the second is now more plausible.
7. The updated local story

So the current state is:

    first jet quotient may survive;

    cubic quotient can collapse completely in the minimal symmetric model;

    therefore the first nontrivial bounded-order exclusion theorem may need to start at quintic, not cubic.

That changes the natural finite-order map from
F2=(ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5)
F2вАЛ=(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ)

to something more like
F2\new=(ќЮ1,ќЮ5,ќЮ7)
F2\newвАЛ=(ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ,ќЮ7вАЛ)

or at least forces us to verify whether ќЮ3ќЮ3вАЛ is actually informative in the real model before using it.
8. Bottom line

The continuation of this branch is now:
Either compute the real cubic model beyond the minimal diagonal approximation, or promote the finite-order exclusion map to skip cubic order if cubic quotient collapses.
Either compute the real cubic model beyond the minimal diagonal approximation, or promote the finite-order exclusion map to skip cubic order if cubic quotient collapses.вАЛ

That is the strongest honest continuation from the latest computation.

Can we somehow consider all odd orders?

Yes. In fact, I think that is probably the right abstraction now.

The clean object is not any fixed odd order, and not even a finite list like (1,3,5)(1,3,5). It is the entire odd quotient germ.

Write the corrected local germ modulo the value/gauge family as
F\core(z)вИИG\odd/V\val,
F\coreвАЛ(z)вИИG\oddвАЛ/V\valвАЛ,

with expansion
F\core(z)=вИСrвЙ•0ќЮ2r+1\corez2r+1.
F\coreвАЛ(z)=rвЙ•0вИСвАЛќЮ2r+1\coreвАЛz2r+1.

Then the real invariant is its order of vanishing
ќљ(\core):=minвБ°{rвЙ•0: ќЮ2r+1\coreвЙ†0},
ќљ(\core):=min{rвЙ•0: ќЮ2r+1\coreвАЛоА†=0},

with the convention ќљ(\core)=вИЮќљ(\core)=вИЮ if the quotient germ vanishes identically.

That is exactly the вАЬconsider all odd ordersвАЭ version.

There are three ways to use it.

First, the soft version. Instead of proving a specific jet survives, prove that the quotient germ is not identically zero:
F\coreвЙ†0.
F\coreвАЛоА†=0.

Then some odd order survives automatically. This is the cleanest conceptual statement, and it matches the fixed-core upgrade very well.

Second, the compactness version. On a compact normalized parameter set, try to prove that ќљ(\core)ќљ(\core) is uniformly bounded:
ќљ(\core)вЙ§Rk
ќљ(\core)вЙ§RkвАЛ

for all genuine kk-cores. That is the bounded-order noncollapse theorem we kept circling around. This is stronger, because it turns вАЬsome odd orderвАЭ into вАЬsome odd order among finitely many.вАЭ

Third, the generating-function version. Instead of looking at coefficients one by one, choose an odd test functional LL and consider a scalar odd germ
H\core(z):=L(F\core(z)).
H\coreвАЛ(z):=L(F\coreвАЛ(z)).

Then you study the whole odd function at once, and the first surviving odd coefficient is just its vanishing order at 00. This is often cleaner than juggling separate jet coordinates.

So yes, considering all odd orders is not only possible, it is probably the right endgame language.

Where it helps most is exactly where the current branch became fragile. We kept finding that:

    first jet can be too weak,

    cubic can collapse in symmetric models,

    a hand-picked detector can become value-like.

The whole odd germ avoids that. You stop betting on one special order and instead ask:
Can a genuine finite off-line core make the entire odd quotient germ vanish?
Can a genuine finite off-line core make the entire odd quotient germ vanish?вАЛ

That is the right local theorem.

If you can prove the answer is no, then RH reduces to one more step: compare the first surviving odd order of the core against the outside bound at that same order. Since the outside contribution gets smaller at higher order,
вИ£a2r+1\outвИ£вЙ™1QH2r+3,
вИ£a2r+1\outвАЛвИ£вЙ™QH2r+31вАЛ,

this is actually compatible with the вАЬall odd ordersвАЭ viewpoint.

The catch is that вАЬconsider all odd ordersвАЭ by itself is too weak for contradiction. To finish, you still need one of these stronger statements:

    a bounded-order theorem: the first surviving odd order is uniformly bounded on compact normalized parameter sets;

    or a quantitative noncollapse theorem: the whole quotient germ stays a positive distance from zero in some Banach norm, which would imply such a bound by compactness.

That is the real reason we kept reducing to finite-order maps. Not because all odd orders are wrong, but because RH needs a uniform finite-order witness eventually.

So my view is:
Yes вАФ the right master object is the full odd quotient germ, and the right theorem is that it cannot vanish identically for a genuine finite core.
Yes вАФ the right master object is the full odd quotient germ, and the right theorem is that it cannot vanish identically for a genuine finite core.вАЛ

Then the technical problem becomes:
upgrade вАЬnot identically zeroвАЭ to вАЬsome uniformly bounded odd order survives on compact normalized families.вАЭ
upgrade вАЬnot identically zeroвАЭ to вАЬsome uniformly bounded odd order survives on compact normalized families.вАЭвАЛ

That is the clean global formulation of everything we have been doing.

The most natural next formal statement is:

    Odd-germ nonvanishing theorem.
    For every genuine normalized finite off-line core, the quotient odd germ F\coreF\coreвАЛ is not identically zero.

And then:

    Compact bounded-order corollary.
    On each compact normalized kk-core parameter set, there exists RkRkвАЛ such that F\coreF\coreвАЛ has some nonzero odd coefficient among orders вЙ§2Rk+1вЙ§2RkвАЛ+1.

That is, I think, the right way to reorganize the local part of the paper now.

So how do we attack it?

Attack it in two layers.

The first layer is the right theorem to aim for. The second is the route that has the best chance of proving it.

The theorem you want is not вАЬsome specific jet is nonzero.вАЭ It is:
F\coreвЙ°ћЄ0in G\odd/V\val
F\coreвАЛоА†вЙ°0in G\oddвАЛ/V\valвАЛ

for every genuine normalized finite off-line core.

Then, by compactness on a fixed kk-core parameter set, that upgrades to a bounded-order statement automatically.

So the attack should be:
1. Prove zero-germ exclusion, not jet nondegeneracy

Assume for contradiction that a genuine normalized finite core satisfies
F\coreвЙ°0.
F\coreвАЛвЙ°0.

That means every odd quotient jet vanishes. So the corrected local germ lies entirely in the value/gauge family.

This is much stronger than any finite-order cancellation. It says the anomalous core is locally indistinguishable from pure value motion to all odd orders.

That is the contradiction you want to attack.
2. Use finite-dimensional analyticity of the core family

For fixed core size kk, the normalized finite core depends on finitely many real parameters. Call them pвИИPkpвИИPkвАЛ.

The map
pвЖ¶Fp(z)
pвЖ¶FpвАЛ(z)

is an analytic family of odd germs.

So the set
Zk:={p: FpвЙ°0}
ZkвАЛ:={p: FpвАЛвЙ°0}

is an analytic subset of finite-dimensional parameter space.

That is powerful, because it means you do not need to defeat infinitely many conditions one by one. You need to show that this analytic subset contains no genuine anomalous point.

So the first real move is:
Show that Zk is contained in the degenerate/value-like locus.
Show that ZkвАЛ is contained in the degenerate/value-like locus.вАЛ

That is the master reduction.
3. Split parameter space into escape and compact parts

You already learned this is the right split.

If some core component escapes in scaled coordinates, its contribution vanishes in the normalized germ. So escaping components are harmless.

That means any genuine obstruction to zero-germ exclusion lives in a compact normalized parameter family.

So the attack becomes:

    prove escaping components disappear;

    reduce to compact parameter sets;

    on compact sets, use analyticity and dimension.

This is already much better than fighting all configurations at once.
4. For compact families, use minimal-counterexample order

Suppose zero-germ exclusion fails. Then among all bad compact-parameter cores, pick one with minimal core size kk, and inside that family, consider a point where the order of contact with V\valV\valвАЛ is maximal.

This is useful because if the whole odd germ vanishes, then every finite truncation vanishes too. You can try to derive structural consequences from the lowest nontrivial interaction between components.

In practice, for a minimal bad kk, one of two things should happen:

    either the core splits into smaller independent subcores, contradicting minimality;

    or the parameters satisfy a rigid resonance system, which should force degeneracy.

So the right contradiction format is:
Assume a minimal genuine bad core with F\coreвЙ°0, and exploit minimality.
Assume a minimal genuine bad core with F\coreвАЛвЙ°0, and exploit minimality.вАЛ
5. Use symmetry-type decomposition, not a single scalar

A big lesson from the recent computations is that one detector can fail for symmetry reasons.

So decompose the odd germ into symmetry sectors:

    trace,

    symmetric traceless,

    skew.

Then ask whether all sectors can vanish to all odd orders.

This is much stronger than any one scalar cancellation. In the trial cubic model, for example, the value-family seemed concentrated in the skew sector. That suggests the real obstruction is:

    can a genuine anomalous core force the non-skew sectors to vanish to all odd orders?

That is much less plausible.

So one concrete attack is:
Project the odd germ into symmetry sectors and try to show at least one sector must survive.
Project the odd germ into symmetry sectors and try to show at least one sector must survive.вАЛ
6. Attack with the full pair-jet curve, not isolated orders

For k=2k=2, define the full one-pair quotient odd germ curve
hвЖ¶Fh(z).
hвЖ¶FhвАЛ(z).

Then two-pair vanishing would mean
a1Fh1(z)+a2Fh2(z)+a1a2Gh1,h2(z)+вЛѓвЙ°0.
a1вАЛFh1вАЛвАЛ(z)+a2вАЛFh2вАЛвАЛ(z)+a1вАЛa2вАЛGh1вАЛ,h2вАЛвАЛ(z)+вЛѓвЙ°0.

At leading order in amplitudes, this says the whole odd germ Fh1Fh1вАЛвАЛ must be negatively proportional to Fh2Fh2вАЛвАЛ.

That is much stronger than matching ќЮ1,ќЮ3,ќЮ5ќЮ1вАЛ,ќЮ3вАЛ,ќЮ5вАЛ. It means the two pair-germs are proportional as analytic germs.

So for k=2k=2, the right attack is:
Show that hвЖ¶Fh(z) is not projectively constant as an odd germ.
Show that hвЖ¶FhвАЛ(z) is not projectively constant as an odd germ.вАЛ

Equivalently, find two odd orders whose coefficient ratios vary with hh. That was the spirit of the R13,R15R13вАЛ,R15вАЛ and Wronskian reductions, but now phrased at the germ level.

If the projective germ curve is nonconstant, two different hh's cannot cancel to all odd orders except on isolated degenerate loci.
7. The best technical route: analytic-subset plus transversality

So the practical strategy is:

First, define
Zk={p:FpвЙ°0}.
ZkвАЛ={p:FpвАЛвЙ°0}.

Second, show ZkZkвАЛ is analytic.

Third, show its tangent space cannot contain the genuine anomalous directions away from obvious degeneracies. That is the transversality part.

вАЛp=pвИЧвАЛвАЛ.

If the image of that differential is not contained in V\valV\valвАЛ, then pвИЧвИЙZkpвИЧвАЛвИИ/ZkвАЛ as a stable point.

So the exact local attack is:
At any putative bad point, show the parameter derivative of the odd germ leaves V\val.
At any putative bad point, show the parameter derivative of the odd germ leaves V\valвАЛ.вАЛ

That is the infinite-order analogue of the Jacobian idea.
8. For k=2k=2, the concrete plan is now clear

If you want the best shot at an actual theorem soon, do this:

9. What I think is the best overall attack

If I had to choose one route now, I would do this:
Prove zero-germ exclusion by showing the projective odd-germ curve of a finite core is nonconstant, and then use compactness to get bounded surviving order.
Prove zero-germ exclusion by showing the projective odd-germ curve of a finite core is nonconstant, and then use compactness to get bounded surviving order.вАЛ

    and use transversality of the full odd germ, not one detector.

5. Why this should work here

    cubic looked fragile in the minimal model, but that is not fatal, because projective nonconstancy only needs some two quotient functionals whose coefficient-ratio sequence is not constant.

5. Why this is plausible here

Everything we have learned points to the same structure:

    the value-family seems low-dimensional in the trial models;

    the one-pair anomalous germ almost certainly has more than one symmetry sector available;

    first jet alone may be fragile, cubic may collapse in simplified models, but that just means the вАЬnext independent sectorвАЭ may occur later.

That is fine. The two-functional argument is indifferent to where the second sector appears.

So the all-orders route is actually more forgiving than the finite-order one.
6. What the actual construction problem is

    Compute the leading one-pair quotient direction q1(h)q1вАЛ(h) in odd-germ space.

    M(q1(h))=0

    L(Fh)L(FhвАЛ) has a nonzero first surviving coefficient,

    M(Fh)M(FhвАЛ) has a later nonzero coefficient,

    so M(Fh)/L(Fh)M(FhвАЛ)/L(FhвАЛ) depends on zz.

7. The best practical way to attack that

The most practical attack is to use the quotient coordinates we were already trying to build.

Suppose at order 11 the quotient is one-dimensional generically. Then LL is fixed up to scale.

Then inspect order 33, 55, 77, ... until you find the first odd order where the one-pair jet has a component outside the line determined by LL.

The first such order produces MM.

So the theorem you really want is:
One-pair second-sector theorem

The one-pair quotient odd germ has at least two independent quotient sectors; equivalently, it is not contained in a one-dimensional quotient line.

That is the all-orders local core.
8. How this kills two-pair cancellation

If FhFhвАЛ is projectively nonconstant, then a two-pair identity
a1Fh1+a2Fh2вЙ°0
a1вАЛFh1вАЛвАЛ+a2вАЛFh2вАЛвАЛвЙ°0

implies
Fh2вЙ°вИТa1a2Fh1,
Fh2вАЛвАЛвЙ°вИТa2вАЛa1вАЛвАЛFh1вАЛвАЛ,

so Fh1Fh1вАЛвАЛ and Fh2Fh2вАЛвАЛ are projectively equal. That can happen only on a very small set:

    perhaps isolated symmetry points,

    perhaps degenerate clustering,

    but not generically.

So the all-orders route really does collapse the k=2k=2 problem to a projective nonconstancy theorem plus a small exceptional-set analysis.
9. The next exact target

So the next theorem to attack is:
The one-pair quotient odd germ is not contained in a one-dimensional quotient subspace as h varies.
The one-pair quotient odd germ is not contained in a one-dimensional quotient subspace as h varies.вАЛ

Equivalently:
вИГвАЙL,M quotient functionals such that M(Fh)/L(Fh) is nonconstant in z for generic h.

That is the cleanest вАЬall odd ordersвАЭ local theorem now.
10. What I would do next

I would now stop trying to force a specific odd order and instead frame the next computation as:

    choose LL to detect the leading surviving quotient sector;

    search for the first odd order where a second independent quotient sector appears;

    define MM to kill the first sector and detect that second one.

That is the most robust continuation of this branch.

Continue

1. The right theorem

2. How to attack it

Suppose the opposite: there is a fixed quotient line, so
Fh(z)=c(h)вАЙG(z).
FhвАЛ(z)=c(h)G(z).

M(ќЮ2r+1(h))L(ќЮ2r+1(h))
L(ќЮ2r+1вАЛ(h))M(ќЮ2r+1вАЛ(h))вАЛ

must be independent of rr.

That gives the exact coefficient test:
If the one-pair quotient germ were projectively constant, then the quotient coefficient ratios would be independent of odd order.
If the one-pair quotient germ were projectively constant, then the quotient coefficient ratios would be independent of odd order.вАЛ

So to disprove projective constancy, it is enough to show that at two different odd orders those ratios differ.
3. The minimal usable criterion

Pick two quotient functionals L,ML,M. Write the scalar odd germs
Hh(z)=L(Fh(z))=вИСrвЙ•0A2r+1(h)вАЙz2r+1,

That is enough.

This is the all-orders master version of the earlier R13,R15R13вАЛ,R15вАЛ logic.
4. The best choice of LL and MM

The robust choice is:

    LL detects the leading surviving quotient sector;

    MM kills that sector and detects the next independent sector.

Then automatically:

    Hh(z)HhвАЛ(z) starts at some lowest odd order 2m+12m+1,

    Kh(z)KhвАЛ(z) starts at a strictly later odd order 2n+12n+1, n>mn>m,

so
Kh(z)Hh(z)
HhвАЛ(z)KhвАЛ(z)вАЛ

has a zero of order 2(nвИТm)2(nвИТm) at z=0z=0, hence is not constant in zz.

This is the cleanest possible attack.

So the actual problem is now:
Show the one-pair quotient odd germ has a second independent sector beyond the leading one.
Show the one-pair quotient odd germ has a second independent sector beyond the leading one.вАЛ
5. Why this is plausible from what we already know

We learned several things that point in exactly this direction.

First, first order is not the whole story. Even when the first surviving quotient sector is fragile or symmetry-restricted, higher odd orders still carry new information.

Second, in the simplified cubic trial model, the value-family looked very small, but the anomalous cubic structure did not obviously collapse for deep reasons. That suggests the quotient odd germ is richer than a single line.

Third, the asymptotic scaling by order is different:

    leading odd pieces scale like hвИТ5,hвИТ7,hвИТ9,вА¶hвИТ5,hвИТ7,hвИТ9,вА¶,

    even corrections scale differently again.

A genuinely one-dimensional projective germ family would force a very rigid pattern across all odd orders. That seems much less plausible than failure at one or two low orders.
6. The next exact theorem

So the sharp next statement is:
One-pair second-sector theorem

There exist quotient functionals L,ML,M such that

    L(Fh)вЙ°ћЄ0L(FhвАЛ)оА†вЙ°0,

    MM annihilates the leading quotient sector detected by LL,

    but M(Fh)вЙ°ћЄ0M(FhвАЛ)оА†вЙ°0.

Then FhFhвАЛ is not projectively constant as a quotient odd germ.

This is exactly the theorem we need.
7. How to prove it concretely

There are two realistic routes.
Route A: finite-order detection

Show that after quotienting by the leading sector, some odd jet at bounded order survives.

In practice:

    compute the leading one-pair quotient sector,

    quotient it out,

    inspect the first few higher odd orders until you find a nonzero remainder.

That produces MM.

This is still finite-order, but now used only to prove an all-orders projective statement.
Route B: symmetry-sector separation

Show the one-pair quotient germ has contributions in two different symmetry sectors that cannot be scalar multiples of each other as germs.

For example:

    one sector dominated by the first odd trace/diagonal behavior,

    another sector dominated by a later symmetric or traceless behavior.

Then LL and MM are just the corresponding symmetry-sector projections.

I think Route B is conceptually cleaner, but Route A may be easier to execute.
8. What this would buy for k=2k=2

If the one-pair quotient germ is projectively nonconstant, then a two-pair full-germ cancellation
a1Fh1+a2Fh2вЙ°0
a1вАЛFh1вАЛвАЛ+a2вАЛFh2вАЛвАЛвЙ°0

forces
[Fh1]=[Fh2],
[Fh1вАЛвАЛ]=[Fh2вАЛвАЛ],

so it can only happen on a very small subset of the (h1,h2)(h1вАЛ,h2вАЛ)-plane:

    symmetry points,

    clustering points,

    or isolated accidental intersections.

So the whole k=2k=2 branch collapses to a small exceptional-set analysis.

That is much stronger than any one chosen jet order.

4. Why this is the right next move

It avoids committing to one fixed odd order globally. It only compares:

    the leading surviving order,

    and the next order where another sector might appear.

5. The actual second-sector theorem

Second-sector theorem

6. What this gives for two-pair cancellation

a1Fh1(z)+a2Fh2(z)вЙ°0
a1вАЛFh1вАЛвАЛ(z)+a2вАЛFh2вАЛвАЛ(z)вЙ°0

in projective quotient-germ space.

hвЖ¶[Fh].
hвЖ¶[FhвАЛ].

That is automatically much smaller than all of parameter space. In particular, it should be at worst isolated or symmetry-forced after compactness.

So the second-sector theorem is exactly the all-orders replacement for the three-jet exclusion story.

1. Work with the full germ first

FhвАЛ(z)=c(h)G(z)

for some fixed quotient germ GG.

Then every odd coefficient of FhFhвАЛ is proportional to the same hh-independent coefficient sequence.

That is the real rigid statement to attack.
2. Use finite orders only to refute projective constancy

A finite-order computation is still useful, but only in this form:

    find some finite truncation where projective constancy would force a relation that fails.

That is very different from saying вАЬthe proof runs through order 55вАЭ or вАЬorder 33 is load-bearing.вАЭ

So the right logic is:

    theorem: full germ not projectively constant;

    method of proof: maybe check two or three low orders to contradict projective constancy.

Not the other way around.
3. The clean invariant is the residual projective germ

The best formulation we found was actually the right one:

pick a functional LL detecting the leading quotient line, normalize by it,
F~h(z):=Fh(z)L(Fh(z)),
F
hвАЛ(z):=L(FhвАЛ(z))FhвАЛ(z)вАЛ,

and then ask whether F~h(z)F
hвАЛ(z) is constant in zz.

That is already order-free.

If it is not constant, then there is a second sector. The first place that second sector shows up may be order 33, or 55, or 77, or later. It does not matter conceptually.

So the real theorem is:
After quotienting by the leading line, the normalized one-pair germ still has nonzero z-dependence.
After quotienting by the leading line, the normalized one-pair germ still has nonzero z-dependence.вАЛ

That is the right вАЬall odd ordersвАЭ statement.
4. Then compactness gives bounded order afterward

Once you know the full germ is not projectively constant, and you restrict to a compact normalized parameter family, standard compactness/analyticity arguments say this nonconstancy must be visible by some bounded odd order.

So bounded-order statements like
вАЬthere exists Rk such that some odd coefficient up to 2Rk+1 survivesвАЭ
вАЬthere exists RkвАЛ such that some odd coefficient up to 2RkвАЛ+1 survivesвАЭ

should be corollaries, not the headline.
5. So what should we do next?

Stop phrasing the goal as вАЬfind the right next odd order.вАЭ

Instead, phrase it as:
Prove the normalized residual quotient germ is not constant in z.
Prove the normalized residual quotient germ is not constant in z.вАЛ

And then use whatever finite-order witness is easiest to show that.

That witness might end up being order 55, but that would just be the first place the contradiction becomes visible, not a structural commitment of the paper.

So your instinct is right: if we keep getting re-attached to specific orders, we are slipping back into the wrong mode. The theorem should live at the full-germ level.

Ok whats the next step?

The next step is to stop chasing вАЬwhich odd orderвАЭ and prove a full-germ normalization lemma.

Precisely:

Take the one-pair quotient odd germ Fh(z)FhвАЛ(z). Choose one quotient functional LL that detects the generic leading one-pair line, and form
F~h(z):=Fh(z)L(Fh(z)).
F
hвАЛ(z):=L(FhвАЛ(z))FhвАЛ(z)вАЛ.

Then try to prove:
F~h(z) is not constant in z for generic h.
F
hвАЛ(z) is not constant in z for generic h.вАЛ

That is the right next theorem. It is the all-orders version of everything we were doing.

Why this is the next step:

    if F~hF
    hвАЛ were constant in zz, the whole one-pair family would lie in a single projective quotient-germ line;

    then two-pair cancellation could plausibly happen along a large set;

    if F~hF
    hвАЛ is nonconstant, then the one-pair family has a genuine second sector, and two-pair full-germ cancellation gets forced into a very small exceptional set.

So the concrete subtask is:

    Define the leading line rigorously: identify the generic first nonzero quotient coefficient line of FhFhвАЛ.

    Normalize by it: write F~h=Fh/L(Fh)F
    hвАЛ=FhвАЛ/L(FhвАЛ).

That last line is the first real witness of projective nonconstancy.

So the next proof target is:

    Residual germ nontriviality lemma.
    After dividing by the generic leading quotient line, the one-pair quotient odd germ still has nonzero zz-dependence.

That is the clean next move.

4. Why this is the right next step

Because now the problem is no longer вАЬwhich odd order matters?вАЭ It is:

4. The asymptotic way to attack M(v1(h))M(v1вАЛ(h))

7. Why this is enough for the paperвАЩs local logic

Once you prove the one-pair quotient germ is projectively nonconstant, then a two-pair full-germ cancellation
a1Fh1+a2Fh2вЙ°0
a1вАЛFh1вАЛвАЛ+a2вАЛFh2вАЛвАЛвЙ°0

forces
[Fh1]=[Fh2],
[Fh1вАЛвАЛ]=[Fh2вАЛвАЛ],

so the bad locus is the self-intersection locus of the projective germ curve. That is automatically much smaller than the full parameter space and should collapse to symmetry/degeneracy plus isolated exceptions.

So this one theorem is the right local upgrade.
8. Bottom line

1. The most useful reformulation of R1R1вАЛ

Pick a second quotient functional MM such that
M(v0(h))=0
M(v0вАЛ(h))=0

on the generic leading line.

Then
M(R1(h))=a0(h)вАЙM(v1(h)).
M(R1вАЛ(h))=a0вАЛ(h)M(v1вАЛ(h)).

M(v1(h))вЙ°ћЄ0.
M(v1вАЛ(h))оА†вЙ°0.

There exists a quotient functional M annihilating the leading line with M(v1вАЛ(h))оА†вЙ°0.вАЛ
